4::amorninginmyneighborhood.go_usage: 为每个社区人物完成档案卡，用"Without…"句型描述其重要性

4::papercranes.title: Paper Cranes
4::papercranes.teaching_focus: Practice past simple 'found', object nouns and short place phrases with 'under'. Good for building substitution drills and speaking: change the subject, the small object, and the place.
4::papercranes.reading_strategy: 推断与结论 (Make Inferences and Draw Conclusions)
4::papercranes.reading_skill: 推断与结论 (Make Inferences and Draw Conclusions)
4::papercranes.questions_type:
1. Reading for Details:
• Where did Ravi find the paper crane? (Under a mango tree)
• What had Ravi learned about origami cranes at school? (They are symbols of good luck)
• Where did Ravi place the crane? (On his bedroom shelf)
• When did Ravi practice folding cranes? (Every afternoon after school)
• Where did Ravi plan to send his best paper crane? (To a school in Tokyo)

2. Reading Skill:
• Why was finding the crane important for Ravi? (It started his interest in Japan and origami)
• How do you know Ravi was determined? (He practiced every afternoon after school)
• What did Ravi believe at the end? (That his dream could come true)

3. Other Comprehension:
• (Main Idea) What is the story mostly about? • (Inference) Why do you think Ravi wanted to send a crane to Tokyo? • (Vocabulary) What does symbol mean? • (Feelings) How did Ravi feel when he learned about origami cranes? • (Connection) Have you ever made something by hand to give to someone? How did it feel?
4::papercranes.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Where did Ravi find the paper crane? (Under a mango tree)
2. (On) What are origami cranes symbols of? (Good luck)
3. (On) Where did Ravi plan to send his best paper crane? (To a school in Tokyo)
4. (On) How do you know Ravi was determined to learn? (He practiced every afternoon after school)
5. (Above) Why do you think Ravi wanted to send a crane to a school in Tokyo? (Because he wanted to share his new skill and connect with Japan / show his good wishes)
4::papercranes.go_description: 左侧记录 Ravi 的情感变化：Found crane（curious）→ Learned about origami（excited）→ Practiced folding（determined）→ Planned to send a crane（hopeful）；右侧写象征：Paper crane = Good luck / Dream of Japan；箭头连接情感与象征
4::papercranes.go_usage: 引导学生推断 Ravi 每个行动背后的情感动机，用象征图理解折纸鹤在故事中的文化意义，练习 '… found a small … under …' 句型

4::japan.title: Japan
4::japan.reading_skill: 主旨与支撑细节 (Main Idea and Supporting Details)
4::japan.questions_type:
1. Reading for Details:
• How many people live in Japan? (About 127 million)
• What is Japan's capital city? (Tokyo)
• How tall is Mount Fuji? (3, 776 metres)
• What happens to streets across Japan in spring? (They turn pink with cherry blossoms)
• What do many tourists visit in Japan? (Temples, gardens, and colorful markets)

2. Reading Skill:
• Name two things Japan is known for. (Fast trains and smart robots / modern technology)
• What is the main idea of the book? (Japan is a unique country that combines old traditions with modern life)
• Why do visitors feel amazed by Japan? (Its clean cities, organized streets, and unique culture)

3. Other Comprehension:
• (Main Idea) What is the book mostly about? • (Inference) Why do you think so many tourists visit Japan? • (Vocabulary) What does tradition mean? • (Fact) Name one famous place in Japan. • (Connection) What would you most like to see or do in Japan? Why?
4::japan.rr_questions:
1. (Under) How tall is Mount Fuji? (3, 776 metres)
2. (On) What is Japan's capital city? (Tokyo)
3. (On) What happens to streets across Japan in spring? (They turn pink with cherry blossoms)
4. (On) Name two things Japan is known for. (Fast trains and smart robots / modern technology)
5. (Above) Why do you think Japan combines old traditions with modern life? (Because Japanese people respect their history but also love new technology)
4::japan.go_description: 中心写 Japan；向外辐射五个气泡，各填一个类别及文中细节：Location（island country, 127 million people）, Capital（Tokyo, one of the busiest cities）, Nature（Mount Fuji, 3, 776 m; cherry blossoms）, Food（rice and fish）, Culture（fast trains, robots, temples, markets）
4::japan.go_usage: 引导学生将文中关于日本的各类信息归入对应气泡，练习 '… is a(n) … country with …' 句型，联系对日本文化的已有认知

4::thetimetravelingtrain.title: The Time-Traveling Train
4::thetimetravelingtrain.teaching_focus: Practice using time clauses with "When they arrived in... [subject] saw/found/discovered" to describe scene-setting in historical narrative.
4::thetimetravelingtrain.reading_skill: 推断与结论 (Make Inferences and Draw Conclusions)
4::thetimetravelingtrain.questions_type:
1. Reading for Details:
• What happened at the beginning of the story? (Accept reasonable summary)
• Who are the main characters in this story? (Accept character names from text)
• What problem did the characters face? (Accept key conflict from text)
• How did the characters solve the problem? (Accept resolution from text)

2. Vocabulary in Context:
• Find the word "era" in the text. What does it mean? (Accept definition)
• Find the word "chariot" in the text. What does it mean? (Accept definition)
• Use "dynasty" in a sentence of your own. (Accept original sentence)

3. Personal Response:
• What was your favourite part of the story? Why?
• What would you do if you were the main character?
• What lesson can we learn from this story?
4::thetimetravelingtrain.rr_questions:
1. (Below) Where did Mia and Tommy find the strange train? (At the old station, with a glowing map)
2. (On) What did Mia and Tommy see when they arrived in ancient Egypt? (The pyramids rising from the sand)
3. (On) What is an "era"? (A long period of time in history with its own distinct features)
4. (On) How did they get home at the end? (The final stamp on the map glowed and the train returned them to the present)
5. (Above) What do you think the author wants us to feel about history from this story? (Accept: that history is exciting and worth exploring / that the past is alive and full of stories / that we can learn from different civilizations)
4::thetimetravelingtrain.go_description: 按故事中的历史顺序排列每次时间旅行的目的地，注明时代名称和主要发现
4::thetimetravelingtrain.go_usage: 引导学生将虚构故事与真实历史连接，讨论每个时代的特点

4::miastriptomalaysia.title: Mia's Trip to Malaysia
4::miastriptomalaysia.teaching_focus: Practice past simple 'traveled' and the preposition 'to' for place plus 'with' to show who or what goes with the subject; use for substitution drills and short speaking answers.
4::miastriptomalaysia.questions_type:
1. Reading for Details:
• What famous buildings did Mia see in Kuala Lumpur? (The Petronas Twin Towers)
• Where did Mia go with her friends? (A night market)
• Who grabbed Mia's camera? (A small monkey)
• What did the monkey eat in the tree? (A durian)
• Who climbed the tree to get the camera back? (The guide)

2. Reading Skill:
• What happened to Mia's camera? (A monkey grabbed it and ran up a tree)
• How did the guide help Mia? (He climbed the tree and got the camera back)
• What did Mia learn from her trip? (About Malaysian culture and the kindness of its people)

3. Other Comprehension:
• (Main Idea) What is the story mostly about? • (Inference) How do you think Mia felt when the monkey grabbed her camera? • (Vocabulary) What does kindness mean? • (Feelings) How did Mia feel at the end of her trip? • (Connection) If you visited Malaysia, what would you most like to see or eat? Why?
4::miastriptomalaysia.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Who grabbed Mia's camera? (A small monkey)
2. (On) What famous buildings did Mia see in Kuala Lumpur? (The Petronas Twin Towers)
3. (On) What did the monkey eat in the tree? (A durian)
4. (On) How did the guide help Mia? (He climbed the tree and got the camera back)
5. (Above) What do you think Mia learned from her trip to Malaysia? (About Malaysian culture and the kindness of its people)
4::miastriptomalaysia.go_description: 上方写 Setting（Malaysia: Kuala Lumpur, night market, forest）和 Characters（Mia, friends, monkey, guide）；中间写 Problem（A monkey grabbed Mia's camera and ran up a tree）；下方写 Solution（Guide climbed the tree and got the camera back）和 Ending（Mia learned about Malaysian culture and kindness）
4::miastriptomalaysia.go_usage: 引导学生视觉化马来西亚的旅行场景，练习 '… traveled to … with …' 句型，了解马来西亚文化亮点（双子塔、夜市、榴莲）

4::brazil.title: Brazil
4::brazil.teaching_focus: Practice the verb 'be' (is/are), using adjectives and noun descriptions, making short factual sentences, and doing substitution drills or simple Q&A (What is X? It is. . .).
4::brazil.reading_skill: 主旨与支撑细节 (Main Idea and Supporting Details)
4::brazil.questions_type:
1. Reading for Details:
• How many people live in Brazil? (Over 200 million)
• What is the Amazon? (The world's largest rainforest)
• What is Brazil's capital city? (Brasília)
• What is Brazil famous for? (Carnival, a colorful festival with music and parades)
• What is the official language of Brazil? (Portuguese)

2. Reading Skill:
• Name two animals that live in Brazil. (Jaguars and macaws)
• How much of Earth's oxygen does the Amazon produce? (About 20 percent)
• What is the main idea of the book? (Brazil is a large, vibrant country with rich culture and amazing nature)

3. Other Comprehension:
• (Main Idea) What is the book mostly about? • (Inference) Why is the Amazon rainforest important for the whole Earth? • (Vocabulary) What does vibrant mean? • (Fact) Name one animal that lives in the Amazon. • (Connection) Which part of Brazil would you most like to visit? Why?
4::brazil.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What is Brazil's capital city? (Brasília)
2. (On) What does the Amazon rainforest produce for the Earth? (About 20 percent of its oxygen)
3. (On) Name two animals that live in Brazil. (Jaguars and macaws)
4. (On) What is Brazil famous for? (Carnival, a colorful festival with music and parades)
5. (Above) Why is the Amazon rainforest important for the whole Earth? (Because it produces about 20 percent of Earth's oxygen and is home to many plants and animals)
4::brazil.go_description: 中心写 Brazil；向外辐射五个气泡：Location（South America, 200 million people）, Nature（Amazon rainforest, Amazon River, jaguars, macaws）, Capital（Brasília, central highlands）, Culture（Carnival, football, Portuguese）, Tourism（millions of tourists every year）
4::brazil.go_usage: 引导学生将文中关于巴西的信息按类别填入气泡图，练习 '[Subject] is [description]' 句型，了解南美洲地理与文化

4::sustainablewaystogetaround.title: Sustainable Ways to Get Around
4::sustainablewaystogetaround.teaching_focus: Practice comparing choices and their effects using "Choosing [A] instead of [B] can [result]", applicable to environmental decision-making.
4::sustainablewaystogetaround.reading_skill: 识别因果关系 (Identifying Cause and Effect)
4::sustainablewaystogetaround.questions_type:
1. Reading for Details:
• What happened at the beginning of the story? (Accept reasonable summary)
• Who are the main characters in this story? (Accept character names from text)
• What problem did the characters face? (Accept key conflict from text)
• How did the characters solve the problem? (Accept resolution from text)

2. Vocabulary in Context:
• Find the word "sustainable" in the text. What does it mean? (Accept definition)
• Find the word "emission" in the text. What does it mean? (Accept definition)
• Use "renewable" in a sentence of your own. (Accept original sentence)

3. Personal Response:
• What was your favourite part of the story? Why?
• What would you do if you were the main character?
• What lesson can we learn from this story?
4::sustainablewaystogetaround.rr_questions:
1. (Below) Name one sustainable way to travel mentioned in the text. (Walking, bicycle, bus, train, electric scooter — accept any one)
2. (On) Why is taking a bus better for the environment than driving a car? (One bus carries many people at once, so it produces far fewer emissions per person)
3. (On) What does "commute" mean? (To travel regularly between home and work or school)
4. (On) What does "emission" mean in this text? (A harmful gas released into the air, usually from vehicles burning fuel)
5. (Above) Why do you think people still choose to drive cars even when greener options exist? (Accept: convenience / distance / habit / lack of public transport / cost of alternatives)
4::sustainablewaystogetaround.go_description: 左列写 交通方式选择，右列写 对环境的影响；包含正面（自行车/公交）和负面（私家车/飞机）两类
4::sustainablewaystogetaround.go_usage: 引导学生对比不同交通方式的环境影响，并用句型"Choosing A instead of B can..."表达建议

4::britishvsamericanmeasurements.title: British vs. American Measurements
4::britishvsamericanmeasurements.questions_type:
1. Reading for Details:
• What does one mile equal? (1, 609 metres)
• What do British people use to measure weight? (Kilograms and stones)
• Which country uses Fahrenheit for temperature? (The United States)
• At what temperature does water freeze in Celsius? (Zero degrees)
• What must people check before using a measurement? (The unit of measurement)

2. Reading Skill:
• Is a British gallon bigger or smaller than a US gallon? (Bigger)
• Name two things people measure every day. (Roads and weight / liquids)
• What is the main idea of the book? (Different countries use different measurements)

3. Other Comprehension:
• (Main Idea) What is the book mainly about? • (Inference) Why can the same number mean different things in another country? • (Vocabulary) What does unit mean? • (Fact) Name one measurement used in Britain. • (Connection) Do you know how tall you are in centimetres or feet?
4::britishvsamericanmeasurements.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What does one mile equal? (1, 609 metres)
2. (On) Which country uses Fahrenheit? (The United States)
3. (On) Is a British gallon bigger or smaller than a US gallon? (Bigger)
4. (On) What must people check before using a measurement? (The unit of measurement)
5. (Above) Why can the same number mean different things in another country? (Because different countries use different units)
4::britishvsamericanmeasurements.go_description: 左列标题 British，右列标题 American；分三行填写对应单位：Length（miles / miles），Weight（kilograms & stones / pounds），Liquids（British gallon / US gallon），Temperature（Celsius / Fahrenheit）
4::britishvsamericanmeasurements.go_usage: 引导学生用对比图整理英美单位差异，练习 'One … equals …' 句型替换，加深对计量单位多样性的认识

5::whatmakesagoodfriend.title: What Makes a Good Friend?
5::whatmakesagoodfriend.example_sentence: Anna helped pick up the books and smiled at the girl.
5::whatmakesagoodfriend.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: help + bare infinitive (no 'to'). Only the action after 'helped' changes.
I helped carry the bags. / She helped find the key. / He helped clean the room. / They helped cook dinner.

core_vocab.example: Anna felt nervous on her first day in the new class.
Her hands shook as she sat down at a small wooden desk.
a pile of
Anna helped pick up a pile of books that had fallen on the floor.
Her hands shook as she sat down at a small wooden desk.

5::whatmakesagoodfriend.core_vocab_raw: nervous, shake, recess, a pile of, wooden
5::whatmakesagoodfriend.text_7page:
Page 1: Anna felt nervous on her first day in the new class. Her hands shook as she sat down at a small wooden desk.
Page 2: At recess she saw a girl drop a pile of books on the floor. Anna helped pick up the books and smiled at the girl.
Page 3: Later she shared pencils and glue with a quiet boy at his table. The boy looked up and said thank you to her softly.
Page 4: A class hamster grabbed Anna's eraser and ran under a chair. The hamster looked like a tiny thief and everyone laughed together.
Page 5: Anna listened when classmates told stories about pets and games. She said, "Tell me more," and asked each person kind questions.
Page 6: Her classmates all liked her because she cared about them and helped them. Anna felt glad she had been kind from her very first day.
Page 7: By the week's end Anna had many new friends and a plan. The next week she would bake cookies and bring them for everyone in the class.
5::whatmakesagoodfriend.pure_text: Anna felt nervous on her first day in the new class. Her hands shook as she sat down at a small wooden desk. At recess she saw a girl drop a pile of books on the floor. Anna helped pick up the books and smiled at the girl. Later she shared pencils and glue with a quiet boy at his table. The boy looked up and said thank you to her softly. A class hamster grabbed Anna's eraser and ran under a chair. The hamster looked like a tiny thief and everyone laughed together. Anna listened when classmates told stories about pets and games. She said, "Tell me more," and asked each person kind questions. Her classmates all liked her because she cared about them and helped them. Anna felt glad she had been kind from her very first day. By the week's end, Anna had many new friends and a plan. The next week she would bake cookies and bring them to everyone in the class.
5::whatmakesagoodfriend.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What did Anna do when she saw a girl drop books on the floor? 2. How did Anna show kindness to the quiet boy at the table next to her? 3. What plan did Anna make for the following week?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. Why do you think Anna felt glad by the end of the week? What changed for her? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Think of a time you helped someone you did not know well. How did it make both of you feel? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why is listening to people just as important as doing things for them? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Who is the main character and what problem did she face on her first day? 2. (Understand) Describe Anna's most important action in the story and explain why it mattered. 3. (Analyse) How did Anna's behaviour change the way her classmates felt about her? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think Anna would have made friends without the hamster incident? Why or why not? 5. (Create) Write Anna's diary entry for the last day of the week, describing how she feels and why.
5::whatmakesagoodfriend.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What did Anna do when she saw the girl drop her books? (She helped pick them up and smiled at her.)
2. (On) What did Anna share with the quiet boy at his table? (Pencils and glue.)
3. (On) What grabbed Anna's eraser and made everyone laugh? (The class hamster.)
4. (On) What was Anna's plan for the following week? (To bake cookies and bring them for everyone in the class.)
5. (Above) Why do you think Anna made so many new friends so quickly? (Because she was kind, helpful, and listened to everyone.)
5::whatmakesagoodfriend.go_description: 呈现故事角色与主要情节发展的图形组织器
5::whatmakesagoodfriend.go_usage: 记录主要角色、遇到的问题和解决方法

5::whatfriendsarelike.title: What Friends Are Like
5::whatfriendsarelike.example_sentence: A kind friend shares food and notices when you feel sad.
5::whatfriendsarelike.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: adjective + noun as subject + simple present habit. The adjective and action change.
A good friend listens when you have a problem. / A helpful friend carries your bag when you are tired. / A funny friend makes you laugh on a bad day.

core_vocab.example: Good friends are kind, honest, helpful, and funny in many small ways.
tell the truth
An honest friend tells the truth even when it is difficult.
For example, an honest friend admits making a mistake.
Research shows people with close friends often live longer and feel happier.

5::whatfriendsarelike.core_vocab_raw: kind, honest, tell the truth, admit, research
5::whatfriendsarelike.text_7page:
Page 1: Think about your best friend and the things they often do. Good friends are kind, honest, helpful, and funny in many small ways.
Page 2: A kind friend shares food and notices when you feel sad. For example, a kind friend will walk home with you after school.
Page 3: An honest friend tells the truth even when it is difficult. For example, an honest friend admits making a mistake.
Page 4: A helpful friend helps with homework and sometimes carries heavy books.
Page 5: A funny friend makes you laugh on a rainy day at school. For example, a funny friend tells a silly story during recess.
Page 6: Research shows people with close friends often live longer and feel happier.
Page 7: Say thank you to friends and help them when they need you. Remember to be kind, honest, helpful, and funny.
5::whatfriendsarelike.pure_text: Think about your best friend and the things they often do. Good friends are kind, honest, helpful, and funny in many small ways. A kind friend shares food and notices when you feel sad. For example, a kind friend will walk home with you after school. An honest friend tells the truth even when it is difficult. For example, an honest friend admits making a mistake. A helpful friend helps with homework and sometimes carries heavy books. A funny friend makes you laugh on a rainy day at school. For example, a funny friend tells a silly story during recess. Research shows people with close friends often live longer and feel happier. Say thank you to friends and help them when they need you. Remember to be kind, honest, helpful, and funny.
5::whatfriendsarelike.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::whatfriendsarelike.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name the four qualities of a good friend described in this passage. 2. Give one specific example from the text of what a kind friend does and one example of what a funny friend does. 3. What does research say about people who have close friends?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. What new information in this passage surprised you the most? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author use examples to make abstract qualities (like 'kind' or 'honest') easier to understand? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences describing a good friend using two different qualities from this passage.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What are the four qualities of a good friend listed in this passage? 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words what it means to be an honest friend. 3. (Analyse) How does the author organise the information? Does each quality get equal space? Why might this be? 4. (Evaluate) Which of the four qualities do you think is most important in a friendship? Give a reason from the text. 5. (Create) Write a short paragraph about yourself as a friend, using at least two qualities from this passage.
5::whatfriendsarelike.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What are the four qualities of a good friend described in this passage? (Kind, honest, helpful, and funny.)
2. (On) What does a kind friend do when you feel sad? (Shares food and notices when you feel sad.)
3. (On) What does an honest friend do if you lose something? (Returns it the next day.)
4. (On) What does research show about people with close friends? (They often live longer and feel happier.)
5. (Above) Which quality do you think is the most important in a friend, and why? (Accept any answer with a clear reason.)
5::whatfriendsarelike.graphic_organizer: Main Idea & Details Web (主旨细节网)
5::whatfriendsarelike.go_description: 中心放主旨、向外辐射四个朋友特质及各自举例的放射状图形组织器
5::whatfriendsarelike.go_usage: 中心写 "What makes a good friend?"，四支分别写 Kind/Honest/Helpful/Funny，每支下方写对应例子

5::tommysbestfriend.title: Tommy's Best Friend
5::tommysbestfriend.example_sentence: Max felt sad because he had not taken Tommy's sticker at all.
5::tommysbestfriend.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: past simple + because + clause for feelings and reasons.
She felt nervous because the test was difficult. / He felt proud because he helped his friend. / I felt happy because my team won.

core_vocab.example: Tommy found his dinosaur sticker missing from his desk.
borrowed
Max often borrowed things without asking first.
He went to Tommy, explained calmly, and said he was sorry.
Tommy listened, apologized for shouting, and said he should have asked first.

5::tommysbestfriend.text_7page:
Page 1: Yesterday Tommy found his dinosaur sticker missing from his school desk. He felt very angry because he loved that bright little dinosaur sticker.
Page 2: He thought Max had taken it because Max often borrowed things without asking. Tommy shouted at Max and left a mean note on Max's desk.
Page 3: Max felt sad because he had not taken Tommy's sticker at all. He had been saving a small sticker to give Tommy at a school party.
Page 4: The next morning Max found Tommy's dinosaur sticker under a big book on the desk. Max realized the sticker had fallen and nobody had stolen it.
Page 5: He went to Tommy, explained calmly, and said he was sorry.
Page 6: Tommy listened, apologized for shouting, and said he should have asked first.
Page 7: At the party a silly banana hat made them both laugh and feel better. They learned that calm talking and apologies can fix many problems.
5::tommysbestfriend.pure_text: Yesterday Tommy found his dinosaur sticker missing from his school desk. He felt very angry because he loved that bright little dinosaur sticker. He thought Max had taken it because Max often borrowed things without asking. Tommy shouted at Max and left a mean note on Max's desk. Max felt sad because he had not taken Tommy's sticker at all. He had been saving a small sticker to give Tommy at a school party. The next morning Max found Tommy's dinosaur sticker under a big book on the desk. Max realized the sticker had fallen and nobody had stolen it. He went to Tommy, explained calmly, and said he was sorry. Tommy listened, apologized for shouting, and said he should have asked first. At the party a silly banana hat made them both laugh and feel better. They learned that calm talking and apologies can fix many problems.
5::tommysbestfriend.reading_strategy: 进行预测 (Making and Confirming Predictions)
5::tommysbestfriend.reading_skill: 问题与解决方案 (Problem and Solution)
5::tommysbestfriend.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Why did Tommy get angry with Max at the beginning of the story? 2. What did Max find the next morning and what did he realise? 3. How did Tommy and Max resolve their argument and what did they learn?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the fact that Max had been saving a sticker for Tommy tell us about Max's character? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever blamed someone for something and later discovered you were wrong? What happened? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Was Tommy's reaction to the missing sticker understandable? What should he have done differently? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Who are the two main characters and what caused the argument between them? 2. (Understand) Describe the moment when Max found the sticker. Why was this moment important? 3. (Analyse) What lesson did both boys learn from this experience? 4. (Evaluate) Was Tommy too quick to blame Max? What evidence from the text supports your view? 5. (Create) Write a short message Tommy might send to Max the day after they made up.
5::tommysbestfriend.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Why was Tommy angry at the beginning of the story? (His dinosaur sticker was missing and he thought Max had taken it.)
2. (On) Where did Max find the sticker the next morning? (Under a big book on the desk.)
3. (On) How did Max feel when Tommy shouted at him? (Sad, because he had not taken the sticker.)
4. (On) What did Tommy do when Max explained the truth? (He apologized for shouting.)
5. (Above) What lesson did both boys learn from this story? (That they should talk calmly and ask before blaming someone.)
5::tommysbestfriend.go_description: 呈现故事角色与主要情节发展的图形组织器
5::tommysbestfriend.go_usage: 记录主要角色、遇到的问题和解决方法

5::waystocommunicatewithfriends.title: Ways to Communicate with Friends
5::waystocommunicatewithfriends.example_sentence: Texting is quick and useful when you are busy or far away.
5::waystocommunicatewithfriends.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: simple present + when + clause for describing useful conditions.
Reading is enjoyable when the book is interesting. / Exercise is important when you feel stressed. / Music is helpful when you study.

core_vocab.example: Talking face to face means looking at the person and asking clear questions.
Teamwork means working together on a problem or on a school project.
Texting is quick and useful when you are busy or far away.
Use emojis and kind replies to show your feelings clearly.
If you are kind, honest, and reliable, friendships grow and trust builds.

5::waystocommunicatewithfriends.text_7page:
Page 1: Good friends use many simple ways to share ideas and feelings.
Page 2: Talking means speaking face to face or calling a friend on the phone. When you talk, look, smile, and ask clear questions to understand better.
Page 3: Every day people send short messages to friends all around the world. Texting is quick and useful when you are busy or far away.
Page 4: Use emojis and kind replies to show your feelings clearly. Writing notes or cards is nice for saying thanks or giving kind words.
Page 5: A quick drawing or a short video call can also show feelings very clearly.
Page 6: Teamwork means working together on a problem or on a school project. In teamwork, listen, ask questions often, share ideas, and help each other.
Page 7: Try different ways at school next week and notice which one helps most. If you are kind, honest, and reliable, friendships grow and trust builds.
5::waystocommunicatewithfriends.pure_text: Good friends use many simple ways to share ideas and feelings. Talking means speaking face to face or calling a friend on the phone. When you talk, look, smile, and ask clear questions to understand better. Every day people send short messages to friends all around the world. Texting is quick and useful when you are busy or far away. Use emojis and kind replies to show your feelings clearly. Writing notes or cards is nice for saying thanks or giving kind words. A quick drawing or a short video call can also show feelings very clearly. Teamwork means working together on a problem or on a school project. In teamwork, listen, ask questions often, share ideas, and help each other. Try different ways at school next week and notice which one helps most. If you are kind, honest, and reliable, friendships grow and trust builds.
5::waystocommunicatewithfriends.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::waystocommunicatewithfriends.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name four different ways of communicating described in this passage. 2. According to the text, when is texting most useful? 3. What does the passage say makes friendships grow?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. What new idea in this passage made you think differently about communication? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author organise the four communication methods? Is this structure effective? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Think of a situation at school where each of the four methods would be the best choice. Write one sentence for each.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name two communication methods described in the text and give one advantage of each. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words why teamwork is included as a way of communicating. 3. (Analyse) The author says 'texting is quick and useful when you are busy or far away'. When might texting NOT be the best choice? 4. (Evaluate) Which communication method do you think is most important for strong friendships? Use evidence from the text. 5. (Create) Design a short guide for a new student explaining which communication method to use in different school situations.
5::waystocommunicatewithfriends.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What are two ways the passage says good friends can share ideas and feelings? (Talking, texting, writing notes, or teamwork — any two.)
2. (On) When is texting most useful according to the passage? (When you are busy or far away.)
3. (On) What does teamwork mean according to the passage? (Working together on a problem or school project.)
4. (On) What does the passage say friendships need to grow? (Kindness, honesty, and reliability.)
5. (Above) Which way of communicating do you think is best for close friends, and why? (Accept any answer with a reason.)
5::waystocommunicatewithfriends.go_description: 以列表呈现四种沟通方式及各自特点和适用场景的图形组织器
5::waystocommunicatewithfriends.go_usage: 四列：Talking | Texting | Notes/Cards | Teamwork，每列填写 how to use + when it helps

5::thehealthylunchchallenge.title: The Healthy Lunch Challenge
5::thehealthylunchchallenge.example_sentence: He decided he would try new healthy foods at home each week.
5::thehealthylunchchallenge.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: decide + that-clause + would (will → would backshift). Only what was decided changes.
She decided she would study for one hour every evening. / He decided he would walk to school. / I decided I would help more at home.

core_vocab.example: Tommy entered the school Healthy Lunch Challenge with a lazy, simple plan.
At first, he wanted only chips and candy for lunch each day.
She showed him a colorful plate and said variety gives steady energy.
Amy put fruit, a small sandwich, yogurt, and carrot sticks on it.
At the contest, judges looked for balance, colour, and healthy choices.

5::thehealthylunchchallenge.text_7page:
Page 1: Tommy entered the school Healthy Lunch Challenge with a lazy, simple plan. At first, he wanted only chips and candy for lunch each day.
Page 2: His friend Amy said the plan was not healthy and wanted to help. She showed him a colourful plate and said variety gives steady energy.
Page 3: Amy put fruit, a small sandwich, yogurt, and carrot sticks on it. Tommy laughed when she added a funny apple with sunglasses for fun.
Page 4: He decided he would try new healthy foods at home each week.
Page 5: At the contest, judges looked for balance, colour, and healthy choices in lunches. One judge asked, "Why is your plate healthy?"
Page 6: Tommy answered clearly and honestly. He said his lunch had fruit, protein, grains, and a small treat.
Page 7: The judges smiled and gave him the most creative healthy lunch prize. After that, he began bringing healthier food more often to school.
5::thehealthylunchchallenge.pure_text: Tommy entered the school Healthy Lunch Challenge with a lazy, simple plan. At first, he wanted only chips and candy for lunch each day. His friend Amy said the plan was not healthy and wanted to help. She showed him a colourful plate and said variety gives steady energy. Amy put fruit, a small sandwich, yogurt, and carrot sticks on it. Tommy laughed when she added a funny apple with sunglasses for fun. He decided he would try new healthy foods at home each week. At the contest, judges looked for balance, colour, and healthy choices in lunches. One judge asked, "Why is your plate healthy?" Tommy answered clearly and honestly. He said his lunch had fruit, protein, grains, and a small treat. The judges smiled and gave him the most creative healthy lunch prize. After that, he began bringing healthier food more often to school.
5::thehealthylunchchallenge.reading_skill: 问题与解决方案 (Problem and Solution)
5::thehealthylunchchallenge.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What was Tommy's original plan for the challenge and why did Amy think it was a problem? 2. How did Amy help Tommy improve his lunch plan? 3. What happened at the contest and how did Tommy do?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does Tommy's decision to try new foods 'each week' suggest about how he is changing? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever changed a habit because a friend encouraged you? What happened? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) What do you think Tommy will do differently at lunchtime after winning the award? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What was Tommy's original plan and why was it unhealthy? 2. (Understand) Describe what Amy's colourful plate contained and explain why each item was healthy. 3. (Analyse) What did the contest judges look for and how did Tommy's answers show he had learned something? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think Tommy would have changed his habits without Amy's help? Support your answer with evidence. 5. (Create) Write Tommy's diary entry on the evening after winning the award.
5::thehealthylunchchallenge.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What was Tommy's original plan for the Healthy Lunch Challenge? (Only chips and candy for lunch each day.)
2. (On) What did Amy put on the colourful plate to show Tommy? (Fruit, a small sandwich, yogurt, and carrot sticks.)
3. (On) What did the contest judges look for in the lunches? (Balance, colour, and healthy choices.)
4. (On) What prize did Tommy win? (The most creative healthy lunch prize.)
5. (Above) How do you think Tommy's lunchtime habits changed after winning the award? (He probably brought healthier food more often because he understood why it mattered.)

5::healthyfoodandabalanceddiet.title: Healthy Food and a Balanced Diet
5::healthyfoodandabalanceddiet.example_sentence: Dairy gives your body calcium and vitamin D.
5::healthyfoodandabalanceddiet.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: give + indirect object (your body) + direct object (benefit). Food and benefit both change.
Fruit gives your body vitamins and natural sugar. / Meat gives your body protein and iron. / Vegetables give your body fibre and minerals.

core_vocab.example: Vegetables: They give vitamins and fiber that help digestion and immunity.
Vegetables: They give vitamins and fiber that help digestion and immunity.
build muscles
Protein from meat, beans, and eggs helps build muscles and repair the body.
Dairy gives your body calcium and vitamin D for strong, healthy bones.

5::healthyfoodandabalanceddiet.core_vocab_raw: vitamin, fiber, protein, build muscles, calcium
5::healthyfoodandabalanceddiet.text_7page:
Page 1: A healthy plate helps your body grow and stay strong every day. MyPlate shows four main food groups on a plate for meals.
Page 2: Vegetables give vitamins and fibre that help digestion and immunity. Eat many colourful vegetables every day for better energy and good health.
Page 3: Whole grains give steady energy for school, sports, and play. Choose whole grain bread, rice, or oats more often at meals.
Page 4: Protein: meat, beans, eggs, and nuts build muscles and repair the body. Protein helps you feel full for a long time after a meal.
Page 5: Dairy: milk, cheese, and yogurt make bones and teeth stronger. Dairy gives calcium and vitamin D for strong, healthy bones and teeth.
Page 6: A balanced plate has vegetables and fruits for half the plate. Try to drink water and limit sugary drinks every day.
Page 7: Small changes now will help you stay healthy in the future.
5::healthyfoodandabalanceddiet.pure_text: A healthy plate helps your body grow and stay strong every day. MyPlate shows four main food groups on a plate for meals. Vegetables give vitamins and fibre that help digestion and immunity. Eat many colourful vegetables every day for better energy and good health. Whole grains give steady energy for school, sports, and play. Choose whole grain bread, rice, or oats more often at meals. Protein: meat, beans, eggs, and nuts build muscles and repair the body. Protein helps you feel full for a long time after a meal. Dairy: milk, cheese, and yogurt make bones and teeth stronger. Dairy gives calcium and vitamin D for strong, healthy bones and teeth. A balanced plate has vegetables and fruits for half the plate. Try to drink water and limit sugary drinks every day. Small changes now will help you stay healthy in the future.
5::healthyfoodandabalanceddiet.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::healthyfoodandabalanceddiet.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What are the four main food groups shown on MyPlate? 2. What does dairy give your body and why is that important? 3. What does the passage suggest about drinks?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. What surprised you most about how different food groups help the body? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the passage use the MyPlate model to organise the information? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Create a balanced meal for one day using all four food groups. Write one sentence explaining why each food was chosen.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name the four food groups and give one food example from each. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words why whole grains give 'steady energy' compared to sugary foods. 3. (Analyse) The text says 'small changes now will help you stay healthy in the future'. What kind of argument is the author making here? 4. (Evaluate) Which food group do you think most people find hardest to eat enough of? Why? 5. (Create) Write two sentences about how you could improve your own diet using ideas from this passage.
5::healthyfoodandabalanceddiet.rr_questions:

4. (On) What does the passage say you should drink more of? (Water.)
5. (Above) Why do you think the author says 'small changes now will help you stay healthy in the future'? (Because healthy habits are easier to build when you are young and they last a long time.)

5::trainingforthefunrun.title: Training for the Fun Run
5::trainingforthefunrun.example_sentence: She ran a little faster without thinking about speed.
5::trainingforthefunrun.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: without + gerund for actions done without consciously doing something else.
He answered without looking at his notes. / She walked in without knocking. / They finished without stopping once.

core_vocab.example: Mia's school will have a Fun Run next month and she signed up.
give up
At first Mia was slow and she wanted to give up many times.
After weeks of practice Mia felt braver and stronger than before.
She started slowly, kept a steady pace, and passed many runners.

5::trainingforthefunrun.text_7page:
Page 1: Mia's school will have a Fun Run next month and she signed up. Coach Ben told her to start with short runs three times a week.
Page 2: At first Mia was slow and she wanted to give up many times. Her friend Sam ran faster and seemed stronger than Mia.
Page 3: One morning Mia ran in the park and a squirrel sat by the path. The squirrel made a sound like a cheer and Mia laughed out loud.
Page 4: She felt happy and ran a little faster without thinking about speed. Her coach taught her to start slowly and save energy for the end.
Page 5: After weeks of practice Mia's legs grew stronger and she felt braver.
Page 6: On race day Mia felt nervous but she remembered her training plan. She started slowly, kept a steady pace, and passed many runners.
Page 7: At the finish line Mia smiled, felt proud, and shouted, "I did it!" She had done it by staying patient and believing in herself.
5::trainingforthefunrun.pure_text: Mia's school will have a Fun Run next month and she signed up. Coach Ben told her to start with short runs three times a week. At first Mia was slow and she wanted to give up many times. Her friend Sam ran faster and seemed stronger than Mia. One morning Mia ran in the park and a squirrel sat by the path. The squirrel made a sound like a cheer and Mia laughed out loud. She felt happy and ran a little faster without thinking about speed. Her coach taught her to start slowly and save energy for the end. After weeks of practice Mia's legs grew stronger and she felt braver. On race day Mia felt nervous but she remembered her training plan. She started slowly, kept a steady pace, and passed many runners. At the finish line Mia smiled, felt proud, and shouted, "I did it!" She had done it by staying patient and believing in herself.
5::trainingforthefunrun.reading_strategy: 进行预测 (Making and Confirming Predictions)
5::trainingforthefunrun.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What did Coach Ben tell Mia to do at the start of her training? 2. What happened when Mia ran in the park and saw the squirrel? 3. How did Mia race on the day of the Fun Run?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the squirrel moment tell us about how emotions affect performance? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever had to keep trying at something even when it felt difficult? What helped you? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why is it important to 'start slowly and save energy for the end' in a race? Can this idea apply to other things in life? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What was Mia's main challenge at the start of her training? 2. (Understand) Describe what happened at the finish line and how Mia felt. 3. (Analyse) How did Mia's strategy in the race show that she had listened to her coach? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think the squirrel moment was the turning point in the story? Give a reason from the text. 5. (Create) Write a training advice card that Coach Ben might give to a new runner, based on what Mia learned.
5::trainingforthefunrun.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Who told Mia to start with short runs three times a week? (Coach Ben.)
2. (On) What happened when Mia ran past the squirrel in the park? (She laughed and ran a little faster without thinking about speed.)
3. (On) What strategy did Mia use on race day? (She started slowly, kept a steady pace, and passed many runners.)
4. (On) What did Mia shout at the finish line? ("I did it!")
5. (Above) What do you think the squirrel moment taught Mia about running? (That when you stop worrying and enjoy the moment, you can run better naturally.)

5::exerciseandthebody.title: Exercise and the Body
5::exerciseandthebody.example_sentence: Muscles get bigger and stronger with regular exercise.
5::exerciseandthebody.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: get + comparative adjective to show physical change over time.
Legs get stronger with regular running. / Your heart gets healthier with daily swimming. / Bones get denser with weight-bearing activity.

core_vocab.example: Regular activity lowers your resting heart rate.
It improves fitness and endurance.
Bones become stronger with weight on them, like walking, running, and jumping.
Exercise also helps your mood and reduces stress and anxiety.
Muscles get bigger and stronger with regular exercise every day.

5::exerciseandthebody.core_vocab_raw: heart rate, fitness, weight, stress, exercise, regular
5::exerciseandthebody.text_7page:
Page 1: Exercise helps your body in many important and simple ways every day. Here are four parts of the body that exercise can change.
Page 2: Muscles get bigger and stronger when you run, jump, or lift things. Small sets often help muscles more than one long workout.
Page 3: Bones become stronger with weight on them, like walking, running, and jumping. Impact moves give a small push to help bone growth over time.
Page 4: The heart becomes stronger with regular exercise. It pumps blood more efficiently.
Page 5: Regular activity lowers your resting heart rate. It improves fitness and endurance.
Page 6: Exercise also helps your mood and reduces stress and anxiety. Moving releases natural chemicals that make you feel happy and calm.
Page 7: Try to move for thirty minutes most days and have fun. Start slowly, warm up, and rest if you feel pain or very tired.
5::exerciseandthebody.pure_text: Exercise helps your body in many important and simple ways every day. Here are four parts of the body that exercise can change. Muscles get bigger and stronger when you run, jump, or lift things. Small sets often help muscles more than one long workout. Bones become stronger with weight on them, like walking, running, and jumping. Impact moves give a small push to help bone growth over time. The heart becomes stronger with regular exercise. It pumps blood more efficiently. Regular activity lowers your resting heart rate. It improves fitness and endurance. Exercise also helps your mood and reduces stress and anxiety. Moving releases natural chemicals that make you feel happy and calm. Try to move for thirty minutes most days and have fun. Start slowly, warm up, and rest if you feel pain or very tired.
5::exerciseandthebody.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name four parts of the body that exercise can change according to this passage. 2. What happens to the heart with regular exercise? 3. According to the text, how does exercise affect mood?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which benefit of exercise surprised you most? Why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author use the structure 'Here are four parts...' to organise the information? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write a one-week exercise plan for a student your age, using ideas from this passage. Explain why each activity is included.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What are the four body parts discussed in this passage? 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words how exercise helps the brain and mood. 3. (Analyse) The passage says 'even thirty minutes a day makes a big difference'. What kind of evidence would make this claim more convincing? 4. (Evaluate) Which form of exercise described or implied in the text would you personally find most useful? Give a reason. 5. (Create) Write two sentences of advice for a friend who says they are too busy to exercise.
5::exerciseandthebody.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What are the four parts of the body that exercise can change? (Muscles, bones, heart, and brain/mood.)
2. (On) What happens to muscles when you run, jump, or lift things? (They get bigger and stronger.)
3. (On) How does exercise help your heart? (It becomes stronger and pumps blood more easily.)
4. (On) How long does the passage say it takes to see a difference from exercising? (About thirty minutes a day.)
5. (Above) Why do you think exercise helps your mood as well as your body? (Because moving your body releases chemicals in the brain that make you feel happier.)

5::takingthemaglev.title: Taking the Maglev
5::takingthemaglev.example_sentence: He had never been on a maglev train before.
5::takingthemaglev.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: had + never + past participle. Students change the past participle only.
She had never visited the capital before. / He had never eaten sushi before. / They had never seen snow before.

core_vocab.example: Tommy waited at the station with his small blue bag and ticket.
Tommy waited at the station with his small blue bag and ticket.
He had never been on a maglev train before that day.
Tommy got on and sat in a comfortable wide seat for children.
The train arrived, shiny and smooth, and Tommy found a comfortable seat by the window.

5::takingthemaglev.text_7page:
Page 1: Tommy waited at the station with his small blue bag and ticket. He had never been on a maglev train before that day.
Page 2: His heart beat fast; he felt excited and a little nervous. The train arrived, shiny and quiet like a silver bird.
Page 3: Tommy got on and sat in a comfortable wide seat for children. The train started slowly and then it floated above the tracks.
Page 4: It moved so fast that the trees and buildings became quick pictures. Tommy felt his stomach tickle and laughed at the new feeling.
Page 5: He remembered his science class and thought magnets must lift the train. He knew magnets can push and pull and make things float.
Page 6: He imagined a magnet making his metal spoon stick to his lunchbox. After twenty minutes the train slowed and they stepped out into the city.
Page 7: "I want to ride again," he said, and his father smiled and promised.
5::takingthemaglev.pure_text: Tommy waited at the station with his small blue bag and ticket. He had never been on a maglev train before that day. His heart beat fast; he felt excited and a little nervous. The train arrived, shiny and quiet like a silver bird. Tommy got on and sat in a comfortable wide seat for children. The train started slowly and then it floated above the tracks. It moved so fast that the trees and buildings became quick pictures. Tommy felt his stomach tickle and laughed at the new feeling. He remembered his science class and thought magnets must lift the train. He knew magnets can push and pull and make things float. He imagined a magnet making his metal spoon stick to his lunchbox. After twenty minutes the train slowed and they stepped out into the city. "I want to ride again," he said, and his father smiled and promised.
5::takingthemaglev.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Why was Tommy nervous and excited at the start of the story? 2. What did Tommy notice about how the train moved compared to a normal train? 3. What did Tommy think about as he looked out of the window?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does Tommy's reaction to the maglev train tell us about his personality? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever experienced something for the first time that was very different from what you expected? What was it? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) What details in the story help you visualise what a maglev train ride feels like? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What had Tommy never done before and why was he excited? 2. (Understand) Describe one detail from the story that shows how a maglev train is different from a normal train. 3. (Analyse) Why does Tommy think about the future at the end of the story? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think the author wants the reader to feel excited about technology? What details suggest this? 5. (Create) Write a short paragraph from the perspective of another passenger watching Tommy during the journey.
5::takingthemaglev.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Had Tommy ever been on a maglev train before? (No, it was his first time.)
2. (On) How did the maglev train move differently from a normal train? (It floated above the track with no wheels touching the ground and moved in silence.)
3. (On) How did Tommy feel when the train started moving? (Amazed, because it was so quiet and smooth.)
4. (On) What did Tommy think about as he looked out of the window? (He thought about what trains might be like in the future.)
5. (Above) Why do you think Tommy felt excited rather than scared, even though this was something completely new? (Because he had looked forward to it and was curious about new technology.)

5::takingthemaglev.go_usage: 记录主要角色、遇到的问题和解决方法

5::bullettrains.title: Bullet Trains
5::bullettrains.example_sentence: A train trip is usually faster and more comfortable than a car trip.
5::bullettrains.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: comparative adjectives — -er and more + adj + than.
A bus is usually cheaper than a taxi. / A bike ride is usually quicker than walking. / A flight is usually more expensive than a train journey.

core_vocab.example: Many bullet trains travel at around three hundred kilometres per hour.
They run on special tracks that are straight and very well maintained.
These trains have a long pointed nose and a smooth metal body.
Per passenger, a bullet train produces much less carbon dioxide than a car.
Bullet trains use electric power and special signals for safe operation.

5::bullettrains.text_7page:
Page 1: Bullet trains are very fast and are used for long-distance travel today. Many bullet trains travel at around three hundred kilometres per hour on regular lines.
Page 2: These trains have a long pointed nose and a smooth metal body. They run on special tracks that are straight and very well maintained.
Page 3: Bullet trains use electric power and special signals for safe operation.
Page 4: A train trip is usually faster and more comfortable than the same car trip. Trains carry many people at once, so the cost per person can be lower.
Page 5: They also reduce traffic on roads and cut overall air pollution in many cities.
Page 6: Per passenger, a modern bullet train produces much less carbon dioxide than cars. Train station staff keep the tracks and stations clean to support public health.
Page 7: Bullet trains are a fast, safe, and greener choice for many travellers. In tests, engineers have reached about six hundred kilometres per hour.
5::bullettrains.pure_text: Bullet trains are very fast and are used for long-distance travel today. Many bullet trains travel at around three hundred kilometres per hour on regular lines. Bullet trains use electric power and special signals for safe operation. A train trip is usually faster and more comfortable than the same car trip. Trains carry many people at once, so the cost per person can be lower. They also reduce traffic on roads and cut overall air pollution in many cities. Per passenger, a modern bullet train produces much less carbon dioxide than cars. Train station staff keep the tracks and stations clean to support public health. Bullet trains are a fast, safe, and greener choice for many travellers. In tests, engineers have reached about six hundred kilometres per hour.
5::bullettrains.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)

Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. What fact about bullet trains in this passage surprised you the most? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author compare bullet trains to cars to make the information clearer? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) If your city were planning new transport, would you argue for bullet trains or another option? Use evidence from the passage.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) At approximately what speed do bullet trains travel and what type of power do they use? 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words why bullet trains produce less carbon dioxide per passenger than cars. 3. (Analyse) How is the information in this passage organised? Why is this structure effective for the topic? 4. (Evaluate) The passage says bullet trains are 'a fast, safe, and greener choice'. Which of these three qualities do you think is most important for the future? Why? 5. (Create) Write two sentences persuading a government official to invest in bullet train technology, using facts from this passage.
5::bullettrains.rr_questions:
1. (Under) How fast do many bullet trains travel? (About three hundred kilometres per hour.)
2. (On) What shape is the front of a bullet train and why? (A long pointed nose — to cut through air and reduce resistance.)

5::miasscienceproject.title: Mia's Science Project
5::miasscienceproject.example_sentence: She used the warmer jar for a final test.
5::miasscienceproject.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: used + noun + for + noun/gerund to state purpose.
He used his phone for taking photos. / She used a ruler for measuring the paper. / They used old boxes for making a model.

core_vocab.example: Mia had a science project about plant growth for her class.
On Monday she set up three jars with different soil types.
At first the seeds did not grow and Mia felt upset.
She wrote notes every day in her blue notebook and checked plants.
Mia had a science project about plant growth for her class — a real experiment.

5::miasscienceproject.core_vocab_raw: plant, jar, seed, notebook, temperature, experiment
5::miasscienceproject.text_7page:
Page 1: Mia had a science project about plant growth for her class. On Monday she set up three jars with different soil types.
Page 2: At first the seeds did not grow and Mia felt upset. She wrote notes every day in her blue notebook and checked the plants.
Page 3: Her first plan failed because she gave each jar too much water. She reduced watering and moved the jars closer to a bright window.
Page 4: One morning she noticed that the largest jar kept its soil warmer in the sunlight. The larger jar held more soil and absorbed more heat each day.
Page 5: She used the warmer jar for a final test and the seeds finally grew.
Page 6: Mia used the results to improve her experiment and continued testing carefully. Her cat sat quietly on the table and watched her work.
Page 7: At the science fair she presented her project and explained her changes. Her teacher said she should be proud of herself, and Mia smiled brightly.
5::miasscienceproject.pure_text: Mia had a science project about plant growth for her class. On Monday she set up three jars with different soil types. At first the seeds did not grow and Mia felt upset. She wrote notes every day in her blue notebook and checked the plants. Her first plan failed because she gave each jar too much water. She reduced watering and moved the jars closer to a bright window. One morning she noticed that the largest jar kept its soil warmer in the sunlight. The larger jar held more soil and absorbed more heat each day. She used the warmer jar for a final test and the seeds finally grew. Mia used the results to improve her experiment and continued testing carefully. Her cat sat quietly on the table and watched her work. At the science fair she presented her project and explained her changes. Her teacher said she should be proud of herself, and Mia smiled brightly.
5::miasscienceproject.reading_skill: 问题与解决方案 (Problem and Solution)
5::miasscienceproject.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What experiment was Mia doing and what problem did she face at first? 2. What change did Mia make after her first plan failed? 3. What did Mia notice about the large jar and how did she use this discovery?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the detail about Mia's cat 'watching quietly' add to the story? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever had to change your plan in a project because something did not work? What did you do? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) What does this story suggest about the connection between making mistakes and learning? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What was the main problem Mia faced in her experiment? 2. (Understand) Describe the observation that helped Mia improve her experiment. 3. (Analyse) How did Mia's approach to the experiment change from the beginning to the end of the story? 4. (Evaluate) The teacher said Mia should be proud of herself. Do you agree? Use evidence from the text to support your view. 5. (Create) Write the three most important steps of Mia's experiment as a simple numbered instruction guide.
5::miasscienceproject.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Why did the seeds fail to grow at first? (Mia gave each jar too much water.)
2. (On) What change did Mia make after her first plan failed? (She reduced watering and moved the jars closer to a bright window.)
3. (On) What did Mia notice about the largest jar? (It kept its soil warmer in the sunlight.)
4. (On) What happened at the science fair? (Mia presented her project and her teacher said she should be proud of herself.)
5. (Above) What do you think Mia learned from this experiment about making mistakes? (That mistakes are part of learning, and changing your plan when things go wrong is what good scientists do.)
5::miasscienceproject.go_description: 呈现故事角色与主要情节发展的图形组织器
5::miasscienceproject.go_usage: 记录主要角色、遇到的问题和解决方法

5::importantinventionsinhistory.title: Important Inventions in History
5::importantinventionsinhistory.example_sentence: The light bulb brought bright electric light into many homes.
5::importantinventionsinhistory.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: past simple of bring + object + into + place for introducing something new.
The internet brought fast communication into schools. / Smartphones brought cameras into everyone's pockets. / The railway brought new goods into small towns.

core_vocab.example: These three inventions changed daily life in big and surprising ways.
The light bulb brought bright electric light into many homes.
The Wright brothers flew the first powered aeroplane in 1903.
In the future, new devices will continue to change how we live.
Inventors often tried many times and failed before they finally succeeded.

5::importantinventionsinhistory.text_7page:
Page 1: Imagine a world without light bulbs, telephones, or aeroplanes at all. These three inventions changed daily life in big and surprising ways.
Page 2: The light bulb brought safe, bright electric light into many homes. Before electric light, people used candles and oil lamps at night.
Page 3: Alexander Graham Bell made the first useful telephone in 1876. People could call friends and family for news, help, or work.
Page 4: The Wright brothers flew the first powered aeroplane in 1903. Their first flight lasted only twelve seconds but changed travel for ever.
Page 5: Now people can travel much faster and visit many countries in days. These inventions also helped medicine, business, and learning around the world.
Page 6: Inventors often tried many times and failed before they succeeded. Their hard work shows that patience and curiosity can change the world.
Page 7: In the future, new devices will continue to change how we live. Think of one small idea you like and try inventing something today.
5::importantinventionsinhistory.pure_text: Imagine a world without light bulbs, telephones, or aeroplanes at all. These three inventions changed daily life in big and surprising ways. The light bulb brought safe, bright electric light into many homes. Before electric light, people used candles and oil lamps at night. Alexander Graham Bell made the first useful telephone in 1876. People could call friends and family for news, help, or work. The Wright brothers flew the first powered aeroplane in 1903. Their first flight lasted only twelve seconds but changed travel for ever. Now people can travel much faster and visit many countries in days. These inventions also helped medicine, business, and learning around the world. Inventors often tried many times and failed before they succeeded. Their hard work shows that patience and curiosity can change the world. In the future, new devices will continue to change how we live. Think of one small idea you like and try inventing something today.
5::importantinventionsinhistory.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name the three inventions discussed and give the date or period for each one. 2. What problem did the light bulb solve for people before electricity? 3. What does the passage say about how most inventors achieved success?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. What new idea in this passage made you think differently about invention or technology? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author move from one invention to the next? Is this structure effective? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) If you could add a fourth invention to this passage, what would it be and when was it invented? Write two sentences explaining why it changed daily life.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name the three inventions and the people or time period associated with each. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words how the telephone changed communication between people. 3. (Analyse) The author says inventors 'often tried many times and failed'. What does this suggest about the qualities needed to be a successful inventor? 4. (Evaluate) Which of the three inventions do you think has had the biggest impact on daily life today? Use evidence from the text. 5. (Create) Write a short paragraph about one modern invention that you think will be considered as important as the telephone in the future.
5::importantinventionsinhistory.rr_questions:
1. (Under) When did Alexander Graham Bell make the first telephone? (In 1876.)
2. (On) What problem did the light bulb solve for people? (Before it, people used candles and oil lamps at night, which were dim and dangerous.)
3. (On) How long did the Wright brothers' first powered flight last? (Only twelve seconds.)
4. (On) What does the passage say inventors did before succeeding? (They tried many times and failed first.)
5. (Above) Which of the three inventions do you think changed daily life the most? Why? (Accept any answer with a reason from the text.)
5::importantinventionsinhistory.go_description: 以时间为轴呈现三项发明历史顺序的线性图形组织器
5::importantinventionsinhistory.go_usage: 标注：Before electricity (candles) → 1876 telephone → 1903 aeroplane → Today → Future

5::thecaterpillarsbusydays.title: The Caterpillar's Busy Days
5::thecaterpillarsbusydays.example_sentence: Mia was watching a tiny caterpillar eating leaves on a green stem.
5::thecaterpillarsbusydays.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: past continuous of perception verb + object + present participle (not 'to').
He was watching a butterfly opening its wings. / She was watching two dogs playing in the park. / We were watching the sun setting behind the hills.

core_vocab.example: Mia wrapped a big leaf around it and made a small shelter.
Mia wrapped a big leaf around it and made a small shelter.
She put a paper cup nearby to keep birds away from the chrysalis at night.
After a few days a hard shell called a chrysalis covered the caterpillar.
One morning the caterpillar stopped moving and attached itself firmly to a stem.

5::thecaterpillarsbusydays.core_vocab_raw: leaf, shelter, keep ... away, chrysalis, attach
5::thecaterpillarsbusydays.text_7page:
Page 1: Day 1: Mia was watching a tiny caterpillar eating leaves on a green stem. By Day 5 the caterpillar was much bigger and ate many leaves each day.
Page 2: Mia often counted how many leaves it ate before noon. She laughed and said it was the hungriest creature in the garden.
Page 3: One morning the caterpillar stopped moving and attached itself firmly to a stem. Mia watched carefully as it stopped eating and became completely still.
Page 4: After a few days a hard protective case called a chrysalis formed around the caterpillar. Mia checked it every morning and watched for any small changes.
Page 5: One bright morning the chrysalis began to move and a wing appeared inside.
Page 6: Slowly the butterfly pushed out of the chrysalis and spread its bright wings to dry in the warm sun.
Page 7: Mia smiled and promised she would always remember the caterpillar's busy days. She had watched a tiny caterpillar become a beautiful butterfly.
5::thecaterpillarsbusydays.pure_text: Day 1: Mia was watching a tiny caterpillar eating leaves on a green stem. By Day 5 the caterpillar was much bigger and ate many leaves each day. Mia often counted how many leaves it ate before noon. She laughed and said it was the hungriest creature in the garden. One morning the caterpillar stopped moving and attached itself firmly to a stem. Mia watched carefully as it stopped eating and became completely still. After a few days a hard protective case called a chrysalis formed around the caterpillar. Mia checked it every morning and watched for any small changes. One bright morning the chrysalis began to move and a wing appeared inside. Slowly the butterfly pushed out of the chrysalis and spread its bright wings to dry in the warm sun. Mia smiled and promised she would always remember the caterpillar's busy days. She had watched a tiny caterpillar become a beautiful butterfly.
5::thecaterpillarsbusydays.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What did Mia observe the caterpillar doing on Day 1 and Day 5? 2. What happened when the caterpillar attached itself to the stem? 3. How did Mia feel at the end when the butterfly spread its wings?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does Mia's habit of counting leaves tell us about her character? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever watched something grow or change over time? What did you observe? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why is Mia described as 'a small scientist' in the story? What does this comparison mean? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What stages did the caterpillar go through during the story? 2. (Understand) Describe the moment when the butterfly appeared. Use details from the text. 3. (Analyse) How did Mia's feelings about the caterpillar change from the beginning to the end of the story? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think the story would be equally effective if the chrysalis stage was left out? Why or why not? 5. (Create) Write Mia's notebook entry for the final day when the butterfly spread its wings.
5::thecaterpillarsbusydays.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What was the caterpillar doing on Day 1 when Mia first saw it? (Eating leaves on a green stem.)
2. (On) What happened when the caterpillar attached itself to the stem? (A hard protective case called a chrysalis formed around it.)
3. (On) What did Mia see one bright morning after watching the chrysalis? (A wing appeared inside, and the butterfly slowly pushed out.)
4. (On) How did Mia feel at the very end of the story? (She smiled and felt glad she had watched the whole process.)
5. (Above) Why do you think Mia called the caterpillar 'the hungriest creature in the garden'? (Because it ate many leaves every day and she counted them — it was eating constantly to grow.)
5::thecaterpillarsbusydays.go_description: 呈现故事角色与主要情节发展的图形组织器
5::thecaterpillarsbusydays.go_usage: 记录主要角色、遇到的问题和解决方法

5::insectsandlifecycles.title: Insects and Life Cycles
5::insectsandlifecycles.example_sentence: Pupae are quiet and safe while the big changes happen inside.
5::insectsandlifecycles.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: while + clause for two things happening at the same time.
Seeds are dry and still while the roots grow underground. / Students are quiet while the teacher explains. / Chicks are hidden while the parent bird finds food.

core_vocab.example: Larvae hatch from eggs and eat a great deal to grow quickly.
The insect stops feeding and rests inside a chrysalis during the pupa stage.
