A hard protective case called a chrysalis forms around the caterpillar.
Adult insects emerge from the chrysalis ready to fly and find a mate.
Larvae hatch from eggs and begin eating a great deal to grow quickly.

5::insectsandlifecycles.text_7page:
Page 1: Insects have life cycles that go through four stages: egg, larva, pupa, and adult.
Page 2: Egg stage: life begins inside a tiny egg laid on a leaf or stem. Some eggs are smaller than a grain of salt and very hard to see.
Page 3: Larva stage: larvae hatch and eat a lot to grow quickly. They shed their outer skin several times as they grow. Caterpillars and grubs are common larval forms.
Page 4: Pupa stage: the insect stops feeding and rests inside a protective case or chrysalis. Inside, the body slowly changes shape and new parts form.
Page 5: Adult stage: adult insects emerge ready to fly, walk, and find a mate. Adults often look completely different from the larva they once were.
Page 6: Some insects complete the whole cycle in only two weeks, while others take many months. The length of each stage can change with temperature and food.
Page 7: You can observe many of these stages in gardens, parks, and classroom science projects.
5::insectsandlifecycles.pure_text: Insects have life cycles that go through four stages: egg, larva, pupa, and adult. Egg stage: life begins inside a tiny egg laid on a leaf or stem. Some eggs are smaller than a grain of salt and very hard to see. Larva stage: larvae hatch and eat a lot to grow quickly. They shed their outer skin several times as they grow. Caterpillars and grubs are common larval forms. Pupa stage: the insect stops feeding and rests inside a protective case or chrysalis. Inside, the body slowly changes shape and new parts form. Adult stage: adult insects emerge ready to fly, walk, and find a mate. Adults often look completely different from the larva they once were. Some insects complete the whole cycle in only two weeks, while others take many months. The length of each stage can change with temperature and food. You can observe many of these stages in gardens, parks, and classroom science projects.
5::insectsandlifecycles.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name the four stages of an insect's life cycle in the correct order. 2. What happens to an insect's body during the pupa stage? 3. How long can it take for some insects to complete their life cycle?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which stage of the insect life cycle do you find most surprising? Why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the passage organise the four stages? Is this the most logical order? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Draw a simple cycle diagram showing the four stages with one key word for each stage.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What are the four stages of an insect's life cycle? 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words what happens inside the chrysalis during the pupa stage. 3. (Analyse) The text says adults 'often look completely different from the larva'. Why do you think such a dramatic change is necessary? 4. (Evaluate) Why is the cycle diagram a particularly good graphic organiser for this passage? 5. (Create) Write a short paragraph describing the life cycle of a butterfly using only information from this passage.
5::insectsandlifecycles.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What are the four stages of an insect's life cycle in order? (Egg, larva, pupa, adult.)
2. (On) What do larvae do right after hatching? (They eat a lot to grow quickly and shed their skin several times.)
3. (On) What happens inside the chrysalis during the pupa stage? (The body slowly changes shape and new parts form.)
4. (On) How quickly can some insects complete their whole life cycle? (In as little as two weeks.)
5. (Above) Why do you think insects look so different as larvae compared to adults? (Because they need different things at each stage — larvae need to eat and grow while adults need to fly and reproduce.)
5::insectsandlifecycles.go_description: 以循环箭头呈现昆虫四阶段生命周期的图形组织器
5::insectsandlifecycles.go_usage: 四环节：Egg → Larva → Pupa → Adult，各环节写主要特点（eating/shedding/changing/flying）

5::webuiltahomeforgardenanimals.title: We Built a Home for Garden Animals
5::webuiltahomeforgardenanimals.sentence_pattern: [Subject] wanted to [verb phrase] so that [purpose clause].
5::webuiltahomeforgardenanimals.example_sentence: They wanted to build a shelter so that garden animals had a safe place.
5::webuiltahomeforgardenanimals.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: want + to-infinitive + so that + clause for expressing purpose.
She wanted to study harder so that she could pass the exam. / He wanted to wake up early so that he would not be late. / They wanted to save money so that they could buy new equipment.

core_vocab.example: They wanted a safe place for insects and small birds near the rose bushes.
They found old wood, dry leaves, clay pots and soft straw to use.
First they measured the space, drew a plan and gathered tools.
First they measured the space, drew a plan and gathered tools.
They found old wood, dry leaves, clay pots, and soft straw to use.

5::webuiltahomeforgardenanimals.core_vocab_raw: a safe place, clay pot, plan, measure, giggle, straw
5::webuiltahomeforgardenanimals.text_7page:
Page 1: Mia and Tommy wanted to build a small home for garden animals together. They wanted a safe place for insects and small birds near the rose bushes.
Page 2: They found old wood, dry leaves, clay pots, and soft straw to use. First they measured the space, drew a plan, and gathered their tools.
Page 3: Tommy painted the wood in bright colours while Mia carefully cut small openings in the wood. Some ants watched, and a ladybird landed on Mia's finger.
Page 4: One night a storm came and the rain knocked some roof pieces onto the grass. They hurried and moved the roof pieces and tools into the garage for safety.
Page 5: Next day they fixed the shelter, tied the roof with string, and finished the build.
Page 6: Friends brought flower seeds and they planted them together nearby. The garden was starting to look like a welcoming place for wildlife.
Page 7: Soon the garden would be a happy home for insects and birds. The children felt proud of their project and promised to check the shelter every single day.
5::webuiltahomeforgardenanimals.pure_text: Mia and Tommy wanted to build a small home for garden animals together. They wanted a safe place for insects and small birds near the rose bushes. They found old wood, dry leaves, clay pots, and soft straw to use. First they measured the space, drew a plan, and gathered their tools. Tommy painted the wood in bright colours while Mia carefully cut small openings in the wood. Some ants watched, and a ladybird landed on Mia's finger. One night a storm came and the rain knocked some roof pieces onto the grass. They hurried and moved the roof pieces and tools into the garage for safety. Next day they fixed the shelter, tied the roof with string, and finished the build. Friends brought flower seeds and they planted them together nearby. The garden was starting to look like a welcoming place for wildlife. Soon the garden would be a happy home for insects and birds. The children felt proud of their project and promised to check the shelter every single day.
5::webuiltahomeforgardenanimals.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What materials did Mia and Tommy use to build the shelter? 2. What happened the night of the storm and how did they solve the problem? 3. How did other friends contribute to the completed garden shelter?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the storm episode add to the story? What would be lost without it? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever worked on a building or making project that did not go as planned? What happened? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why do you think the children promised to 'check the shelter every single day'? What does this promise show about them? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What was the children's goal and what materials did they use? 2. (Understand) Describe the storm problem and explain how the children solved it. 3. (Analyse) What did the children learn about planning and teamwork from this project? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think adding flower seeds at the end was an important detail? Why or why not? 5. (Create) Write a short instruction guide for building a simple garden shelter, based on the steps in this story.
5::webuiltahomeforgardenanimals.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What materials did Mia and Tommy use to build the shelter? (Old wood, dry leaves, clay pots, and soft straw.)
2. (On) What happened to the shelter during the storm? (Rain knocked some roof pieces onto the grass.)
3. (On) How did the children fix the shelter after the storm? (They tied the roof with string and finished the build the next day.)
4. (On) What did friends bring when the shelter was finished? (Flower seeds, which they planted nearby.)
5. (Above) Why do you think the children promised to check the shelter every day? (Because they cared about the animals and wanted to make sure the shelter was working and safe.)
5::webuiltahomeforgardenanimals.go_description: 呈现故事角色与主要情节发展的图形组织器
5::webuiltahomeforgardenanimals.go_usage: 记录主要角色、遇到的问题和解决方法

5::animalhomesandhabitats.title: Animal Homes and Habitats
5::animalhomesandhabitats.sentence_pattern: Each [place] gives [living things] what they need.
5::animalhomesandhabitats.example_sentence: Each habitat gives animals food, water, and shelter every day.
5::animalhomesandhabitats.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: each + singular noun + gives + object. The place and the beneficiary change.
Each classroom gives students the space and tools they need. / Each park gives visitors a place to rest and breathe fresh air. / Each kitchen gives a family warmth and a place to eat together.

core_vocab.example: Each habitat gives animals food, water, and shelter every day.
A forest has tall trees and a variety of living things.
Birds, deer, and insects live in different forest layers and places.
A single coral reef can support thousands of different sea creatures.
Swamps are warm and wet, and many animals find shelter in trees and mud.

5::animalhomesandhabitats.core_vocab_raw: habitat, forest, insect, ocean, a variety of, swamp
5::animalhomesandhabitats.text_7page:
Page 1: Where do animals live on Earth and why do they choose certain places? Each habitat gives animals food, water, and shelter every day.
Page 2: A desert is very hot and often very dry for most of the year. Many deserts get very little rain, and nights are much cooler.
Page 3: That's why many desert animals are active at night to avoid daytime heat. A forest has tall trees and a variety of living things.
Page 4: Birds, deer, and insects live in different forest layers and places. The ocean covers about seventy percent of Earth's surface.
Page 5: Fish, whales, and corals live in deep and shallow water. Wow fact: A single coral reef can support thousands of different creatures.
Page 6: The Arctic is very cold and icy, and winters can be dark. Polar bears, seals, and snowy owls hunt, rest, and travel on ice.
Page 7: We can learn about habitats and help protect these special homes. Simple actions like reducing plastic and saving water really help wildlife.
5::animalhomesandhabitats.pure_text: Where do animals live on Earth and why do they choose certain places? Each habitat gives animals food, water, and shelter every day. A desert is very hot and often very dry for most of the year. Many deserts get very little rain, and nights are much cooler. That's why many desert animals are active at night to avoid daytime heat. A forest has tall trees and a variety of living things. Birds, deer, and insects live in different forest layers and places. The ocean covers about seventy percent of Earth's surface. Fish, whales, and corals live in deep and shallow water. Wow fact: A single coral reef can support thousands of different creatures. The Arctic is very cold and icy, and winters can be dark. Polar bears, seals, and snowy owls hunt, rest, and travel on ice. We can learn about habitats and help protect these special homes. Simple actions like reducing plastic and saving water really help wildlife.
5::animalhomesandhabitats.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::animalhomesandhabitats.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name four different habitats described in this passage and one animal that lives in each. 2. What does each habitat provide for the animals that live there? 3. Why do animals choose certain habitats over others?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which habitat described in this passage surprised you most? Why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the question 'Where do animals live on Earth and why?' function as an effective opening for this passage? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Choose one habitat from the passage and write two sentences explaining why it would or would not suit a human being.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What four things does a habitat provide for animals, according to the first paragraph? 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words why a desert is a suitable habitat for certain animals. 3. (Analyse) How does the passage move from one habitat to the next? Is each habitat given equal treatment? 4. (Evaluate) If a habitat is destroyed, what do you think would happen to the animals living there? Use ideas from the text. 5. (Create) Write two sentences adding information about a fifth habitat not mentioned in the passage.
5::animalhomesandhabitats.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name two habitats mentioned in this passage. (Desert, forest, ocean, swamp, coral reef — any two.)
2. (On) What three things does each habitat give the animals living there? (Food, water, and shelter.)
3. (On) Why are many desert animals active at night? (Because it is too hot during the day.)
4. (On) What can coral reefs do for sea animals? (Support thousands of different sea creatures.)
5. (Above) Why do you think it is important to protect different habitats around the world? (Because if a habitat is destroyed, the animals that depend on it lose their food, shelter, and water.)
5::animalhomesandhabitats.go_description: 中心为主题，分支呈现不同栖息地及其代表动物的放射状图形组织器
5::animalhomesandhabitats.go_usage: 中心写 "Animal Habitats"，五支：Desert/Forest/Ocean/Arctic/[Habitat]，每支写动物和特点

5::thegardenonourplate.title: The Garden on Our Plate
5::thegardenonourplate.sentence_pattern: It was [comparative adjective] than any [subject] had [past participle] before.
5::thegardenonourplate.example_sentence: The tomato was bigger than any they had grown at home before.
5::thegardenonourplate.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: comparative + than any + past perfect for 'the most X ever' in a past story.
It was colder than any winter she had experienced before. / The cake was better than any he had tasted before. / The climb was harder than any they had attempted before.

core_vocab.example: One night a strong storm shook the plants and leaves fell everywhere.
One morning Mia, Tommy and their family planted tiny seeds in their backyard garden.
They watered the soil and sang while they planted each seed carefully.
Months passed and some plants like sunflowers grew taller than the fence.
They picked many vegetables and fresh herbs together from the garden.

5::thegardenonourplate.core_vocab_raw: storm, backyard, soil, sunflower, roast, harvest
5::thegardenonourplate.text_7page:
Page 1: One morning Mia, Tommy, and their family planted tiny seeds in their backyard garden. They watered the soil and sang songs while planting each seed carefully.
Page 2: Months passed and some plants like sunflowers grew taller than the garden fence. One night a strong storm shook the plants and leaves fell everywhere.
Page 3: Dad quickly built a wooden frame to protect the young plants from the wind. While they worked, Mia found a huge red tomato behind a cluster of leaves.
Page 4: The tomato was bigger than any they had grown at home before. Mia had a bright idea to cook a farm-to-table dinner that evening.
Page 5: They picked many vegetables and fresh herbs together from the garden.
Page 6: Mom made vegetable soup and roasted potatoes while Dad cut warm bread. Mia and Tommy tasted the soup and said it was the best dinner they had ever eaten.
Page 7: Everyone smiled and agreed to save seeds and invite friends to share dinner next time.
5::thegardenonourplate.pure_text: One morning Mia, Tommy, and their family planted tiny seeds in their backyard garden. They watered the soil and sang songs while planting each seed carefully. Months passed and some plants like sunflowers grew taller than the garden fence. One night a strong storm shook the plants and leaves fell everywhere. Dad quickly built a wooden frame to protect the young plants from the wind. While they worked, Mia found a huge red tomato behind a cluster of leaves. The tomato was bigger than any they had grown at home before. Mia had a bright idea to cook a farm-to-table dinner that evening. They picked many vegetables and fresh herbs together from the garden. Mom made vegetable soup and roasted potatoes while Dad cut warm bread. Mia and Tommy tasted the soup and said it was the best dinner they had ever eaten. Everyone smiled and agreed to save seeds and invite friends to share dinner next time.
5::thegardenonourplate.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What problem did the storm cause and how did the family solve it? 2. What did Mia discover while they were fixing the storm damage? 3. What did the family do with the vegetables they harvested that evening?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the phrase 'farm-to-table' mean and how does it connect to the story's theme? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever grown a plant or helped prepare food from scratch? What did it feel like? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why might eating food you have grown yourself feel different from buying it at a shop? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What happened to the garden during the storm? 2. (Understand) Describe the dinner the family made and explain why it felt special. 3. (Analyse) What did the family agree to do next time they had a good harvest? 4. (Evaluate) The tomato was 'bigger than any they had grown at home before'. Why is this detail important to the story? 5. (Create) Write the family's recipe card that Mia might stick on the fridge after this evening.
5::thegardenonourplate.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What did the family plant in their backyard garden? (Tiny seeds, including sunflowers.)
2. (On) What happened to the garden during the storm? (A strong storm shook the plants and leaves fell everywhere.)
3. (On) What did Mia find while they were fixing the storm damage? (A huge red tomato behind a cluster of leaves.)
4. (On) What did the family make for dinner that evening? (Vegetable soup and roasted potatoes with warm bread.)
5. (Above) Why do you think Mia and Tommy said it was the best dinner they had ever eaten? (Because they had grown the food themselves and worked hard for it, which made it feel more special.)
5::thegardenonourplate.go_description: 呈现故事角色与主要情节发展的图形组织器
5::thegardenonourplate.go_usage: 记录主要角色、遇到的问题和解决方法

5::plantsweeat.title: Plants We Eat
5::plantsweeat.example_sentence: Tomatoes are classified as fruits, although many people think of them as vegetables.
5::plantsweeat.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: although + clause for contrast within one sentence.
Dolphins live in the sea, although they are mammals. / The film was long, although we did not feel bored. / She passed, although she had not studied much.

core_vocab.example: Stems hold the plant up and move water and nutrients inside.
Stems hold the plant up and move water and nutrients inside.
Leaves make food for plants using sunlight and a green pigment.
Flowers can be beautiful and sometimes they are also edible parts.
Roots grow under the ground and store food for the plant to use and digest.

5::plantsweeat.core_vocab_raw: nutrient, stem, sunlight, edible, cucumber, digest
5::plantsweeat.text_7page:
Page 1: We eat many parts of plants every day without thinking about them. Plants have five main parts: roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and fruits.
Page 2: Roots grow under the ground and store food for the plant. We often eat carrots, potatoes, beetroots, and other cooked roots.
Page 3: Stems hold the plant up and move water and nutrients inside. We eat stems like celery and asparagus in salads or cooked dishes.
Page 4: Leaves make food for plants using sunlight and a green pigment. We eat leaves such as lettuce, spinach, and cabbage every day.
Page 5: Flowers can be beautiful and sometimes they are also edible parts. Broccoli and cauliflower are examples of flower parts we commonly eat.
Page 6: Fruits hold seeds and usually taste sweet or sometimes a little sour. Tomatoes, cucumbers and pumpkins are all fruits, although many people think them as vegetables.
Page 7: You should always wash fruits and vegetables well before you eat them. Look for roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and fruits on your plate tomorrow.
5::plantsweeat.pure_text: We eat many parts of plants every day without thinking about them. Plants have five main parts: roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and fruits. Roots grow under the ground and store food for the plant. We often eat carrots, potatoes, beetroots, and other cooked roots. Stems hold the plant up and move water and nutrients inside. We eat stems like celery and asparagus in salads or cooked dishes. Leaves make food for plants using sunlight and a green pigment. We eat leaves such as lettuce, spinach, and cabbage every day. Flowers can be beautiful and sometimes they are also edible parts. Broccoli and cauliflower are examples of flower parts we commonly eat. Fruits hold seeds and usually taste sweet or sometimes a little sour. Tomatoes, cucumbers and pumpkins are all fruits, although many people think them as vegetables. You should always wash fruits and vegetables well before you eat them. Look for roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and fruits on your plate tomorrow.
5::plantsweeat.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::plantsweeat.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What are the five main parts of a plant and which foods come from each part? 2. Why are tomatoes and cucumbers classified as fruits even though people treat them as vegetables? 3. What does the passage mean when it says we eat parts of plants 'without thinking about them'?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. What surprised you most about the scientific classification of fruits and vegetables? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the passage use the five-part plant structure to organise the information? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Think of three foods you eat regularly. For each one, write which part of the plant it comes from.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name the five parts of a plant and give one food example for each. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words the difference between how scientists and everyday people use the word 'fruit'. 3. (Analyse) The passage says roots 'store food for the plant'. How does this connect to why we find root vegetables nutritious? 4. (Evaluate) Which plant part do you think provides the most variety of foods that people eat? Use evidence from the text. 5. (Create) Write two sentences that could be added to this passage about a plant food not currently mentioned.
5::plantsweeat.rr_questions:
1. (Under) How many main parts does a plant have? (Five — roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and fruits.)
2. (On) Why are tomatoes and cucumbers scientifically classified as fruits? (Because they grow from the flower and contain seeds.)
3. (On) Give one example of a food we eat that is a root and one that is a stem. (Carrot or potato for root; celery or asparagus for stem.)
4. (On) What is one thing roots do for the plant? (They grow underground and store food for the plant.)
5. (Above) Why do you think most people call tomatoes vegetables even though they are scientifically fruits? (Because in cooking and eating, people group them with savoury foods rather than sweet ones, so the everyday meaning is different from the scientific one.)
5::plantsweeat.go_description: 以五列呈现植物各部位名称、功能和可食用例子的表格型图形组织器
5::plantsweeat.go_usage: 五列：Root/Stem/Leaf/Flower/Fruit，每列填 Function + Example we eat

5::plantsweuseeveryday.title: Plants We Use Every Day
5::plantsweuseeveryday.example_sentence: Grandma explained that farmers collect and process these plants carefully.
5::plantsweuseeveryday.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: explain + that-clause for reporting information. Tense stays present for permanent facts.
The teacher explained that plants need sunlight to grow. / She explained that the museum closes on Mondays. / He explained that the train would arrive late.

core_vocab.example: Then Grandma picked herbs and showed him plant parts used for medicine.
Next she showed cotton, with soft white balls on tall green plants.
Then Grandma picked herbs and showed him plant parts used for medicine.
He was curious and wanted to learn what the green plants could become.
Grandma explained how farmers carefully collect and process these plants.

5::plantsweuseeveryday.core_vocab_raw: medicine, cotton, herb, curious, waste, process
5::plantsweuseeveryday.text_7page:
Page 1: Tommy visited his grandma's garden and small workshop on a sunny Saturday. He was curious and wanted to learn what the green plants around him could become.
Page 2: Grandma showed him four surprising things that plants can make for people. First she talked about paper, which often comes from wood pulp made from trees.
Page 3: Next she showed him cotton, with soft white balls on tall green plants. Tommy gently touched the cotton and smiled at its fluffy feeling.
Page 4: Then Grandma picked herbs and showed him plant parts used in medicine. She explained that doctors and pharmacists use roots, leaves, and flowers to help treat illnesses.
Page 5: Finally she showed strong timber wood used to make chairs, tables, and houses. Tommy lifted a small wooden chair and felt how solid it was.
Page 6: Grandma explained how farmers and factory workers carefully collect and process these plants. Many everyday objects around us began as plants in a field or forest.
Page 7: Tommy promised not to waste any of these things. He left with a new respect for every plant he saw.
5::plantsweuseeveryday.pure_text: Tommy visited his grandma's garden and small workshop on a sunny Saturday. He was curious and wanted to learn what the green plants around him could become. Grandma showed him four surprising things that plants can make for people. First she talked about paper, which often comes from wood pulp made from trees. Next she showed him cotton, with soft white balls on tall green plants. Tommy gently touched the cotton and smiled at its fluffy feeling. Then Grandma picked herbs and showed him plant parts used in medicine. She explained that doctors and pharmacists use roots, leaves, and flowers to help treat illnesses. Finally she showed strong timber wood used to make chairs, tables, and houses. Tommy lifted a small wooden chair and felt how solid it was. Grandma explained how farmers and factory workers carefully collect and process these plants. Many everyday objects around us began as plants in a field or forest. Tommy promised not to waste any of these things. He left with a new respect for every plant he saw.
5::plantsweuseeveryday.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What four plant products did Grandma show Tommy in her garden and workshop? 2. How is paper made from plants, according to Grandma's explanation? 3. What promise did Tommy make before leaving?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does Tommy's surprise at each new plant product tell us about how we take everyday items for granted? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever found out something surprising about where an everyday object comes from? What was it? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why do you think Tommy promised 'not to waste any of these things'? What had he understood? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What were the four plant products Grandma showed Tommy? 2. (Understand) Describe one product and explain the process Grandma used to explain how it comes from plants. 3. (Analyse) How did Tommy's understanding of plants change between the beginning and the end of the story? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think visiting Grandma's garden was more effective than reading about plants in a book? Why? 5. (Create) Write Tommy's thank-you note to Grandma, mentioning two things he learned.
5::plantsweuseeveryday.rr_questions:
1. (Under) How many surprising things did Grandma show Tommy? (Four.)
2. (On) What is paper made from according to Grandma? (Wood pulp made from trees, processed at a mill.)
3. (On) What part of plants do doctors and pharmacists use for medicine? (Roots, leaves, and flowers.)
4. (On) What promise did Tommy make before leaving? (Not to waste any of these plant products.)
5. (Above) Why do you think visiting Grandma's workshop changed how Tommy thought about everyday objects? (Because he could see and touch where they came from, which made the connection between plants and products real for him.)
5::plantsweuseeveryday.go_description: 按用途分支呈现四类植物产品的层级图形组织器
5::plantsweuseeveryday.go_usage: 顶层写 "Plants We Use"，四支：Paper/Cotton/Medicine/Timber，每支填 source plant + how used

5::amazingplants.title: Amazing Plants
5::amazingplants.example_sentence: The Venus flytrap is a small plant that catches insects for food.
5::amazingplants.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: defining relative clause with 'that' to define or classify a thing.
A bicycle is a simple machine that helps people travel without fuel. / A library is a quiet place that gives people free access to books. / A penguin is a bird that cannot fly but swims very well.

core_vocab.example: Plants can be surprising and sometimes look like animals or small boats.
Cactus lives in dry places and stores water inside its thick stem.
The giant water lily grows on warm rivers and lakes in South America.
Its sharp spines protect the plant and stop animals from eating it.
The giant water lily is so large that a small child can sit on it and float.

5::amazingplants.core_vocab_raw: surprising, cactus, giant, spine, venus flytrap, float
5::amazingplants.text_7page:
Page 1: Plants can be surprising and sometimes look like animals or small boats. We are going to see three special plants from different parts of the world.
Page 2: Venus flytrap is a small plant that eats insects for food. Its two leaf halves close quickly when an insect touches tiny hairs.
Page 3: The trap can shut in less than one second to catch prey. Cactus lives in dry places and stores water inside its thick stem.
Page 4: Its sharp spines protect the plant and stop animals from eating it. Some cactus flowers open at night to attract bats, moths, or other pollinators.
Page 5: The giant water lily grows on warm rivers and lakes in South America. Its round leaf can be more than two meters across and very strong.
Page 6: People long ago used the large leaves like boats for small children. It is amazing that plants help people, animals, and even the whole planet every day.
Page 7: They make oxygen, food, medicines, and habitats for many living things. Learn more about these plants and you will find many surprises.
5::amazingplants.pure_text: Plants can be surprising and sometimes look like animals or small boats. We are going to see three special plants from different parts of the world. Venus flytrap is a small plant that eats insects for food. Its two leaf halves close quickly when an insect touches tiny hairs. The trap can shut in less than one second to catch prey. Cactus lives in dry places and stores water inside its thick stem. Its sharp spines protect the plant and stop animals from eating it. Some cactus flowers open at night to attract bats, moths, or other pollinators. The giant water lily grows on warm rivers and lakes in South America. Its round leaf can be more than two meters across and very strong. People long ago used the large leaves like boats for small children. It is amazing that plants help people, animals, and even the whole planet every day. They make oxygen, food, medicines, and habitats for many living things. Learn more about these plants and you will find many surprises.
5::amazingplants.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::amazingplants.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name the three special plants described and one unique feature of each. 2. How does the Venus flytrap catch its food? 3. What is the giant water lily and where does it grow?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which of the three plants did you find most surprising and why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the opening sentence ('Plants can be surprising and sometimes look like animals or small boats') prepare the reader for the rest of the passage? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences describing a plant you know about that could be added to this passage.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name the three plants and give one surprising fact about each. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words how the Venus flytrap gets the nutrients it needs. 3. (Analyse) The passage says the cactus 'knows how to live without water'. Is this accurate scientific language? How could it be improved? 4. (Evaluate) Which plant in this passage would be hardest to keep at home? Why? 5. (Create) Write a short description of a fourth 'amazing plant' that could be added to this passage.
5::amazingplants.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name the three special plants described in this passage. (Venus flytrap, cactus, and giant water lily.)
2. (On) How does the Venus flytrap catch insects? (Its two leaf halves close quickly when an insect touches tiny hairs.)
3. (On) How does a cactus survive without rain? (It stores water inside its thick stem and uses sharp spines to protect itself.)
4. (On) What is surprising about the giant water lily? (It is so large that a small child can sit on it and float.)
5. (Above) Why do you think plants that live in difficult environments often have the most unusual features? (Because they have had to develop special solutions to survive where other plants cannot.)
5::amazingplants.graphic_organizer: Main Idea & Details Web (主旨细节网)
5::amazingplants.go_description: 中心为主旨、向外辐射三种神奇植物特点的放射状图形组织器
5::amazingplants.go_usage: 中心写 "Amazing Plants"，三支：Venus Flytrap/Cactus/Giant Water Lily，每支写 habitat + special feature

5::familytime.title: Family Time
5::familytime.example_sentence: Talking and exploring together could be more fun than spending time online.
5::familytime.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: gerund as subject + could be + comparative. Students change the activity and the comparison.
Cooking at home could be more enjoyable than eating out. / Walking to school could be more relaxing than taking the bus. / Reading together could be more interesting than watching TV.

core_vocab.example: Grandma gave Mia and Tommy an old map and a few clues.
Inside was an old family recipe card and a short note from Grandma.
Dad packed sandwiches for a picnic while Mom drew a treasure route on paper.
Mia’s family planned a special day without phones or tablets.
Behind some bushes, the children discovered a tiny wooden chest with old items.

5::familytime.core_vocab_raw: clue, recipe, treasure, tablet, device, discover
5::familytime.text_7page:
Page 1: Mia's family planned a special day without phones or tablets. Before leaving home, they put all their devices into a big box.
Page 2: At first, both Mia and Tommy worried that the day would be boring. Grandma gave them an old map and a few mystery clues to follow.
Page 3: Dad packed sandwiches for a picnic while Mom drew a treasure route on paper. Soon the whole family walked to the park together.
Page 4: They followed the map past a bridge and under a large oak tree. Behind some bushes, the children found a tiny wooden chest.
Page 5: Inside was an old family recipe card and a short note from Grandma. Mia and Tommy smiled as they read the note together.
Page 6: They realized that talking, laughing, and exploring together could be more fun than spending time online.
Page 7: The family sat on a picnic blanket and shared lunch in the park. Everyone laughed and agreed to have another family day very soon.
5::familytime.pure_text: Mia's family planned a special day without phones or tablets. Before leaving home, they put all their devices into a big box. At first, both Mia and Tommy worried that the day would be boring. Grandma gave them an old map and a few mystery clues to follow. Dad packed sandwiches for a picnic while Mom drew a treasure route on paper. Soon the whole family walked to the park together. They followed the map past a bridge and under a large oak tree. Behind some bushes, the children found a tiny wooden chest. Inside was an old family recipe card and a short note from Grandma. Mia and Tommy smiled as they read the note together. They realized that talking, laughing, and exploring together could be more fun than spending time online. The family sat on a picnic blanket and shared lunch in the park. Everyone laughed and agreed to have another family day very soon.
5::familytime.reading_strategy: 进行预测 (Making and Confirming Predictions)
5::familytime.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Why did the family decide to put their devices away for the day? 2. What did Grandma give the children to help them start the day and what did they find? 3. What did the family realise about spending time together without technology?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the fact that Grandma planned the treasure hunt secretly tell us about her character? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Has your family ever had a day without screens? How did it feel? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why do you think the recipe card inside the chest was a meaningful discovery? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Why did the family put all their devices in a box? 2. (Understand) Describe the treasure hunt and explain what the children found at the end. 3. (Analyse) What did the family learn about themselves from this no-device day? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think the children would have enjoyed the day as much if they had known about the treasure hunt in advance? Why? 5. (Create) Write a plan for a no-device family day of your own, including three specific activities and explaining why each one would bring the family together.
5::familytime.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What did the family put in a big box before leaving home? (All their phones and tablets.)
2. (On) What did Grandma give the children to help them start the day? (An old map and a few mystery clues.)
3. (On) What did the children find inside the wooden chest? (An old family recipe card and a short note from Grandma.)
4. (On) Where did the family have their picnic lunch? (In the park, sitting on a picnic blanket.)
5. (Above) Why do you think the children found the treasure hunt more exciting than spending time online? (Because it was real, active, and involved the whole family working and exploring together.)
5::familytime.go_description: 以路线形式呈现家庭探险步骤的图形组织器
5::familytime.go_usage: 标注步骤：Pack devices away → Get map & clues → Walk to park → Follow map → Find chest → Share lunch

5::familymembersandsimilarities.title: Family Members and Similarities
5::familymembersandsimilarities.example_sentence: My father is tall and enjoys gardening on weekends.
5::familymembersandsimilarities.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: possessive + noun + is + adjective + and + verb phrase for personal description.
My sister is twelve and loves playing football. / My grandmother is kind and often cooks traditional food. / My teacher is patient and always explains things clearly.

core_vocab.example: My sister is ten and practises the piano every afternoon after school.
Three people in our family can speak two languages well.
Even though we have different hobbies and talents, we always respect each other.
Even though we have different hobbies and talents, we always respect and help each other.
Even though we have different hobbies and talents, we always admire each other.

5::familymembersandsimilarities.core_vocab_raw: practice the piano, language, gardening, respect, childhood memories, admire
5::familymembersandsimilarities.text_7page:
Page 1: My family has six people and we live in a small house near our school. Everyone in the family is different in many ways.
Page 2: My father is tall and enjoys gardening on weekends. He also likes cooking dinner for the whole family.
Page 3: My mother is shorter and works as a teacher during the week. She reads books every evening before bed.
Page 4: My brother is twelve and loves football, but he does not enjoy reading very much. My sister is ten and practises the piano every afternoon after school.
Page 5: My grandparents live nearby and often tell us stories about our family history and their childhood memories.
Page 6: Three people in our family can speak two languages well. Even though we have different hobbies and talents, we always respect and support each other.
Page 7: We also share things in common: we enjoy the same holiday foods, play board games together, and always laugh at the same jokes.
5::familymembersandsimilarities.pure_text: My family has six people and we live in a small house near our school. Everyone in the family is different in many ways. My father is tall and enjoys gardening on weekends. He also likes cooking dinner for the whole family. My mother is shorter and works as a teacher during the week. She reads books every evening before bed. My brother is twelve and loves football, but he does not enjoy reading very much. My sister is ten and practises the piano every afternoon after school. My grandparents live nearby and often tell us stories about our family history and their childhood memories. Three people in our family can speak two languages well. Even though we have different hobbies and talents, we always respect and support each other. We also share things in common: we enjoy the same holiday foods, play board games together, and always laugh at the same jokes.
5::familymembersandsimilarities.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Describe two differences between family members mentioned in the passage. 2. What things does the family have in common, according to the passage? 3. How many people in the family can speak two languages and why might this be an advantage?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. What surprised you most about the similarities and differences in this family? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author move from describing differences to describing similarities? Is this an effective structure? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences comparing yourself to a family member, using the same structure as the author.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name two family members and describe one difference between them. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words what the family members have in common at the end. 3. (Analyse) The author says 'Even though we have different hobbies and talents, we always respect and support each other.' What does this suggest about what makes a family work? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think speaking two languages is an advantage or a challenge in a family? Give a reason. 5. (Create) Write two sentences about your own family using the structure: 'Even though we are different in [X], we share [Y].'
5::familymembersandsimilarities.rr_questions:
1. (Under) How many people are in the narrator's family? (Six.)
2. (On) What does the narrator's father enjoy doing at weekends? (Gardening — and cooking dinner for the family.)
3. (On) What hobby does the narrator's sister have? (She practises the piano every afternoon.)
4. (On) Name two things the family shares in common. (Holiday foods, board games, and the same jokes — any two.)
5. (Above) Why do you think the narrator says 'even though we are different, we always respect each other'? (Because respecting differences is what keeps a family happy and united, even when people have different interests.)
5::familymembersandsimilarities.go_description: 左列记录家庭成员各自不同之处，右列记录共同点的双列对比图
5::familymembersandsimilarities.go_usage: 左列写每位家庭成员的不同特点，右列写三个共同点（holiday food/board games/same jokes）

5::thefamilygathering.title: The Family Gathering
5::thefamilygathering.example_sentence: He asked everyone to pick a card from the deck.
5::thefamilygathering.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: ask + object + to-infinitive for reported requests.
The teacher asked the class to open their books. / She asked her friend to help carry the bag. / He asked the waiter to bring the bill.

core_vocab.example: Mia walked into a big hall with her parents.
Allen explained that he had practiced card magic for many years.
Later, the family played a quiz using old photos, and Mia guessed the oldest picture correctly.
Later, the family played a quiz using old photos, and Mia guessed the oldest picture correctly.
Mia walked into a big hall and felt nervous to meet distant relatives.

5::thefamilygathering.core_vocab_raw: hall, card magic, quiz, guess, name tag, distant
5::thefamilygathering.text_7page:
Page 1: Mia walked into a big hall with her parents. She felt nervous but excited to meet distant relatives she had never seen before.
Page 2: The long table smelled of cake and flowers, and people introduced themselves warmly. Children ran and laughed around the room, making the hall feel lively and bright.
Page 3: Mia tried to read the name tags on older cousins and grandparents nearby. Suddenly, a cousin named Allen stood beside the table holding a deck of cards.
Page 4: He smiled and asked everyone to pick a card. Then he performed several clever card tricks that made the family laugh and clap loudly.
Page 5: Allen explained that he had practiced card magic for many years. He also shared stories about performing tricks at school parties.
Page 6: Mia asked many questions and wanted to learn some easy tricks too. Later, the family played a quiz using old photos, and Mia guessed the oldest picture correctly.
Page 7: She felt proud, hugged her relatives, and smiled all evening. She hoped to hear more family stories next summer.
5::thefamilygathering.pure_text: Mia walked into a big hall with her parents. She felt nervous but excited to meet distant relatives she had never seen before. The long table smelled of cake and flowers, and people introduced themselves warmly. Children ran and laughed around the room, making the hall feel lively and bright. Mia tried to read the name tags on older cousins and grandparents nearby. Suddenly, a cousin named Allen stood beside the table holding a deck of cards. He smiled and asked everyone to pick a card. Then he performed several clever card tricks that made the family laugh and clap loudly. Allen explained that he had practiced card magic for many years. He also shared stories about performing tricks at school parties. Mia asked many questions and wanted to learn some easy tricks too. Later, the family played a quiz using old photos, and Mia guessed the oldest picture correctly. She felt proud, hugged her relatives, and smiled all evening. She hoped to hear more family stories next summer.
5::thefamilygathering.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Why did Mia feel nervous but excited at the beginning of the story? 2. What activities happened at the gathering to help people get to know each other? 3. What did the old woman's story about the grandfather reveal?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. Why do you think the card magic trick was a good activity for bringing strangers together? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever met a large group of relatives or people you did not know well? How did it feel? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) What does the old woman's story suggest about the importance of family memory and storytelling? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Why was Mia nervous at the start and what helped her feel more comfortable? 2. (Understand) Describe the two activities that helped everyone at the gathering connect. 3. (Analyse) What unexpected discovery happened when the old woman told her story? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think family gatherings are important? Use one example from the story to support your view. 5. (Create) Write Mia's message to a friend describing the best moment of the family gathering.
5::thefamilygathering.rr_questions:
1. (Under) How did Mia feel when she walked into the hall to meet her relatives? (Nervous but excited.)
2. (On) What activity did a kind uncle use to help everyone relax and laugh? (Card magic tricks — asking everyone to pick a card.)
3. (On) What did the old woman's story reveal? (A story about the grandfather as a young man that nobody had heard before.)
4. (On) What quiz did cousins set up after lunch? (A family history quiz with teams.)
5. (Above) Why do you think family gatherings are important even when you don't know everyone there? (Because they help you discover new things about your family history and feel connected to people you share a background with.)
5::thefamilygathering.go_description: 呈现故事角色与主要情节发展的图形组织器
5::thefamilygathering.go_usage: 记录主要角色、遇到的问题和解决方法

5::familytraditions.title: Family Traditions
5::familytraditions.sentence_pattern: We [verb phrase], [verb phrase], and [verb phrase] together.
5::familytraditions.example_sentence: We sing, cut the cake, and play games together every birthday.
5::familytraditions.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: three-part parallel verb list for shared activities.
We cook, decorate, and eat together every New Year. / We run, jump, and laugh together at sports day. / We read, discuss, and draw together in art class.

core_vocab.example: They also create happy memories that children can remember for many years.
At festivals families visit relatives, cook special food, and decorate the house together.
We have a family dinner every Sunday evening at our kitchen table.
Last spring we prepared red lanterns and hung them in our street.
At festivals, families visit relatives, cook special food, and decorate.

5::familytraditions.core_vocab_raw: remember, decorate, family dinner, lantern, family tradition, relative
5::familytraditions.text_7page:
Page 1: Family traditions make home life warm, special, and full of happy memories. They are things families do regularly, like festivals, birthdays, and Sunday dinners.
Page 2: At festivals families visit relatives, cook special food, and decorate the house together. Some families have many traditional dishes to share with guests and neighbours.
Page 3: Last spring we prepared red lanterns and hung them along our street. The lanterns glowed all night and made the neighbourhood feel bright and welcoming.
Page 4: Birthdays are often the most fun and most personal family events. We sing, cut cake, give small gifts, and play games together.
Page 5: We have a family dinner every Sunday evening at our kitchen table. At dinner we talk about school, work, and plans for the coming week.
Page 6: We sometimes cook together to learn old recipes and share family stories. Cooking the same dishes connects us to our grandparents and their memories.
Page 7: Family traditions help people feel close to each other. They also create happy memories that children can carry with them for many years.
5::familytraditions.pure_text: Family traditions make home life warm, special, and full of happy memories. They are things families do regularly, like festivals, birthdays, and Sunday dinners. At festivals families visit relatives, cook special food, and decorate the house together. Some families have many traditional dishes to share with guests and neighbours. Last spring we prepared red lanterns and hung them along our street. The lanterns glowed all night and made the neighbourhood feel bright and welcoming. Birthdays are often the most fun and most personal family events. We sing, cut cake, give small gifts, and play games together. We have a family dinner every Sunday evening at our kitchen table. At dinner we talk about school, work, and plans for the coming week. We sometimes cook together to learn old recipes and share family stories. Cooking the same dishes connects us to our grandparents and their memories. Family traditions help people feel close to each other. They also create happy memories that children can carry with them for many years.
5::familytraditions.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::familytraditions.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What three types of family tradition does the passage describe? 2. What happens at festival time in the narrator's family? 3. Why does the narrator say cooking together 'connects us to our grandparents'?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which family tradition described in this passage is most similar to one you have? Explain. 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author support the claim that traditions 'create happy memories'? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences describing a family tradition you have or would like to have, using the structure of this passage.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name three family traditions described in the passage. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words why traditions help family members feel close to each other. 3. (Analyse) The author says traditions are 'things families do regularly'. Why is regularity important for a tradition? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think all families need the same types of tradition? Why or why not? 5. (Create) Write a paragraph about one tradition you would like your family to start and explain why.
5::familytraditions.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name three types of family tradition described in this passage. (Festivals, birthdays, and Sunday family dinners.)
2. (On) What does the narrator's family do at festival time? (Visit relatives, cook special food, and decorate the house.)
3. (On) When do they have their regular family dinner? (Every Sunday evening.)
4. (On) Why does the narrator say cooking together connects them to their grandparents? (Because they cook the same recipes and share family stories while cooking.)
5. (Above) Why do you think traditions make families feel closer to each other? (Because doing the same things together regularly creates shared memories and a sense of belonging.)
5::familytraditions.graphic_organizer: Main Idea & Details Web (主旨细节网)
5::familytraditions.go_description: 中心为主旨、向外辐射三种家庭传统及各自特点的放射状图形组织器
5::familytraditions.go_usage: 中心写 "Family Traditions"，三支：Festivals/Birthdays/Sunday Dinners，每支写我们的具体做法

5::letsgogreen.title: Let's Go Green
5::letsgogreen.example_sentence: Ken said they would count bottles and put up a sign near the bins.
5::letsgogreen.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: say + that-clause + would (backshift from will). Only the reported action changes.
She said she would bring a reusable bag every day. / He said he would help clean the bins after school. / They said they would meet at the park at noon.

core_vocab.example: Ken said they could count plastic bottles and make a big sign.
The teacher said that if everyone reduces one bottle each day, we can save many bottles.
Lina explained they must teach friends to bring reusable bottles and bags.
Lina said they were going to pick up trash after lunch today.
The pond was full of litter, and Mia felt sad when she saw it.

5::letsgogreen.core_vocab_raw: plastic bottle, reduce, reusable, trash, green, litter
5::letsgogreen.text_7page:
Page 1: Mia started a green club at school to help protect the environment. At their first meeting, she and her friends Lina and Ken made a list of changes to try.
Page 2: Lina suggested picking up rubbish around the pond after lunch each day. Ken said they could count plastic bottles in the school bins and make a big sign.
Page 3: At noon they walked by the pond and saw many bottles floating on the surface. The sight was funny at first, but then it felt like a serious problem that needed a solution.
Page 4: They counted three hundred bottles in the school bins that week. The teacher said that if everyone reduced just one bottle a day, they could save many bottles each week.
Page 5: Lina explained that friends needed to learn to bring reusable bottles and bags. Ken suggested making art from old bottles and giving prizes to creative winners.
Page 6: Soon the school bins had fewer bottles and more students were smiling about the change. In just one month they had kept over one thousand bottles out of the bins.
Page 7: Mia felt much happier and the pond looked cleaner than ever before. Everyone in the club agreed: small actions by many people can make the biggest difference for Earth.
5::letsgogreen.pure_text: Mia started a green club at school to help protect the environment. At their first meeting, she and her friends Lina and Ken made a list of changes to try. Lina suggested picking up rubbish around the pond after lunch each day. Ken said they could count plastic bottles in the school bins and make a big sign. At noon they walked by the pond and saw many bottles floating on the surface. The sight was funny at first, but then it felt like a serious problem that needed a solution. They counted three hundred bottles in the school bins that week. The teacher said that if everyone reduced just one bottle a day, they could save many bottles each week. Lina explained that friends needed to learn to bring reusable bottles and bags. Ken suggested making art from old bottles and giving prizes to creative winners. Soon the school bins had fewer bottles and more students were smiling about the change. In just one month they had kept over one thousand bottles out of the bins. Mia felt much happier and the pond looked cleaner than ever before. Everyone in the club agreed: small actions by many people can make the biggest difference for Earth.
5::letsgogreen.reading_skill: 问题与解决方案 (Problem and Solution)
5::letsgogreen.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What three steps did Mia's green club take to reduce plastic use at school? 2. What did the teacher say about reducing one bottle per day? 3. What change happened to the school bins after one month of effort?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does saving over one thousand bottles in one month tell you about the impact of small actions? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever taken part in an environmental action at school or in your community? What was it? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Ken suggested making art from old bottles. Can creative solutions sometimes be more effective than rules? Why? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What was the green club's first action and what did they observe? 2. (Understand) Describe the change in the school bins after one month of the club's efforts. 3. (Analyse) What does the statistic 'over one thousand bottles' show about the collective impact of individual choices? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think a prize system for recycling is a good motivator? What are the advantages and possible disadvantages? 5. (Create) Design a poster for the school's green club. Include a slogan and two specific actions students can take.
5::letsgogreen.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What did Mia start at school to help the environment? (A green club.)
2. (On) What did Lina suggest the club do? (Pick up rubbish around the pond after lunch each day.)
3. (On) What did the club keep out of the bins in just one month? (Over one thousand plastic bottles.)
4. (On) What did Ken suggest as a creative idea? (Making art from old bottles and giving prizes to winners.)
5. (Above) Why do you think small actions by many people can make such a big difference? (Because when everyone does even a little, the total effect adds up quickly — one thousand bottles saved shows how fast that happens.)
5::letsgogreen.go_description: 呈现故事角色与主要情节发展的图形组织器
5::letsgogreen.go_usage: 记录主要角色、遇到的问题和解决方法

5::pollutionandclimatechange.title: Pollution and Climate Change
5::pollutionandclimatechange.example_sentence: Cars and trucks release harmful gases into the air every day.
5::pollutionandclimatechange.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: release + object + into + place. The subject and the released substance change.
Factories release thick smoke into the air above cities. / Burning coal releases carbon dioxide into the air. / Old vehicles release more exhaust fumes into the air than new ones.

core_vocab.example: Pollution and climate change affect people, animals, cities, and food every day.
People throw plastics and chemicals into rivers and seas every day.
Pollution and climate change affect people, animals, cities, and food every day.
Many problems start because people burn coal, oil, and gas.
Cars and trucks release harmful gases into the air every day.

5::pollutionandclimatechange.core_vocab_raw: pollution, chemical, climate change, coal, particle, release
5::pollutionandclimatechange.text_7page:
Page 1: Pollution and climate change affect people, animals, cities, and food every day. Many problems start because people burn coal, oil, and gas.
Page 2: Power stations and some factories make electricity and heat this way. Cars and trucks also release gases and tiny harmful particles into the air.
Page 3: People throw plastics and chemicals into rivers and seas every day. These gases and waste make the Earth warmer and the weather worse.
Page 4: Warmer Earth melts ice on mountains and at the poles faster than before. Melting ice and stronger storms can harm homes, farms, and animals nearby.
Page 5: A surprising fact is one plastic bottle can last about 450 years. This waste can hurt fish and birds and reach our food sometimes.
Page 6: We can slow damage by using less energy and wasting fewer things. Walk more, use buses, recycle, and plant trees near homes.
Page 7: Schools and cities can choose clean energy like sun and wind. Young people can teach adults and make small changes every day. If many people act now, our world will be healthier and safer.
5::pollutionandclimatechange.pure_text: Pollution and climate change affect people, animals, cities, and food every day. Many problems start because people burn coal, oil, and gas. Power stations and some factories make electricity and heat this way. Cars and trucks also release gases and tiny harmful particles into the air. People throw plastics and chemicals into rivers and seas every day. These gases and waste make the Earth warmer and the weather worse. Warmer Earth melts ice on mountains and at the poles faster than before. Melting ice and stronger storms can harm homes, farms, and animals nearby. A surprising fact is one plastic bottle can last about 450 years. This waste can hurt fish and birds and reach our food sometimes. We can slow damage by using less energy and wasting fewer things. Walk more, use buses, recycle, and plant trees near homes. Schools and cities can choose clean energy like sun and wind. Young people can teach adults and make small changes every day. If many people act now, our world will be healthier and safer.
5::pollutionandclimatechange.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name three main causes of air pollution mentioned in the passage. 2. What are two effects of climate change described in the text? 3. What does the passage suggest people can do to reduce pollution?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which effect of climate change mentioned in this passage do you find most serious? Why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the passage move from causes to effects to solutions? Is this structure effective? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences explaining one action you could take to reduce pollution, using evidence from the passage.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What are three things that release harmful gases into the air? 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words what 'greenhouse effect' means based on the passage. 3. (Analyse) The passage says 'warming temperatures melt ice'. What further effects does this cause, according to the text? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think individual actions or government policies are more important for fighting pollution? Use the text to support your view. 5. (Create) Write a short public awareness message (2-3 sentences) about climate change, using facts from this passage.
5::pollutionandclimatechange.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What are two main causes of air pollution mentioned in the passage? (Burning coal, oil, and gas; and cars and trucks releasing gases — any two.)
2. (On) What effect does warming have on ice, according to the passage? (It melts ice on mountains and at the poles, causing sea levels to rise.)
3. (On) What do greenhouse gases do to the Earth's heat? (They trap heat in the atmosphere and make the planet warmer.)
4. (On) What can individuals do to help, according to the passage? (Use less electricity, choose cleaner transport, and reduce waste.)
5. (Above) Why do you think it is difficult to solve pollution and climate change even when people know the causes? (Because it requires many people and governments to change habits and systems at the same time, which is slow and complicated.)
5::pollutionandclimatechange.go_description: 呈现污染和气候变化主要原因与结果的因果图形组织器
5::pollutionandclimatechange.go_usage: 左列写三个原因（burning fuel/plastic waste/industry），右列写对应结果（warming/melting ice/pollution）

5::tommysschoolproject.title: Tommy's School Project
5::tommysschoolproject.sentence_pattern: [Subject] found fewer [noun] than expected, but much more [noun].
5::tommysschoolproject.example_sentence: Tommy found fewer bottles than expected, but much more paper in the bins.
5::tommysschoolproject.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: fewer (countable) vs much more (uncountable) — key quantifier contrast.
She found fewer mistakes than expected, but much more confusion about the rules. / He found fewer students than expected, but much more noise in the corridor.

core_vocab.example: During practice, Tommy accidentally glued one sign onto the wrong bin, but he quickly fixed it.
He glued recycled cardboard together and painted the recycling bins bright blue.
Tommy wanted to make a school project about the environment this term.
The cleaner said small labels on bins could change students’ habits quickly.
He hopes the school will become cleaner with more recycling every day.

5::tommysschoolproject.core_vocab_raw: bin, cardboard, environment, label, recycling, recycle
5::tommysschoolproject.text_7page:
Page 1: Tommy wanted to make a school project about the environment this term. He decided to research what people threw away each day.
Page 2: He walked around the playground and counted cans, bottles, and pieces of paper. He found fewer bottles than expected but much more paper than he thought.
Page 3: Next, Tommy interviewed the school cleaner, two teachers, and a parent volunteer. The cleaner said small labels on bins could change students' habits quickly.
Page 4: Tommy used this idea to build a model recycling station for the school. He glued recycled cardboard together and painted the recycling bins bright blue and green.
Page 5: His sister made colourful paper signs for each bin. During practice, Tommy accidentally glued one sign onto the wrong bin, but he quickly fixed it.
Page 6: On Friday morning the model stood in the school hall for everyone to see. Tommy explained his project clearly to students, teachers, and parents who came to look.
Page 7: His teacher said the project could help the school become cleaner and greener. Tommy smiled and understood that small actions by many people make a real difference.
5::tommysschoolproject.pure_text: Tommy wanted to make a school project about the environment this term. He decided to research what people threw away each day. He walked around the playground and counted cans, bottles, and pieces of paper. He found fewer bottles than expected but much more paper than he thought. Next, Tommy interviewed the school cleaner, two teachers, and a parent volunteer. The cleaner said small labels on bins could change students' habits quickly. Tommy used this idea to build a model recycling station for the school. He glued recycled cardboard together and painted the recycling bins bright blue and green. His sister made colourful paper signs for each bin. During practice, Tommy accidentally glued one sign onto the wrong bin, but he quickly fixed it. On Friday morning the model stood in the school hall for everyone to see. Tommy explained his project clearly to students, teachers, and parents who came to look. His teacher said the project could help the school become cleaner and greener. Tommy smiled and understood that small actions by many people make a real difference.
5::tommysschoolproject.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What process did Tommy follow from research to presentation? 2. What did the school cleaner suggest and how did Tommy use this idea? 3. What happened on Friday morning when the model stood in the hall?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. The cleaner's advice turned out to be the most important part of Tommy's project. What does this suggest about where good ideas come from? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever done a project where things did not go as planned? What did you change? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why is it important to test your model before presenting it? What did Tommy's mistake teach him? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What was Tommy's research question and how did he gather information? 2. (Understand) Describe the recycling model Tommy built and explain what went wrong during practice. 3. (Analyse) What was Tommy's teacher's reaction and what did it suggest about the project's impact? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think interviewing the school cleaner was Tommy's best research decision? Why or why not? 5. (Create) Write the three most important steps of Tommy's project as a numbered guide for future students.
5::tommysschoolproject.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What did Tommy count when he walked around the playground? (Cans, bottles, and pieces of paper.)
2. (On) What did the school cleaner suggest that Tommy used in his project? (Small labels on bins could change students' habits quickly.)
3. (On) What did Tommy do by mistake during practice? (He accidentally glued one sign onto the wrong bin.)
4. (On) Who came to see the model on Friday morning? (Students, teachers, and parents.)
5. (Above) Why do you think the school cleaner gave better advice than the teachers did in this story? (Because the cleaner sees the bins every day and knows exactly what problems exist, so their experience is very practical.)
5::tommysschoolproject.go_description: 以链条呈现环保项目步骤的线性图形组织器
5::tommysschoolproject.go_usage: 记录 Tommy 的项目步骤：Survey → Interview → Design → Build model → Present → School goes green

5::renewableenergyandrecycling.title: Renewable Energy and Recycling
5::renewableenergyandrecycling.example_sentence: Recycling paper can save seventeen trees for every tonne collected.
5::renewableenergyandrecycling.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: gerund as subject + can + base verb for expressing potential impact.
Turning off lights can reduce your electricity bill. / Planting trees can help clean the air. / Bringing a reusable bag can prevent hundreds of plastic bags from reaching the sea.

core_vocab.example: Hydropower plants have produced clean electricity for many years now.
A roof with ten panels can power a small school in summer.
Renewable sources make less air and water pollution than fossil fuels.
Solar energy comes from the sun and is easy to find.
A single wind turbine can power hundreds of homes on a windy day.

5::renewableenergyandrecycling.text_7page:
Page 1: Our planet needs cleaner energy and less waste every single day. People can use sunlight, wind, and water to make power.
