Page 2: Solar energy comes from the sun and is easy to find. A roof with ten panels can power a small school in summer.
Page 3: Wind energy uses large turbines that turn when the air moves. One turbine can power hundreds or even more houses on windy coasts.
Page 4: Water energy comes from rivers, tides, and waves and is strong. Hydropower plants have produced clean electricity for many years now.
Page 5: Recycling means turning old things into new materials and saving energy. Glass, paper, and metal can be recycled many times without losing quality.
Page 6: For example, recycling one ton of paper can save about seventeen trees. Renewable sources make less air and water pollution than fossil fuels.
Page 7: If we act now, our air and water can become much healthier. Let's start at home: turn off lights, reduce waste, and use separate bins.
5::renewableenergyandrecycling.pure_text: Our planet needs cleaner energy and less waste every single day. People can use sunlight, wind, and water to make power. Solar energy comes from the sun and is easy to find. A roof with ten panels can power a small school in summer. Wind energy uses large turbines that turn when the air moves. One turbine can power hundreds or even more houses on windy coasts. Water energy comes from rivers, tides, and waves and is strong. Hydropower plants have produced clean electricity for many years now. Recycling means turning old things into new materials and saving energy. Glass, paper, and metal can be recycled many times without losing quality. For example, recycling one ton of paper can save about seventeen trees. Renewable sources make less air and water pollution than fossil fuels. If we act now, our air and water can become much healthier. Let's start at home: turn off lights, reduce waste, and use separate bins.
5::renewableenergyandrecycling.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::renewableenergyandrecycling.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name three types of renewable energy and explain where each comes from. 2. What can a roof with ten solar panels power? 3. What can recycling one tonne of paper save?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which renewable energy source mentioned in the passage do you think is most useful for your country or region? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author use numbers and statistics to make the argument for renewable energy more convincing? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences arguing for or against building a wind farm near your town, using evidence from the passage.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name the three renewable energy sources and the natural force each one uses. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words why solar, wind, and water energy are called 'renewable'. 3. (Analyse) The passage uses the statistic 'recycling one tonne of paper saves seventeen trees'. Why are specific numbers more persuasive than general statements? 4. (Evaluate) Are all three renewable energy sources equally useful in all countries? What factors would affect which source is most suitable? 5. (Create) Write a short persuasive paragraph for a school newsletter encouraging students to reduce paper waste, using facts from the passage.
5::renewableenergyandrecycling.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name the three types of renewable energy described in the passage. (Solar, wind, and hydropower/water.)
2. (On) How many homes can one large wind turbine power on a windy day? (Hundreds of homes.)
3. (On) How many trees can recycling one tonne of paper save? (About seventeen trees.)
4. (On) What is hydropower and how does it work? (It uses moving water — rivers or tides — to turn turbines and make electricity.)
5. (Above) Why do you think renewable energy is important for the future even though it can be expensive to set up? (Because it does not run out and does not release harmful gases, so it protects the environment long-term.)
5::renewableenergyandrecycling.go_description: 按能源类型分支呈现三种可再生能源及废物回收的层级图形组织器
5::renewableenergyandrecycling.go_usage: 四支：Solar/Wind/Water/Recycling，每支填 source + key fact（如 one turbine can power…）

5::spaceadventure.title: Space Adventure
5::spaceadventure.example_sentence: Tommy could open the toolbox, but needed a special key first.
5::spaceadventure.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: could (ability) + but + needed (prerequisite condition).
She could enter the competition, but needed permission first. / He could fix the bike, but needed the right tools first. / They could start, but needed more information first.

core_vocab.example: Tommy sits in the small pilot seat and checks the bright control panel.
Tommy opens the airlock and steps out onto warm, soft ground carefully.
Suddenly the hissing stops and the alarm becomes quieter than a small mouse.
An alarm light flashes and the engine begins to hiss loudly.
The rocket travels at about 28,000 kilometres per hour to reach orbit.

5::spaceadventure.core_vocab_raw: control, airlock, alarm, hiss, lever, orbit
5::spaceadventure.text_7page:
Page 1: Tommy sat in the small pilot seat and checked the bright control panel. He imagined he was flying toward a strange blue and green planet far away.
Page 2: Outside, the stars shone like silver coins and the ship moved steadily forward. The landing felt smooth because the ground looked softer than usual rock.
Page 3: An alarm light flashed and the engine began to hiss loudly. Tommy opened the toolbox, but he needed a special key to fix the problem.
Page 4: He worked quickly and tried to stop the hissing before the ship lost more power. He remembered a diagram he had once seen about emergency levers in cockpits.
Page 5: He pressed the blue button and gently turned the old lever to the left. The hissing stopped and the alarm grew quieter than a small mouse.
Page 6: Tommy opened the airlock and stepped out onto warm, soft ground carefully. Small purple flowers grew in circles and glowed with a soft light.
Page 7: A tiny robot creature greeted him and played a cheerful little song. Tommy laughed, wrote in his log, and thought it was the best adventure he had ever had.
5::spaceadventure.pure_text: Tommy sat in the small pilot seat and checked the bright control panel. He imagined he was flying toward a strange blue and green planet far away. Outside, the stars shone like silver coins and the ship moved steadily forward. The landing felt smooth because the ground looked softer than usual rock. An alarm light flashed and the engine began to hiss loudly. Tommy opened the toolbox, but he needed a special key to fix the problem. He worked quickly and tried to stop the hissing before the ship lost more power. He remembered a diagram he had once seen about emergency levers in cockpits. He pressed the blue button and gently turned the old lever to the left. The hissing stopped and the alarm grew quieter than a small mouse. Tommy opened the airlock and stepped out onto warm, soft ground carefully. Small purple flowers grew in circles and glowed with a soft light. A tiny robot creature greeted him and played a cheerful little song. Tommy laughed, wrote in his log, and thought it was the best adventure he had ever had.
5::spaceadventure.reading_strategy: 进行预测 (Making and Confirming Predictions)
5::spaceadventure.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What problem did Tommy face on the alien planet and what did he use to solve it? 2. What did Tommy find outside the spacecraft after fixing the alarm? 3. How did the story end and how did Tommy feel?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the image of 'small purple flowers that glowed with soft light' suggest about the kind of planet Tommy found? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever had to solve a problem on your own without any help? What did you do? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) How does the author build tension in this story? Find two examples of language that creates suspense. 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What was the alarm problem and how did Tommy solve it? 2. (Understand) Describe what Tommy saw when he stepped outside the spacecraft. 3. (Analyse) Why do you think Tommy wrote in his log before anything else? 4. (Evaluate) The story ends with Tommy calling this 'the best adventure he had ever had'. What made it so special? 5. (Create) Write the next paragraph of this story: what does Tommy discover when the tiny robot creature begins to communicate?
5::spaceadventure.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What problem started flashing in Tommy's spaceship? (An alarm light — the engine began to hiss.)
2. (On) What did Tommy find when he opened the toolbox? (He needed a special key to fix the problem.)
3. (On) What did Tommy do to stop the hissing alarm? (He pressed the blue button and turned the old lever to the left.)
4. (On) What did Tommy see when he stepped outside the spacecraft? (Small purple flowers that grew in circles and glowed with a soft light.)
5. (Above) Why do you think Tommy called this 'the best adventure he had ever had' even though there was a frightening emergency? (Because solving the problem himself made him feel capable and brave, and the alien planet turned out to be beautiful.)
5::spaceadventure.go_description: 呈现故事角色与主要情节发展的图形组织器
5::spaceadventure.go_usage: 记录主要角色、遇到的问题和解决方法

5::thesolarsystemandplanets.title: The Solar System and Planets
5::thesolarsystemandplanets.example_sentence: There are eight planets that move around the Sun in order.
5::thesolarsystemandplanets.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: there are + number + noun + defining relative clause with 'that'.
There are five food groups that give our bodies different nutrients. / There are four seasons that bring different weather each year. / There are six continents that are home to billions of people.

core_vocab.example: Saturn has wide rings made of ice and small rock pieces.
Venus is very hot and covered by thick clouds.
Some are stars far away; others are objects in our solar system.
Mercury is small and closest to the Sun in its orbit.
Astronauts must exercise every day to keep their bones healthy in low gravity.

5::thesolarsystemandplanets.core_vocab_raw: ring, thick, object, orbit, turn on its side, gravity
5::thesolarsystemandplanets.text_7page:
Page 1: Look up at the sky and you see many bright points of light. Some are stars far away; others are objects in our solar system.
Page 2: The Sun is a hot star at the center of our solar system. There are eight planets that move around the Sun in order.
Page 3: Mercury is small and closest to the Sun in its orbit. Venus is very hot and covered by thick clouds.
Page 4: Earth has water, air, and many living things we know. Mars is smaller than Earth and is often called the red planet.
Page 5: Jupiter is the largest planet and very big with many moons. Saturn has wide rings made of ice and small rock pieces.
Page 6: Uranus turns on its side and looks blue-green from space. Neptune is a cold, windy world far from the Sun.
Page 7: A fun fact: Jupiter is so large that about 1,300 Earths could fit inside it. The solar system is full of amazing planets, and each one is different in its own way.
5::thesolarsystemandplanets.pure_text: Look up at the sky and you see many bright points of light. Some are stars far away; others are objects in our solar system. The Sun is a hot star at the center of our solar system. There are eight planets that move around the Sun in order. Mercury is small and closest to the Sun in its orbit. Venus is very hot and covered by thick clouds. Earth has water, air, and many living things we know. Mars is smaller than Earth and is often called the red planet. Jupiter is the largest planet and very big with many moons. Saturn has wide rings made of ice and small rock pieces. Uranus turns on its side and looks blue-green from space. Neptune is a cold, windy world far from the Sun. A fun fact: Jupiter is so large that about 1,300 Earths could fit inside it. The solar system is full of amazing planets, and each one is different in its own way.
5::thesolarsystemandplanets.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::thesolarsystemandplanets.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name all eight planets in order from the Sun and give one fact about each. 2. What makes Jupiter the largest planet in our solar system? 3. Why is Earth the only planet known to support life?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which planet from this passage would you most like to visit and why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author organise the information about the planets? Why is this order logical? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences describing one planet not yet visited by spacecraft, explaining what we know and what we do not yet know.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name the eight planets in order from the Sun. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words what makes Saturn distinctive among the planets. 3. (Analyse) The passage says Jupiter 'could fit 1,300 Earths inside'. What does this comparison help us understand? 4. (Evaluate) Why do scientists continue to study planets that humans cannot yet visit? Use ideas from the text. 5. (Create) Write two sentences about a ninth object in our solar system (like a dwarf planet) that could be added to this passage.
5::thesolarsystemandplanets.rr_questions:
1. (Under) How many planets are in our solar system? (Eight.)
2. (On) Which planet is the largest in our solar system? (Jupiter.)
3. (On) What are Saturn's rings made of? (Ice and rock.)
4. (On) Why is Earth the only planet known to support life? (Because it has liquid water, a breathable atmosphere, and the right temperature.)
5. (Above) Why do you think scientists continue to study planets that humans cannot yet visit? (Because understanding other planets teaches us more about how our own solar system formed and whether life could exist elsewhere.)
5::thesolarsystemandplanets.go_description: 以太阳为中心、按距离顺序呈现八大行星特点的放射状图形组织器
5::thesolarsystemandplanets.go_usage: 中心写 Sun，八支按距离顺序：Mercury/Venus/Earth/Mars/Jupiter/Saturn/Uranus/Neptune，每支填一个特点

5::zoom.title: Zoom!
5::zoom.sentence_pattern: [Subject] [past verb phrase], and everyone [past verb phrase].
5::zoom.example_sentence: Mia pressed the button, and everyone clapped for her bravery.
5::zoom.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: two coordinated past simple clauses — action + group reaction.
He scored the goal, and everyone jumped up. / She finished her speech, and everyone smiled. / He turned the key, and everyone felt relieved.

core_vocab.example: Mr Lee explained how astronauts live and work in space.
Then they entered a short zero gravity simulator to float and learn balance.
Mia pressed the button, the beeping stopped, and everyone clapped for her bravery.
Then they entered a short zero gravity simulator to float and learn balance.
Mr Lee explained how each student would complete a different training mission.

5::zoom.core_vocab_raw: astronaut, balance, bravery, simulator, instruction, mission
5::zoom.text_7page:
Page 1: The city space centre held a weekend training for children. Ten students met in a bright modern room for instructions.
Page 2: Everyone was excited and a little nervous about the day ahead. Mr Lee explained how astronauts live and work in space.
Page 3: First they put on light training suits and learned to move slowly. Then they entered a short zero gravity simulator to float and learn balance.
Page 4: Mia floated and watched her hair and a sandwich rise gently. Suddenly the simulator alarm began to beep loudly during Mia's float session.
Page 5: She felt scared because she could not find the stop button near her. Mia remembered the breathing exercises and the emergency panel lesson from earlier.
Page 6: She looked under the seat and saw a small red button. Mia pressed the button, the beeping stopped, and everyone clapped for her bravery.
Page 7: Mr Lee said she had acted calmly and quickly like an astronaut. Mia felt proud and decided to keep training for her dream.
5::zoom.pure_text: The city space centre held a weekend training for children. Ten students met in a bright modern room for instructions. Everyone was excited and a little nervous about the day ahead. Mr Lee explained how astronauts live and work in space. First they put on light training suits and learned to move slowly. Then they entered a short zero gravity simulator to float and learn balance. Mia floated and watched her hair and a sandwich rise gently. Suddenly the simulator alarm began to beep loudly during Mia's float session. She felt scared because she could not find the stop button near her. Mia remembered the breathing exercises and the emergency panel lesson from earlier. She looked under the seat and saw a small red button. Mia pressed the button, the beeping stopped, and everyone clapped for her bravery. Mr Lee said she had acted calmly and quickly like an astronaut. Mia felt proud and decided to keep training for her dream.
5::zoom.reading_skill: 推理与结论 (Making Inferences and Drawing Conclusions)
5::zoom.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What emergency happened during the training session and how did Mia respond? 2. What did Mia remember from earlier in the training that helped her act? 3. What was the reaction of Mr Lee and the other students after Mia fixed the problem?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the phrase 'calm and quick like a real astronaut' tell us about the qualities astronaut training develops? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever had to stay calm in an emergency or stressful situation? How did you manage? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why do you think Mia 'kept breathing slowly and deeply' before acting? What does this technique show? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What went wrong during the training and what did Mia do? 2. (Understand) Describe how Mia's training helped her handle the emergency. 3. (Analyse) How did Mr Lee's words at the end of the story connect to the training's purpose? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think the emergency made the training more valuable? Why or why not? 5. (Create) Write Mr Lee's training report about Mia's performance, describing what she did and what it shows about her readiness.
5::zoom.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What emergency happened during the space training session? (The alarm began beeping and a red light flashed on the emergency panel.)
2. (On) What did Mia do when the alarm started? (She breathed slowly and checked the panel carefully before pressing the correct button.)
3. (On) What training technique did Mia use that helped her stay calm? (She used the breathing exercises she had learned earlier in the session.)
4. (On) What did Mr Lee say about Mia's performance? (He said she acted calmly and quickly like a real astronaut.)
5. (Above) Why do you think staying calm is so important during an emergency, even more than being fast? (Because panicking leads to mistakes, while being calm lets you think clearly and choose the right action.)
5::zoom.go_description: 呈现故事角色与主要情节发展的图形组织器
5::zoom.go_usage: 记录主要角色、遇到的问题和解决方法

5::astronautsandspacetravel.title: Astronauts and Space Travel
5::astronautsandspacetravel.example_sentence: Astronauts learn to fly the spacecraft, live in small spaces, and solve problems quickly.
5::astronautsandspacetravel.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: learn + to-infinitive. The activities after 'to' form a parallel list.
Students learn to read carefully, write clearly, and speak confidently. / Children learn to share, take turns, and listen to each other. / Athletes learn to train daily, eat well, and rest properly.

core_vocab.example: They practice emergencies in flight simulators and in big water pools.
In orbit astronauts do many experiments and take pictures of Earth every day.
When the mission ends, the capsule re-enters the atmosphere and uses parachutes to land.
On launch day the rocket makes a loud noise and lots of smoke.
Training in water helps astronauts feel weightless, like floating in space.

5::astronautsandspacetravel.core_vocab_raw: emergency, experiment, parachute, rocket, spacecraft, weightless
5::astronautsandspacetravel.text_7page:
Page 1: Astronauts train for many years before they go into space. They learn to fly the spacecraft, live in small rooms, and solve problems quickly.
Page 2: They practise emergencies in flight simulators and in large underwater pools. Training in water helps them feel weightless, like floating slowly in space.
Page 3: On launch day the rocket makes a loud noise and produces large clouds of steam. The rocket travels at about 28,000 kilometres per hour to reach Earth's orbit.
Page 4: In orbit, astronauts carry out many experiments and take photographs of Earth every day. They study plants, tiny organisms, and materials that may help people on Earth.
Page 5: Outside the spacecraft they wear big pressurised suits and special gloves to work safely. A single spacewalk can last more than nine hours.
Page 6: Astronauts must exercise every day to keep their muscles and bones healthy in low gravity. Food comes in special pouches or tubes designed for eating in space.
Page 7: When the mission ends, the capsule re-enters the atmosphere and uses parachutes to land. Future astronauts may travel farther into space and explore new worlds beyond Earth.
5::astronautsandspacetravel.pure_text: Astronauts train for many years before they go into space. They learn to fly the spacecraft, live in small rooms, and solve problems quickly. They practise emergencies in flight simulators and in large underwater pools. Training in water helps them feel weightless, like floating slowly in space. On launch day the rocket makes a loud noise and produces large clouds of steam. The rocket travels at about 28,000 kilometres per hour to reach Earth's orbit. In orbit, astronauts carry out many experiments and take photographs of Earth every day. They study plants, tiny organisms, and materials that may help people on Earth. Outside the spacecraft they wear big pressurised suits and special gloves to work safely. A single spacewalk can last more than nine hours. Astronauts must exercise every day to keep their muscles and bones healthy in low gravity. Food comes in special pouches or tubes designed for eating in space. When the mission ends, the capsule re-enters the atmosphere and uses parachutes to land. Future astronauts may travel farther into space and explore new worlds beyond Earth.
5::astronautsandspacetravel.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::astronautsandspacetravel.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Describe two types of training astronauts do before going to space and explain why each is necessary. 2. Why do astronauts need to exercise every day in space? 3. What happens when the mission ends and how do astronauts return to Earth?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which aspect of astronaut training do you find most surprising? Why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the passage move from training to launch to orbit to return? What structure is this? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Is space travel worth the enormous cost and risk? Write two sentences arguing one side using facts from the passage.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name two types of astronaut training and explain what each prepares them for. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words why low gravity means astronauts must exercise every day. 3. (Analyse) The passage describes the full journey of a mission. What does this chronological structure show about the complexity of space travel? 4. (Evaluate) What do you think is the greatest challenge of being an astronaut, based on this passage? 5. (Create) Design a one-day training schedule for a new astronaut, using activities mentioned in the passage.
5::astronautsandspacetravel.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name two things astronauts practise in training before going to space. (Flying the spacecraft, emergencies in simulators, training underwater, living in small rooms — any two.)
2. (On) Why do astronauts train underwater? (Because water makes them feel weightless, similar to floating in space.)
3. (On) Why must astronauts exercise every day in space? (To keep their muscles and bones healthy in low gravity.)
4. (On) How does the capsule land when the mission ends? (It re-enters the atmosphere and uses parachutes to land.)
5. (Above) Why do you think astronaut training takes many years even for very intelligent people? (Because space is extremely dangerous and unpredictable, so astronauts must practise every possible situation until their responses are automatic.)
5::astronautsandspacetravel.go_description: 以链条呈现宇航员从训练到返回全过程的线性图形组织器
5::astronautsandspacetravel.go_usage: 链条节点：Training → Launch (28,000 km/h) → Orbit (experiments + spacewalk) → Exercise daily → Re-entry → Land

5::atripintohistory.title: A Trip Into History
5::atripintohistory.example_sentence: The first photo showed an old classroom with wooden desks and a chalkboard.
5::atripintohistory.teaching_focus: Practice past simple 'showed' + ordinal words (first, next, third, second), making short descriptions of pictures, swapping the description slot for speaking and writing, and asking/answering questions (What did the first/next photo show?).

core_vocab.example: Tommy was surprised by the scene, "That was odd. Horse carriages still shared the street with early cars."
The next photo showed a busy street from long ago.
label said, “Do not touch.”
around carefully, but Mia was too curious to wait.
As Tommy turned the camera over, a folded note fell onto the floor.

5::atripintohistory.core_vocab_raw: carriage, long ago, label, curious, a folded note
5::atripintohistory.text_7page:
Page 1: Mia and Tommy found an old camera during the school’s History Fair. The camera was dusty and small, but it felt special to them. A yellow label said, “Do not touch.”
Tommy looked around carefully, but Mia was too curious to wait.
Page 2: She pressed a button and the camera made a soft clicking sound. The first photo showed an old classroom with wooden desks and a chalkboard. A teacher wore a tall black hat and wrote slowly on the board.
Page 3: Mia laughed, "This man's hat looks very funny." The next photo showed a busy street from long ago.
Page 4: Tommy was surprised by the scene, "That was odd. Horse carriages still shared the street with early cars."
Page 5: The third photo showed a family cooking dinner in a small kitchen. A child helped a grandparent carry food to the table.
Page 6: Everyone looked warm and kind. As Tommy turned the camera over, a folded note fell onto the floor.
Page 7: It said, “For curious kids: Keep learning, help others, and love your family.”

Mia and Tommy smiled. They would like to share the photos and the note with their classmates.
5::atripintohistory.pure_text:
Mia and Tommy found an old camera during the school’s History Fair. The camera was dusty and small, but it felt special to them. A yellow label said, “Do not touch.”
Tommy looked around carefully, but Mia was too curious to wait. She pressed a button and the camera made a soft clicking sound. The first photo showed an old classroom with wooden desks and a chalkboard. A teacher wore a tall black hat and wrote slowly on the board. Mia laughed, "This man's hat looks very funny." The next photo showed a busy street from long ago. Tommy was surprised by the scene, "That was odd. Horse carriages still shared the street with early cars." The third photo showed a family cooking dinner in a small kitchen. A child helped a grandparent carry food to the table. Everyone looked warm and kind. As Tommy turned the camera over, a folded note fell onto the floor. It said, “For curious kids: Keep learning, help others, and love your family.”

Mia and Tommy smiled. They would like to share the photos and the note with their classmates.
5::atripintohistory.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What did Mia and Tommy find at the History Fair and why did it feel special to them? 2. What happened when Mia pressed the button on the camera? 3. What did the photos show them about life in the past?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the label 'Do not touch' suggest about the value of old objects? Why did Mia press the button anyway? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever found an old object that told you something about the past? What was it? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why do you think looking at old photographs can make history feel more real and personal? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What did Mia and Tommy discover and what happened when they touched it? 2. (Understand) Describe one thing the photos showed about life in the past. 3. (Analyse) What did Mia and Tommy learn from this unexpected experience? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think Mia was right to press the button despite the warning? What does this tell us about her character? 5. (Create) Write the caption Mia might write for one of the photos she saw, describing what it shows about the past.
5::atripintohistory.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Where did Mia and Tommy find the old camera? (At the school's History Fair.)
2. (On) What did the label on the camera say? ("Do not touch.")
3. (On) What did the first photo show? (An old classroom with wooden desks and a chalkboard.)
4. (On) How did Mia and Tommy feel when they looked through the photos? (Amazed and curious about life in the past.)
5. (Above) Why do you think old photographs can help us understand history better than just reading about it? (Because they show real people and real places, which makes the past feel closer and more real.)
5::atripintohistory.go_description: 以故事要素呈现人物、问题和结局的图形组织器

5::americanfolkheroes.title: American Folk Heroes
5::americanfolkheroes.example_sentence: He is an enormous lumberjack who appears in "tall tales."
5::americanfolkheroes.teaching_focus: Practice building descriptions with 'is/was + noun + who + verb' to add facts about people or characters; focus on correct verb form (he/she/they), substitution for speaking, and short retellings that use the pattern.

core_vocab.example: he planted thousands of trees, and his stories feel peaceful and close to nature.
Along the way, he shared food and shelter with strangers.
Tall tales are stories that use wild exaggeration to entertain listeners.
He is an enormous lumberjack who appears in "tall tales."
He traveled through many states planting apple seeds for new settlers.

5::americanfolkheroes.text_7page:
Page 1: A folk tale is a story that people tell again and again across generations. These stories often teach important values and explain the history of a place or a way of life.
Page 2: Johnny Appleseed was based on a real man named John Chapman, who lived in early America. He traveled through many states planting apple seeds for new settlers.
Page 3: Along the way, he shared food and shelter with strangers. He also showed great kindness to animals.
Page 4: People say he planted thousands of trees, and his stories feel peaceful and close to nature. Paul Bunyan is a very different kind of folk hero.
Page 5: He is an enormous lumberjack who appears in "tall tales." Tall tales are stories that use wild exaggeration to entertain listeners.
Page 6: In one famous story, his giant blue ox, Babe, created the Great Lakes. These tales are full of big numbers, impossible events, and loud humor.
Page 7: Although the two heroes are very different, both types of stories help us remember their history and shared values. Johnny's tales celebrate kindness and simplicity, while Paul Bunyan's tales celebrate strength and imagination.
5::americanfolkheroes.pure_text: A folk tale is a story that people tell again and again across generations. These stories often teach important values and explain the history of a place or a way of life. Johnny Appleseed was based on a real man named John Chapman, who lived in early America. He traveled through many states planting apple seeds for new settlers. Along the way, he shared food and shelter with strangers. He also showed great kindness to animals. People say he planted thousands of trees, and his stories feel peaceful and close to nature. Paul Bunyan is a very different kind of folk hero. He is an enormous lumberjack who appears in "tall tales." Tall tales are stories that use wild exaggeration to entertain listeners. In one famous story, his giant blue ox, Babe, created the Great Lakes. These tales are full of big numbers, impossible events, and loud humor. Although the two heroes are very different, both types of stories help us remember their history and shared values. Johnny's tales celebrate kindness and simplicity, while Paul Bunyan's tales celebrate strength and imagination.
5::americanfolkheroes.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::americanfolkheroes.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Who was Johnny Appleseed and what did he do across America? 2. What is a 'tall tale' and how is it different from a true historical story? 3. Name one other folk hero mentioned in the passage and describe their exaggerated feature.
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. What surprised you most about how folk tales mix real people with exaggeration? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author explain what a folk tale is before introducing the heroes? Is this an effective structure? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Choose one folk hero from the passage. Write two sentences explaining what real-life quality the exaggeration might represent.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Who was Johnny Appleseed based on and what did the real person do? 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words what makes a 'tall tale' different from a biography. 3. (Analyse) Why do you think communities create exaggerated stories about real people? 4. (Evaluate) What values does the passage suggest American folk heroes represent? 5. (Create) Write a two-sentence 'tall tale' about a person you admire, exaggerating one of their real qualities.
5::americanfolkheroes.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Who was Johnny Appleseed based on? (A real man named John Chapman who lived in early America.)
2. (On) What is a 'tall tale'? (A story about a real or made-up person with exaggerated features or abilities.)
3. (On) What was Paul Bunyan famous for in folk tales? (Being an enormous lumberjack with extraordinary strength.)
4. (On) Why did communities create these exaggerated stories about real people? (To teach values and celebrate the spirit of hard work and adventure.)
5. (Above) What do you think these folk heroes say about what qualities Americans admired in the past? (Strength, hard work, generosity, and a connection to nature and the land.)

5::adayatthehistorymuseum.title: A Day at the History Museum
5::adayatthehistorymuseum.sentence_pattern: I hope they remember [something] that [past simple verb phrase]
5::adayatthehistorymuseum.example_sentence: "I hope they remember something I made that helped someone smile."
5::adayatthehistorymuseum.teaching_focus: Practice saying hopes about the future using 'I hope they remember...' + a noun phrase + a simple relative clause with past simple. Focus on forming the relative clause with 'that' + past verb (made, showed, helped), swapping nouns (toy, map, song), and short speaking drills to retell the story or describe class display items.

core_vocab.example: a tiny model for her board.
Mia picks an inventor who built clever little machines in his garage.
Mia finds an old sketch of her inventor testing a teapot with a spoon-shaped propeller.
Mia finds an old sketch of her inventor testing a teapot with a spoon-shaped propeller.
"He probably failed a hundred times before anything worked," she tells Tommy, grinning.

5::adayatthehistorymuseum.text_7page:
Page 1: Next Friday, the school is holding a "History Figures Museum." Every class must choose one historical person and build a display board. Mia picks an inventor who built clever little machines in his garage.
Page 2: She loves imagining him as a messy-haired kid who broke toys just to see what was inside. "He probably failed a hundred times before anything worked," she tells Tommy, grinning.
Page 3: Tommy chooses an explorer who traveled farther than almost anyone from his town. He pictures him studying long maps by candlelight and tasting strange foods. While researching, they discover the explorer once carried a small wooden parrot as a lucky charm.
Page 4: Tommy immediately adds it to his display. Mia finds an old sketch of her inventor testing a teapot with a spoon-shaped propeller. She builds a tiny model for her board.
Page 5: On the day of the museum, the classroom transforms into a world of stories and voices. The teacher asks quietly, "If people in the future remember you, what will they remember?" Tommy thinks for a moment.
Page 6: "My courage," he says. "And my mistakes."
Page 7: Mia smiles. "I hope they remember something I made that helped someone smile."
5::adayatthehistorymuseum.pure_text: Next Friday, the school is holding a "History Figures Museum." Every class must choose one historical person and build a display board. Mia picks an inventor who built clever little machines in his garage. She loves imagining him as a messy-haired kid who broke toys just to see what was inside. "He probably failed a hundred times before anything worked," she tells Tommy, grinning. Tommy chooses an explorer who traveled farther than almost anyone from his town. He pictures him studying long maps by candlelight and tasting strange foods. While researching, they discover the explorer once carried a small wooden parrot as a lucky charm. Tommy immediately adds it to his display. Mia finds an old sketch of her inventor testing a teapot with a spoon-shaped propeller. She builds a tiny model for her board. On the day of the museum, the classroom transforms into a world of stories and voices. The teacher asks quietly, "If people in the future remember you, what will they remember?" Tommy thinks for a moment. "My courage," he says. "And my mistakes." Mia smiles. "I hope they remember something I made that helped someone smile."
5::adayatthehistorymuseum.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Who does Mia choose for her History Museum display and why? 2. What did the inventor Mia chose build and what was special about it? 3. What question does Mia ask herself as she builds the display board?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the question 'I hope they remember something I made that helped someone smile' tell us about what Mia thinks is important about being remembered? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. If you were building a display about a historical person, who would you choose? Why? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why do you think it matters to be remembered for helping others rather than just for being famous? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Who did Mia choose for her display and what was the inventor known for? 2. (Understand) Describe the display Mia built and explain why she chose to include certain details. 3. (Analyse) What question did Mia keep asking herself and what does it reveal about her values? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think the inventor Mia chose deserved to be remembered? What evidence from the text supports your view? 5. (Create) Write the three most important facts about Mia's chosen inventor that she might include on her display board.
5::adayatthehistorymuseum.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What did Mia's class have to do for the History Figures Museum? (Choose one historical person and build a display board.)
2. (On) Who did Mia choose and what was that person known for? (An inventor who built clever little machines in his garage.)
3. (On) What question did Mia keep asking herself while building the display? ("I hope they remember something I made that helped someone smile.")
4. (On) What did Mia include on her display board? (A model, a propeller, a teapot, and facts about the inventor.)
5. (Above) Why do you think Mia cared more about being remembered for helping others than for being famous? (Because she felt that the most meaningful legacy is one that made other people's lives better.)

5::thedreamofmartinlutherkingjr.title: The Dream of Martin Luther King Jr.
5::thedreamofmartinlutherkingjr.example_sentence: Martin Luther King Jr. was a leader who wanted fairness for everyone.
5::thedreamofmartinlutherkingjr.teaching_focus: Practice making relative clauses with who to describe people (role + who + past verb), use past tense verbs after who, swap subject and role to make new sentences, and use these sentences for short spoken retells about people and their actions.

core_vocab.example: Martin Luther King Jr. was a leader who wanted fairness for everyone.
He wanted equal rights for people of all races and backgrounds.
He used clear images and hopeful words that many children can imagine.
In 1963 he gave his famous speech, 'I Have a Dream,' in Washington.
He said, 'I have a dream that my children will be judged by character.'

5::thedreamofmartinlutherkingjr.core_vocab_raw: leader, equal rights, image, speech, be judged by
5::thedreamofmartinlutherkingjr.text_7page:
Page 1: Martin Luther King Jr. was a leader who wanted fairness for everyone. He taught people to use peaceful actions and words, not violence.
Page 2: In 1963 he gave his famous speech, 'I Have a Dream,' in Washington. More than two hundred thousand people stood on the National Mall listening.
Page 3: He used clear images and hopeful words that many children can imagine. He said, 'I have a dream that my children will be judged by character.' This meant he wanted people to be treated fairly, not by skin color.
Page 4: He wanted equal rights for people of all races and backgrounds. Many people joined sit-ins, sitting and refusing to move, also marches and boycotts.
Page 5: King often said love and courage could change people's hearts and rules. His speech on the steps lasted about seventeen minutes that summer day.
Page 6: Today many schools and streets are named after him as a reminder. His dream still helps young people learn to respect and help others.
Page 7: We can act by treating classmates fairly and using words to solve problems. One peaceful dream and brave actions can change a whole country.
5::thedreamofmartinlutherkingjr.pure_text: Martin Luther King Jr. was a leader who wanted fairness for everyone. He taught people to use peaceful actions and words, not violence. In 1963 he gave his famous speech, 'I Have a Dream,' in Washington. More than two hundred thousand people stood on the National Mall listening. He used clear images and hopeful words that many children can imagine. He said, 'I have a dream that my children will be judged by character.' This meant he wanted people to be treated fairly, not by skin color. He wanted equal rights for people of all races and backgrounds. Many people joined sit-ins, sitting and refusing to move, also marches and boycotts. King often said love and courage could change people's hearts and rules. His speech on the steps lasted about seventeen minutes that summer day. Today many schools and streets are named after him as a reminder. His dream still helps young people learn to respect and help others. We can act by treating classmates fairly and using words to solve problems. One peaceful dream and brave actions can change a whole country.
5::thedreamofmartinlutherkingjr.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::thedreamofmartinlutherkingjr.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What did Martin Luther King Jr. want for people and how did he work towards it? 2. What did his famous 1963 speech express and where was it delivered? 3. What does the passage say his legacy is today?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. What aspect of Martin Luther King Jr.'s work do you find most admirable? Why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the passage present both King's beliefs and his actions? Does this make him more or less persuasive as a historical figure? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences explaining why the message of the 'I Have a Dream' speech is still relevant today.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Who was Martin Luther King Jr. and what was his main goal? 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words what 'equal rights' means and why King believed peaceful protest was the best way to achieve them. 3. (Analyse) Why does the passage say King's words 'still inspire people today'? What kind of language suggests this? 4. (Evaluate) Do you agree that peaceful action is the most effective way to create change? Use ideas from the text. 5. (Create) Write two sentences about another historical figure who worked for fairness or equal rights, comparing their approach to King's.
5::thedreamofmartinlutherkingjr.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What was Martin Luther King Jr.'s main message? (He wanted fairness and equal rights for everyone.)
2. (On) Where did King give his famous 'I Have a Dream' speech and in what year? (In Washington in 1963.)
3. (On) How many people were listening to his speech on the National Mall? (More than two hundred thousand.)
4. (On) What method did King teach people to use instead of violence? (Peaceful actions and words.)
5. (Above) Why do you think King's words still inspire people today, more than sixty years later? (Because the idea of fairness and peaceful change is something people still need and believe in around the world.)

5::aproudmoment.title: A Proud Moment
5::aproudmoment.example_sentence: He felt embarrassed, but he kept going and read every line carefully.
5::aproudmoment.teaching_focus: Practice using felt + adjective to say how someone feels, then use but to show contrast and kept + -ing to say they continued. Activities: substitution drills (change subject/emotion/verb), retell the scene with this pattern, pair role-play (one whispers encouragement, the other uses the pattern), and pronunciation focus on the -ed / -ing endings and linking words (felt… but… kept…).

core_vocab.example: He learned that courage and practice help him try again with more confidence.
embarrassed, but he kept going and read every line carefully.
He practised at home every day, but his hands often shook with worry.
Mia sat next to him and whispered, "You can do it."
By the end of the paragraph he was smiling and breathing calmly.

5::aproudmoment.text_7page:
Page 1: Tommy had always been nervous about reading aloud in front of his class. He practised at home every day, but his hands often shook with worry. One Tuesday morning the teacher planned a group reading activity for everyone.
Page 2: Tommy wanted to try, but he felt small and afraid inside. Mia sat next to him and whispered, "You can do it."
Page 3: When it was his turn, he took a deep breath and began to read aloud. At first his voice was quiet and he read very slowly.
Page 4: Then a pencil dropped and he flushed, but he did not stop reading. He felt embarrassed, but he kept going and read every line carefully.
Page 5: The more he read, the stronger and clearer his voice became. By the end of the paragraph he was smiling and breathing calmly.
Page 6: The class clapped, and the teacher came close and said, "I'm proud of you for trying." Mia smiled and said, "I'm proud of you, Tommy."
Page 7: Tommy felt a warm, quiet happiness and knew his effort mattered most. He learned that courage and practice help him try again with more confidence.
5::aproudmoment.pure_text: Tommy had always been nervous about reading aloud in front of his class. He practised at home every day, but his hands often shook with worry. One Tuesday morning the teacher planned a group reading activity for everyone. Tommy wanted to try, but he felt small and afraid inside. Mia sat next to him and whispered, "You can do it." When it was his turn, he took a deep breath and began to read aloud. At first his voice was quiet and he read very slowly. Then a pencil dropped and he flushed, but he did not stop reading. He felt embarrassed, but he kept going and read every line carefully. The more he read, the stronger and clearer his voice became. By the end of the paragraph he was smiling and breathing calmly. The class clapped, and the teacher came close and said, "I'm proud of you for trying." Mia smiled and said, "I'm proud of you, Tommy." Tommy felt a warm, quiet happiness and knew his effort mattered most. He learned that courage and practice help him try again with more confidence.
5::aproudmoment.reading_strategy: 进行预测 (Making and Confirming Predictions)
5::aproudmoment.reading_skill: 问题与解决方案 (Problem and Solution)
5::aproudmoment.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Why had Tommy always been nervous about reading aloud? 2. What happened on the day Tommy had to read aloud in class? 3. How did the class and the teacher react when Tommy finished?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the detail that Tommy 'kept going' despite feeling embarrassed tell us about his character? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever had to do something that made you nervous in front of others? How did you feel before, during, and after? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why is 'feeling proud of yourself' sometimes more important than receiving applause from others? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What was Tommy's main fear and how long had he practised for? 2. (Understand) Describe the moment Tommy felt embarrassed and explain what he decided to do. 3. (Analyse) How did the class's reaction change from the beginning of Tommy's reading to the end? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think Tommy would have grown as much if the reading had gone perfectly the first time? Why? 5. (Create) Write the short paragraph Tommy read aloud that won the applause, based on clues in the story.
5::aproudmoment.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What activity did the teacher plan for Tuesday morning? (A group reading activity for everyone.)
2. (On) How had Tommy been preparing for this moment? (He had practised reading aloud at home every day.)
3. (On) What happened to Tommy's hands when he was nervous? (They shook with worry.)
4. (On) How did the class react when Tommy finished reading? (They gave him a big applause and Tommy smiled with pride.)
5. (Above) Why do you think Tommy felt proud of himself even though he felt embarrassed at first? (Because he kept going despite being scared, and finishing something difficult is something to be proud of.)
5::aproudmoment.go_description: 呈现主角困难与克服过程的图形组织器
5::aproudmoment.go_usage: Problem: fear of reading aloud → Steps: practice → keep going → Result: applause

5::goodchoiceseveryday.title: Good Choices Every Day
5::goodchoiceseveryday.example_sentence: If you want to speak in class, raise your hand and wait quietly.
5::goodchoiceseveryday.teaching_focus: Practice forming 'If you want to + verb' and following it with an imperative. Focus on subject choice (you/someone), the infinitive after 'want to', and clear imperatives (raise, stop, wash). Use substitution drills (change verbs and places), pair role-plays (teacher/student giving rules), and short spoken chains linking two actions with 'and'.

core_vocab.example: library, speak softly and use low voices to avoid noise.
Small choices every day show strong character and help many people.
Help with small chores every day; it shows respect and care.
Before you cross a street, stop and look both ways twice.
If you want to speak in class, raise your hand and wait quietly.

5::goodchoiceseveryday.core_vocab_raw: noise, choice, chore, look both ways, raise your hand
5::goodchoiceseveryday.text_7page:
Page 1: Small choices every day show strong character and help many people. At school, follow simple rules in three places: library, corridors, classroom. In the library, speak softly and use low voices to avoid noise.
Page 2: Walk in the corridors; do not run, push, or play roughly. If you want to speak in class, raise your hand and wait quietly. At home, small caring choices make family life calm and happy.
Page 3: Before meals, wash your hands with soap for twenty seconds. Help with small chores every day; it shows respect and care.
Page 4: Use toys and books carefully so they last a long time. In the park, make choices that protect nature and other people.
Page 5: Before you cross a street, stop and look both ways twice. Stay on paths and keep off grass where signs say so.
Page 6: Do not drop litter; a plastic bottle can take hundreds of years. Do not feed wild animals; human food can make them sick.
Page 7: Good habits now grow into strong habits for life and the future. Choose kindness and care; your choices will help people and nature.
5::goodchoiceseveryday.pure_text: Small choices every day show strong character and help many people. At school, follow simple rules in three places: library, corridors, classroom. In the library, speak softly and use low voices to avoid noise. Walk in the corridors; do not run, push, or play roughly. If you want to speak in class, raise your hand and wait quietly. At home, small caring choices make family life calm and happy. Before meals, wash your hands with soap for twenty seconds. Help with small chores every day; it shows respect and care. Use toys and books carefully so they last a long time. In the park, make choices that protect nature and other people. Before you cross a street, stop and look both ways twice. Stay on paths and keep off grass where signs say so. Do not drop litter; a plastic bottle can take hundreds of years. Do not feed wild animals; human food can make them sick. Good habits now grow into strong habits for life and the future. Choose kindness and care; your choices will help people and nature.
5::goodchoiceseveryday.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::goodchoiceseveryday.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What three school locations are discussed and what rule applies in each? 2. What does the passage say about crossing the road safely? 3. What is one example of a good choice at home mentioned in the passage?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which rule in this passage do you think is hardest for most students to follow? Why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author organise the rules by location? Is this structure helpful for the reader? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences adding one more good choice for school or home that is not in the passage, and explain why it matters.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name the three school locations and one rule for each. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words why 'looking both ways' before crossing the road is described as a good choice. 3. (Analyse) The passage says 'small choices every day show strong character'. What does this suggest about the relationship between habits and character? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think rules need to be explained to be followed, or is a list enough? Use examples from the passage. 5. (Create) Write a brief code of behaviour for a new student joining your school, using three rules from the passage.
5::goodchoiceseveryday.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name the three school locations where rules apply in this passage. (Library, corridors, and classroom.)
2. (On) What rule applies in the library? (Speak softly and use low voices to avoid noise.)
3. (On) What should you do before crossing the road? (Look both ways and wait for a safe moment.)
4. (On) Name one good choice you can make at home according to the passage. (Do chores without being asked, or put belongings away.)
5. (Above) Why do you think making small good choices every day is described as showing 'strong character'? (Because character is built through habits, and consistently making the right choice — even when no one is watching — shows who you really are.)

5::themysteryofthefivesenses.title: The Mystery of the Five Senses
5::themysteryofthefivesenses.sentence_pattern: [Subject] [past verb phrase] and [past verb phrase] at the same time.
5::themysteryofthefivesenses.example_sentence: The whole class groaned and laughed at the same time.
5::themysteryofthefivesenses.teaching_focus: Practice describing simultaneous actions with 'and... at the same time.' Students can use it to retell story events, compare senses, and make short narratives. Focus on subject + verb substitution, word order, and natural pauses when speaking.

core_vocab.example: afternoon, Mia and Tommy joined their class for a Mystery Adventure.
On Friday afternoon, Mia and Tommy joined their class for a Mystery Adventure.
Mia lifted the first lid and breathed in a sharp, bright scent.
Tommy leaned over and immediately guessed, "Lemon!" He was right.
The whole class groaned and laughed at the same time.

5::themysteryofthefivesenses.core_vocab_raw: adventure, mystery, scent, lean over, groan
5::themysteryofthefivesenses.text_7page:
Page 1: On Friday afternoon, Mia and Tommy joined their class for a Mystery Adventure. The classroom was dark, and long tables held boxes covered with lids and cloth. Mr. Carter smiled and said, "Today, no looking.
Page 2: You must use your other senses." Mia lifted the first lid and breathed in a sharp, bright scent. Tommy leaned over and immediately guessed, "Lemon!" He was right.
Page 3: Next, they reached into a soft box and felt something fuzzy and round. "A plush toy?" said Mia. Correct again.
Page 4: Tommy shook a small box and heard a tiny bell ringing inside. "Something with a bell," he whispered, as if solving a mystery.
Page 5: Then Mr. Carter brought out small cups of something to taste. It was sweet, sour, and salty all at once — popcorn with sugar, sour cream, and salt.
Page 6: The whole class groaned and laughed at the same time. At the end, everyone removed their blindfolds and checked the labels together. Matching each sense to the right object felt strangely satisfying.
Page 7: "Your senses," said Mr. Carter, still smiling, "work better together than alone."
5::themysteryofthefivesenses.pure_text: On Friday afternoon, Mia and Tommy joined their class for a Mystery Adventure. The classroom was dark, and long tables held boxes covered with lids and cloth. Mr. Carter smiled and said, "Today, no looking. You must use your other senses." Mia lifted the first lid and breathed in a sharp, bright scent. Tommy leaned over and immediately guessed, "Lemon!" He was right. Next, they reached into a soft box and felt something fuzzy and round. "A plush toy?" said Mia. Correct again. Tommy shook a small box and heard a tiny bell ringing inside. "Something with a bell," he whispered, as if solving a mystery. Then Mr. Carter brought out small cups of something to taste. It was sweet, sour, and salty all at once — popcorn with sugar, sour cream, and salt. The whole class groaned and laughed at the same time. At the end, everyone removed their blindfolds and checked the labels together. Matching each sense to the right object felt strangely satisfying. "Your senses," said Mr. Carter, still smiling, "work better together than alone."
5::themysteryofthefivesenses.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What was the Mystery Adventure activity and why was the classroom set up the way it was? 2. What did Mia smell in the first box and what did she think it was? 3. What was the final surprise that made the whole class groan and laugh?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. Why do you think the teacher said 'Today, no looking'? What was the purpose of this rule? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever had to use a sense other than sight to identify something? Describe what happened. 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why are our senses most powerful when we use them together, rather than just relying on one? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What was the activity and what rule did Mr Carter give the class? 2. (Understand) Describe one item Mia or Tommy identified using only smell or touch. 3. (Analyse) What was the final surprise and why did the class react the way they did? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think this kind of sensory activity is a useful way to learn about the senses? Why? 5. (Create) Write Mr Carter's instructions for a new sensory mystery box activity using two different senses.
5::themysteryofthefivesenses.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Why was the classroom dark for the Mystery Adventure? (So students could not use their eyes and had to use their other senses.)
2. (On) What did Mia smell when she lifted the first lid? (A sharp, bright scent — like lemons or oranges.)
3. (On) What did Tommy feel when he reached into the second box? (Something soft and bumpy, which he thought was a strange creature.)
4. (On) What did the whole class do at the same time when the final surprise was revealed? (Groaned and laughed at the same time.)
5. (Above) Why do you think this kind of sensory activity helps students understand their senses better than reading about them? (Because they experience the senses directly and without their most relied-upon sense — sight — which makes them notice things they normally ignore.)
5::themysteryofthefivesenses.go_description: 以故事要素呈现感官探险活动的图形组织器
5::themysteryofthefivesenses.go_usage: Setting → Challenge (use only senses) → Each box discovered → Conclusion

5::exploringourfivesenses.title: Exploring Our Five Senses
5::exploringourfivesenses.example_sentence: A bowl of soup involves sight, smell, taste, and touch all at once.
5::exploringourfivesenses.teaching_focus: Students can practice building lists and using the verb involves to describe combined experiences. Focus on word order in lists (commas + and), choosing nouns to substitute, and speaking sentences about everyday events that use several senses. Encourage short oral substitution drills and pair work describing experiences 'all at once.'

core_vocab.example: through the tongue, detecting sweet, sour, salty, bitter.
They recognize faces, read signs, and store visual memories that last a lifetime.
Our five senses work together to help us understand the world every day.
Touch, felt through skin and fingers, tells us about temperature, texture, and pressure.
Scientists believe humans can recognize up to one trillion different smells.

5::exploringourfivesenses.text_7page:
Page 1: Our five senses work together to help us understand the world every day. Eyes do more than help us read or find a lost toy. They recognize faces, read signs, and store visual memories that last a lifetime.
Page 2: Ears pick up music, birdsong, and a friend's voice across a noisy room. They also help us locate where a sound is coming from.
Page 3: The nose is perhaps the most powerful memory tool we have. Scientists believe humans can recognize up to one trillion different smells.
Page 4: Sometimes a single scent can instantly bring back a long-forgotten memory. Taste works through the tongue, detecting sweet, sour, salty, bitter.
Page 5: It helps us enjoy food and warns us when something has gone bad. Touch, felt through skin and fingers, tells us about temperature, texture, and pressure.
Page 6: It helps young children explore the world and connects us to the things around us. Most of the time, our senses work as a team.
Page 7: A bowl of soup involves sight, smell, taste, and touch all at once. This combination is exactly what makes it feel comforting.
5::exploringourfivesenses.pure_text: Our five senses work together to help us understand the world every day. Eyes do more than help us read or find a lost toy. They recognize faces, read signs, and store visual memories that last a lifetime. Ears pick up music, birdsong, and a friend's voice across a noisy room. They also help us locate where a sound is coming from. The nose is perhaps the most powerful memory tool we have. Scientists believe humans can recognize up to one trillion different smells. Sometimes a single scent can instantly bring back a long-forgotten memory. Taste works through the tongue, detecting sweet, sour, salty, bitter. It helps us enjoy food and warns us when something has gone bad. Touch, felt through skin and fingers, tells us about temperature, texture, and pressure. It helps young children explore the world and connects us to the things around us. Most of the time, our senses work as a team. A bowl of soup involves sight, smell, taste, and touch all at once. This combination is exactly what makes it feel comforting.
5::exploringourfivesenses.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What does each of the five senses do for us beyond its most obvious function? 2. How many taste receptors does the human tongue have, according to the passage? 3. What example in the passage shows that the brain processes multiple senses at once?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which sense do you think you rely on most in daily life? Use evidence from the passage. 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the passage use specific numbers (like 'trillions of receptor cells') to make the information more convincing? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences describing what it would be like to lose one of the five senses, using information from the passage.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name the five senses and give one function of each beyond its most obvious use. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words how the brain processes information from multiple senses at once. 3. (Analyse) The passage says senses 'work together'. Give one example from the text where two or more senses combine. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact about the human senses in this passage do you find most remarkable? Why? 5. (Create) Write two sentences describing a moment in daily life where all five senses are active at the same time.
5::exploringourfivesenses.rr_questions:
1. (Under) How many senses do humans have? (Five — sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch.)
2. (On) What can eyes do besides help us read? (They recognise faces, read signs, and store visual memories that last a lifetime.)
3. (On) What does the tongue contain that helps us taste? (Taste buds with tiny receptor cells — thousands of them.)
4. (On) What example does the passage give of all the senses working together? (A bowl of soup — it involves sight, smell, taste, and touch all at once.)
5. (Above) Why do you think we barely notice our senses working unless one of them stops working? (Because when everything works normally, the brain processes sense information automatically without us having to think about it.)
5::exploringourfivesenses.go_description: 按感官类别分支呈现五种感官功能的层级图
5::exploringourfivesenses.go_usage: 五支：Sight / Hearing / Smell / Taste / Touch，每支写功能和例子

5::followingthesounds.title: Following the Sounds
5::followingthesounds.example_sentence: Mia and Tommy stared, then burst out laughing.
5::followingthesounds.teaching_focus: Practice past simple verbs and sequencing words (then). Students can substitute different subjects and verbs to retell events, add short details (objects or adverbs), and speak short retellings. Focus on correct past tense forms, the comma before 'then' for natural pauses, and oral timing to show the sequence of actions.

core_vocab.example: schoolyard gate, a cheerful bicycle bell made them both smile.
teacher, out of nowhere, lifted a large pot and began drumming it like a real instrument.
Their first discovery came quickly.
Then came something unexpected — a steady drumbeat from the field, growing louder with every step.
They pressed their faces against the gate and peered through.

5::followingthesounds.core_vocab_raw: cheerful, instrument, discovery, drumbeat, press against
5::followingthesounds.text_7page:
Page 1: On a sunny Saturday morning, Mia and Tommy decided to become sound hunters. Mia brought her notebook and wrote "Sound Log" across the top in careful letters.
Page 2: Their first discovery came quickly. A bright chirp rang out from the trees above the pond. "Sound A: bird call,"
Page 3: Mia wrote. Then a long whoosh swept through the leaves. "Sound B: wind,"
Page 4: she added, without looking up. Near the schoolyard gate, a cheerful bicycle bell made them both smile. "Sound C: bell,"
Page 5: Tommy said, already writing it himself. Then came something unexpected — a steady drumbeat from the field, growing louder with every step. They pressed their faces against the gate and peered through.
Page 6: Inside, students were rehearsing for a school concert. The music teacher, out of nowhere, lifted a large pot and began drumming it like a real instrument. Then she placed it on her head like a helmet and grinned at them.
Page 7: Mia and Tommy stared, then burst out laughing. The teacher waved them inside and handed each of them a rhythm stick. Before they left, Mia added one final entry: "Sound D: the loudest, silliest drumbeat ever heard."
5::followingthesounds.pure_text: On a sunny Saturday morning, Mia and Tommy decided to become sound hunters. Mia brought her notebook and wrote "Sound Log" across the top in careful letters. Their first discovery came quickly. A bright chirp rang out from the trees above the pond. "Sound A: bird call," Mia wrote. Then a long whoosh swept through the leaves. "Sound B: wind," she added, without looking up. Near the schoolyard gate, a cheerful bicycle bell made them both smile. "Sound C: bell," Tommy said, already writing it himself. Then came something unexpected — a steady drumbeat from the field, growing louder with every step. They pressed their faces against the gate and peered through. Inside, students were rehearsing for a school concert. The music teacher, out of nowhere, lifted a large pot and began drumming it like a real instrument. Then she placed it on her head like a helmet and grinned at them. Mia and Tommy stared, then burst out laughing. The teacher waved them inside and handed each of them a rhythm stick. Before they left, Mia added one final entry: "Sound D: the loudest, silliest drumbeat ever heard."
5::followingthesounds.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What did Mia and Tommy decide to do on Saturday morning and how did they prepare? 2. Describe three sounds they recorded in their Sound Log. 3. What was the final discovery that made them both stare and then burst out laughing?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does keeping a 'Sound Log' suggest about Mia's approach to exploring the world? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever stopped to really listen to the sounds around you? What did you notice? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why do you think the author chose to end the story with laughter? What does this ending suggest? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What was the children's Sound Log activity and why did they start it? 2. (Understand) Describe the most surprising sound they discovered and where it came from. 3. (Analyse) How does the story use the sequence of sounds to build towards the final funny discovery? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think paying close attention to everyday sounds can change how you experience the world? Why? 5. (Create) Write three entries from Mia and Tommy's Sound Log, using sensory language to describe each sound.
5::followingthesounds.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What did Mia write at the top of her notebook? ("Sound Log.")
2. (On) What was the first sound they recorded and where did it come from? (A bright chirp from a bird in the trees above the pond.)
3. (On) What sound did Tommy discover by pressing his ear against the hollow pipe? (An echo that bounced back to his ears — a sound he described as drumbeat-like.)
4. (On) What was the final discovery that made them stare and then burst out laughing? (Accept the detail from the text about the source of the final unexpected sound.)
5. (Above) Why do you think keeping a log made their sound hunt more interesting? (Because writing things down made them pay closer attention and helped them compare what they found.)
5::followingthesounds.go_description: 以链条呈现声音探险各阶段发现的图形组织器
5::followingthesounds.go_usage: Sound A (bird) → Sound B (whoosh) → Sound C (drumbeat) → Final discovery

5::fasterthansound.title: Faster Than Sound
5::fasterthansound.example_sentence: When a plane passes the sound speed, it makes a loud sonic boom.
