5::fasterthansound.teaching_focus: Practice making cause–result sentences with when at the start: use a full condition clause + comma + result clause. Focus on subject–verb agreement, vocabulary from the passage (jet, plane, engineers, air, boom), and speaking drills where students substitute new subjects and results to describe experiments or ideas.

core_vocab.example: Scientists call this hypersonic speed, and it could one day change long-distance travel.
This is about 1,235 kilometres per hour, much faster than cars on a highway.
A sonic boom comes from a sudden build-up of air pressure, like a sharp wave.
When a plane passes the sound speed, it makes a loud sonic boom.
Fast jets can reach and pass that speed, becoming supersonic in flight.

5::fasterthansound.core_vocab_raw: hypersonic, kilometre per hour, pressure, sonic boom, supersonic
5::fasterthansound.text_7page:
Page 1: Have you ever heard a loud crack high in the sky above you? This sound happens when something moves faster than the air around it.
Page 2: Sound travels as waves through air, water, and solid materials. Our ears hear sound when these waves reach them and make tiny vibrations.
Page 3: In normal air, the speed of sound is about 340 meters per second. This is about 1,235 kilometres per hour, much faster than cars on a highway.
Page 4: Fast jets can reach and pass that speed, becoming supersonic in flight. When a plane passes the sound speed, it makes a loud sonic boom.
Page 5: A sonic boom comes from a sudden build-up of air pressure, like a sharp wave. People on the ground may hear a crash and see windows shake.
Page 6: Engineers now design quieter supersonic planes to make flights safer and less noisy. Some experimental planes today fly at over five times the speed of sound.
Page 7: Scientists call this hypersonic speed, and it could one day change long-distance travel. It is exciting to think that in the future a flight from London to New York might take under two hours instead of seven!
5::fasterthansound.pure_text: Have you ever heard a loud crack high in the sky above you? This sound happens when something moves faster than the air around it. Sound travels as waves through air, water, and solid materials. Our ears hear sound when these waves reach them and make tiny vibrations. In normal air, the speed of sound is about 340 meters per second. This is about 1,235 kilometres per hour, much faster than cars on a highway. Fast jets can reach and pass that speed, becoming supersonic in flight. When a plane passes the sound speed, it makes a loud sonic boom. A sonic boom comes from a sudden build-up of air pressure, like a sharp wave. People on the ground may hear a crash and see windows shake. Engineers now design quieter supersonic planes to make flights safer and less noisy. Some experimental planes today fly at over five times the speed of sound. Scientists call this hypersonic speed, and it could one day change long-distance travel. It is exciting to think that in the future a flight from London to New York might take under two hours instead of seven!
5::fasterthansound.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::fasterthansound.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What is the speed of sound in normal air, according to the passage? 2. What causes a sonic boom and when does it happen? 3. What is the difference between supersonic and hypersonic speed?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which fact about the speed of sound in this passage surprised you most? Why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the author use the question 'Have you ever heard a loud crack?' as an opening? Is this effective? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences about one use of supersonic or hypersonic technology and explain its possible benefit or risk.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What is the speed of sound and through what materials does it travel? 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words what a sonic boom is and what causes it. 3. (Analyse) Why can sound travel faster through solids than through air? 4. (Evaluate) The passage says hypersonic speeds 'could make long journeys much shorter'. What challenges might this create? 5. (Create) Write two sentences arguing either for or against developing hypersonic passenger aircraft.
5::fasterthansound.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What happens when something moves faster than sound in the air? (It creates a loud sonic boom.)
2. (On) At what speed does sound travel in normal air? (About 343 metres per second, or roughly 1,235 kilometres per hour.)
3. (On) What is the difference between supersonic and hypersonic? (Supersonic is faster than sound; hypersonic is five or more times faster than sound.)
4. (On) Name one use of supersonic or hypersonic technology mentioned in the passage. (Military aircraft, or future passenger travel — accept any from text.)
5. (Above) Why do you think sound travels faster through water and solid materials than through air? (Because molecules in water and solids are packed closer together, so sound waves pass between them more quickly.)
5::fasterthansound.graphic_organizer: Main Idea & Details Web (主旨细节网)
5::fasterthansound.go_description: 中心为声速概念、向外辐射关键事实的放射状图形组织器
5::fasterthansound.go_usage: 中心写 "Faster Than Sound"，分支：speed / sonic boom / supersonic / hypersonic / uses

5::lightsinthedark.title: Lights in the Dark
5::lightsinthedark.sentence_pattern: [Thing A] was [comparative adjective] than [Thing B], but [Thing B] [past verb] [adjective].
5::lightsinthedark.example_sentence: The flashlight was brighter than the candle, but the candle felt warmer.
5::lightsinthedark.teaching_focus: Practice forming comparatives with than and linking contrasting ideas with but; substitute different nouns and adjectives (brighter/stronger/hotter, softer/warmer/cozier) and verbs like felt/looked/seemed; speaking drills: compare two objects in the classroom or story pictures, then add a contrasting feeling sentence. Use pair work, swapping roles to ask and answer: "Which is brighter? Which feels warmer?"

core_vocab.example: smiled and said they should sit together on the balcony.
Soon the family counted many stars and planned to learn constellations tomorrow.
He had been very scared, but the funny shadow gave him courage.
Mia's dad opened a drawer and found a small flashlight.
He had been very scared, but the funny shadow gave him courage.

5::lightsinthedark.text_7page:
Page 1: The lights went out on a rainy evening at Mia's house. The family sat quietly and listened to rain on the roof. Tommy felt afraid in the sudden deep dark of the room.
Page 2: Mia's dad opened a drawer and found a small flashlight. Mia lit a candle and placed it on the kitchen table. Their mother turned on a tiny lamp near the sofa for reading.
Page 3: The flashlight was brighter than the candle, but the candle felt warmer. Tommy held the flashlight and looked at the window with care.
Page 4: A surprise shadow on the wall looked like a dragon and made him laugh. He had been very scared, but the funny shadow gave him courage.
Page 5: Mia said, "Light can show the way and make us feel safe." Their father smiled and said they should sit together on the balcony.
Page 6: At the balcony they placed the candle and watched the clearer night sky. Soon the family counted many stars and planned to learn constellations tomorrow.
Page 7: Tommy whispered that the light was softer and more like a warm hug. Everyone laughed because the flashlight made their cat look like a tiny astronaut.
5::lightsinthedark.pure_text: The lights went out on a rainy evening at Mia's house. The family sat quietly and listened to rain on the roof. Tommy felt afraid in the sudden deep dark of the room. Mia's dad opened a drawer and found a small flashlight. Mia lit a candle and placed it on the kitchen table. Their mother turned on a tiny lamp near the sofa for reading. The flashlight was brighter than the candle, but the candle felt warmer. Tommy held the flashlight and looked at the window with care. A surprise shadow on the wall looked like a dragon and made him laugh. He had been very scared, but the funny shadow gave him courage. Mia said, "Light can show the way and make us feel safe." Their father smiled and said they should sit together on the balcony. At the balcony they placed the candle and watched the clearer night sky. Soon the family counted many stars and planned to learn constellations tomorrow. Tommy whispered that the light was softer and more like a warm hug. Everyone laughed because the flashlight made their cat look like a tiny astronaut.
5::lightsinthedark.reading_strategy: 进行预测 (Making and Confirming Predictions)
5::lightsinthedark.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Why did the lights go out at Mia's house and how did the family react at first? 2. What did Mia's dad find in the drawer and how did the family use it? 3. How did Tommy's fear change during the evening?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. The flashlight was brighter but the candle felt warmer. What does this comparison suggest about different kinds of light? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever experienced a power cut? What did your family do and how did it feel? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Why do you think stars on the balcony at night helped Tommy feel less afraid? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What caused the power cut and how did the family first react? 2. (Understand) Describe two ways the family used light during the evening. 3. (Analyse) How did Tommy's feelings change from the beginning to the end of the story? 4. (Evaluate) What message do you think the story is giving about fear and the unknown? 5. (Create) Write Tommy's description of the stars he saw from the balcony, using descriptive language from the story.
5::lightsinthedark.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Why did the lights go out at Mia's house? (There was a power cut on a rainy evening.)
2. (On) What did Mia's dad find in the drawer to help? (A small flashlight.)
3. (On) What two sources of light did the family use during the power cut? (A flashlight and a candle.)
4. (On) What did Mia and Tommy see from the balcony that helped Tommy feel less afraid? (Bright stars in the night sky.)
5. (Above) Why do you think the candle felt 'warmer' than the flashlight even though the flashlight was brighter? (Because the candle gives a soft, flickering light that feels cosy and familiar, while the flashlight gives a harsh, cold beam — warm and bright mean different things here.)
5::lightsinthedark.go_description: 以故事要素呈现停电夜晚家庭经历的图形组织器
5::lightsinthedark.go_usage: Setting (rainy evening, no lights) → Fear → Discovery (stars) → Courage → Resolution

5::lightaroundus.title: Light Around Us
5::lightaroundus.example_sentence: Light from phones and computers helps us read, learn, and play at home and school.
5::lightaroundus.teaching_focus: Practice substituting different light sources and useful actions (find, read, stay safe). Students can make speaking chains: change the source, keep 'helps us', and add a short reason to describe the function of light.

core_vocab.example: traffic lights, flashlights, light from screens, and lighthouses.
hospitals, good light helps doctors do careful operations each day.
Artificial light is the light people make with lamps, bulbs, and screens.
A lighthouse sends a bright beam to guide ships and avoid dangerous rocks at sea.
Natural light comes from the Sun and gives warm, strong light to Earth.

5::lightaroundus.core_vocab_raw: traffic, operation, artificial light, beam, natural light
5::lightaroundus.text_7page:
Page 1: Light makes our world easy to see and understand every day. Natural light comes from the Sun and gives warm, strong light to Earth. Sunlight is bright and helps plants grow and people wake up.
Page 2: Artificial light is the light people make with lamps, bulbs, and screens. Many things produce artificial light at night in homes and cities.
Page 3: Examples are traffic lights, flashlights, light from screens, and lighthouses. Traffic lights and signs help drivers and pedestrians stop and go safely.
Page 4: A flashlight gives focused light to help people find things in the dark. Light from phones and computers helps us read, learn, and play at home and school.
Page 5: A lighthouse sends a bright beam to guide ships and avoid dangerous rocks at sea. Light helps people see details, do careful work, and stay safe.
Page 6: In hospitals, good light helps doctors do careful operations each day. Street lights and lamps keep walkers and drivers safe when it is dark.
Page 7: Light travels very fast, and sunlight takes about eight minutes to reach Earth. Think of light as a helpful friend that shows the way every day.
5::lightaroundus.pure_text: Light makes our world easy to see and understand every day. Natural light comes from the Sun and gives warm, strong light to Earth. Sunlight is bright and helps plants grow and people wake up. Artificial light is the light people make with lamps, bulbs, and screens. Many things produce artificial light at night in homes and cities. Examples are traffic lights, flashlights, light from screens, and lighthouses. Traffic lights and signs help drivers and pedestrians stop and go safely. A flashlight gives focused light to help people find things in the dark. Light from phones and computers helps us read, learn, and play at home and school. A lighthouse sends a bright beam to guide ships and avoid dangerous rocks at sea. Light helps people see details, do careful work, and stay safe. In hospitals, good light helps doctors do careful operations each day. Street lights and lamps keep walkers and drivers safe when it is dark. Light travels very fast, and sunlight takes about eight minutes to reach Earth. Think of light as a helpful friend that shows the way every day.
5::lightaroundus.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. What is the difference between natural light and artificial light? 2. Name three examples of artificial light mentioned in the passage. 3. What does the passage say about how surgeons use light?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which use of artificial light mentioned in this passage do you think is most important? Why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the passage structure the comparison between natural and artificial light? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences adding information about one use of light technology not mentioned in this passage.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Explain the difference between natural and artificial light in your own words. 2. (Understand) Give two examples from the passage of how artificial light helps people in important jobs. 3. (Analyse) The passage says 'without artificial light, many parts of modern life would stop'. Is this claim supported by the examples in the text? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think we use too much artificial light in daily life? Use ideas from the passage. 5. (Create) Write two sentences describing how your daily routine would change without artificial light.
5::lightaroundus.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What are the two types of light described in this passage? (Natural light and artificial light.)
2. (On) Where does natural light come from? (The Sun.)
3. (On) Give two examples of artificial light from the passage. (Lamps, bulbs, screens, traffic lights, operating theatre lights — any two.)
4. (On) How do surgeons use artificial light? (They use very bright lights in operating theatres to see clearly and safely during operations.)
5. (Above) Why do you think too much artificial light at night can be a problem for people and animals? (Because it can disturb sleep and the natural day-night cycles that both humans and animals depend on for health.)
5::lightaroundus.go_description: 对比自然光与人工光特点的双列图形组织器
5::lightaroundus.go_usage: 左列：Natural Light，右列：Artificial Light，各写来源、特点、用途

5::aroundtheworldwithmyfamily.title: Around the World with My Family
5::aroundtheworldwithmyfamily.sentence_pattern: In [Place], [Subject] [past verb] onto [animal/thing] and [adverb] [past verb] its [body part] when it [past verb].
5::aroundtheworldwithmyfamily.example_sentence: In Egypt, Mia climbed onto a camel and immediately grabbed its neck when it stood up.
5::aroundtheworldwithmyfamily.teaching_focus: Practice past simple verbs and sequence words, use of 'when' to show timing, short adverbs (e.g. immediately/quickly), and pronouns like 'its'. Students can swap places, animals and actions to make and tell their own travel sentences.

core_vocab.example: Dad got syrup on his chin from a warm pastry and didn't notice for an embarrassingly long time.
In Japan, they rode a bullet train so fast that mountains blurred past the window.
They wandered through a market full of bright fabrics, spices, and sounds.
Last summer, Mia's family travelled across four countries in one unforgettable trip.
Then a gust of wind lifted Mia's red hat straight off her head and sent it spinning out of sight.

5::aroundtheworldwithmyfamily.core_vocab_raw: pastry, bullet train, spice, unforgettable, a gust of wind
5::aroundtheworldwithmyfamily.text_7page:
Page 1: Last summer, Mia's family travelled across four countries in one unforgettable trip. In Japan, they rode a bullet train so fast that mountains blurred past the window. In Egypt, Mia climbed onto a camel and immediately grabbed its neck when it stood up.
Page 2: The camel didn't seem to mind. In Italy, the family shared an enormous pizza on a narrow street while a man nearby played accordion. Dad got syrup on his chin from a warm pastry and didn't notice for an embarrassingly long time.
Page 3: Morocco was their last stop — and the most surprising. They wandered through a market full of bright fabrics, spices, and sounds.
Page 4: Then a gust of wind lifted Mia's red hat straight off her head and sent it spinning out of sight. She searched everywhere, growing more worried with every minute.
Page 5: Later, a market seller called them back and pointed quietly at his camel. There was Mia's hat, tied neatly to the saddle like a little flag.
Page 6: The seller had found it and kept it safe without being asked. Mia put the hat back on and said "shukran"
Page 7: — thank you in Arabic. The seller smiled and nodded.
5::aroundtheworldwithmyfamily.pure_text: Last summer, Mia's family travelled across four countries in one unforgettable trip. In Japan, they rode a bullet train so fast that mountains blurred past the window. In Egypt, Mia climbed onto a camel and immediately grabbed its neck when it stood up. The camel didn't seem to mind. In Italy, the family shared an enormous pizza on a narrow street while a man nearby played accordion. Dad got syrup on his chin from a warm pastry and didn't notice for an embarrassingly long time. Morocco was their last stop — and the most surprising. They wandered through a market full of bright fabrics, spices, and sounds. Then a gust of wind lifted Mia's red hat straight off her head and sent it spinning out of sight. She searched everywhere, growing more worried with every minute. Later, a market seller called them back and pointed quietly at his camel. There was Mia's hat, tied neatly to the saddle like a little flag. The seller had found it and kept it safe without being asked. Mia put the hat back on and said "shukran" — thank you in Arabic. The seller smiled and nodded.
5::aroundtheworldwithmyfamily.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name the four countries the family visited and one memorable experience in each. 2. What happened when Mia climbed onto the camel in Egypt? 3. What did Mia notice about the spice market in India?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does calling the trip 'unforgettable' suggest about what travel does for families? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever visited a place that was very different from home? What surprised you most? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) Which of the four destinations would you most like to visit? Use details from the story to explain why. 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name the four countries visited and the main experience in each. 2. (Understand) Describe the funniest or most surprising moment of the trip and explain why it was memorable. 3. (Analyse) What does the detail about mountains 'blurring past the window' on the bullet train show about the author's writing style? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think travelling to different countries is the best way to learn about the world? What else could teach you the same things? 5. (Create) Write a postcard from Mia to a friend at home, describing her favourite moment of the trip.
5::aroundtheworldwithmyfamily.rr_questions:
1. (Under) How many countries did Mia's family visit on their summer trip? (Four.)
2. (On) What did Mia do on the bullet train in Japan and what happened? (The mountains blurred past the window because the train was so fast.)
3. (On) What happened when Mia climbed onto the camel in Egypt? (She immediately grabbed its neck when the camel stood up suddenly.)
4. (On) What did they smell at the spice market in India? (A mix of warm and sharp spices filling the air.)
5. (Above) Why do you think travelling to different countries stays in people's memories for so long? (Because the sights, smells, and experiences are so different from everyday life that they make a strong impression on your mind.)
5::aroundtheworldwithmyfamily.go_description: 以路线呈现四国旅行经历的图形组织器
5::aroundtheworldwithmyfamily.go_usage: Japan (bullet train) → Egypt (camel) → France (Eiffel Tower) → India (spice market)

5::amazingaustralia.title: Amazing Australia
5::amazingaustralia.example_sentence: They eat eucalyptus leaves, which are so tough that most other animals cannot digest them at all.
5::amazingaustralia.teaching_focus: Practice the form 'so + adjective + that' (+ result clause). Focus on choosing strong adjectives, making clear results, and changing the subject. Activities: substitution drills, make-your-own sentences about animals or places, and short pair dialogues explaining surprising facts.

core_vocab.example: Beneath the surface, thousands of species of fish, coral, and sea creatures share one of the most complex ecosystems on the planet.
They eat eucalyptus leaves, which are so tough that most other animals cannot digest them at all.
The Great Barrier Reef stretches over 2,300 kilometres along Australia's northeastern coast.
Kangaroos can travel over ten kilometres in a single day, using powerful hind legs that make them look effortless in motion.
Koalas, meanwhile, sleep for up to twenty hours a day.

5::amazingaustralia.core_vocab_raw: ecosystem, digest, great barrier reef, effortless, koala
5::amazingaustralia.text_7page:
Page 1: Australia is the sixth largest country in the world, but in many ways it feels like a world of its own. Its animals are unlike those found almost anywhere else on Earth.
Page 2: Kangaroos can travel over ten kilometres in a single day, using powerful hind legs that make them look effortless in motion. Koalas, meanwhile, sleep for up to twenty hours a day.
Page 3: They eat eucalyptus leaves, which are so tough that most other animals cannot digest them at all. The Great Barrier Reef stretches over 2,300 kilometres along Australia's northeastern coast.
Page 4: It is so large that it can to be seen from space. Beneath the surface, thousands of species of fish, coral, and sea creatures share one of the most complex ecosystems on the planet.
Page 5: Australia's weather varies more than many visitors expect. Northern regions stay warm year-round, while the south experiences cool winters and crisp mornings.
Page 6: In Sydney, the harbor is the heart of city life.
Page 7: The Opera House, with its distinctive sail-shaped roof, has become one of the most recognized buildings in the world — though locals often say it looks better from the water.
5::amazingaustralia.pure_text: Australia is the sixth largest country in the world, but in many ways it feels like a world of its own. Its animals are unlike those found almost anywhere else on Earth. Kangaroos can travel over ten kilometres in a single day, using powerful hind legs that make them look effortless in motion. Koalas, meanwhile, sleep for up to twenty hours a day. They eat eucalyptus leaves, which are so tough that most other animals cannot digest them at all. The Great Barrier Reef stretches over 2,300 kilometres along Australia's northeastern coast. It is so large that it can to be seen from space. Beneath the surface, thousands of species of fish, coral, and sea creatures share one of the most complex ecosystems on the planet. Australia's weather varies more than many visitors expect. Northern regions stay warm year-round, while the south experiences cool winters and crisp mornings. In Sydney, the harbor is the heart of city life. The Opera House, with its distinctive sail-shaped roof, has become one of the most recognized buildings in the world — though locals often say it looks better from the water.
5::amazingaustralia.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::amazingaustralia.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Why are Australian animals described as 'unlike those found almost anywhere else on Earth'? 2. How do koalas survive on a diet that most other animals cannot digest? 3. What is the Great Barrier Reef and why is it significant?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which Australian animal or place from this passage do you find most impressive? Why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the passage use the phrase 'a world of its own' to introduce Australia? Is this an effective opening? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences about an aspect of Australia not mentioned in the passage, using information you already know.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Why are Australian animals described as unique and give one example from the text. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words how koalas have adapted to their diet. 3. (Analyse) What does the term 'ecosystem' mean in context? How does the Great Barrier Reef function as an ecosystem? 4. (Evaluate) The passage says Australia 'feels like a world of its own'. Is this metaphor accurate based on the evidence in the text? 5. (Create) Write two sentences about an environmental threat to the Great Barrier Reef and what could be done about it.
5::amazingaustralia.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Where does Australia rank in size among the world's countries? (Sixth largest.)
2. (On) What do koalas eat and why is it unusual? (Eucalyptus leaves, which are so tough that most other animals cannot digest them.)
3. (On) What is the Great Barrier Reef? (A vast coral reef system that supports thousands of sea creatures.)
4. (On) What do kangaroos use to move long distances? (Powerful hind legs that look effortless in motion.)
5. (Above) Why do you think so many unique animals evolved in Australia compared to other parts of the world? (Because Australia has been separated from other continents for millions of years, so animals there evolved independently without competition from species elsewhere.)
5::amazingaustralia.graphic_organizer: Main Idea & Details Web (主旨细节网)
5::amazingaustralia.go_description: 中心为澳大利亚特点、向外辐射关键信息的放射状图形组织器
5::amazingaustralia.go_usage: 中心写 "Amazing Australia"，分支：animals / Great Barrier Reef / ecosystem / size

5::theworldadventuregame.title: The World Adventure Game
5::theworldadventuregame.example_sentence: She found three hidden flag stickers before Tommy had finished reading the first clue.
5::theworldadventuregame.teaching_focus: Use past simple + past perfect to show sequence (found ... before ... had finished ...). Practice substitution to retell events, describe quick actions, and ask/answer what happened first.

core_vocab.example: The class was divided into 10 teams and each received a mission card.
"It's the Eiffel Tower," said Mia, appearing beside him.
Tommy, meanwhile, was staring at a pile of cardboard pieces that were supposed to become a famous landmark.
One morning, Mr. Cater said the class would have a "World Map Mission".
Then she remembered a detail from a lesson weeks ago and snapped the label into place.

5::theworldadventuregame.core_vocab_raw: be divided into, eiffel tower, landmark, mission, snap
5::theworldadventuregame.text_7page:
Page 1: One morning, Mr. Cater said the class would have a "World Map Mission". The class was divided into 10 teams and each received a mission card. Their teacher explained the rules quickly: five stations, five challenges, and only one winning team.
Page 2: Mia shot off immediately. She found three hidden flag stickers before Tommy had finished reading the first clue. Tommy, meanwhile, was staring at a pile of cardboard pieces that were supposed to become a famous landmark. "It's the Eiffel Tower,"
Page 3: said Mia, appearing beside him. "I knew that," said Tommy, who had not known that. At the capitals station, Mia suddenly froze.
Page 4: She had forgotten whether Canberra or Sydney was Australia's capital. Then she remembered a detail from a lesson weeks ago and snapped the label into place. "How did you know that?"
Page 5: Tommy asked. "I just remembered," she said, looking slightly surprised herself.
Page 6: Then something small and orange fell from Tommy's sleeve — a hamster sticker that had been stuck there since breakfast. It landed perfectly on the map, right on top of Russia. They both stared at it, then burst out laughing.
Page 7: At the end, Mr. Cater counted every team's points carefully. Mia and Tommy had won — by exactly one point.
5::theworldadventuregame.pure_text: One morning, Mr. Cater said the class would have a "World Map Mission". The class was divided into 10 teams and each received a mission card. Their teacher explained the rules quickly: five stations, five challenges, and only one winning team. Mia shot off immediately. She found three hidden flag stickers before Tommy had finished reading the first clue. Tommy, meanwhile, was staring at a pile of cardboard pieces that were supposed to become a famous landmark. "It's the Eiffel Tower," said Mia, appearing beside him. "I knew that," said Tommy, who had not known that. At the capitals station, Mia suddenly froze. She had forgotten whether Canberra or Sydney was Australia's capital. Then she remembered a detail from a lesson weeks ago and snapped the label into place. "How did you know that?" Tommy asked. "I just remembered," she said, looking slightly surprised herself. Then something small and orange fell from Tommy's sleeve — a hamster sticker that had been stuck there since breakfast. It landed perfectly on the map, right on top of Russia. They both stared at it, then burst out laughing. At the end, Mr. Cater counted every team's points carefully. Mia and Tommy had won — by exactly one point.
5::theworldadventuregame.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. How was the class organised for the World Map Mission and what was each team's task? 2. Who was the first person to find all their landmarks and what was her strategy? 3. What did students have to do at each station to score a point?
Reading Skill: 1. What are the key story elements (character, setting, problem, solution)? 2. What does the fact that Mia found all her stickers 'before Tommy had finished reading the first clue' tell us about their different approaches? 3. Find one sentence in the text that shows a character's feeling rather than just stating it.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you predict would happen? Was your prediction correct? 2. Have you ever taken part in a team game or treasure hunt? What strategy did you use? 3. What question would you like to ask the author?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Story Elements) Complete a story map: character, setting, problem, solution. 2. (Inference) What do you think the character learned by the end? 3. (Vocabulary) Choose one vocabulary word and use it in your own sentence. 4. (Analyse) What does this type of game teach students that a normal geography lesson might not? 5. (Connection) How does the theme of this story connect to something in your own life?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) How was the game set up and what were the teams competing to do? 2. (Understand) Describe the strategy one character used to find their landmarks quickly. 3. (Analyse) What happened at the end of the game and which team won? 4. (Evaluate) Do you think competitive games are a good way to learn geography? What are the advantages and disadvantages? 5. (Create) Write the rules of the World Map Mission game as Mr Carter might have explained them to the class.
5::theworldadventuregame.rr_questions:
1. (Under) How many teams did the class divide into for the World Map Mission? (Ten teams.)
2. (On) What was Mia's strategy at the start of the game? (She shot off immediately and found hidden flag stickers before anyone else had finished reading the first clue.)
3. (On) How many stations did each team have to visit? (Five stations with five challenges.)
4. (On) What did teams have to do at each station to earn a point? (Identify the correct landmark or answer a geography question.)
5. (Above) Why do you think a competitive game like this can be a good way to learn about world geography? (Because competing makes students more motivated to pay attention, and the excitement of the game helps them remember the information.)
5::theworldadventuregame.go_description: 以链条呈现世界地图任务各步骤的图形组织器
5::theworldadventuregame.go_usage: Receive card → Find clues → Identify landmarks → Collect flags → Present results

5::lifeinnortherneurope.title: Life in Northern Europe
5::lifeinnortherneurope.example_sentence: Long winters have shaped the way people live.
5::lifeinnortherneurope.teaching_focus: Practice present perfect with have/has + 'shaped' to express cause and effect, use 'the way' + clause to describe habits or designs, substitute different subjects and verbs for speaking and short writings (retelling why things are like that).

core_vocab.example: Norway, Sweden, Finland, and Denmark share long coastlines, vast forests, and lakes that freeze solid each year.
In the far north, winter nights bring one of nature's most spectacular sights: the aurora borealis, or northern lights.
Northern Europe is a place where harsh winters and quiet landscapes have produced some of the world's most resilient and inventive cultures.
is one of the coldest regions on Earth, yet the people who live there have turned winter into something almost beautiful.
Bands of green and pink ripple silently across a dark sky, appearing and vanishing like slow curtains of color.

5::lifeinnortherneurope.core_vocab_raw: freeze, aurora boreali, harsh, region, ripple
5::lifeinnortherneurope.text_7page:
Page 1: Northern Europe is one of the coldest regions on Earth, yet the people who live there have turned winter into something almost beautiful. Countries like Norway, Sweden, Finland, and Denmark share long coastlines, vast forests, and lakes that freeze solid each year.
Page 2: Finland alone has around 188,000 lakes — more than almost any country in the world. In the far north, winter nights bring one of nature's most spectacular sights: the aurora borealis, or northern lights.
Page 3: Bands of green and pink ripple silently across a dark sky, appearing and vanishing like slow curtains of color. Long winters have shaped the way people live.
Page 4: Homes are built for warmth, meals are simple and hearty, and families spend long evenings together around candlelight. In Denmark, bicycles remain the most popular way to travel, even in cold weather.
Page 5: The region's history runs deep. Museums across Norway and Denmark display original Viking ships.
Page 6: They are enormous wooden vessels that once crossed the Atlantic Ocean centuries before modern navigation existed.
Page 7: Northern Europe is a place where harsh winters and quiet landscapes have produced some of the world's most resilient and inventive cultures.
5::lifeinnortherneurope.pure_text: Northern Europe is one of the coldest regions on Earth, yet the people who live there have turned winter into something almost beautiful. Countries like Norway, Sweden, Finland, and Denmark share long coastlines, vast forests, and lakes that freeze solid each year. Finland alone has around 188,000 lakes — more than almost any country in the world. In the far north, winter nights bring one of nature's most spectacular sights: the aurora borealis, or northern lights. Bands of green and pink ripple silently across a dark sky, appearing and vanishing like slow curtains of color. Long winters have shaped the way people live. Homes are built for warmth, meals are simple and hearty, and families spend long evenings together around candlelight. In Denmark, bicycles remain the most popular way to travel, even in cold weather. The region's history runs deep. Museums across Norway and Denmark display original Viking ships. They are enormous wooden vessels that once crossed the Atlantic Ocean centuries before modern navigation existed. Northern Europe is a place where harsh winters and quiet landscapes have produced some of the world's most resilient and inventive cultures.
5::lifeinnortherneurope.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
5::lifeinnortherneurope.questions_type:
Reading for Details: 1. Name three countries in Northern Europe and one feature they share. 2. How have people in Northern Europe adapted their buildings to the cold climate? 3. What is the aurora borealis and when can it be seen?
Reading Skill: 1. What is the main idea and what two details support it? 2. How is the information organised? (sequence / categories / cause-effect / comparison) 3. Find one key vocabulary word and explain its meaning in context.
Reading Strategy: 1. What did you already know about this topic before reading? 2. Which aspect of life in Northern Europe described in this passage do you find most interesting? Why? 3. What question do you still have after reading?
Other Comprehension: 1. (Main Idea) Write one sentence that summarises the whole passage. 2. (Analysis) How does the opening sentence 'turned winter into something almost beautiful' prepare the reader for the rest of the passage? 3. (Vocabulary) Use one vocabulary word in a new sentence about your own experience. 4. (Evaluate) Which fact or idea from the passage do you find most interesting? Explain why. 5. (Create) Write two sentences about how the climate in Northern Europe might be different from the climate where you live.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name three countries in Northern Europe and give one fact about each from the passage. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words how people in Northern Europe have adapted their daily lives to cope with harsh winters. 3. (Analyse) The passage says long winters 'have shaped the way people live'. Give two specific examples from the text that support this claim. 4. (Evaluate) Do you think a very cold climate makes life harder or creates unique advantages? Use evidence from the passage. 5. (Create) Write two sentences about one way climate change might affect life in Northern Europe in the future.
5::lifeinnortherneurope.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name three countries that are part of Northern Europe. (Norway, Sweden, Finland, and Denmark — any three.)
2. (On) What natural feature appears in the night sky and draws many visitors to Northern Europe? (The aurora borealis, or Northern Lights.)
3. (On) How have buildings in Northern Europe adapted to the cold climate? (They are built with thick walls, triple-glazed windows, and efficient heating systems.)
4. (On) What do people in Northern Europe do in winter to stay active and happy? (Ice skating, cross-country skiing, saunas — accept any from text.)
5. (Above) Why do you think people in cold climates often develop very strong community traditions around warmth and togetherness? (Because the long dark winters make warmth, light, and company especially valuable — people rely on each other more when the environment is harsh.)
5::lifeinnortherneurope.graphic_organizer: Main Idea & Details Web (主旨细节网)
5::lifeinnortherneurope.go_description: 中心为北欧生活特点、向外辐射关键信息的放射状图形组织器
5::lifeinnortherneurope.go_usage: 中心写 "Life in Northern Europe"，分支：climate / aurora / lifestyle / lakes / adaptation

6::builttosurvive.title: Built to Survive
6::builttosurvive.sentence_pattern: [Subject] pointed to [noun] and said it [verb phrase].
6::builttosurvive.example_sentence: The guide pointed to the camel's hump and said it stored fat for long journeys.
6::builttosurvive.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: said + it + past simple VP (reported fact). The noun and the fact both change.
She pointed to the label and said it listed all the ingredients. / He pointed to the map and said it showed the safest route. / The teacher pointed to the diagram and said it explained how the heart works.

core_vocab.example: At the snow station a penguin figure had dense feathers and a thick blubber layer.
The brown fur gave camouflage, while quills made predators think twice before attacking.
Working quickly, they discussed each animal using facts about movement, insulation, and protection.
The brown fur gave camouflage, while quills made predators think twice before attacking.
Today you must help animals survive at three different challenge stations.

6::builttosurvive.text_7page:
Page 1: Mia and Tommy arrived at the science museum and hurried to the challenge table. A guide explained, "Today you must help animals survive at desert, snow, and forest stations."
Page 2: At the desert station, they saw a camel model with broad feet and long legs. The guide pointed to the camel's hump and said it stored fat for energy and water.
Page 3: At the snow station, a penguin figure had dense feathers and a thick blubber layer. The guide explained that these features trapped heat and helped penguins survive freezing weather.
Page 4: At the forest station, they examined a porcupine display with sharp quills and brown fur. The brown fur provided camouflage, while the quills made predators think twice before attacking.
Page 5: Mia said, "Speed and strong legs can help desert and forest animals escape quickly." The guide smiled and nodded in agreement.
Page 6: Tommy and Mia worked together at the challenge table. Suddenly the timer began flashing, and they realized wrong choices would cost their team points.
Page 7: Working quickly, they discussed each animal using facts about movement, insulation, and protection. They placed the final labels just before time ran out and waited nervously as the screen changed from red to green.
6::builttosurvive.pure_text: Mia and Tommy arrived at the science museum and hurried to the challenge table. A guide explained, "Today you must help animals survive in desert, snow, and forest stations." At the desert station, they saw a camel model with broad feet and long legs. The guide pointed to the camel’s hump and said it stored fat for energy and helped the camel live a long time without water. At the snow station, a penguin figure had dense feathers and a thick blubber layer. The guide explained that these features trapped heat and helped penguins survive freezing weather. At the forest station, they examined a porcupine display with sharp quills and brown fur. The brown fur provided camouflage, while the quills made predators think twice before attacking. Mia said, "Speed and strong legs can help desert and forest animals escape quickly." The guide smiled and nodded in agreement. Tommy and Mia worked together at the challenge table. Suddenly the timer began flashing, and they realized that wrong choices would cost their team points. Working quickly, they discussed each animal using facts about movement, insulation, and protection. They placed the final labels just before time ran out and waited nervously as the screen turned from red to green.
6::builttosurvive.reading_strategy: 进行预测 (Making and Confirming Predictions)
6::builttosurvive.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) How does the challenge format (helping animals 'survive' at three stations) mirror the real process of learning about animal adaptations? 2. (Analyze) Compare the three habitats (desert, snow, forest). What structural principle explains why animals in each environment need different adaptations? 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) Mia says 'speed and strong legs' help desert and forest animals escape. Is this claim fully supported by the information given in the text? What evidence would make it stronger? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) How does participating in a challenge (rather than reading a textbook) change the way Mia and Tommy learn? What does this suggest about effective education methods? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Design a fourth station for a habitat of your choice. Describe one animal that would appear there, its key structural adaptation, and what label the children would need to place correctly.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name one adaptation mentioned for each of the three animal stations. 2. (Understand) Why does blubber help penguins survive in freezing weather? Explain in your own words. 3. (Analyse) How do Mia and Tommy use facts from each station to complete the challenge? 4. (Evaluate) Which adaptation described in the text do you think is most impressive? Give a reason using evidence from the text. 5. (Create) Suggest one additional animal that could fit into the desert station. Describe its key adaptation and explain why it belongs there.
6::builttosurvive.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What were the three animal stations at the science museum? (Desert, snow, and forest stations.)
2. (On) What does the camel's hump store according to the guide? (Fat for energy and water.)
3. (On) What two features help penguins survive in freezing weather? (Dense feathers and a thick blubber layer that traps heat.)
4. (On) What happened when Mia and Tommy placed their final labels? (The screen slowly changed from red to green — they got it right just in time.)
5. (Above) Why do you think animals in very different habitats sometimes develop similar features? (Because they face similar survival challenges — for example, both seals and penguins developed streamlined bodies to swim efficiently in cold water.)
6::builttosurvive.go_description: 以故事要素呈现情境、挑战和结局的图形组织器
6::builttosurvive.go_usage: 记录：Setting (science museum) → Challenge (help animals survive) → Key animal facts used → Result (green light)

6::structuraladaptations.title: Structural Adaptations
6::structuraladaptations.example_sentence: Polar bears have thick blubber that keeps heat inside their bodies.
6::structuraladaptations.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: have + noun + that + relative clause describing function.
Giraffes have long necks that help them reach leaves high in trees. / Ducks have webbed feet that push them through water efficiently. / Cacti have thick stems that store water during dry periods.

core_vocab.example: Beaks and claws have many shapes that help animals catch food or defend themselves.
Sometimes very different animals evolve the same structure independently, a process called convergent evolution.
Animals survive in cold, hot, or watery places because their bodies have special parts.
Webbing increases surface area so each paddle stroke pushes more water and makes swimming easier.
A long neck also helps giraffes spot danger early across the savanna.

6::structuraladaptations.text_7page:
Page 1: Animals survive in cold, hot, or watery places because their bodies have special parts. Polar bears have dense fur and a thick layer of blubber, which is fat, under their skin.
Page 2: This combination keeps heat in and lets them swim in icy water for food. Giraffes evolved very long necks to reach leaves high in tall trees.
Page 3: A long neck also helps giraffes spot danger early across the savanna. Camels store fat in their humps; this fat becomes an energy and water reserve during long travel.
Page 4: Because of that reserve, camels can travel for days without food or water. Ducks and frogs have skin between their toes that forms webbed feet.
Page 5: Webbing increases surface area so each paddle stroke pushes more water and makes swimming easier. Beaks and claws have many shapes that help animals catch food or defend themselves.
Page 6: These structural adaptations work like built-in tools and save animals energy every day. Sometimes very different animals evolve the same structure independently, a process called convergent evolution.
Page 7: For example, seals and penguins both have streamlined bodies that reduce water resistance and help them swim well. Structural adaptations have helped species survive for millions of years and will likely continue doing so.
6::structuraladaptations.pure_text: Animals survive in cold, hot, or watery places because their bodies have special parts. Polar bears have dense fur and a thick layer of blubber, which is fat, under their skin. This combination keeps heat in and lets them swim in icy water for food. Giraffes evolved very long necks to reach leaves high in tall trees. A long neck also helps giraffes spot danger early across the savanna. Camels store fat in their humps; this fat becomes an energy and water reserve during long travel. Because of that reserve, camels can travel for days without food or water. Ducks and frogs have skin between their toes that forms webbed feet. Webbing increases surface area so each paddle stroke pushes more water and makes swimming easier. Beaks and claws have many shapes that help animals catch food or defend themselves. These structural adaptations work like built-in tools and save animals energy every day. Sometimes very different animals evolve the same structure independently, a process called convergent evolution. For example, seals and penguins both have streamlined bodies that reduce water resistance and help them swim well. Structural adaptations have helped species survive for millions of years and will likely continue doing so.
6::structuraladaptations.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
6::structuraladaptations.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The text introduces the concept of 'convergent evolution' through seals and penguins. What does this process reveal about the relationship between environment and body structure? 2. (Analyze) How does the author move from specific examples (polar bears, giraffes, camels) to a general conclusion? Evaluate whether this inductive structure is effective. 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) The text claims adaptations 'work like built-in tools'. Analyse this metaphor: in what ways is it accurate and in what ways does it oversimplify the biological reality? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) How does the concept of structural adaptation connect to the concept of behavioural adaptation discussed in Book 4? What is the relationship between the two types? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Choose an environment not mentioned in the text (e.g., deep ocean, Arctic tundra). Predict what structural adaptations an animal living there might have and explain your reasoning using principles from the text.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What is convergent evolution? Give the example from the text. 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words why webbed feet help ducks and frogs in water. 3. (Analyse) How is the camel's hump different from a simple food store? What additional function does it serve? 4. (Evaluate) The text says adaptations 'help species survive for millions of years'. What assumptions is this claim making about the future? Is it fully justified? 5. (Create) Choose a new environment (e.g., deep ocean). Predict two structural adaptations an animal living there would need and explain the reasoning behind each.
6::structuraladaptations.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What does a polar bear's blubber do? (It keeps heat in and allows the bear to swim in icy water.)
2. (On) Why did giraffes evolve long necks? (To reach leaves high in tall trees and to spot danger early across the savanna.)
3. (On) What is convergent evolution? (When very different animals independently evolve the same structure to solve the same problem.)
4. (On) How does webbing help ducks and frogs? (It increases surface area so each paddle stroke pushes more water, making swimming easier.)
5. (Above) Why do you think structural adaptations take millions of years to develop rather than happening in one generation? (Because they are passed on genetically — only animals with a useful feature survive to reproduce, so the feature gradually becomes more common over many generations.)
6::structuraladaptations.graphic_organizer: Main Idea & Details Web (主旨细节网)
6::structuraladaptations.go_description: 中心为主旨、向外辐射五种动物结构适应特点的放射状图形组织器
6::structuraladaptations.go_usage: 中心写 "Structural Adaptations"，五支：Polar Bear/Giraffe/Camel/Duck/Beak-Claw，每支写 body part + function

6::howanimalschangetheirbehavior.title: How Animals Change Their Behavior
6::howanimalschangetheirbehavior.example_sentence: The squirrel showed me how it hid small nuts under the moss for winter.
6::howanimalschangetheirbehavior.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: show + object + how + embedded clause. The observed method changes.
The chef showed us how she prepared the dough before baking. / My father showed me how he repaired the bicycle step by step. / The guide showed the group how the river shaped the valley over time.

core_vocab.example: It waited until dusk to eat, because being active at night kept it safer from predators.
High above, a noisy flock of birds practised flapping and talked about migration.
We walked slowly together until we discovered a deep hollow under an old tree.
I wrote everything in a tiny journal and felt like a small scientist learning quickly.
High above, a noisy flock of birds practiced flapping and talked about migration.

6::howanimalschangetheirbehavior.core_vocab_raw: dusk, a flock of, hollow, journal, migration
6::howanimalschangetheirbehavior.text_7page:
Page 1: I woke in a soft bed of leaves and realized I was the size of a coin. A curious squirrel rushed over and showed me how it hid food for winter.
Page 2: It invited me to tuck small nuts under moss so they would stay safe and dry. I watched and copied its careful way of hiding each nut in a hole.
Page 3: Later I smelled sweet berries and found a large bear searching for a den. The bear asked, "Will you help me find a dry, warm place to sleep?"
Page 4: We walked slowly together until we discovered a deep hollow under an old tree. High above, a noisy flock of birds practiced flapping and talked about migration.
Page 5: They invited me to ride on wind and go south, but I could only watch. The birds explained that flying to warmer places helped them find food each winter.
Page 6: At night a shy rabbit appeared and showed me how it stayed hidden by day. It waited until dusk to eat, because being active at night kept it safer from predators.
Page 7: I wrote everything in a tiny journal and felt like a small scientist learning quickly. When morning came I woke in my bed, and the little journal stayed on my pillow.
6::howanimalschangetheirbehavior.pure_text: I woke in a soft bed of leaves and realized I was the size of a coin. A curious squirrel rushed over and showed me how it hid food for winter. It invited me to tuck small nuts under moss so they would stay safe and dry. I watched and copied its careful way of hiding each nut in a hole. Later I smelled sweet berries and found a large bear searching for a den. The bear asked, "Will you help me find a dry, warm place to sleep?" We walked slowly together until we discovered a deep hollow under an old tree. High above, a noisy flock of birds practiced flapping and talked about migration. They invited me to ride on wind and go south, but I could only watch. The birds explained that flying to warmer places helped them find food each winter. At night a shy rabbit appeared and showed me how it stayed hidden by day. It waited until dusk to eat, because being active at night kept it safer from predators. I wrote everything in a tiny journal and felt like a small scientist learning quickly. When morning came I woke in my bed, and the little journal stayed on my pillow.
6::howanimalschangetheirbehavior.reading_skill: 推理与结论 (Make Inferences and Draw Conclusions)
6::howanimalschangetheirbehavior.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The narrator is the 'size of a coin'. How does this perspective device change the reader's understanding of animal behaviours compared to a standard informational text? 2. (Analyze) Which animal behaviour (food storing, hibernation, migration, nocturnal activity) do you consider most significant for survival? Construct an argument using evidence from both this text and Book 4. 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) The journal 'stayed on my pillow' when the narrator woke up. Is this detail evidence that the experience was real, a dream, or something else? How does this ambiguity affect the text's meaning? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) Compare how this fiction text and the nonfiction Book 4 present the same information about animal behaviour. Which approach is more effective for learning? Support your view with specific examples. 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Write a diary entry for one of the animals in this story (squirrel, bear, or rabbit) describing the encounter with the tiny visitor from their own perspective.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What three animal behaviours does the narrator observe during the dream journey? 2. (Understand) Why does the rabbit wait until dusk to eat? Use evidence from the text. 3. (Analyse) How does the first-person narrator being 'the size of a coin' change your experience of reading about animal behaviour? 4. (Evaluate) The journal stays on the pillow when the narrator wakes up. Does this mean the experience was real? What interpretation do you find most convincing? 5. (Create) Write the first paragraph of a continuation: the narrator wakes up and shows the journal to a friend. What happens next?
6::howanimalschangetheirbehavior.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What was the narrator doing at the start of the story? (Waking up in a soft bed of leaves and realising they were the size of a coin.)
2. (On) What did the squirrel teach the narrator to do? (How to hide small nuts under moss to keep them safe and dry for winter.)
3. (On) Why does the rabbit wait until dusk to eat? (Because being active at night keeps it safer from predators.)
4. (On) Where was the little journal when the narrator woke up at the end? (Still on the pillow — just as it had been in the dream.)
5. (Above) What do you think each animal in this story represents about how living things adapt to their environment? (Each animal showed a different strategy — storing food, hibernating, migrating, or being nocturnal — all to survive the changing seasons.)
6::howanimalschangetheirbehavior.go_description: 深度分析故事主题、人物动机和情节发展的组织器
6::howanimalschangetheirbehavior.go_usage: 分析人物动机、主要冲突、转折点和主题意义

6::behavioraladaptations.title: Behavioral Adaptations
6::behavioraladaptations.example_sentence: Flying in V-formation reduces air resistance, so birds use less energy.
6::behavioraladaptations.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: gerund subject + reduces + noun, so + result. The action and the result change.
Wearing a helmet reduces the risk of injury, so cyclists are safer. / Using public transport reduces traffic, so city air becomes cleaner. / Stretching before exercise reduces injury risk, so athletes can train more safely.

core_vocab.example: Animals use behavioral adaptations to survive when weather, food, or threats change.
Flying in V-formation reduces air resistance so birds use less energy.
Many birds migrate thousands of kilometres each year to reach rich feeding grounds.
Many birds migrate thousands of kilometers each year to reach rich feeding grounds.
Hibernation lets animals like bears and ground squirrels survive long cold months with little food.

6::behavioraladaptations.core_vocab_raw: adaptation, air resistance, cooperate, feeding ground, hibernation
6::behavioraladaptations.text_7page:
Page 1: Animals use behavioral adaptations to survive when weather, food, or threats change. These actions help animals find food, save energy, and avoid dangers in nature.
Page 2: Migration is a seasonal movement that moves animals to better climates and food. Many birds migrate thousands of kilometers each year to reach rich feeding grounds.
Page 3: Some butterflies travel up to four thousand kilometers during migration each year. Flying in V-formation lowers air resistance so birds use less energy while flying.
Page 4: Hibernation lets animals like bears and ground squirrels survive long cold months with little food. A bear may hibernate for up to seven months and live without eating during that time.
Page 5: Teamwork is another important behavior for hunting, sharing food, or defending the group. Wolves, dolphins, and ants cooperate in nature to increase their chances of success.
Page 6: Camouflage helps animals blend into their surroundings and hide from predators more easily. Some insects look like leaves, and some fish can change color very quickly.
Page 7: Some behaviors are inherited, while others are learned by young animals over time. Humans can protect animals better by understanding these behavioral adaptations and their habitats.
6::behavioraladaptations.pure_text: Animals use behavioral adaptations to survive when weather, food, or threats change. These actions help animals find food, save energy, and avoid dangers in nature. Migration is a seasonal movement that moves animals to better climates and food sources. Many birds migrate thousands of kilometers each year to reach rich feeding grounds. Some butterflies travel up to four thousand kilometers during migration each year. Flying in V-formation lowers air resistance so birds use less energy while flying. Hibernation lets animals like bears and ground squirrels survive long cold months with little food. A bear may hibernate for up to seven months and live without eating during that time. Teamwork is another important behavior for hunting, sharing food, or defending the group. Wolves, dolphins, and ants cooperate in nature to increase their chances of success. Camouflage helps animals blend into their surroundings and hide from predators more easily. Some insects look like leaves, and some fish can change color very quickly. Some behaviors are inherited, while others are learned by young animals over time. Humans can protect animals better by understanding these behavioral adaptations and their habitats.
6::behavioraladaptations.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The text claims flying in V-formation 'lowers air resistance'. Evaluate this claim: what physical principle does it rely on and what additional information would you need to assess its accuracy fully? 2. (Analyze) How does the author distinguish between inherited and learned behaviours in the final paragraph? Why is this distinction scientifically important? 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) The text ends with the statement that humans 'can protect animals better by understanding these behavioural adaptations'. Evaluate this claim: is understanding sufficient, or are other factors required? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) Synthesise information from Books 2, 3, and 4: how do structural adaptations and behavioural adaptations work together to help an animal survive? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Choose one behaviour from the text (migration, hibernation, camouflage, or teamwork). Write a persuasive paragraph explaining why it is the most important survival behaviour, using evidence from the text.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What is the difference between inherited and learned behaviours? Give one example of each from the text. 2. (Understand) Explain how flying in V-formation helps birds conserve energy. 3. (Analyse) The text claims that understanding animal behaviour helps humans protect animals. What additional steps beyond understanding would actually be required? 4. (Evaluate) Of the four adaptations discussed (migration, hibernation, teamwork, camouflage), which do you think has the most parallels in human behaviour? Justify your answer. 5. (Create) Write a short persuasive paragraph for a wildlife charity, using information from this text to argue for protecting one specific animal behaviour.
6::behavioraladaptations.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name two examples of behavioural adaptation described in this passage. (Migration, hibernation, teamwork, camouflage, V-formation — any two.)
2. (On) How does flying in V-formation help birds? (It lowers air resistance so birds use less energy on long journeys.)
3. (On) What is the difference between an inherited and a learned behaviour? (Inherited behaviours are built into animals from birth; learned behaviours are developed through experience or by watching others.)
4. (On) How long can a bear hibernate during cold months? (Up to seven months without eating.)
5. (Above) Why do you think understanding animal behaviour can help humans protect endangered species more effectively? (Because if we know why and when animals migrate, hibernate, or cooperate, we can time conservation efforts to avoid disturbing critical survival moments.)
6::behavioraladaptations.go_description: 以四列呈现四种行为适应类型、代表动物和功能的表格型图形组织器
6::behavioraladaptations.go_usage: 四列：Migration/Hibernation/Teamwork/Camouflage，每列填 animal example + how it helps survival

6::gettoknowthemmore.title: Get to Know Them More
6::gettoknowthemmore.example_sentence: Tommy explained that sloths move very slowly and sleep for fifteen hours a day.
6::gettoknowthemmore.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: explain + that-clause. Present tense stays when the fact is still true.
Mia explained that dolphins use clicks to communicate with each other. / The teacher explained that the museum was closed on Sundays. / The guide explained that the volcano last erupted two hundred years ago.

core_vocab.example: Mr. Carter asked, "Why do dolphins leap? Are they playing, hunting, or communicating?"
Last Friday, Mia's class held an Animal Presentation Day and everyone felt excited.
Tommy explained that sloths move very slowly and sleep for about fifteen hours a day.
Everyone's idea sounded hopeful and practical, and everyone felt a new responsibility.
Everyone's idea sounded hopeful and practical, and everyone felt a new responsibility.

6::gettoknowthemmore.text_7page:
Page 1: Last Friday, Mia's class held an Animal Presentation Day and everyone felt excited. Each child chose a special animal to introduce to the whole class.
Page 2: Tommy explained that sloths move very slowly and sleep for about fifteen hours a day. His classmates laughed because sloths look lazy, but Tommy shared a surprising fact.
Page 3: "Sloths can turn their heads almost all the way around," Tommy said. His classmates were amazed and clapped when they heard this.
Page 4: Mia stood up with a bright poster and a short video ready to play. "I watched dolphins swim for hours at the zoo last summer. Dolphins are very smart and can remember people they meet."
Page 5: Mr. Carter asked, "Why do dolphins leap? Are they playing, hunting, or communicating?" Mia smiled and answered that dolphins leap to catch food, signal others, and play.
Page 6: Mr. Carter then asked the class, "How can humans help animals live safer lives?" Tommy replied, "We should provide animals with shelters and safer roads."
Page 7: Mia suggested protecting their habitats and others suggested cleaner beaches. Everyone's idea sounded hopeful and practical, and everyone felt a new sense of responsibility. By the end, they all agreed to learn more and act kindly toward animals.
6::gettoknowthemmore.pure_text: Last Friday, Mia's class held an Animal Presentation Day and everyone felt excited. Each child chose a special animal to introduce to the whole class. Tommy explained that sloths move very slowly and sleep for about fifteen hours a day. His classmates laughed because sloths look lazy, but Tommy shared a surprising fact. "Sloths can turn their heads almost all the way around," Tommy said. His classmates were amazed and clapped when they heard this. Mia stood up with a bright poster and a short video ready to play. "I watched dolphins swim for hours at the zoo last summer. Dolphins are very smart and can remember people they meet." Mr. Carter asked, "Why do dolphins leap? Are they playing, hunting, or communicating?" Mia smiled and answered that dolphins leap to catch food, signal others, and play. Mr. Carter then asked the class, "How can humans help animals live safer lives?" Tommy replied, "We should provide animals with shelters and safer roads." Mia suggested protecting their habitats and others suggested cleaner beaches. Everyone's idea sounded hopeful and practical, and everyone felt a new sense of responsibility. By the end, they all agreed to learn more and act kindly toward animals.
6::gettoknowthemmore.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The class presentation format reveals information through character dialogue rather than direct exposition. How does this narrative choice affect the reader's engagement with the animal facts? 2. (Analyze) Mr. Carter asks whether dolphins leap to play, hunt, or communicate. Mia answers that they do all three. What does this response suggest about the complexity of animal behaviour? 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) The class ends by discussing how humans can help animals. Evaluate the specific suggestions made (shelters, safer roads, habitat protection, cleaner beaches). Which do you consider most impactful and why? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) Compare the way animal facts are presented in this fiction story with the way they are presented in Book 8 (Do You Know). Which approach is more effective for a reader who wants to remember the information? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Choose one animal not mentioned in this text. Write a short presentation script (3-4 sentences) in the style of Tommy or Mia, including one surprising fact and one way humans can help.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What surprising fact did Tommy share about sloths? 2. (Understand) Mia says dolphins 'leap to catch food, signal others, and play'. Why is this a more complete answer than just one reason? 3. (Analyse) How does the class presentation format help students learn more effectively than simply reading facts? 4. (Evaluate) Which suggestion for helping animals (shelters, safer roads, habitat protection, cleaner beaches) do you consider most urgent and most achievable? Justify your ranking. 5. (Create) Prepare a two-sentence 'Did you know?' fact about an animal of your choice that would fit naturally into this class presentation.
6::gettoknowthemmore.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What animal did Tommy choose to present and what is one surprising fact about it? (A sloth — it can turn its head almost all the way around, or it sleeps fifteen hours a day.)
2. (On) What animal did Mia present and what did she say about dolphins? (Dolphins — that they are very smart and can remember people they meet.)
3. (On) Why do dolphins leap according to Mia's answer? (To catch food, signal others, and play.)
4. (On) What did Mr Carter ask the class about at the end of the presentation? (How humans can help animals live safer lives.)
5. (Above) Why do you think learning about animals through presentations is more engaging than just reading a textbook? (Because it involves listening to peers, hearing surprising facts, and discussing questions together, which makes the information more memorable.)
6::gettoknowthemmore.go_description: 呈现不同角色汇报动物知识及反应的图形组织器
6::gettoknowthemmore.go_usage: 分支对应 Tommy (sloths)、Mia (dolphins)、Mr Carter (questions)，各写出关键信息

6::wonderfulanimals.title: Wonderful Animals
6::wonderfulanimals.example_sentence: Cheetahs have a flexible spine, which makes their stride longer and faster.
6::wonderfulanimals.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: non-defining relative 'which makes + noun + adjective'. The preceding clause and the resulting quality both change.
The library stays open late, which makes studying after school much easier. / The new road was built, which made the journey much quicker for everyone. / The team trained every day, which made their performance far stronger.

core_vocab.example: They are also intelligent; they open jars and use simple tools to get food.
Each animal uses its special ability in different ways to survive in changing habitats.
Chameleons change color to communicate and to control their body temperature quickly.
Cheetahs are the fastest land animals and reach about sixty miles per hour.
They have long legs and a flexible spine that help them accelerate quickly.

6::wonderfulanimals.text_7page:
Page 1: Animals can do amazing things that surprise us and spark our curiosity about nature. Here are four wonderful examples showing nature's diversity and clever designs today.
Page 2: Chameleons change color to communicate and to control their body temperature quickly. Their skin contains special cells and layers that reflect light and shift color.
Page 3: Cheetahs are the fastest land animals and reach about sixty miles per hour. They have long legs and a flexible spine that help them accelerate quickly.
Page 4: Octopuses spray dark ink and hide in small holes to escape predators. They are also intelligent; they open jars and use simple tools to get food.
Page 5: If an octopus loses an arm, it can regrow a new one over time. The Arctic tern makes the longest migration of any bird on Earth.
Page 6: It travels up to seventy thousand kilometres each year, nearly circling the planet twice. Each animal uses its special ability in different ways to survive in changing habitats.
Page 7: Scientists study these skills and sometimes gain ideas for new technology and medicine. Learning about animals helps us respect nature and protect their homes for the future.
6::wonderfulanimals.pure_text: Animals can do amazing things that surprise us and spark our curiosity about nature. Here are four wonderful examples showing nature's diversity and clever designs.
