Chameleons change color to communicate and to control their body temperature quickly. Their skin contains special cells and layers that reflect light and shift color. Cheetahs are the fastest land animals and can reach about sixty miles per hour. They have long legs and a flexible spine that help them accelerate quickly. Octopuses spray dark ink and hide in small holes to escape predators. They are also intelligent; they open jars and use simple tools to get food. If an octopus loses an arm, it can regrow a new one over time. The Arctic tern makes the longest migration of any bird on Earth. It travels up to seventy thousand kilometres each year, nearly circling the planet twice. Each animal uses its special ability in different ways to survive in changing habitats. Scientists study these skills and sometimes gain ideas for new technology and medicine. Learning about animals helps us respect nature and protect their homes for the future.
6::wonderfulanimals.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The text presents four very different animals (chameleon, cheetah, octopus, Arctic tern). What single underlying principle connects all four examples and supports the text's main argument? 2. (Analyze) The octopus section includes the claim 'they open jars and use simple tools'. What does this tell us about the definition of intelligence? Is tool use a reliable measure of intelligence? Discuss. 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) The author states 'scientists study these skills and sometimes gain ideas for new technology'. This process is called biomimicry. Find one example from the text where an animal ability could inspire a technological application and explain how. 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) The text uses the phrase 'nature's diversity and clever designs'. Critically examine this phrase: in what sense can evolution be described as 'design'? What are the limitations of this metaphor? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Research challenge: The text mentions Arctic terns travel 70,000 km per year. Calculate roughly how many km per day this is, assuming they travel for six months. What does this number reveal about the physical demands of migration?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What is unique about how the Arctic tern migrates? 2. (Understand) Explain in your own words how a chameleon changes colour. Is it primarily for camouflage? 3. (Analyse) The text says scientists 'gain ideas for new technology' from animals. Give one specific idea from the text where an animal ability could inspire technology. 4. (Evaluate) 'Animals are better adapted to their environments than any human technology.' Do you agree? Use evidence from the text and your own knowledge. 5. (Create) Write a 'Did you know?' sentence about one of the four animals in the text, in the style of Book 8.
6::wonderfulanimals.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name the four animals described in this passage. (Chameleon, cheetah, octopus, and Arctic tern.)
2. (On) How does a chameleon change colour? (By using special cells in its skin that reflect light differently.)
3. (On) How far does the Arctic tern travel each year? (Up to seventy thousand kilometres.)
4. (On) What can an octopus do if it loses an arm? (It can regrow a new one over time.)
5. (Above) Why do you think scientists study animal abilities when designing new technology? (Because millions of years of evolution have already solved many engineering problems — copying nature's solutions is often more efficient than starting from scratch.)
6::wonderfulanimals.graphic_organizer: Main Idea & Details Web (主旨细节网)
6::wonderfulanimals.go_description: 中心为主旨、向外辐射四种神奇动物能力的放射状图形组织器
6::wonderfulanimals.go_usage: 中心写 "Wonderful Animals"，四支：Chameleon/Cheetah/Octopus/Arctic Tern，每支写 special ability + why it matters

6::myfavoritepet.title: My Favorite Pet
6::myfavoritepet.example_sentence: Why don't you care for Mrs Lee's cat for a month first?
6::myfavoritepet.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: Why don't you + base verb — a suggestion form.
Why don't you try a smaller pet first? / Why don't you speak to the teacher after class? / Why don't you ask your parents for advice?

core_vocab.example: Amy's parents saw her effort and agreed she could adopt a cat that suited their home.
A dog needs walks, training, and lots of time for play and affection.
Tommy suggested, "Why don't you care for Mrs Lee's cat for one month?"
Fish are beautiful in tanks, need clean water, and careful feeding schedules.
A cat is quiet sometimes, loves to climb, and enjoys a clean litter box.

6::myfavoritepet.core_vocab_raw: adopt, affection, care for, feeding schedule, litter box
6::myfavoritepet.text_7page:
Page 1: Sasha and Tommy were good friends who often made plans together after school. One day Sasha told Tommy she really wanted to have a pet.
Page 2: Tommy gave four choices: a cat, a friendly dog, a bright fish, and a rabbit. Tommy described each animal and what care it might need at home.
Page 3: A cat is quiet sometimes, loves to climb, and enjoys a clean litter box. A dog needs walks, training, and lots of time for play and affection.
Page 4: Fish are beautiful in tanks but need clean water and careful feeding schedules. A rabbit is soft, likes to hop, and needs space for daily exercise.
Page 5: They talked about who would help at home and who had the time. Sasha felt worried because her parents often worked late and returned tired.
Page 6: Tommy suggested, "Why don't you care for Mrs Lee's cat for one month?" Sasha agreed and spent each afternoon that month visiting Mrs Lee to feed, clean, and play with the cat.
Page 7: She learned to be patient when the cat hid or scratched sometimes. Sasha's parents saw her effort and agreed she could adopt a cat that suited their home. Sasha smiled because she had learned responsibility, patience, and how to care kindly.
6::myfavoritepet.pure_text: Sasha and Tommy were good friends who often made plans together after school. One day Sasha told Tommy she really wanted to have a pet. Tommy gave four choices: a cat, a friendly dog, a bright fish, and a rabbit. Tommy described each animal and what care it might need at home. A cat is quiet sometimes, loves to climb, and enjoys a clean litter box. A dog needs walks, training, and lots of time for play and affection. Fish are beautiful in tanks but need clean water and careful feeding schedules. A rabbit is soft, likes to hop, and needs space for daily exercise. They talked about who would help at home and who had the time. Sasha felt worried because her parents often worked late and returned tired. Tommy suggested, "Why don't you care for Mrs. Lee's cat for one month?" Sasha agreed and spent each afternoon that month visiting Mrs. Lee to feed, clean, and play with the cat. She learned to be patient when the cat hid or scratched sometimes. Sasha’s parents saw her effort and agreed she could adopt a cat that suited their home. Sasha smiled because she had learned responsibility, patience, and how to care kindly.
6::myfavoritepet.reading_strategy: 进行预测 (Making and Confirming Predictions)
6::myfavoritepet.reading_skill: 问题与解决方案 (Problem and Solution)
6::myfavoritepet.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) Tommy lists four pets with their care requirements. How does presenting the information this way prepare Amy to make a more informed decision? Is listing advantages and disadvantages always the best decision-making strategy? 2. (Analyze) Amy's worry is that her parents work late and return tired. How does Tommy's suggestion (caring for a neighbour's cat for a month) address this specific concern in a practical and low-risk way? 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) The story argues that responsibility and patience are learned through caring for a pet. Do you agree that caring for an animal is an effective way to develop these qualities? What might be the risks or limitations? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) Consider the perspective of Mrs. Lee's cat. Using evidence from the story, evaluate whether the arrangement (a child caring for the cat each afternoon) was likely to be beneficial, neutral, or stressful for the animal. 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Write a set of guidelines for a child who wants to convince their parents to let them adopt a pet. Base your guidelines on the approach Amy took in this story.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What four pets did Tommy suggest and what is one care requirement for each? 2. (Understand) Why was Amy worried about getting a pet? Was her concern reasonable? 3. (Analyse) How does Tommy's suggestion of a one-month trial solve Amy's problem without fully committing? What principle of problem-solving does this demonstrate? 4. (Evaluate) Amy's parents agreed to an adoption because they 'saw her effort'. Is effort alone sufficient evidence that someone is ready for a long-term responsibility? 5. (Create) Write a checklist of five questions a family should ask themselves before adopting a pet. Base your checklist on the considerations discussed in the story.
6::myfavoritepet.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What four pets did Tommy suggest Amy could consider? (A cat, a dog, a fish, and a rabbit.)
2. (On) Why was Amy worried about getting a pet? (Her parents often worked late and returned tired, so she was not sure who would help care for it.)
3. (On) What did Tommy suggest Amy do first before adopting a pet? (Care for Mrs Lee's cat for one month to test her commitment.)
4. (On) What happened after Amy spent a month caring for the neighbour's cat? (Her parents saw her effort and agreed she could adopt a cat that suited their home.)
5. (Above) Why do you think trying something on a trial basis — like caring for someone else's pet — is a good way to make an important decision? (Because it lets you experience the real responsibilities before fully committing, which prevents regret and teaches you what is actually involved.)
6::myfavoritepet.go_description: 以故事要素呈现人物、问题和结局的图形组织器
6::myfavoritepet.go_usage: 记录：Setting → Amy wants a pet → Tommy suggests trial → Amy cares for Mrs Lee's cat → Parents agree → Adoption

6::doyouknow.title: Do You Know
6::doyouknow.example_sentence: Did you know that bees perform a waggle dance to show other bees where flowers are?
6::doyouknow.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: Did you know + that + statement word order (no question inversion after 'that').
Did you know that elephants can recognise themselves in a mirror? / Did you know that the Earth takes 365.25 days to go around the Sun? / Did you know that some fish can change colour to hide from predators?

core_vocab.example: Some frogs survive freezing by producing natural chemicals that act like antifreeze.
Did you know hummingbirds can fly backward and hover in front of flowers like tiny helicopters?
Did you know a sea star can regrow a lost arm and sometimes even its whole body?
Did you know some turtles and tortoises can live for more than a hundred years?
Bees perform a waggle dance to tell other bees the direction and distance to flowers.

6::doyouknow.core_vocab_raw: natural chemical, hover, regrow, tortoise, waggle dance
6::doyouknow.text_7page:
Page 1: Did you know that animals have surprising skills that many people never expect? Did you know elephants use their long trunks to drink, smell, touch, and even hug baby elephants?
Page 2: Did you know a sea star can regrow a lost arm and sometimes even its whole body? Did you know octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and clever minds that can solve puzzles?
Page 3: Did you know some turtles and tortoises can live for more than a hundred years? Did you know whales use very low sounds that travel for many miles across oceans?
Page 4: Did you know hummingbirds can fly backward and hover in front of flowers like tiny helicopters? Did you know cheetahs are the fastest land animals and reach very high speeds in just a few seconds?
Page 5: Did you know bees perform a waggle dance to tell other bees the direction and distance to flowers? Did you know some frogs survive freezing by producing natural chemicals that act like antifreeze?
Page 6: Did you know kangaroos cannot walk backward, which is why they represent always moving forward in Australia? Did you know a dog's wet nose helps it detect smells better and also helps cool its body?
Page 7: These amazing facts show nature's clever solutions to survival. Learning about animals helps us respect and protect the natural world around us.
6::doyouknow.pure_text: Did you know that animals have surprising skills that many people never expect? Did you know elephants use their long trunks to drink, smell, touch, and even hug baby elephants? Did you know a sea star can regrow a lost arm and sometimes even its whole body? Did you know octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and clever minds that can solve puzzles? Did you know some turtles and tortoises can live for more than a hundred years? Did you know whales use very low sounds that travel for many miles across oceans? Did you know hummingbirds can fly backward and hover in front of flowers like tiny helicopters? Did you know cheetahs are the fastest land animals and reach very high speeds in just a few seconds? Did you know bees perform a waggle dance to tell other bees the direction and distance to flowers? Did you know some frogs survive freezing by producing natural chemicals that act like antifreeze? Did you know kangaroos cannot walk backward, which is why they represent always moving forward in Australia? Did you know a dog's wet nose helps it detect smells better and also helps cool its body? These amazing facts show nature's clever solutions to survival. Learning about animals helps us respect and protect the natural world around us.
6::doyouknow.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The text uses 'Did you know…?' questions to present every fact. Analyse why this structural choice is effective for a nonfiction text about surprising information. 2. (Analyze) Evaluate three facts from the text: identify which are most scientifically significant, which are most likely to be memorable for readers, and whether these two categories overlap or differ. 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) The final sentence states that readers 'will probably notice animals differently now'. Is changing a reader's perception a legitimate purpose for a nonfiction text? What does this reveal about the author's intent? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) Two animals in this text (cheetah and octopus) also appear in Book 6. Compare how the same animals are described across both texts. What does each text emphasise and why? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Choose three facts from the text and rank them from 'most surprising' to 'least surprising'. Then write a paragraph explaining your ranking criteria and what it reveals about your prior knowledge of animals.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name three animals mentioned and one surprising fact about each. 2. (Understand) The text says bees perform a 'waggle dance'. What information does this dance communicate and why is this system remarkable? 3. (Analyse) The final sentence says readers 'will probably notice animals differently now'. What specific change in perception is the author hoping for? Is this a realistic expectation? 4. (Evaluate) Choose the fact you find most scientifically significant (not just most surprising). Explain why it matters beyond being an interesting curiosity. 5. (Create) Add a new 'Did you know?' sentence about an animal not in the text. Your sentence must be factually accurate and written in the same style as the other questions.
6::doyouknow.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What is one unusual fact about octopuses from this passage? (They have three hearts, blue blood, and can open jars — any one.)
2. (On) What is a waggle dance and what information does it communicate? (A dance that bees perform to tell other bees the direction and distance to flowers.)
3. (On) What ability do sea stars have that most animals do not? (They can regrow a lost arm and sometimes even their whole body.)
4. (On) How do some frogs survive freezing temperatures? (By producing natural chemicals that act like antifreeze in their bodies.)
5. (Above) Why do you think these 'Did you know?' facts make people more interested in protecting animals and their habitats? (Because when people are amazed by an animal's abilities, they feel more connected to it and more motivated to ensure it is not lost.)
6::doyouknow.go_description: 以放射状结构呈现多个动物趣味知识的图形组织器
6::doyouknow.go_usage: 中心写 "Did You Know?"，每支写一个 Did you know 事实（共7支），最外侧写 why it matters

6::ilovefestivals.title: I Love Festivals
6::ilovefestivals.example_sentence: Mia felt happier every time her house smelled of spices and fresh baking.
6::ilovefestivals.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: every time + clause for repeated emotional responses.
He felt more nervous every time he walked onto the stage to perform. / She felt calmer every time she listened to music before bed. / They felt prouder every time another person joined their project.

core_vocab.example: They planned paper chains, paper lanterns and a banner saying "Happy Friday!"
Their class decided to prepare decorations and invite parents to a small party on Friday afternoon.
They planned paper chains, paper lanterns, and a banner for the party.
She felt happier every time her house smelled of spices and fresh baking.
At the end Tommy told Mia she had been a great leader, and she blushed.

6::ilovefestivals.text_7page:
Page 1: Mia loved festivals because they brought food, music and family together at home. She felt happier every time her house smelled of spices and fresh baking.
Page 2: At school, Mia and Tommy were asked to share their favourite festival activities. Mia said she liked making lanterns and dancing with her cousins every year.
Page 3: Tommy described the food his grandmother cooked, and the songs everyone sang together. Their class decided to prepare decorations and invite parents to a small party on Friday afternoon.
Page 4: They planned paper chains, paper lanterns and a banner saying "Happy Friday!" On Thursday they practised folding paper while the teacher played soft festive music.
Page 5: Suddenly they realised the banner was too short because they had cut the paper wrong. If it fell, the decorations would look messy and everyone might feel disappointed.
Page 6: Mia suggested they join the chains and make a longer banner before the party. Tommy and the others worked quickly after school, cutting and gluing until the banner was finished.
Page 7: On Friday afternoon the classroom looked brighter than anyone had expected when parents arrived. Parents smiled and clapped when they saw the colourful decorations and happy children. Mia felt proud and learnt that festivals are about making memories, not only food. At the end Tommy told Mia she had been a great leader, and she smiled.
6::ilovefestivals.pure_text: Mia loved festivals because they brought food, music and family together at home. She felt happier every time her house smelled of spices and fresh baking. At school, Mia and Tommy were asked to share their favourite festival activities. Mia said she liked making lanterns and dancing with her cousins every year. Tommy described the food his grandmother cooked, and the songs everyone sang together. Their class decided to prepare decorations and invite parents to a small party on Friday afternoon. They planned paper chains, paper lanterns and a banner saying "Happy Friday!" On Thursday they practised folding paper while the teacher played soft festive music. Suddenly they realised the banner was too short because they had cut the paper wrong. If it fell, the decorations would look messy and everyone might feel disappointed. Mia suggested they join the chains and make a longer banner before the party. Tommy and the others worked quickly after school, cutting and gluing until the banner was finished. On Friday afternoon the classroom looked brighter than anyone had expected when parents arrived. Parents smiled and clapped when they saw the colourful decorations and happy children. Mia felt proud and learnt that festivals are about making memories, not only food. At the end Tommy told Mia she had been a great leader, and she smiled.
6::ilovefestivals.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The banner problem occurs because the class 'had cut the paper wrong'. How does this practical mistake create an opportunity to explore a deeper theme in the story? 2. (Analyze) Mia's solution 'join the chains and make a longer banner' solves a physical problem and also demonstrates a leadership skill. What leadership quality does this solution reveal and how does it connect to the story's theme? 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) Tommy is told by Mia that she has been 'a great leader'. Evaluate this assessment: based on evidence from the text, what specific actions qualify as leadership in this story? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) The story moves from individual sharing (Mia and Tommy's festival memories) to a collective problem (the banner) to a collaborative solution. How does this narrative arc reinforce the idea that festivals bring people together? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) The class planned a 'Happy Friday' party rather than a specific religious or national festival. Why might a school choose a neutral celebration rather than a cultural one? What are the advantages and potential disadvantages of this approach?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What was the problem with the banner and how did Mia solve it? 2. (Understand) Why did the class decide to prepare decorations and invite parents rather than have a traditional festival celebration? 3. (Analyse) How does the banner problem function as more than just a practical obstacle? What does it allow the story to show about teamwork and creativity? 4. (Evaluate) Tommy says Mia 'has been a great leader'. Based on the story, do you agree? What specific leadership actions justify or challenge this assessment? 5. (Create) Design one more decoration for the classroom party that reflects a different cultural tradition not already mentioned. Describe it and explain why it would be an appropriate addition.
6::ilovefestivals.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Why did Mia feel happier every time her house smelled of spices and baking? (Because festivals brought food, music, and family together, and those smells reminded her of those happy moments.)
2. (On) What went wrong with the banner the class was making? (It was too short because they had cut the paper wrong.)
3. (On) How did Mia solve the banner problem? (She suggested joining the paper chains together to make a longer banner.)
4. (On) What did Tommy tell Mia at the end of the party? (That she had been a great leader.)
5. (Above) Why do you think festivals are described as being about 'making memories, not only food'? (Because the activities, laughter, and shared effort create emotional connections that last much longer than the food itself.)
6::ilovefestivals.go_description: 以线性结构呈现故事关键事件的图形组织器
6::ilovefestivals.go_usage: 依序记录：Mia loves festivals → Class plans party → Banner too short → Problem solved → Parents arrive → Proud feeling

6::winterfestivals.title: Winter Festivals
6::winterfestivals.example_sentence: If you visit these celebrations, you will feel warmth and hear wonderful music.
6::winterfestivals.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: first conditional — If + present simple, will + base verb.
If you study every day, you will improve your English quickly. / If the team trains hard, they will be ready for the match. / If we recycle more, we will produce less waste.

core_vocab.example: Winter festivals show how lights, food, and family create lasting memories for people.
They eat fried foods like latkes and play with spinning dreidels with children.
Families exchange gifts, sing carols, and often share a roast dinner after church.
Winter festivals bring light and warmth to cold nights in many different countries.
Winter festivals show how lights, food, and family create lasting memories for people.

6::winterfestivals.core_vocab_raw: create lasting memories, dreidel, exchange, warmth, millions of
6::winterfestivals.text_7page:
Page 1: Winter festivals bring light and warmth to cold nights in many different countries. People celebrate with lights, special food, songs, and family gatherings during this season.
Page 2: In Christmas celebrations, decorated trees glow in homes and in public squares. Families exchange gifts, sing carols, and often share a roast dinner with family.
Page 3: Chinese New Year usually starts in late winter and often continues for fifteen days. Colorful lanterns and dragon dances make streets brighter and full of movement.
Page 4: People visit relatives, give red envelopes, and eat dumplings and rice cakes. Hanukkah lasts eight nights, and Jewish families light a menorah each evening.
Page 5: They eat fried foods like latkes and play with spinning dreidels with children. In many cities, people decorate streets and buildings with millions of tiny lights.
Page 6: One famous public display once used more than one million bulbs on a single tree. If you visit these celebrations, you will feel warmth, music, and busy streets.
Page 7: A fun fact is that many festivals mix old stories with new decorations today. Winter festivals show how lights, food, and family create lasting memories for people.
6::winterfestivals.pure_text: Winter festivals bring light and warmth to cold nights in many different countries. People celebrate with lights, special food, songs, and family gatherings during this season. In Christmas celebrations, decorated trees glow in homes and in public squares. Families exchange gifts, sing carols, and often share a roast dinner with their family. Chinese New Year usually starts in late winter and often continues for fifteen days. Colorful lanterns and dragon dances make streets brighter and full of movement. People visit relatives, give red envelopes, and eat dumplings and rice cakes. Hanukkah lasts eight nights, and Jewish families light a menorah each evening. They eat fried foods like latkes and play with spinning dreidels with children. In many cities, people decorate streets and buildings with millions of tiny lights. One famous public display once used more than one million bulbs on a single tree. If you visit these celebrations, you will feel warmth, music, and busy streets. A fun fact is that many festivals mix old stories with new decorations today. Winter festivals show how lights, food, and family create lasting memories for people.
6::winterfestivals.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The text presents Christmas, Chinese New Year, and Hanukkah. Analyse how the author balances the coverage of three different cultural traditions: does each receive equal depth, and does this balance matter? 2. (Analyze) The text states 'many festivals mix old stories with new decorations today'. What does this observation suggest about the relationship between tradition and change? Is mixing old and new a positive or negative development? 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) Evaluate the first-conditional sentence 'If you visit these celebrations, you will feel warmth, music, and busy streets'. Is this claim universally true? What factors might determine whether a visitor actually experiences warmth and welcome? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) The text was written for an international audience that may include students from very different cultural backgrounds. How successfully does the author navigate the challenge of presenting religious and cultural celebrations without excluding or offending any reader? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Compare two of the three festivals described. Create a Venn diagram showing what they share and what makes each unique. Then write one sentence arguing that despite their differences, both celebrations serve the same fundamental human purpose.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name three winter festivals from the text and one key activity associated with each. 2. (Understand) The text says festivals 'mix old stories with new decorations'. Give one example of this mixing from the text or from your own knowledge. 3. (Analyse) The author chooses to present Christmas, Chinese New Year, and Hanukkah. What do these three choices have in common and what important winter celebrations might be missing from the list? 4. (Evaluate) Is it possible to fully respect and appreciate a festival from a culture or religion different from your own? What would respectful participation look like? 5. (Create) Research one winter festival not mentioned in the text. Write two sentences in the style of this text that could be added to the passage.
6::winterfestivals.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name three winter festivals described in this passage. (Christmas, Chinese New Year, and Hanukkah.)
2. (On) What do people do during Hanukkah each evening? (They light a candle on the menorah and eat fried foods like latkes while playing with dreidels.)
3. (On) What happens during Chinese New Year celebrations on the streets? (Colourful lanterns and dragon dances make the streets brighter and full of movement.)
4. (On) What does the passage say many festivals mix together? (Old stories with new decorations.)
5. (Above) Why do you think light is such a common symbol in winter festivals across different cultures? (Because in winter the days are short and dark, so bringing light — candles, lanterns, or lights — symbolises hope, warmth, and celebration in the coldest time of year.)
6::winterfestivals.go_description: 以三列对比呈现圣诞、春节、光明节三个冬季节日特点的表格型图形组织器
6::winterfestivals.go_usage: 三列：Christmas/Chinese New Year/Hanukkah，每列填 when + key activities + food/symbol

6::fourfamiliesfourcelebrations.title: Four Families, Four Celebrations
6::fourfamiliesfourcelebrations.example_sentence: Rina offered to teach a traditional song to the whole class.
6::fourfamiliesfourcelebrations.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: offer + to-infinitive (volunteering to do something).
Tommy offered to bring paper gifts for everyone. / She offered to design the poster and present it. / He offered to carry the boxes and help set up the tables.

core_vocab.example: Amir added quietly, "After we sing and pray, my family shares sweet pastries and hot tea."
Amir said each child could bring one small instrument to play in a music circle.
Mrs. Perez listened with a warm face, but then she mentioned an important school allergy rule.
Rina smiled and said, "We light small candles, give thanks, and remember our ancestors with songs."
Mia suggested a solution: "If we cannot share food, we will bring photos and our family recipes."

6::fourfamiliesfourcelebrations.text_7page:
Page 1: Mrs. Perez asked four students to present a family celebration to the whole class today.
Page 2: Mia began: "Every winter our family makes dumplings and we sing about good luck together." Rina smiled and said, "We light small candles, give thanks, and remember our ancestors with songs."
Page 3: Luca laughed: "At our festival we hide little gifts, then everyone searches the house together." Amir added quietly, "After we sing, my family shares sweet pastries and warm tea together."
Page 4: Mrs. Perez listened with a warm face, but then she mentioned an important school allergy rule.
Page 5: She explained that shared snacks might be banned this term to keep every student safe. The class grew quiet because food was central to many family plans and memories.
Page 6: Mia suggested a solution: "If we cannot share food, we will bring photos and our family recipes." Rina offered to teach a safe light display and a short traditional song to everyone.
Page 7: Luca proposed hiding paper gifts in class and telling quick stories about their meaning. Amir said each child could bring one small instrument to play in a music circle. Mrs. Perez liked the ideas and approved the plan because it kept students safe and kept the celebrations true.
6::fourfamiliesfourcelebrations.pure_text: Mrs. Perez asked four students to present a family celebration to the whole class today. Mia began: "Every winter our family makes dumplings and we sing about good luck together." Rina smiled and said, "We light small candles, give thanks, and remember our ancestors with songs." Luca laughed: "At our festival we hide little gifts, then everyone searches the house together." Amir added quietly, "After we sing, my family shares sweet pastries and warm tea together." Mrs. Perez listened with a warm face, but then she mentioned an important school allergy rule. She explained that shared snacks might be banned this term to keep every student safe. The class grew quiet because food was central to many family plans and memories. Mia suggested a solution: "If we cannot share food, we will bring photos and our family recipes." Rina offered to teach a safe light display and a short traditional song to everyone. Luca proposed hiding paper gifts in class and telling quick stories about their meaning. Amir said each child could bring one small instrument to play in a music circle. Mrs. Perez liked the ideas and approved the plan because it kept students safe and kept the celebrations true.
6::fourfamiliesfourcelebrations.reading_skill: 推理与结论 (Make Inferences and Draw Conclusions)
6::fourfamiliesfourcelebrations.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The school allergy rule creates a conflict between safety and cultural expression. How does the story resolve this conflict and does the resolution fully satisfy both needs? What might be lost in the compromise? 2. (Analyze) Mia, Rina, Luca, and Amir each represent different cultural traditions. How does the author ensure that no single cultural tradition is presented as more important or authentic than the others? 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) Evaluate Mia's solution ('If we cannot share food, we will bring photos and our family recipes'). Is this an example of creative problem-solving, a reasonable compromise, or an inadequate substitute for the original plan? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) The story implicitly argues that cultural diversity enriches a classroom community. Do you agree? What challenges can cultural diversity also create, and how might they be addressed constructively? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Imagine you are Mrs. Perez. Write a letter to parents explaining the decision to adapt the celebration plans. Your letter must acknowledge the allergy concern, respect cultural values, and explain the alternative approach positively.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What were the four different family celebrations described by Mia, Rina, Luca, and Amir? 2. (Understand) Why did the school allergy rule create a problem for the class party? How serious was this problem? 3. (Analyse) Mia's solution involves bringing photos and recipes instead of food. What does 'food' symbolise in this context and what does the substitute preserve or fail to preserve? 4. (Evaluate) Mrs. Perez approved the plan 'because it kept students safe and kept the celebrations true'. Is keeping celebrations 'true' compatible with removing their most central element (food)? 5. (Create) Imagine you are one of the four students. Write a short journal entry after the class event: what worked well, what felt like a compromise, and what would you do differently next year?
6::fourfamiliesfourcelebrations.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What did Mia's family do at their winter celebration? (Made dumplings and sang about good luck together.)
2. (On) What problem did Mrs Perez mention that affected the class party plans? (A school allergy rule meant shared snacks might be banned to keep students safe.)
3. (On) What solution did Mia suggest? (If they could not share food, they would bring photos and family recipes instead.)
4. (On) What did Amir offer to bring to share his culture? (A small instrument to play in a music circle.)
5. (Above) Why do you think Mrs Perez approved the alternative plan even though it did not include food? (Because it still kept the spirit of sharing culture and celebration alive while keeping all students safe — a compromise that respected both safety and cultural expression.)
6::fourfamiliesfourcelebrations.go_description: 呈现问题与多种解决方案对应关系的图形组织器
6::fourfamiliesfourcelebrations.go_usage: 左写 Problem (allergy rule)，右写 4 Solutions (photos, light display, paper gifts, music circle)

6::wecelebrate.title: We Celebrate
6::wecelebrate.example_sentence: Many festivals bring families together to share meals and remember their history.
6::wecelebrate.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: bring + object + together + to-infinitive (purpose).
School sports days bring students together to celebrate fitness and teamwork. / Community events bring neighbours together to enjoy music and share food. / Book clubs bring readers together to discuss ideas and discover new stories.

core_vocab.example: Some festivals remember important events, like victories or the lives of leaders.
People celebrate festivals for many different reasons that go back many years.
Some festivals remember important events, like victories or the lives of leaders.
People also celebrate to send blessings, ask for luck, or start a new year.
Think: if you attend two festivals every month you will see twenty-four festivals.

6::wecelebrate.text_7page:
Page 1: People celebrate festivals for many different reasons that go back many years. Some festivals remember important events, like victories or the lives of leaders.
Page 2: Other festivals thank nature and farmers for food after a hard season. Many festivals bring families together to share meals and stories late into night.
Page 3: People also celebrate to send blessings, ask for luck, or start a new year. Different countries have different ways to celebrate that feel special and bright.
Page 4: In China families eat special food and enjoy lanterns during the Lunar New Year. In India Diwali fills streets with lights, sweets, and prayers for hope and luck.
Page 5: In Mexico Day of the Dead remembers ancestors with colorful altars and shared food. People also light candles, dance, play music, and give small gifts during festivals.
Page 6: Some cities hold more than one hundred festivals a year, a surprising fact. Think: if you attend two festivals every month you will see twenty-four festivals.
Page 7: Festivals help people feel closer and keep stories and traditions alive for children. When you travel, you might find a new celebration that becomes your favorite. We should enjoy the fun but also remember the reasons behind each festival.
6::wecelebrate.pure_text: People celebrate festivals for many different reasons that go back many years. Some festivals remember important events, like victories or the lives of leaders. Other festivals thank nature and farmers for food after a hard season. Many festivals bring families together to share meals and stories late into night. People also celebrate to send blessings, ask for luck, or start a new year. Different countries have different ways to celebrate that feel special and bright. In China families eat special food and enjoy lanterns during the Lunar New Year. In India Diwali fills streets with lights, sweets, and prayers for hope and luck. In Mexico Day of the Dead remembers ancestors with colorful altars and shared food. People also light candles, dance, play music, and give small gifts during festivals. Some cities hold more than one hundred festivals a year, a surprising fact. Think: if you attend two festivals every month you will see twenty-four festivals. Festivals help people feel closer and keep stories and traditions alive for children. When you travel, you might find a new celebration that becomes your favorite. We should enjoy the fun but also remember the reasons behind each festival.
6::wecelebrate.reading_skill: 作者观点 (Author's Point of View)
6::wecelebrate.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The text offers multiple reasons why people celebrate (history, gratitude, luck, family). Analyse which reason the author appears to consider most important, based on the text structure and the amount of space devoted to each. 2. (Analyze) The mathematical example ('if you attend two festivals every month you will see twenty-four festivals') appears in a nonfiction informational text. Why might the author include a calculation? Is this an effective technique? 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) The text ends with the advice: 'We should enjoy the fun but also remember the reasons behind each festival.' Critically evaluate this claim: is it always possible or desirable to fully understand the historical or religious origins of a festival you celebrate? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) Compare this text with Book 10 (Winter Festivals) and Book 14 (Holiday Celebrations). What does each text emphasise about festivals and celebrations? Together, what do the three texts reveal about the role of celebration in human society? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) The text mentions festivals from China, India, Mexico, and cities generally. Choose a festival from your own cultural background or one you find interesting. Write a paragraph that could be added to this text, following the same style.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Give three different reasons why people celebrate festivals according to the text. 2. (Understand) The text says some festivals 'remember important events like victories'. What does this suggest about the relationship between collective memory and celebration? 3. (Analyse) The text covers festivals from China, India, and Mexico but also makes general statements about 'many countries'. Is this balance between specific examples and general claims effective? 4. (Evaluate) The text advises us to 'remember the reasons behind each festival'. How realistic is this advice for children who may have grown up celebrating a festival without knowing its history? 5. (Create) Choose one festival you know well. Write a paragraph explaining its origin, its main activities, and what human value it expresses (e.g., gratitude, memory, hope).
6::wecelebrate.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name two reasons why people celebrate festivals, according to this passage. (To remember important events, thank nature, send blessings, ask for luck, bring families together — any two.)
2. (On) How many festivals can you see if you attend two per month for a year? (Twenty-four.)
3. (On) Give one example of a festival from the passage and explain what it celebrates. (Diwali fills streets with lights and prayers for hope and luck; Day of the Dead remembers ancestors — any one from text.)
4. (On) What does the passage say festivals help people feel? (Closer to each other and connected to their stories and traditions.)
5. (Above) Why do you think the author says 'we should enjoy the fun but also remember the reasons behind each festival'? (Because knowing the history or meaning of a celebration makes it more significant and helps traditions survive into the future rather than becoming just a party.)
6::wecelebrate.graphic_organizer: Main Idea & Details Web (主旨细节网)
6::wecelebrate.go_description: 中心为主旨、向外辐射节日庆典不同方面的放射状图形组织器
6::wecelebrate.go_usage: 中心写 "Why People Celebrate"，分支：Remember history/Thank nature/Family gathering/New year/Cultural identity

6::iwish.title: I Wish
6::iwish.example_sentence: She had helped many people feel happier than before.
6::iwish.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: past perfect — had + past participle for a completed action seen from a later point.
He had improved more than anyone expected. / She had saved enough money to travel. / They had made a real difference to the community.

core_vocab.example: She had thought about her wish for a long, quiet moment before she whispered.
They delivered warm boxes of cookies and the neighbor's eyes filled with grateful surprise.
She had thought about her wish for a long, quiet moment before she whispered.
They delivered warm boxes of cookies and the neighbor's eyes filled with grateful surprise.
They decided to bake cookies for the elderly neighbor and to decorate the park bench.

6::iwish.text_7page:
Page 1: Mia smiled as she blew out ten candles on her small chocolate cake. She had thought about her wish for a long, quiet moment before she whispered.
Page 2: She wished her family would stay healthy and happy for many more years. Tommy, Mia's twin brother, watched and smiled but looked thoughtful afterwards.
Page 3: When Mia asked him later, he said his wish was for more joy everywhere. Mia felt surprised because his wish was so big and very kind.
Page 4: That night they planned small, clear ways to bring their wishes to life. They decided to bake cookies for the elderly neighbor and to decorate the park bench.
Page 5: Preparing the cookies was fun, and Mia and Tommy learned how giving felt better. They delivered warm boxes of cookies and the neighbor's eyes filled with grateful surprise.
Page 6: Other children and adults saw the bench and joined, bringing paint, plants, and songs. Soon the small park looked brighter and people left little notes of hope on the bench.
Page 7: Tommy grinned and said his wish had started a small, real change in their street. Mia still wanted her family to be healthy and happy, and now she knew she had helped many people. This made her feel even happier.
6::iwish.pure_text: Mia smiled as she blew out ten candles on her small chocolate cake. She had thought about her wish for a long, quiet moment before she whispered. She wished her family would stay healthy and happy for many more years. Tommy, Mia's twin brother, watched and smiled but looked thoughtful afterwards. When Mia asked him later, he said his wish was for more joy everywhere. Mia felt surprised because his wish was so big and very kind. That night they planned small, clear ways to bring their wishes to life. They decided to bake cookies for the elderly neighbor and to decorate the park bench. Preparing the cookies was fun, and Mia and Tommy learned how giving felt better. They delivered warm boxes of cookies and the neighbor's eyes filled with grateful surprise. Other children and adults saw the bench and joined, bringing paint, plants, and songs. Soon the small park looked brighter and people left little notes of hope on the bench. Tommy grinned and said his wish had started a small, real change in their street. Mia still wanted her family to be healthy and happy, and now she knew she had helped many people. This made her feel even happier.
6::iwish.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) Mia's wish is personal (family health) while Tommy's is universal (joy everywhere). Analyse what this contrast reveals about the characters and about the nature of wishes themselves. 2. (Analyze) The story suggests that acting on a wish (baking cookies, decorating a bench) creates a 'ripple effect' that spreads beyond the original intention. Find specific evidence of this ripple effect and explain how the author develops it. 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) Evaluate the story's central argument: that making others happy makes you happier. Is this claim supported fully by the events of the story? Are there any counter-examples or complications the story ignores? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) The neighbours leave 'little notes of hope on the bench'. What does this detail suggest about the relationship between public spaces and community wellbeing? How does a decorated bench become more than just a physical object? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Tommy says his wish 'started a small, real change in their street'. Write a reflective journal entry from Tommy's perspective six months later: has the change continued? What new challenges or opportunities have appeared?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What two specific actions did Mia and Tommy take to make their wishes real? 2. (Understand) Describe the 'ripple effect' that the cookies and bench decoration created. What does this chain of events suggest about community? 3. (Analyse) The story argues that 'helping others felt even better than applause'. What is the structural difference between these two rewards and why might one feel more lasting? 4. (Evaluate) Is the story's conclusion (helping others always leads to personal happiness) always true in real life? When might helping others feel difficult or unrewarding? 5. (Create) Write the notes of hope that two different people might leave on the decorated bench. Make each note reflect a different wish or need.
6::iwish.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What wish did Mia make when she blew out her birthday candles? (She wished her family would stay healthy and happy for many more years.)
2. (On) What did Tommy wish for, and how was his wish different from Mia's? (He wished for more joy everywhere — his wish was bigger and more universal than Mia's personal wish.)
3. (On) What did the children deliver to the elderly neighbour? (Warm boxes of cookies.)
4. (On) What happened to the park bench after the children decorated it? (Other people joined in, bringing paint, plants, and songs, and people left notes of hope on it.)
5. (Above) Why do you think helping others made Mia feel even happier than her original birthday wish? (Because making someone else happy creates a real, immediate effect you can see — which feels more rewarding than simply wishing for something for yourself.)
6::iwish.go_description: 以故事结构呈现角色愿望、行动和结果的图形组织器
6::iwish.go_usage: 记录：Mia's wish → Tommy's bigger wish → Plan actions → Bake cookies → Decorate bench → Community joins

6::holidaycelebrations.title: Holiday Celebrations
6::holidaycelebrations.sentence_pattern: In some cultures/countries, [verb phrase]; in others, [verb phrase].
6::holidaycelebrations.example_sentence: In some cultures, people exchange gifts; in others, they prefer visits or kind words.
6::holidaycelebrations.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: contrastive structure 'In some...in others' with semicolon.
In some countries, people celebrate with fireworks; in others, they prefer quiet family meals. / In some schools, students wear uniforms; in others, they choose their own clothes.

core_vocab.example: Different countries celebrate differently because of religion, history, climate, and local stories.
People express celebration through fireworks, singing, parades, big meals, and gift giving.
Holidays often began long ago when communities gathered to give thanks and celebrate harvests.
Cities often hire professionals to set off fireworks safely for large crowds.
Parades show floats, bands and dancers moving through streets for neighbors to enjoy.

6::holidaycelebrations.text_7page:
Page 1: Holidays bring people together in many joyful ways around the world each year. People express celebration through fireworks, singing, parades, big meals, and gift giving.
Page 2: Fireworks light the night sky with bright colors and loud sounds at public events. Cities often hire professionals to set off fireworks safely for large crowds.
Page 3: Families sing traditional songs together, which makes celebrations feel warm and personal. Parades show floats, bands and dancers moving through streets for neighbors to enjoy.
Page 4: Food is central to most holidays; relatives prepare special meals and share dishes at home. In some cultures, gifts are common, while others prefer visits, help, or kind wishes instead.
Page 5: For example, people in Spain eat twelve grapes at midnight for good luck on New Year. In Sydney, more than one million people watch spectacular New Year fireworks over the harbour.
Page 6: Many families have followed their holiday traditions for generations, keeping customs alive. Holidays often began long ago when communities gathered to give thanks and celebrate harvests.
Page 7: Different countries celebrate differently because of religion, history, climate, and local stories. Some places celebrate quietly at home, while others celebrate loudly with music and dancing. Holiday celebrations help people feel connected to each other and to their past.
6::holidaycelebrations.pure_text: Holidays bring people together in many joyful ways around the world each year. People express celebration through fireworks, singing, parades, big meals, and gift giving. Fireworks light the night sky with bright colors and loud sounds at public events. Cities often hire professionals to set off fireworks safely for large crowds. Families sing traditional songs together, which makes celebrations feel warm and personal. Parades show floats, bands and dancers moving through streets for neighbors to enjoy. Food is central to most holidays; relatives prepare special meals and share dishes at home. In some cultures, gifts are common, while others prefer visits, help, or kind wishes instead. For example, people in Spain eat twelve grapes at midnight for good luck on New Year. In Sydney, more than one million people watch spectacular New Year fireworks over the harbour. Many families have followed their holiday traditions for generations, keeping customs alive. Holidays often began long ago when communities gathered to give thanks and celebrate harvests. Different countries celebrate differently because of religion, history, climate, and local stories. Some places celebrate quietly at home, while others celebrate loudly with music and dancing. Holiday celebrations help people feel connected to each other and to their past.
6::holidaycelebrations.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The text presents fireworks, parades, food, and songs as universal forms of celebration. Analyse whether these four elements are truly universal or whether they reflect a particular cultural bias. 2. (Analyze) The author mentions that 'holidays often began long ago when communities gathered to give thanks and celebrate harvests'. How does this historical origin compare to the way modern holidays are celebrated? What has been preserved and what has changed? 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) Different countries celebrate 'differently because of religion, history, climate, and local stories'. Evaluate these four factors: which do you consider most powerful in shaping how a celebration evolves over time? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) The text states that celebration 'helps people feel connected to each other and to their past'. Consider a person who has moved away from their home culture: can they maintain this connection through celebration? What challenges might they face? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) The Sydney New Year's fireworks attract over one million spectators. Consider the environmental impact of large-scale fireworks displays. Write a balanced argument: should cities continue this tradition or find alternative ways to celebrate?

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What specific celebration takes place in Sydney at New Year and how many people attend? 2. (Understand) The text says holidays 'began long ago when communities gathered to give thanks'. How does this origin connect to the modern celebrations described in the text? 3. (Analyse) The author mentions environmental concerns indirectly through the example of professional fireworks. What is the implicit message here and how is it delivered without being preachy? 4. (Evaluate) Some people celebrate 'quietly at home' while others celebrate 'loudly with music and dancing'. Is one way of celebrating more valid than the other? What does the difference reveal about cultural values? 5. (Create) Write a brief guide for a new resident in your city explaining how to respectfully observe or participate in a local holiday celebration. Include three practical suggestions and explain the reason for each.
6::holidaycelebrations.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name four ways people express celebration in holidays, according to the passage. (Fireworks, singing, parades, big meals, and gift giving — any four.)
2. (On) How many people watch the New Year's fireworks in Sydney? (More than one million people.)
3. (On) Why do families sing traditional songs together during holidays? (The passage says it makes celebrations feel warm and personal.)
4. (On) What do people eat in Spain at midnight on New Year? (Twelve grapes for good luck in the new year.)
5. (Above) Why do you think holiday celebrations help people feel connected to their past as well as to each other? (Because traditions come from history — when families repeat them, they are doing the same things their grandparents did, which creates a sense of continuity and identity.)
6::holidaycelebrations.graphic_organizer: Main Idea & Details Web (主旨细节网)
6::holidaycelebrations.go_description: 中心为主旨、向外辐射多种节假日庆祝方式的放射状图形组织器
6::holidaycelebrations.go_usage: 中心写 "Holiday Celebrations"，五支：Fireworks/Songs/Parades/Food/Gifts，每支填一个具体例子

6::happinessforeveryone.title: Happiness for Everyone
6::happinessforeveryone.example_sentence: Some students were so quiet that nobody could hear them at all.
6::happinessforeveryone.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: so + adjective + that for expressing a result caused by a degree of quality.
The test was so difficult that half the class did not finish it. / She was so tired that she fell asleep before dinner. / The music was so loud that we could not hear each other talking.

core_vocab.example: "We should help everyone feel included," Mia said.
They also planned short performances so quieter students could join the show.
The school announced a "Happy Day" to celebrate kindness and small joys.
Mia and Tommy realized that helping others felt even better than any applause.
Handmade gifts and honest smiles warmed every heart more than any applause did.

6::happinessforeveryone.text_7page:
Page 1: The school announced a "Happy Day" to celebrate kindness and small joys. Mia and Tommy were excited and quickly began making cards and small gifts.
Page 2: "We should help everyone feel included," Mia said. They also planned short performances so quieter students could join the show.
Page 3: At first, the class worked happily, but some children sat silently with no ideas. "I'm terrible at drawing," one boy muttered.
Page 4: "And I hate performing," another girl whispered nervously. Tommy had an idea.
Page 5: "Maybe the older students can help us." The idea worked quickly. Older pupils soon arrived with paper, bright pens and musical instruments.
Page 6: Before long, the card table was crowded with students laughing and sharing ideas. The older students led the shy students to practice the show together. At first, some spoke so quietly that nobody could hear them, but little by little they grew braver.
Page 7: Soon the room filled with music, laughter, and cheering. The handmade gifts and honest smiles warmed every listener's heart more than the decorations did. Mia and Tommy realized that helping others felt even better than any applause. Before leaving, the principal smiled and announced that Happy Day would become a new tradition. Everyone cheered at the idea.
6::happinessforeveryone.pure_text: The school announced a "Happy Day" to celebrate kindness and small joys. Mia and Tommy were excited and quickly began making cards and small gifts. "We should help everyone feel included," Mia said. They also planned short performances so quieter students could join the show. At first, the class worked happily, but some children sat silently with no ideas. "I'm terrible at drawing," one boy muttered. "And I hate performing," another girl whispered nervously. Tommy had an idea. "Maybe the older students can help us." The idea worked quickly. Older pupils soon arrived with paper, bright pens and musical instruments. Before long, the card table was crowded with students laughing and sharing ideas. The older students led the shy students to practice the show together. At first, some spoke so quietly that nobody could hear them, but little by little they grew braver. Soon the room filled with music, laughter, and cheering. The handmade gifts and honest smiles warmed every listener's heart more than the decorations did. Mia and Tommy realized that helping others felt even better than any applause. Before leaving, the principal smiled and announced that Happy Day would become a new tradition. Everyone cheered at the idea.
6::happinessforeveryone.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The story includes two structural problems: some students have no ideas, and some speak too quietly to be heard. How does Tommy's solution (inviting older students) address both problems simultaneously? 2. (Analyze) Mia says 'we should help everyone feel included'. Evaluate this statement as a guiding principle: is inclusion always achievable in practice? What tensions can arise when trying to include everyone? 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) The story's main realisation is that 'helping others felt even better than any applause'. Critically examine this claim: is selfless action always more rewarding than recognition? Can these two motivations coexist? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) The principal announces that Happy Day 'would become a new tradition'. What does this decision reveal about how institutions (like schools) can formalise and preserve moments of human connection? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) Design a Happy Day for your own school. Consider: Who would be invited? What activities would ensure all students feel included? How would you evaluate whether it was successful? Write a proposal of 100-120 words.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) What two problems did some students face during the Happy Day preparations? 2. (Understand) Why did inviting older students solve both problems at once? Explain the connection. 3. (Analyse) The story ends with the principal making Happy Day a 'new tradition'. What does this institutional decision add to the story's theme about kindness and community? 4. (Evaluate) The story implies that helping others is intrinsically rewarding. Do you think this is always true, or are there situations where helping others is difficult or unrewarding? Use evidence from the story and your own experience. 5. (Create) The principal has asked you to write a one-paragraph description of Happy Day for the school newsletter. Include what happened, what was learned, and why it will become a yearly tradition.
6::happinessforeveryone.rr_questions:
1. (Under) What event did the school announce? (A 'Happy Day' to celebrate kindness and small joys.)
2. (On) What problem did some students have during the preparations? (Some had no ideas and some spoke so quietly that nobody could hear them.)
3. (On) How did Tommy solve the problem? (He suggested inviting older students to come and help the younger ones.)
4. (On) What announcement did the principal make at the end? (That Happy Day would become a new yearly tradition.)
5. (Above) Why do you think Mia and Tommy found that helping others felt better than receiving applause? (Because creating something real that made others happy gave them a deeper sense of satisfaction — being useful to someone is more fulfilling than being praised.)
6::happinessforeveryone.go_description: 以故事要素呈现Happy Day活动、挑战和结局的图形组织器
6::happinessforeveryone.go_usage: 记录：Setting → Problem (some students have no ideas) → Solution (older students help) → Result (room fills with music) → New tradition announced

6::holidaywishesaroundtheworld.title: Holiday Wishes Around the World
6::holidaywishesaroundtheworld.example_sentence: Although holiday traditions are different, many celebrations share the same values.
6::holidaywishesaroundtheworld.teaching_focus:
Core grammar: although + clause for presenting contrast.
Although dolphins live in the sea, they are mammals, not fish. / Although she studied hard, she did not pass the test. / Although the weather was cold, we enjoyed the outdoor festival.

core_vocab.example: In China children often receive red envelopes with money during Lunar New Year celebrations.
In Spain many people eat twelve grapes at midnight for good luck in the new year.
One surprising tradition is that many people in Japan enjoy eating fried chicken at Christmas.
In Japan families often visit quiet shrines early on New Year morning to pray for health and happiness.
In China children often receive red envelopes with money during Lunar New Year celebrations.

6::holidaywishesaroundtheworld.core_vocab_raw: envelope, midnight, fried chicken, shrine, Lunar New Year
6::holidaywishesaroundtheworld.text_7page:
Page 1: People around the world celebrate holidays in many different ways. Some traditions may look surprising, but they help families feel close and happy together.
Page 2: If people travel during holiday seasons, they can learn many interesting customs from other cultures. In Spain many people eat twelve grapes at midnight for good luck in the new year.
Page 3: In Japan families often visit quiet shrines early on New Year morning to pray for health and happiness. In Germany holiday markets are filled with lights, cakes, warm drinks, and handmade toys.
Page 4: In China children often receive red envelopes with money during Lunar New Year celebrations. In the Philippines Christmas celebrations can begin in September and continue for several months.
Page 5: Some traditions are connected to food. Many families around the world prepare special meals and enjoy long dinners together during important holidays.
Page 6: One surprising tradition is that many people in Japan enjoy eating fried chicken at Christmas. Visitors are often amazed by this custom.
Page 7: Although holiday traditions are different, many celebrations share the same ideas. People hope for peace, health, happiness, and good luck in the coming year. Holiday traditions help people stay connected to their families, cultures, and communities around the world
.
6::holidaywishesaroundtheworld.pure_text:
People around the world celebrate holidays in many different ways. Some traditions may look surprising, but they help families feel close and happy together. If people travel during holiday seasons, they can learn many interesting customs from other cultures. In Spain many people eat twelve grapes at midnight for good luck in the new year. In Japan families often visit quiet shrines early on New Year morning to pray for health and happiness. In Germany holiday markets are filled with lights, cakes, warm drinks, and handmade toys. In China children often receive red envelopes with money during Lunar New Year celebrations. In the Philippines Christmas celebrations can begin in September and continue for several months. Some traditions are connected to food. Many families around the world prepare special meals and enjoy long dinners together during important holidays. One surprising tradition is that many people in Japan enjoy eating fried chicken at Christmas. Visitors are often amazed by this custom. Although holiday traditions are different, many celebrations share the same ideas. People hope for peace, health, happiness, and good luck in the coming year. Holiday traditions help people stay connected to their families, cultures, and communities around the world
.
6::holidaywishesaroundtheworld.reading_skill: 主旨与细节 (Main Idea and Details)
6::holidaywishesaroundtheworld.questions_type:
Critical Reading: 1. (Analyze) The text presents holiday traditions from six countries (Spain, Japan, Germany, China, Philippines, and implicitly others). Analyse whether the selection of countries creates a balanced global picture or whether it reflects a particular geographic or cultural bias. 2. (Analyze) The fried chicken Christmas tradition in Japan is described as 'surprising'. Evaluate the author's use of the word 'surprising': surprising for whom? What assumption does this word reveal about the intended reader? 3. (Evidence) Identify two specific pieces of textual evidence that best support the main argument or theme. Explain why each piece of evidence is important.
Evaluation & Argument: 1. (Evaluate) The text concludes that 'holiday traditions help people stay connected to their families, cultures, and communities'. Evaluate this claim in the context of people who live far from their home culture: does distance strengthen or weaken the role of tradition? 2. (Evaluate) Does the author present a balanced view or a one-sided perspective? Use evidence from the text to support your answer. 3. (Author's Craft) Identify one rhetorical or literary technique the author uses (e.g., contrast, repetition, metaphor). Explain how it strengthens the text.
Synthesis & Creation: 1. (Synthesize) The text opens with the claim that some traditions 'may look surprising, but they help families feel close and happy'. Compare this argument with the conclusions of Books 10, 11, 12, and 14. What consistent message emerges across all five texts about the purpose of celebration? 2. (Perspective) How might a different character, reader, or cultural group interpret this text differently? 3. (Create) You have been asked to create a new international holiday that could be celebrated by people of any culture, religion, or background. Describe: what would be celebrated, what activities would be included, and how you would ensure it was genuinely inclusive. Justify each of your choices.

Assessment Questions:
1. (Recall) Name three countries from the text and one holiday tradition associated with each. 2. (Understand) The fried chicken Christmas tradition in Japan is described as 'surprising'. Surprising to whom? What assumption does this word reveal? 3. (Analyse) The text opens with 'Some traditions may look surprising, but they help families feel close'. Evaluate this claim: is 'helping families feel close' a sufficient justification for any tradition, no matter how unusual? 4. (Evaluate) The text presents holiday traditions from six specific countries. Consider what regions or cultures are absent. Does this selection create a balanced or biased global picture? 5. (Create) Design a genuinely inclusive international holiday that people from any cultural, religious, or national background could celebrate. Describe three specific activities and explain why each is culturally neutral yet meaningful.
6::holidaywishesaroundtheworld.rr_questions:
1. (Under) Name two countries mentioned in this passage and one holiday tradition from each. (Spain: eating twelve grapes at midnight; Japan: visiting shrines; Germany: holiday markets with lights and warm drinks; China: red envelopes; Philippines: Christmas beginning in September — any two.)
2. (On) When does the Christmas season begin in the Philippines? (In September — several months before Christmas Day.)
3. (On) What surprising Christmas tradition does Japan have? (Many people eat fried chicken at Christmas.)
4. (On) What does the passage say holiday traditions help people stay connected to? (Their families, cultures, and communities around the world.)
5. (Above) Why do you think holiday traditions, even very different ones, share the same underlying purpose? (Because all celebrations — no matter how different they look — are ways for people to express hope, gratitude, and belonging; those human needs are universal.)
6::holidaywishesaroundtheworld.go_description: 以多列对比呈现世界各国节日传统的表格型图形组织器
6::holidaywishesaroundtheworld.go_usage: 列标题：Spain/Japan/Germany/China/Philippines，每列填 festival + key tradition + shared value
