1::hecanrun.title: He Can Run
1::hecanrun.reader_text: “He runs very fast. He runs to the hill. He rolls down the hill. He sees a friend. He stops to talk. He sits on grass. He rests a while. He has a smile.”
1::hecanrun.objective: Build verb/action vocabulary; support basic sequencing; identify simple story events; build print concepts with repeated patterned sentences.
1::hecanrun.core_vocab_raw: run, hill, down, rest, smile, friend, grass, roll
1::hecanrun.sor:
Before: Set a Purpose for Reading
Support comprehension by giving a goal:
“Let’s find out what the boy does first.”
“We are looking for how the problem gets solved.”

Phonological Awareness Warm-Up (2 minutes)
Examples:
Identifying beginning/ending sounds
Oral blending and segmenting: /r–/u/–/n/ → run
Onset-rime blending: r–un → run (f- un → fun, b-un → bun)

During:
Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
Use these steps:
Look at all letters
Sound each part
Blend
Check the picture for meaning AFTER decoding
Avoid: “What word would make sense?”
Use: “Let’s sound it out together.”

Finger Tracking for Print Concepts
Students point to each word to reinforce:
left-to-right
return sweep
spaces between words
This supports fluency and 1:1 correspondence.

After:
Sequencing With Visuals
SoR Component: Text Structure & Oral Language
Use printed picture cards or book pages.
Students arrange and explain what happened in
order.

Text-to-Self Connection (Simple + Concrete)
SoR Component: Comprehension / Background Knowledge
Ask:
“Tell me something you can do fast?”
“What would you like to do fast?”
Helps deepen understanding and oral expression.
1::hecanrun.reading_strategy: Picture walk; verb TPR (run/sit/rest); echo reading
1::hecanrun.questions_type: 1. Where does he run? 2. What does he see? 3. Where does he sit? 4. What does he do after talking? 5. What action comes first? (skill) 6. What action comes last? (skill) 7. Tell me something YOU can do fast.
1::hecanrun.answers: 1. To the hill. 2. A friend. 3. On the grass. 4. He rests. 5. He runs. 6. He smiles. 7. I can ___.
1::hecanrun.oral_prompts: “Show me run → stop → rest.” “How does he feel?”
1::hecanrun.extension: Sequence cards; act out story; draw yourself doing an action
1::hecanrun.phonics_rule: short u CVC (run), short a CVC (fast), short i CVC (hill)
1::hecanrun.syntax_focus: Simple subject + verb pattern repetition (“He runs ___.”)
1::hecanrun.comprehension_tier: Repeated sentence frame reading; build phrased fluency with “He runs ___.”; avoid robotic word-by-word reading

1::animalfamilies.title: Animal Families
1::animalfamilies.reader_text: “A cat has a kitten. A dog has a puppy. A cow has a calf. A duck has a duckling. A horse has a foal. A sheep has a lamb. A goat has a kid. They are animal families.”
1::animalfamilies.objective: Build naming vocabulary; understand parent-baby relationships; categorize animals by type; support oral output.
1::animalfamilies.core_vocab_raw: kitten, puppy, calf, duckling, family, baby
1::animalfamilies.sor:
Before: 
High-Frequency Word Preview (1–2 words)
Follow orthographic mapping steps:
Meaning → sounds → spelling → sentence frame
Examples: the, and, has, see

Set a Purpose for Reading
Support comprehension by giving a goal:
“Let's find as many baby animals as we can!”
“We want to match baby animals with their moms.”

During;
Reread for Automaticity
Encourage the student to reread:
one sentence
one page
a predictable pattern
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping.

Monitor Understanding (Simple Checks)
Use quick prompts:
“Tell me what baby animal you see.”
“What is the baby animal's name?”

After:
Vocabulary Reinforcement
Rediscuss the 3–4 words taught before
reading:
“Show me the picture that matches this
word.”
“Use it in a sentence.”

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
Supports oral language + comprehension:
“Which of these animals have you seen
before?”
“Which animal was your favorite?”
1::animalfamilies.reading_strategy: Picture sort; parent-baby cards; echo reading
1::animalfamilies.questions_type: 1. What is a baby cat called? 2. Which baby belongs to the cow? 3. What is a duckling? 4. Which baby is small? 5. Which animals go together? (skill) 6. How do we know these are families? (skill) 7. Do YOU know another baby animal?
1::animalfamilies.answers: 1. Kitten. 2. Calf. 3. A baby duck. 4. A kitten. 5. Cat-kitten, dog-puppy… 6. They have babies. 7. I know ___.
1::animalfamilies.oral_prompts: “Who is the mom? Who is the baby?”
1::animalfamilies.extension: Match babies to parents; draw your own animal family
1::animalfamilies.phonics_rule: short a CVC (cat), short o CVC (dog), short u (puppy)
1::animalfamilies.comprehension_tier: Choral reading of patterned “A ___ has a ___.” with smooth phrasing

1::inandoutwego.title: In and Out We Go
1::inandoutwego.reader_text: I go in the room. I go out the door. I go in the car. I go out to play. I go in the shop. I go out to walk. I go in the house. I go out again.”
1::inandoutwego.objective: Build spatial concept vocabulary; strengthen reading of repeated pattern text; support concept of direction.
1::inandoutwego.sor:
Before:
Picture Walk
Flip through the pages together.
Ask simple questions: “What do you see?” “Who is this?” “What is happening?”
Build oral language before encountering text.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Concrete Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

During:
Finger Tracking for Print Concepts
Students point to each word to reinforce:
left-to-right
return sweep
spaces between words

Text-Based Questions That Support Comprehension
Ask during natural pauses:
“Who is in this part?”
“What is the girl doing now?”
“How do you think he feels? Why?”

Reread for Automaticity
Encourage the student to reread:
one sentence
one page
a predictable pattern
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping.

After:
Vocabulary Reinforcement
Rediscuss words 'in' and 'out' in the reading:
“Show me the picture that matches this word.”
“Use it in a sentence.”

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
Supports oral language + patterns:
Identify the pattern and continue it.”
Use the words 'in' or 'out' to describe the
pictures.”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Choose 2–3 words from the text and practice:
building with magnetic letters
substituting sounds (cat → bat → bag → big)
mapping the phonemes to graphemes
1::inandoutwego.questions_type: 1. Where do you go in? 2. Where do you go out? 3. What do you do outside? 4. Where do you walk? 5. What words repeat in the story? (skill) 6. Is “in” the same as “out”? (skill-only yes/no Q) 7. Where do YOU like to go in or out?
1::inandoutwego.answers: 1. The room/car/house. 2. The door/shop. 3. Play. 4. Outside. 5. In/out. 6. No. 7. I like to go ___.
1::inandoutwego.extension: In/out sorting; act out actions; draw a place you go in/out of
1::inandoutwego.phonics_rule: short i (in), long ou (out), long o (go)
1::inandoutwego.syntax_focus: Prepositional phrase repetition (“in the ___ / out the ___”)
1::inandoutwego.comprehension_tier: TPR-supported echo reading; emphasize contrast words

1::hairandcare.title: Hair and Care
1::hairandcare.reader_text: “My hair is long. My hair is curly. My hair is black. My hair is soft. I comb my hair. I wash my hair. I dry my hair. I like my hair.”
1::hairandcare.objective: Build descriptive vocabulary; describe physical attributes; understand self-care actions.
1::hairandcare.core_vocab_raw: hair, curly, black, soft, comb, wash, dry
1::hairandcare.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Short + Explicit)
Focus on concepts needed for meaning:
“This story is about hair.”
“How do you take care of your hair?”
Show 2–3 related images or model actions (comb, wash, dry)

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Concrete Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
TPR for actions (comb, wash, dry)
Keep vocabulary tied directly to the text.

During Reading:

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
After decoding a word, ask:
“What does that mean?”
“Does that match the picture?”
Encourage students to look at all letters, sound it out, and blend.

Reread for Automaticity
Encourage the student to reread:
one sentence
one page
the repeated pattern: “My hair is ___.”
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping.

After Reading:

Vocabulary Reinforcement
Rediscuss describing and action words:
“Which words tell about hair?”
“Which words tell actions?”
“Use it in a sentence.”

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
Supports oral language + patterns:
Describe their own hair
Share how they take care of their hair

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Work with the word my:
build with magnetic letters
substitute sounds (my → by → try → fly)
map sounds to letters
1::hairandcare.questions_type: 1. What color is the hair? 2. Is the hair curly or straight? 3. What do they do to the hair? 4. What is soft? 5. Which words describe hair? (skill) 6. Which words tell actions? (skill) 7. What is YOUR hair like?
1::hairandcare.answers: 1. Black. 2. Curly. 3. Comb, wash, dry. 4. The hair. 5. Long/curly/black/soft. 6. Comb/wash/dry. 7. My hair is ___.
1::hairandcare.extension: Hair description chart; draw yourself; sort adjectives vs. actions
1::hairandcare.phonics_rule: long a vowel team (ai in hair)

1::lookwhaticando.title: Look What I Can Do!
1::lookwhaticando.reader_text: “I can run fast. I can climb high. I can draw pictures. I can swim well. I can write my name. I can jump and swim. I can dance, too. I can do many things.”
1::lookwhaticando.objective: Build action verb fluency; strengthen confidence language; support motor/action vocabulary.
1::lookwhaticando.core_vocab_raw: run, climb, draw, dance, can, jump, skip, thing
1::lookwhaticando.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Short + Explicit)
Focus on concepts needed for meaning:
“This story is about things we can do.”
“What are some things you can do?”
Show 2–3 actions (run, jump, swim)

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Concrete Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support + TPR (run, climb, jump, draw)
Model each action
Keep vocabulary tied directly to the text.

During Reading:

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
After decoding, ask:
“What does that mean?”
“Show me.”
Encourage students to look at all letters, sound it out, and blend.

Reread for Automaticity
Encourage the student to reread:
one sentence
one page
the repeated pattern: “I can ___.”
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping.

After Reading:

Vocabulary Reinforcement
Act it out and identify:
“Show me run/climb/jump.”
“Which word matches this action?”
“Use it in a sentence.”

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
Supports oral language + confidence:
Share actions they can do
Continue the pattern: “I can ___.”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Work with can and do:
build and read (c-a-n, d-o)
tap sounds and blend
substitute initial sounds (can → man → ran)
1::lookwhaticando.reading_strategy: TPR for each action; picture walk; echo reading
1::lookwhaticando.questions_type: 1. What can the child do? 2. What action uses hands? 3. What action uses feet? 4. What can they write? 5. Which actions are fast? (skill) 6. Which actions are slow or careful? (skill) 7. What can YOU do?
1::lookwhaticando.answers: 1. Run/climb/draw/swim/write/jump/skip/dance. 2. Draw/write. 3. Run/jump/skip. 4. Their name. 5. Run/jump. 6. Draw/write. 7. I can ___.
1::lookwhaticando.extension: Action charades; sort verbs by body part; draw yourself doing an action

1::wayswego.title: Ways We Go
1::wayswego.reader_text: “We go by car. We go by bike. We go by boat. We go by plane. We go together. We like to go. It is so fun. Let us go now!”
1::wayswego.objective: Build transportation vocabulary; strengthen repeated patterned sentences; support categorization (ways to travel).
1::wayswego.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Short + Explicit)
Focus on concepts needed for meaning:
“This story is about ways we go.”
“How do you go to school?”
Show 2–3 examples (car, bike, plane)

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Concrete Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support (bike, boat, plane)
Act out or gesture (ride, row, fly)
Keep vocabulary tied directly to the text.

During Reading:

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
After decoding, ask:
“What does that mean?”
“Does that match the picture?”
Encourage students to look at all letters, sound it out, and blend.

Reread for Automaticity
Encourage the student to reread:
one sentence
one page
the repeated pattern: “We go by ___.”
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping.

After Reading:

Vocabulary Reinforcement
Categorize ways to travel:
“Which one goes on land? water? air?”
“Show me bike/boat/plane.”
“Use it in a sentence.”

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
Supports oral language + pattern use:
Share how they travel
Continue the pattern: “We go by ___.”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Work with go and by:
build and read (g-o, b-y)
tap sounds and blend
substitute initial sounds (go → so → no)
1::wayswego.reading_skill: Categorizing by type (transportation); identifying main idea
1::wayswego.questions_type: 1. How do they go? 2. What goes on water? 3. What goes in the sky? 4. Who goes together? 5. Which things are transportation? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. How do YOU like to go places?
1::wayswego.answers: 1. By car/bike/boat/plane. 2. Boat. 3. Plane. 4. They do. 5. Car/bike/boat/plane. 6. Ways to go places. 7. I go by ___.
1::wayswego.oral_prompts: “Show me fly/ride/row.” “Which one is slow? Fast?”
1::wayswego.extension: Sort land/air/water transport; draw your favorite way to travel
1::wayswego.comprehension_tier: Steady rhythmic reading of repeated frame

1::myhappyface.title: My Happy Face
1::myhappyface.reader_text: “My face has two eyes. My face has a nose. My face has a mouth. My face has a smile. My face has two ears. My face has a chin. My face has cheeks. My face is happy.”
1::myhappyface.objective: Build body-part vocabulary; support labeling and identification; connect facial features to simple emotions.
1::myhappyface.core_vocab_raw: eyes, nose, mouth, smile, face, ears, cheeks, happy
1::myhappyface.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Short + Explicit)
Focus on concepts needed for meaning:
“This story is about your face.”
“What is on your face?”
Students point to eyes, nose, mouth

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Concrete Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support (eyes, nose, mouth, ears)
Point to real face for meaning
Keep vocabulary tied directly to the text.

During Reading:

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
After decoding, ask:
“What is this?”
“Point to it.”
Encourage students to look at all letters, sound it out, and blend.

Reread for Automaticity
Encourage the student to reread:
one sentence
one page
the repeated pattern: “My face has ___.”
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping.

After Reading:

Vocabulary Reinforcement
Identify and label:
“Point to your nose/eyes/mouth.”
“Which word matches?”
“Use it in a sentence.”

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
Supports oral language + emotion:
Describe their face: “My face has ___.”
Show feelings: “My face is happy/sad.”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Work with face and has:
build and read (f-a-ce, h-a-s)
tap sounds and blend
substitute initial sounds (has → gas → mass)
1::myhappyface.reading_strategy: Label your face; echo reading; pointing strategy
1::myhappyface.reading_skill: Identifying parts of the face; labeling
1::myhappyface.questions_type: 1. What does the face have? 2. How many eyes? 3. What helps you smell? 4. What helps you smile? 5. Which words name face parts? (skill) 6. Which word shows emotion? (skill) 7. What does YOUR face look like today?
1::myhappyface.answers: 1. Eyes/nose/mouth/smile/ears/cheeks. 2. Two. 3. Nose. 4. Mouth. 5. Eyes/nose/mouth/ears/cheeks. 6. Happy. 7. My face is ___.
1::myhappyface.extension: Draw a face; sort parts vs. feelings; mirror activity

1::mybodycanmove.title: My Body Can Move
1::mybodycanmove.reader_text: “My body can move. My arms move up. My hands can clap. My feet jump high. My head can nod. My legs run fast. My body works well. I love to move.”
1::mybodycanmove.objective: Build action/body part connections; support motor vocabulary; strengthen verb-noun pairing.
1::mybodycanmove.core_vocab_raw: arm, leg, hand, foot, clap, head, nod, work
1::mybodycanmove.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Short + Explicit)
Focus on concepts needed for meaning:
“This story is about how our body can move.”
“What can your body do?”
Students show actions (clap, jump, nod)

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Concrete Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support (arm, hand, leg, foot)
Point and move each body part
Keep vocabulary tied directly to the text.

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find out how the body moves.”

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Look → sound → blend → check meaning

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
“What can it do?”
“Show me.”

Echo and Choral Reading for Fluency
Repeat pattern: “My ___ can ___.”

Reread for Automaticity
one sentence
one page
repeated action pattern
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping.

After Reading:

Vocabulary Reinforcement
Connect body parts + actions:
“Show me your hands/feet/arms.”
“What can they do?”
“Use it in a sentence.”

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
Supports oral language + movement:
Share actions: “My ___ can ___.”
Combine body + action

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus: short o in body
build and read (b-o-d-y)
identify /d/ sound in words
segment and blend simple words
1::mybodycanmove.reading_strategy: TPR for each action; picture walk
1::mybodycanmove.questions_type: 1. What moves up? 2. What runs fast? 3. What claps? 4. What jumps? 5. Which actions match each body part? (skill) 6. Which action shows “yes/no”? (skill) 7. What can YOUR body do?
1::mybodycanmove.answers: 1. Arms. 2. Legs. 3. Hands. 4. Feet. 5. Arms→up; legs→run; hands→clap; feet→jump. 6. Nod. 7. My body can ___.
1::mybodycanmove.extension: Draw body actions; sort actions by body part
1::mybodycanmove.phonics_rule: short o (body), identify the initial /d/ sound in pictured words.

1::gettingready.title: Getting Ready
1::gettingready.reader_text: I put on my shirt. I put on my pants.  I put on my socks. I put on my shoes. I put on my hat. I look in the mirror. I am ready now. I am ready to go!
1::gettingready.objective: Build vocabulary of clothing/wearing routines; strengthen sequencing (dressing steps); build independence language.
1::gettingready.core_vocab_raw: shirt, pants, sock, shoe, hat, mirror, ready
1::gettingready.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Short + Explicit)
Focus on concepts needed for meaning:
“This story is about getting dressed.”
“What do you put on first?”
Act out putting on clothes

High-Frequency Word Preview (1–2 words)
Follow orthographic mapping steps:
put, on
Meaning → sounds → spelling → use in sentence

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find out what he puts on first.”

During Reading:

Echo and Choral Reading for Fluency
Teacher models → students repeat
Repeat pattern: “I put on ___.”

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
“What is he putting on?”
“Does that match the picture?”
Encourage sound it out → blend → confirm meaning

Text-Based Questions That Support Comprehension
“What comes next?”
“What is he going to do?”

Reread for Automaticity
one sentence
one page
repeated routine pattern
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping.

After Reading:

Retell Using Sequence Words
First… Next… Then… Last…
Act out dressing steps in order

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show me shirt/pants/shoes.”
“Use it in a sentence.”

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you put on first?”
Use pattern: “I put on ___.”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus: shirt / get / ready
build and read simple words
identify short vowel sounds
segment and blend
1::gettingready.questions_type: 1. What does the child put on first? 2. What goes on the feet? 3. What goes on the head? 4. Where do they look at the end? 5. What steps come in order? (skill) 6. What is the last step? (skill) 7. What do YOU put on to get ready?
1::gettingready.answers: 1. Shirt. 2. Socks/shoes. 3. Hat. 4. Mirror. 5. Shirt→pants→socks→hat→shoes. 6. Getting ready to go. 7. I put on ___.
1::gettingready.oral_prompts: “Act out getting dressed.” “What do you put on first?”
1::gettingready.extension: Draw clothing items; sort head/body/feet items
1::gettingready.phonics_rule: short e (get), long e (ready), short i (shirt)

1::firstdayofschool.title: First Day of School
1::firstdayofschool.reader_text: Mia packs her bag. She walks to school. She sees her teacher. She sees her friends. She sits in class. She reads a book. She likes her school. It is a good day.
1::firstdayofschool.objective: Strengthen school-related vocabulary; identify main events; build emotional literacy (good day, likes school).
1::firstdayofschool.core_vocab_raw: bag, walk, class, teacher, friend, read, day
1::firstdayofschool.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Short + Explicit)
Focus on concepts needed for meaning:
“This story is about the first day of school.”
“What do you do at school?”
Show simple school actions (walk, read, sit)

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Concrete Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support (bag, teacher, class, read)
Connect to real-life school experience
Keep vocabulary tied directly to the text.

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find out what she does first.”

During Reading:

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
After decoding, ask:
“What is she doing?”
“Does that match the picture?”
Encourage sound it out → blend → confirm meaning.

Echo and Choral Reading for Fluency
Teacher models → students repeat.
Use pattern: “She ___.”

Reread for Automaticity
one sentence
one page
sequence of events
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping.

After Reading:

Retell Using Sequence Words
First… Next… Then… Last…
Identify key events in order

Identify the Central Idea
“What was this story about?”
“Was it a good day or not?”

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you do at school?”
“Who do you see at school?”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus: first / day
build and read simple words
identify vowel sounds
segment and blend
1::firstdayofschool.questions_type: 1. What does Mia pack? 2. Who does she see? 3. What does she read? 4. Where does she sit? 5. Which events happen first/next? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What do YOU like about school?
1::firstdayofschool.answers: 1. Her bag. 2. Her teacher and friends. 3. A book. 4. In class. 5. Pack→walk→see→sit→read. 6. Maria’s first day at school. 7. I like ___.
1::firstdayofschool.oral_prompts: “Who do YOU see at school?” “Act out reading.”
1::firstdayofschool.extension: Sequence strips; draw your school scene; classroom role-play

1::countingkittens.title: Counting Kittens
1::countingkittens.reader_text: Tommy sees one kitten. He sees two kittens. He sees three kittens. The kittens are small. The kittens play together. They are very cute. He sees many kittens. So many kittens!”
1::countingkittens.objective: Build counting vocabulary; connect numerals to quantities; develop early descriptive language; strengthen pattern-based reading.
1::countingkittens.core_vocab_raw: one, two, three, many, small, cute
1::countingkittens.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Conceptual Knowledge
Briefly activate number knowledge:
“Let’s count to five together.”
Use fingers or objects to count

Vocabulary Preview (Concrete Math Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles:
one, two, three, many
Use real objects or pictures
Connect spoken numbers to what students see

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s count how many kittens we see.”

During Reading:

One-to-One Correspondence Practice
Students point and count kittens
Match spoken number to quantity

Decode + Quantify
“Let’s sound it out.”
Read number word → connect to amount
“How many do you see?”

Reread for Automaticity
one sentence
one page
counting pattern
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping

After Reading:

Hands-On Extension
“Show me three kittens.” (use blocks or fingers)
Recreate groups to match numbers

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Which group is one/two/three?”
“Which group is many?”

Oral Language Expansion
Use pattern: “He sees ___.”
Students describe quantities in full sentences
1::countingkittens.questions_type:
1. What do the kittens do together?
2. How many kittens are there?  4. Which word tells how big they are? 5. What word tells the number of kittens?(skill)
1::countingkittens.answers: 1. One. 2. Three. 3. Playing. 4. Small. 5. Many. 6. Many. 7. I can count ___.
1::countingkittens.extension: Count objects; make groups of 1–3; draw kittens

1::soundswemake.title: Sounds We Make
1::soundswemake.reader_text: We can make sounds. We clap our hands. We sing a song. We laugh out loud. We talk to friends. Sounds are around us. We like the sounds. They are happy sounds.
1::soundswemake.objective: Build action vocabulary (clap, sing, laugh, talk); strengthen auditory awareness; practice describing sounds.
1::soundswemake.core_vocab_raw: clap, sing, laugh, talk, sound, hands, loud, around
1::soundswemake.sor:
Before Reading:

Activate Sensory Schema
“Listen.” (clap, whisper, stomp)
“Is this loud or soft?”

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Concrete Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles:
clap, sing, laugh, talk
Use gestures and real-life examples
Connect actions to sounds

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s listen for the sounds we hear.”

During Reading:

Prosody Modeling
Model expressive reading for sound words
Students echo to match tone and expression

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
“Let’s sound it out.”
“What sound is this?”
Connect word → sound → meaning

Reread for Automaticity
one sentence
one page
sound/action pattern
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping

After Reading:

Sound Sorting
Sort sounds into loud or quiet
“Which sounds are loud? Which are quiet?”

Oral Language Expansion
“What sounds did we hear?”
Use pattern: “We ___.”

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What sounds can you make?”
“Show me a quiet sound.”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus: make / clap
Build and read simple words
Practice initial blend cl-
Segment and blend sounds
1::soundswemake.reading_strategy: TPR (clap, sing, laugh); echo reading; sound matching
1::soundswemake.questions_type: 1. What sound do hands make? 2. What sound do we make when we are happy? 3. Who do we talk to? 4. What sound is quiet? 5. Which words are action words? (skill) 6. Which words describe sounds? (skill) 7. What sound can YOU make?
1::soundswemake.answers: 1. Clap. 2. Laugh. 3. Friends. 4. Loud laugh/clap. 5. Clap, sing, laugh, talk. 6. Loud, happy. 7. I can ___.
1::soundswemake.oral_prompts: “Make a soft sound. Make a loud sound.”
1::soundswemake.extension: Sound cards; draw something that makes noise; clap/sing/laugh activity

1::thingsthatfeelhotandcold.title: Things That Feel Hot and Cold
1::thingsthatfeelhotandcold.reader_text: Some things feel hot. The soup is hot. The tea is hot, too. Some things feel cold. The ice is cold. The snow is cold, too. I like hot soup. I like cold ice.
1::thingsthatfeelhotandcold.objective: Teach temperature vocabulary; build comparison skills; introduce opposites through real items.
1::thingsthatfeelhotandcold.core_vocab_raw: hot, cold, soup, tea, ice, snow
1::thingsthatfeelhotandcold.sor:
Before Reading:

Teach Opposites Explicitly
“Show me hot.” (pretend hot)
“Show me cold.” (shiver)
Use gestures or real examples

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Concrete Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles:
hot, cold, soup, ice
Use pictures or real-life examples
Connect words to real experiences

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find things that are hot and cold.”

During Reading:

Contrastive Questioning
“Which one is hot?”
“Which one is cold?”
Encourage students to compare using the text

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
“Let’s sound it out.”
“Does that match the picture?”
Connect word → meaning → comparison

Reread for Automaticity
one sentence
one page
contrast pattern
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping

After Reading:

Sort & Explain
Sort items into hot or cold
“This is hot. This is cold.”
Supports categorization and comparison skills

Sentence Frames
“The ___ is hot.”
“The ___ is cold.”
Supports syntax development and complete sentence production

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you like that is hot?”
“What do you like that is cold?”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus: hot / cold
Build and read simple words
Identify short and long vowel sounds
Segment and blend
1::thingsthatfeelhotandcold.reading_strategy: Real/pretend TPR; picture sort; echo reading
1::thingsthatfeelhotandcold.questions_type: 1. What food is hot? 2. What thing is cold? 3. What else feels cold? 4. What does the child like? 5. Which things go in the “hot” group? (skill) 6. Which go in the “cold” group? (skill) 7. Do YOU like hot or cold things?
1::thingsthatfeelhotandcold.answers: 1. Soup/tea. 2. Ice. 3. Snow/ice. 4. Hot soup. 5. Soup/tea. 6. Ice/snow. 7. I like ___.
1::thingsthatfeelhotandcold.extension: Sort hot vs. cold; draw a hot/cold item; temperature TPR
1::thingsthatfeelhotandcold.phonics_rule: short o (hot), long o (cold), digraph th (that, things)

1::mypetdog.title: My Pet Dog
1::mypetdog.reader_text: This is my dog. My dog runs fast. My dog plays with a ball. My dog drinks water. My dog eats food. My dog wags its tail. My dog sleeps now. I love my dog.
1::mypetdog.objective: Build pet-related action vocabulary; strengthen simple sequence understanding; build SEL connection (“I love my dog”).
1::mypetdog.core_vocab_raw: dog, play, drink, eat, food, tail, sleep
1::mypetdog.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Short + Explicit)
“This story is about a pet dog.”
“What does a dog do?”
Students share or act out simple actions.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Concrete Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles.
play, drink, eat, sleep.
Act out each action.
Connect spoken word to meaning and print.

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find out what the dog does.”

During Reading:

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
“Let’s sound it out.”
“What is the dog doing?”
Connect word to action and meaning.

Echo and Choral Reading for Fluency
Repeat pattern: “My dog ___.”
Teacher models and students echo.

Reread for Automaticity
Read one sentence, one page, and the action pattern.
Repeated reading strengthens orthographic mapping.

Light Sequencing Support
“What happened first?”
“What happened next?”

After Reading:

Act-It-Out Retell
Students act out actions in order.
Support comprehension through movement.

Oral Sequencing
Use simple order: First, Next, Then.
Support early sequence understanding.

Sentence Frames
“My dog ___.”
Support verb use and sentence building.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What does your pet do?”
“Do you love your pet?”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on dog and pet.
Build and read simple words.
Identify short vowel sounds.
Segment and blend.
1::mypetdog.questions_type: 1. What does the dog play with? 2. What does the dog drink? 3. When does the dog sleep? 4. What does the tail do? 5. Which words show actions? (skill) 6. What comes last in the story? (skill) 7. What does YOUR pet do?
1::mypetdog.answers: 1. A ball. 2. Water. 3. At the end. 4. Wags. 5. Runs/plays/drinks/eats/wags/sleeps. 6. Sleeping. 7. My pet ___.

1::springdays.title: Spring Days
1::springdays.reader_text: “The weather is warm and nice. Beautiful flowers grow in gardens. Happy birds sing pretty songs. I go outside to play. The sunshine feels soft on my face. I like the fresh spring air. Spring is a lovely season. ”
1::springdays.objective: Introduce descriptive season vocabulary; build sensory connections; support early inference based on descriptive details.
1::springdays.core_vocab_raw: warm, grow, garden, sing, sunshine, outside
1::springdays.sor:
Before Reading:

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find out what spring feels like.”
“We are looking for words that describe spring.”

Sensory Activation (Oral Language Development)
“What can you see in spring?”
“What can you feel or smell?”
Build sensory schema before reading.

Vocabulary Preview (2–3 Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles.
warm, sunshine, outside.
Use gestures, visuals, and student-friendly definitions.

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Look at letters, sound, blend, then check meaning.

Track Descriptive Language in Text
“Which word tells about spring?”
Students point to describing words.
Build awareness of descriptive vocabulary.

Reread for Automaticity
Reread sentences to strengthen phrasing and understanding.
Repeated reading supports fluency and comprehension.

After Reading:

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show me something warm.”
“Which word tells about sunshine?”
Reinforce descriptive language.

Oral Language Production (Sentence Frames)
“I like ___.”
“It feels ___.”
Support sentence building and expression.

Simple Inference Discussion
“How does spring feel?”
“Why do people like spring?”
Support early inference using text clues.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on sun → run → fun.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::springdays.reading_strategy: Picture walk; sensory discussion; echo reading
1::springdays.questions_type: 1. What grows in spring? 2. Who sings songs? 3. What does the sunshine feel like? 4. Where does the child play? 5. Which words describe spring? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What do YOU see in spring?
1::springdays.answers: 1. Flowers. 2. Birds. 3. Soft/warm. 4. Outside. 5. Warm, pretty, soft, fresh. 6. Spring is nice and full of nature. 7. I see ___.
1::springdays.oral_prompts: “How does spring feel?” “What do you see outside?”
1::springdays.extension: Draw spring scene; sort spring vs. winter; describe using senses

1::upanddownwego.title: Up and Down We Go
1::upanddownwego.reader_text: “The red ball goes up. The ball goes down. The swing goes up high. The swing goes down low. I throw the ball up. I catch it down. Up and down we go. It is so much fun!”
1::upanddownwego.objective: Introduce directional concepts; support describing movement; build verb vocabulary (throw, catch).
1::upanddownwego.core_vocab_raw: swing, up, down, high, low, throw, catch, fun
1::upanddownwego.sor:
Before Reading:

Set a Purpose for Reading
“We are looking for how things move.”
“Let’s find words that show direction.”

TPR Direction Practice (Concept Development)
“Show me up.”
“Show me down.”
Act out high and low with body movement.
Connect movement to meaning before reading.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles.
up, down, high, low.
Use gestures and repetition.

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Map and read high-frequency words: up, down.
Look at letters, sound, blend, then confirm meaning.

Track Direction Words in Text
“Which word shows up?”
“Which word shows down?”
Students point to direction words while reading.
Build concept and print connection.

Reread for Automaticity
Repeat pattern: “goes up… goes down.”
Repeated reading strengthens fluency and orthographic mapping.

After Reading:

Physical Application (TPR Transfer)
“Put your hands up high.”
“Touch the floor down low.”
Reinforce meaning through movement.

Sentence Production
“It goes up.”
“It goes down.”
Support sentence structure and concept use.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
Match word to movement.
“Show up/down/high/low.”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on up → cup → pup.
Practice short vowel sounds.
Segment and blend words.
1::upanddownwego.questions_type: 1. Where does the ball go?  2. Where does the ball go? 3. Where does the swing go? 4. Where do I throw the ball?
1::upanddownwego.answers: 1. The ball goes up. 2. The ball goes low. 3. The swing goes high. 4. I throw the ball up.
1::upanddownwego.extension: Toss a soft ball; sort up/down pictures; draw “up” and “down.”
1::upanddownwego.phonics_rule: short u (up), long ow (down), long o (go)

1::withmymom.title: With My Mom
1::withmymom.reader_text: “My mom cooks food. I help her too. My mom reads books. I listen closely. We play a game. We laugh and smile. I love my mom. She is the best!”
1::withmymom.objective: Build family-themed vocabulary; develop action verbs; reinforce simple narrative sequence.
1::withmymom.core_vocab_raw: mom, help, read, listen, smile, cook, food, game, best
1::withmymom.sor:
Before Reading:

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find out what the child and mom do together.”
“We are looking for actions in the story.”

Activate Background Knowledge (Relationships)
“What do you do with your mom?”
Build connection to lived experience.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles.
cook, read, play, listen.
Use gestures and modeling.

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Sound out action words, then confirm meaning.

Track Sentence Pattern
“My mom ___.”
“We ___.”
Students repeat and read using the pattern.
Support syntax awareness.

Reread for Automaticity
Reread short sentences with expression.
Repeated reading supports fluency.

After Reading:

Oral Retell (Sequencing)
Use simple order: First, Next.
Students describe actions in order.
Support early sequence understanding.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
Act out verbs (cook, read, play, listen).
Connect action to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you like to do with your family?”
“Who do you love?”
Build personal connection and SEL language.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on cook → book → look.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::withmymom.reading_strategy: Echo reading; TPR for actions; picture walk
1::withmymom.questions_type: 1. What does the mom cook? 2. What does the child do? 3. What does mom read? 4. What do they play? 5. Which actions show they are happy? (skill) 6. What comes first in the story? (skill) 7. What do YOU do with your family?
1::withmymom.answers: 1. Food. 2. Helps. 3. Books. 4. A game. 5. Laugh/smile. 6. Mom cooks. 7. I ___ with my family.
1::withmymom.extension: Draw a family activity; sequence cards; dramatize actions
1::withmymom.syntax_focus: Simple compound sentences (“My mom ___ and I ___.”)

1::atthepond.title: At the Pond
1::atthepond.reader_text:
See the pond.
A frog is green. The frog jumps.
A duck is white. The duck swims.
A turtle is brown. The turtle rests.
A fish is small. The fish swims.
They are at the pond.
The pond is their home.
1::atthepond.objective: Build animal habitat vocabulary; teach describing animals by color and action; connect animals to “home.”
1::atthepond.core_vocab_raw: frog, turtle, fish, pond, green, white, brown, home
1::atthepond.sor:
Before Reading:

Set a Purpose for Reading
“We will learn about animals at the pond.”
“We are looking for what animals do.”

Picture Walk & Background Knowledge
“What animals do you see?”
“Where do they live?”
Build connection to habitat and prior knowledge.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles.
pond, frog, turtle, fish.
Use visuals and gestures to support meaning.

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Sound out animal names, then confirm meaning.

Track Sentence Structure
“A ___ is ___.”
Students read and repeat the pattern.
Support noun and description pairing.

Reread for Automaticity
Repeat patterned sentences.
Repeated reading supports fluency and comprehension.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“A frog is green.”
“The fish swims.”
Support descriptive sentence building.

Categorization Task
Sort animals by where they live.
“These animals live in the pond.”
Support understanding of habitat.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Which animal jumps/swims/rests?”
Match animals to actions.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What animals do you see outside?”
“Where do they live?”
Build real-world connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on fish → dish → wish.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::atthepond.reading_strategy: Picture walk; color sort; echo reading
1::atthepond.questions_type: 1. What animals live in the pond? 2. What color is the frog? 3. What does the turtle do? 4. Where do the animals live? 5. Which words describe the animals? (skill) 6. Which animals swim or jump? (skill) 7. What animals do YOU see near water?
1::atthepond.answers: 1. Frog/duck/turtle/fish. 2. Green. 3. Rests. 4. In the pond. 5. Green/white/brown/small. 6. Frog jumps; duck/fish swim. 7. I see ___.
1::atthepond.extension: Classify animals by action; draw pond animals; color-by-habitat

1::thingsthatareopposite.title: Things That Are Opposite
1::thingsthatareopposite.reader_text: This dog is big. That dog is small. This car goes fast. That car goes slow. The bird flies up. The worm goes down. The soup is hot. The ice is cold. These are opposites.
1::thingsthatareopposite.objective: Teach opposites; support attribute comparison; develop early descriptive vocabulary.
1::thingsthatareopposite.sor:
Before Reading:

Set a Purpose for Reading
“We will learn how things can be opposite.”
“Let’s find pairs that are different.”

Activate Background Knowledge (Opposites)
“What is big?”
“What is small?”
Use real-life examples and gestures.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles.
big, fast, slow, worm.
Model opposite pairs (big/small, fast/slow).

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Sound out simple words, then confirm meaning.

Track Opposite Pairs in Text
“Which one is big?”
“Which one is small?”
Students point to contrasting pairs.

Build comparison and meaning.
Reread for Pattern Recognition
Repeat pattern: “This is ___.” “That is ___.”
Build fluency and syntax awareness.

After Reading:

Opposite Matching Task
Match opposite pairs.
“Big goes with small.”
Support comparison skills.

Sentence Production
“This is big.”
“That is small.”
Support complete sentence use and structure.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
Act out opposite pairs.
“Show fast. Show slow.”

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What is something big?”
“What is something small?”

Build real-world connection.
Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on big → dig → pig and hot → not → pot.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::thingsthatareopposite.questions_type: 1. Which dog is big? 2. Which dog is small? 3. What goes up? 4. What goes down? 5. Which pairs show opposites? (skill) 6. What does “opposite” mean? (skill) 7. Can YOU name something that is hot or cold?
1::thingsthatareopposite.answers: 1. This dog. 2. That dog. 3. The bird. 4. The worm. 5. Big/small; fast/slow; hot/cold; up/down. 6. Two things that are very different. 7. A ___ is big/small.
1::thingsthatareopposite.extension: Opposites sort; draw opposite pairs; demonstrate fast vs. slow
1::thingsthatareopposite.syntax_focus: Simple compare structure (“___ is ___ but ___ is ___.”)

1::facesshowfeelings.title: Faces Show Feelings
1::facesshowfeelings.reader_text:
Faces can show feelings. 
This is a happy face. 
This is a sad face. 
This is a tired face. 
This is a funny face. 
I can make faces. 
My friend laughs at them. 
Feelings are on faces.
1::facesshowfeelings.objective: Build emotion vocabulary; support SEL through facial expression identification; make connections between visuals and words.
1::facesshowfeelings.sor:
Before Reading:
Set a Purpose for Reading
“We will learn how faces show feelings.”

Activate Background Knowledge (SEL)
“How does your face look when you are happy?”
Students show facial expressions.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: sad, tired, funny, feeling.
Show each feeling using facial expressions and a mirror.
Say each word and have students repeat.
Model each expression and connect the word to the feeling.

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Sound out feeling words, then confirm meaning.

Track Sentence Pattern
“This is a ___ face.”
Students read and repeat the pattern.

Support fluency and sentence structure.
Monitor Understanding
“How does this person feel?”
“How can you tell?”
Support visual-to-word connection.

After Reading:

Emotion Identification Task
Match face to feeling word.
“This face is happy/sad.”
Support emotion recognition.

Sentence Production
“This is a ___ face.”
Support complete sentence use.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“When do you feel happy?”
“Show me a tired face.”
Build personal connection and SEL language.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on sad → mad → bad.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::facesshowfeelings.reading_skill: Identifying feelings; matching facial expressions; categorizing
1::facesshowfeelings.questions_type: 1. What feeling is the face? 2. What face is tired? 3. Who laughs? 4. What can faces show? 5. Which faces are emotions? (skill) 6. How do we know a face is happy or sad? (skill) 7. What face can YOU make?
1::facesshowfeelings.answers: 1. Happy. 2. The tired face. 3. The friend. 4. Feelings. 5. Happy/sad/tired/funny. 6. By the mouth/eyes. 7. I can make a ___ face.
1::facesshowfeelings.extension: Make emotion masks; draw feelings; sort faces

1::savingforsomethingspecial.title: Saving for Something Special
1::savingforsomethingspecial.reader_text: I have one coin. I put it in my jar. I want a new book. I save my coins. I have many coins now. I can buy my book. I feel very proud. Saving is a good thing.”
1::savingforsomethingspecial.objective: Build early financial literacy vocabulary; introduce saving and goal setting; develop SEL feeling (“proud”).
1::savingforsomethingspecial.core_vocab_raw: coin, jar, save, book, proud, buy
1::savingforsomethingspecial.sor:
Before:

Set a Purpose for Reading
“We will learn how saving helps you get something you want.”

Activate Background Knowledge
Ask:
“Have you ever saved money?”

Explicit Vocabulary Instruction
Teach: coin, save, buy, proud
Use visuals and simple examples.

Phonological Awareness Warm-Up
Segment: coin, buy
Blend sounds.

During:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
Sound out decodable words.

Track Sequence of Events
Identify: coin → save → buy → proud

Monitor Understanding
Ask:
“What does the child want?”
“What happens first?”

Reread for Fluency
Practice key sentences.

After:

Sequencing Task
Use: “First… Next… Then…”

Sentence Production
“I save for ___.”

Text-to-Self Connection
“What would you save for?”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
coin (oi pattern) → point → join
1::savingforsomethingspecial.reading_strategy: Picture walk; sequencing strips; echo reading
1::savingforsomethingspecial.questions_type: 1. What does the child save? 2. Where does the child put the coin? 3. What does the child want to buy? 4. How does the child feel at the end? 5. What comes first, next, last? (skill) 6. What is the main idea of the story? (skill) 7. What would YOU save for?
1::savingforsomethingspecial.answers: 1. Coins. 2. In the jar. 3. A book. 4. Proud. 5. Coin → jar → saving → buying. 6. Saving helps you get something special. 7. I would save for ___.
1::savingforsomethingspecial.extension: Draw what they would save for; sort “want vs. need”; pretend store

1::tommygetsready.title: Tommy Gets Ready
1::tommygetsready.reader_text: “Tommy gets dressed now. He eats his breakfast. He takes his school bag. He walks to school. He sees his friends there. He goes into class. He is ready to learn. He has a good day.”
1::tommygetsready.objective: Build morning routine vocabulary; reinforce sequencing; build school-readiness language.
1::tommygetsready.sor:
Before Reading:

Set a Purpose for Reading
“We will read about getting ready for school.”
“Let’s find out what he does first.”

Activate Background Knowledge (Routine)
“What do you do before school?”
Students share or act out simple steps.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach key words using SoR principles.
dress, breakfast, learn.
Use gestures and real-life connections.

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Sound out routine words, then confirm meaning.

Track Sequence of Actions
“What does he do first?”
“What comes next?”
Students follow the order of events.
Support understanding of sequence.

Reread for Automaticity
Repeat short sentences and routine pattern.
Repeated reading supports fluency.

After Reading:

Sequencing Task
Students retell steps in order.
“First… Next… Then…”
Support sequence understanding.

Sentence Production
“He ___.”
“He walks to school.”
Support sentence building and clarity.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
Act out routine verbs.
Connect actions to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you do before school?”
Build real-life connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on bag → tag → rag.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::tommygetsready.questions_type: 1. What does Tom do first? 2. What does he eat? 3. Who does he see? 4. Where does he go? 5. What steps happen in order? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What do YOU do in the morning?
1::tommygetsready.answers: 1. Gets dressed. 2. Breakfast. 3. Friends. 4. School/class. 5. Dressed→eat→bag→walk→friends→class. 6. Getting ready for school. 7. I ___.
1::tommygetsready.extension: Sequence cards; draw your morning; dramatize school walk

1::countingpumpkins.title: Counting Pumpkins
1::countingpumpkins.reader_text: Mia sees one pumpkin. Mia sees two pumpkins. Mia sees three pumpkins. The pumpkins are orange. The pumpkins are different. The pumpkins are in a patch. She likes the pumpkins. Mia picks her favorite pumpkin.”
1::countingpumpkins.objective: Build counting and describing vocabulary; introduce attribute language (pumpkin, orange, patch, pick).
1::countingpumpkins.core_vocab_raw: pumpkin, orange, patch, different, pick, favorite
1::countingpumpkins.sor:
Before Reading:

Set a Purpose for Reading
“We will count and describe pumpkins.”
“Let’s find how many pumpkins we see.”

Activate Background Knowledge
“Have you seen pumpkins?”
Build connection to real-life experience.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: pumpkin, orange, patch, pick.
Show each word using pictures or real objects.
Say each word and have students repeat.
Explain each word with a short, simple meaning.

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Sound out number words and nouns, then confirm meaning.

Track Counting in Text
“How many pumpkins?”
Students count aloud with each sentence.
Support number and quantity connection.

Reread for Automaticity
Repeat counting sentences.
Repeated reading supports fluency and pattern recognition.

After Reading:

Counting Application Task
“Show me three pumpkins.”
Count objects using fingers or classroom items.
Reinforce number-to-quantity connection.

Sentence Production
“She sees ___ pumpkins.”
Support number + noun structure.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Which pumpkins are the same?”
“Which are different?”
Support attribute understanding.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“Which pumpkin do you like?”
Build personal connection and language use.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on patch → match → catch.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::countingpumpkins.reading_strategy: Picture sort; color recognition; echo reading
1::countingpumpkins.questions_type: 1. How many pumpkins does Mia see first? 2. How many pumpkins does she see next? 3. What color are the pumpkins? 4. Where are they? 5. Which pumpkin is her favorite? (skill) 6. What word shows they are not the same? (skill) 7. What is YOUR favorite fruit or vegetable?
1::countingpumpkins.answers: 1. One. 2. Two/three. 3. Orange. 4. In a patch. 5. (Student answer based on picture.) 6. Different. 7. My favorite is ___.
1::countingpumpkins.extension: Count classroom objects; draw a pumpkin; sort same/different

1::whereanimalslive.title: Where Animals Live
1::whereanimalslive.reader_text: “Animals live in homes. A bird lives in a tree. A fish lives in water. A rabbit lives in a hole. A bear lives in a cave. Each home is different. It is right for them.”
1::whereanimalslive.objective: Build habitat vocabulary; compare animal homes; strengthen categorization.
1::whereanimalslive.sor:
Before Reading:

Set a Purpose for Reading
“We will learn where animals live.”
“Let’s find each animal’s home.”

Activate Background Knowledge
“Where do birds live?”
“Where do fish live?”
Build connection to real-world knowledge.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: home, hole, cave.
Show each word using pictures or simple drawings.
Say each word and have students repeat.
Explain each word with a short, simple meaning.

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Sound out animal and home words, then confirm meaning.

Track Sentence Pattern
“A ___ lives in ___.”
Students read and repeat the pattern.
Support sentence structure and meaning.

Reread for Automaticity
Repeat pattern sentences.
Repeated reading supports fluency and comprehension.

After Reading:

Matching Task
Match animals to their homes.
“The bear lives in a cave.”
Support categorization and understanding.

Sentence Production
“A ___ lives in ___.”
Support complete sentence use.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“What is a home?”
Discuss meaning using examples.
Support concept understanding.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“Where do you live?”
Build real-world connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on home → dome → come.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::whereanimalslive.reading_strategy: Picture walk; sort animals by home; echo reading
1::whereanimalslive.reading_skill: Matching animals to homes; describing; comparing
1::whereanimalslive.questions_type: 1. Where does the bird live? 2. Where does the rabbit live? 3. What home is for the fish? 4. What home is for the bear? 5. Which homes are different? (skill) 6. Why is each home “right” for them? (skill) 7. What animal home do YOU know?
1::whereanimalslive.answers: 1. Tree. 2. Hole. 3. Water. 4. Cave. 5. Tree/hole/water/cave. 6. It fits the animal. 7. A ___ lives in ___.
1::whereanimalslive.extension: Sort animals by home; draw habitat; dramatize animal movements

1::colorfulfruits.title: Colorful Fruits
1::colorfulfruits.reader_text: “An apple is red. A banana is yellow. A grape is purple. An orange is orange. I see many colors. I like fruit.”
1::colorfulfruits.objective: Introduce color and fruit vocabulary; reinforce simple present tense (is); strengthen reading of a highly repetitive sentence pattern.
1::colorfulfruits.core_vocab_raw: apple, red, banana, yellow, grape, purple, orange, see, many, color, like, fruit
1::colorfulfruits.sor:
Before Reading:

Set a Purpose for Reading
“We will learn the names of fruits and their colors.”
“Let’s find the color for each fruit.”

Activate Background Knowledge (Colors + Food)
“What color is a banana?”
“What fruits do you know?”
Build connection to real-life experience.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: red, yellow, purple, orange.
Show each color using visuals or real objects.
Say each word and have students repeat.
Point to the color and connect it to meaning.

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Sound out simple words, then confirm meaning.

Track Sentence Pattern
“A ___ is ___.”
Students point and read with the teacher.
Support pattern recognition and fluency.

Reread for Automaticity
Repeat sentences to build fluency.
Repeated reading supports pattern recognition.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“A banana is yellow.”
Support complete sentence use and structure.

Application Task
Students describe objects using the same pattern.
“A ___ is ___.”
Support transfer of learning.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Which fruit is red?”
“Which fruit is purple?”
Match fruit to color.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What fruit do you like?”
Build personal connection and language use.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on red → bed → fed.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::colorfulfruits.reading_strategy: Picture walk; color sort; echo reading
1::colorfulfruits.questions_type: 1. What fruit is red? 2. What fruit is yellow? 3. What fruit is purple? 4. What does the child like? 5. Which fruits belong in the “sweet” group? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What is YOUR favorite fruit?
1::colorfulfruits.answers: 1. Apple. 2. Banana. 3. Grapes. 4. Colorful fruits. 5. All fruits in story. 6. Fruits have many colors. 7. My favorite fruit is ___.
1::colorfulfruits.extension: Fruit color sort; draw a fruit; taste/smell activity (if allowed)

1::myhamstershome.title: My Hamster’s Home
1::myhamstershome.reader_text: My hamster runs on the wheel. My hamster eats a small seed. My hamster drinks fresh water. My hamster sleeps in its house. I clean its cage often. My hamster is happy. I love my small pet.
1::myhamstershome.objective: Build pet-care vocabulary; connect actions to purpose; support describing and sequencing.
1::myhamstershome.core_vocab_raw: hamster, wheel, seed, cage, clean, pet
1::myhamstershome.sor:
Before Reading:

Care & Responsibility Awareness
“This story is about taking care of a pet.”
“What does a pet need?”
Connect actions to care (food, water, home).

Experience Activation (Pets)
“Do you have a pet?”
“What do you do for your pet?”
Build real-life connection.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: hamster, wheel, seed, cage.
Show each word with a picture or object.
Say the word, have students repeat.
Connect the word to meaning with a simple explanation.

During Reading:

Action-to-Purpose Connection
“What is the hamster doing?”
“Why does it do that?”
Connect actions to needs (eat, drink, sleep).

Meaning-Focused Reading
“Does that action make sense?”
Students connect each sentence to meaning.
Build comprehension alongside reading.

Fluency Through Repetition
Repeat sentences across pages.
Build smooth reading and confidence.

After Reading:

Routine Sequencing
“What does the hamster do first?”
Use simple order: First, Next, Then.
Support understanding of routines.

Sentence Production
“My hamster ___.”
Support verb use and sentence building.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show me eat.”
“Show me drink.”
“Show me sleep.”
Students act out each action.
Connect words to meaning through movement.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“How do you take care of a pet?”
Build real-world connection and responsibility.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on pet → get → set.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::myhamstershome.questions_type: 1. Where does the hamster run? 2. What does it eat? 3. What does it drink? 4. Where does it sleep? 5. Which words tell the hamster’s actions? (skill) 6. What do we learn about taking care of pets? (skill) 7. What pet do YOU like?
1::myhamstershome.answers: 1. On the wheel. 2. A seed. 3. Water. 4. In its house. 5. Run/eat/drink/sleep. 6. Pets need food/water/clean homes. 7. I like ___.
1::myhamstershome.extension: Draw pet’s home; sort pet needs; sequence hamster actions

1::mytoybear.title: My Toy Bear
1::mytoybear.reader_text: This is my toy bear. It is brown and soft. It has a red bow. I hug it at night. It is my good friend. I take it everywhere. It makes me feel safe. I love my toy bear so much.
1::mytoybear.objective: Build describing vocabulary; introduce emotional safety language; support simple narrative.
1::mytoybear.sor:
Before Reading:

Set a Purpose for Reading
“We will describe a special toy.”
“Let’s find words that tell about the toy.”

Activate Background Knowledge
“What is your favorite toy?”
“How does it feel?”
Build personal connection.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: bow, soft, bear, safe.
Show each word using pictures or a toy if possible.
Say each word and have students repeat.
Explain each word with a short, simple meaning.

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
“Let’s sound it out together.”
Sound out descriptive words, then confirm meaning.

Track Descriptive Language
“Which word tells about the bear?”
Students point to describing words (soft, brown).
Support adjective awareness.

Reread for Automaticity
Repeat sentences to build fluency and confidence.

After Reading:

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show soft.”
“Show hug.”
Students act out the words.
Connect words to meaning through movement.

Sentence Production
“He is ___.”
“I ___ him.”
Support describing and action language.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“Describe your toy.”
“What makes you feel safe?”
Build personal connection and SEL language.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on soft → loft → off.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::mytoybear.reading_skill: Describing attributes; emotional connections; main idea
1::mytoybear.questions_type: 1. What color is the bear? 2. What does the bear wear? 3. When does the child hug the bear? 4. Why does the bear feel special? 5. Which words describe the bear? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What special toy do YOU love?
1::mytoybear.answers: 1. Brown. 2. A red bow. 3. At night. 4. It feels safe. 5. Brown, soft, red bow. 6. The toy bear is a special friend. 7. I love my ___.
1::mytoybear.extension: Draw your bear; describe textures; sort soft vs. hard objects

1::colorsaroundme.title: Colors Around Me
1::colorsaroundme.reader_text: “I see a red apple. I see a blue car. I see a green leaf. I see a yellow sun. Colors are here. Colors are there. I see colors all day. I like to name the colors I see.”
1::colorsaroundme.objective: Build color-word vocabulary; identify objects by attributes; connect color to world awareness.
1::colorsaroundme.sor:
Before Reading:

Color Awareness Activation
“What colors do you see around you?”
“Point to something red or blue.”
Build awareness of colors in the environment.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: red, blue, green, yellow.
Show each color using objects or pictures.
Say each word and have students repeat.
Point to the color and connect it to meaning.

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find the colors we see in the story.”

During Reading:

Patterned Observation Reading
“I see a ___ ___.”
Students point and read with the teacher.
Support pattern recognition and fluency.

Attribute Identification
“What color is it?”
Students identify the color of each object.
Connect word to visual detail.

Reread for Automaticity
Repeat sentences across pages.
Build confidence with repeated structure.

After Reading:

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Point to colors.”
Students identify colors in the text or classroom.
Strengthen word-to-object connection.

Sentence Production
“I see a ___ ___.”
Students describe objects using the pattern.
Support complete sentence use.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“Name colors around you.”
Build real-world awareness and language use.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on red → bed → fed.
Map sounds to letters.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::colorsaroundme.reading_strategy: Color sort; picture walk; echo reading
1::colorsaroundme.questions_type: 1. What color is the apple? 2. What color is the leaf? 3. What does the sun look like? 4. What colors does Tommy see? 5. Which words describe brightness? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What colors do YOU see now?
1::colorsaroundme.answers: 1. Red. 2. Green. 3. Yellow. 4. Red/blue/green/yellow. 5. Bright. 6. Colors are everywhere. 7. I see ___.
1::colorsaroundme.extension: Color scavenger hunt; draw colored objects; sort warm/cool colors
1::colorsaroundme.syntax_focus: Location + description structure (“I see ___ around me.”)

1::mynewshoes.title: My New Shoes
1::mynewshoes.reader_text: “These are my new shoes. They are shiny and blue. They have long white laces. I can tie them myself. I run fast in them. I like my new shoes. My shoes make me feel proud. I wear them everywhere I go.”
1::mynewshoes.objective: Build descriptive vocabulary (color/texture); encourage independence (tying); reinforce SEL (“proud”).

Before Reading:

Personal Connection Activation
“Do you have new shoes?”
“What do your shoes look like?”

Descriptive Language Focus

Students identify describing words (shiny, blue, long).
Support attribute awareness.

Action & Meaning Connection
“What can the child do?”
“Show tie/run.”
Students connect actions to meaning.

Fluency Through Repetition
Repeat sentences to build smooth reading.
Build confidence with repeated structure.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“My shoes are ___.”
Support describing using complete sentences.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show tie.”
“Show run.”
Students act out actions.
Connect words to meaning through movement.

1::mynewshoes.questions_type: 1. What color are the shoes? 2. What do the shoes have? 3. What can the child do with the laces? 4. How does the child feel? 5. Which words describe the shoes? (skill) 6. Why does the child feel proud? (skill) 7. What shoes do YOU like to wear?

1::thebigcat.title: The Big Cat
1::thebigcat.reader_text: The cat is very big. The cat runs very fast. The cat jumps so high. The cat sleeps all day. It is a strong cat. The big cat plays with a ball. The big cat is my friend. I like the big cat.

1::countingatbedtime.title: Counting at Bedtime
1::countingatbedtime.reader_text: My bed is cozy. I love my bedtime. I have one soft pillow. I have two warm blankets. I have three small toys. I close my eyes and feel safe.  I have four happy dreams. I sleep well all night long.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: pillow, warm, small, soft.

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s count what we have at bedtime.”

During Reading:

Count & Connect to Meaning
“How many do you have?”
Students count pillows, blankets, and toys.
Connect number words to quantity.

Patterned Sentence Reading
“I have ___.”
Students point and read with the teacher.
Support number + noun structure.

Fluency Through Repetition
Repeat counting sentences.
Build smooth reading and confidence.

After Reading:

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show soft.”
“Show warm.”
“Show cozy.”
Students act out meanings.
Connect words to sensory experience.

Sentence Production
“I have ___.”
Support counting and sentence building.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you have at bedtime?”
Build personal connection and routine language.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on bed / time.
Identify short and long vowel sounds.
Practice blending and segmenting.

1::countingatbedtime.questions_type: 1. How many blankets does the child have? 2. How many toys? 3. What word describes the pillow? 4. Where does the child sleep? 5. Which words describe how bedtime feels? (skill) 6. Which numbers appear in the story? (skill) 7. What do YOU have at bedtime?

1::countingatbedtime.phonics_rule: short e (bed), long i (time silent e)

1::inthegarden.reader_text: “I see a red flower. I see a blue flower. I see a yellow flower. I see a white flower. The garden is pretty. I like the colors. The flowers smell very sweet. The garden makes me smile.”

“Point to colors.”

1::inthegarden.questions_type: 1. What colors are the flowers? 2. What does the garden smell like? 3. Which flower is white? 4. What does the garden make the child do? 5. Which colors describe the garden? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What do YOU see in a garden?

1::letsdance.title: Let’s Dance!
1::letsdance.reader_text: “You can jump up high. You can spin around fast. You can clap your hands. You can dance with me! Dancing is so much fun. Let us dance together! We move to the happy music. We laugh as we dance around.”
1::letsdance.objective: Build action vocabulary; support rhythm + physical response; connect movement to joy.

1::thebutterfly.title: The Butterfly
1::thebutterfly.reader_text:
A butterfly flies away.
 It lands on a flower.
 It drinks sweet nectar.

It flaps its wings.
 It flies gently through the air.
 It rests in the sun.

Butterflies are beautiful.
 I like to watch them.”
1::thebutterfly.objective: Build nature vocabulary; describe butterfly actions; introduce simple life-science observation words.
1::thebutterfly.core_vocab_raw: butterfly, fly, drink, rest, wing, gently
1::thebutterfly.sor:
Before Reading:

Observation & Nature Awareness
“Have you seen a butterfly?”
“What does it do?”
Build connection to real-world experience.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: butterfly, fly, drink, rest.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what the butterfly does.”

During Reading:

Action Tracking in Text
“What does it do?”
Students identify actions (flies, drinks, rests).
Build verb awareness and meaning.

Meaning & Movement Connection
“Show flap/drink/rest.”
Students act out actions.
Connect words to meaning through movement.

Reread for Fluency
Repeat short sentences.
Build smooth reading and confidence.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“It ___.”
Support simple action sentence use.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Which action is flying?”
“Which action is resting?”
Connect words to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection

“What do they do?”
Build observation and language use.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on butterfly.
Break into parts (butter + fly).

1::thebutterfly.questions_type: 1. What does the butterfly land on? 2. What does it drink? 3. Where does it rest? 4. What moves when it flaps? 5. Which words describe the butterfly? (skill) 6. Which words show actions? (skill) 7. Have YOU seen a butterfly?
1::thebutterfly.answers: 1. A flower. 2. Nectar. 3. In the sun. 4. Its wings. 5. Beautiful, gentle. 6. Flies/lands/drinks/rests/flaps. 7. Yes/No → (only yes/no question allowed).
1::thebutterfly.extension: Draw a butterfly; sort actions; color wings
1::thebutterfly.phonics_rule: long y (butterfly), compound word structure (butter + fly)
