1::atthemall.reading_skill: Identifying categories; describing; connecting to places
1::atthemall.questions_type: 1. What clothes does the child see? 2. What food does the child see? 3. What toys does the child see? 4. Who is the child with? 5. Which things belong at a mall? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What do YOU like to see at the mall?
1::atthemall.answers: 1. Pretty clothes. 2. Yummy food. 3. Cool toys. 4. Family. 5. Clothes/food/toys. 6. The mall has many things to see. 7. I like to see ___.
1::atthemall.extension: Sort mall items; draw a mall; categorize objects

1::raccoonsnightout.title: Raccoon’s Night Out
1::raccoonsnightout.reader_text: “I see a raccoon. The raccoon has a mask. The raccoon runs at night. The raccoon climbs a tree. The raccoon eats some food. The raccoon sleeps all day. Raccoons are nocturnal. They are wild animals.”
1::raccoonsnightout.objective: Build animal-behavior vocabulary; introduce nocturnal/diurnal concepts; connect actions to time of day.
1::raccoonsnightout.core_vocab_raw: raccoon, climb, eat, sleep, nocturnal, wild
1::raccoonsnightout.sor:
Before Reading:

Time-of-Day Awareness
“What do you do at night?”
“What do animals do at night?”
Build connection to day and night routines.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: raccoon, climb, eat, sleep.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what the raccoon does at night.”

During Reading:

Action & Time Connection
“What does the raccoon do?”
“When does it do that?”
Students connect actions to night/day.
Build understanding of behavior.

Meaning & Movement Connection
“Show night/day.”
Students act out or signal time of day.
Connect words to meaning.

Reread for Fluency
Repeat sentences across pages.
Build smooth reading and confidence.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“The raccoon ___.”
Support action sentence building.

Concept Reinforcement (Night vs Day)
“Does it sleep at night or day?”
“What does it do at night?”
Support understanding of nocturnal behavior.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on raccoon / night.
Identify long vowel patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::raccoonsnightout.reading_strategy: Picture walk; day/night sort; echo reading
1::raccoonsnightout.questions_type: 1. When does the raccoon run? 2. Where does it climb? 3. What does it eat? 4. When does it sleep? 5. What does “nocturnal” mean? (skill) 6. Which actions show nighttime behavior? (skill) 7. What animal do YOU see at night?
1::raccoonsnightout.answers: 1. At night. 2. A tree. 3. Food. 4. Daytime. 5. Animals active at night. 6. Running/climbing/eating at night. 7. I see ___.
1::raccoonsnightout.extension: Night vs. day animal sort; draw raccoon; simple fact chart

1::icanskate.title: I Can Skate!
1::icanskate.reader_text: “I can skate forward. I can stop quickly. I can turn around. I can smile big. Skating is so fun. I feel very free. I practice every day. I get better and better.”
1::icanskate.objective: Build action vocabulary related to movement; reinforce growth mindset (“practice,” “better and better”).
1::icanskate.core_vocab_raw: skate, stop, turn, can, quickly, forward, free
1::icanskate.sor:
Before Reading:

Skill & Practice Awareness
“Can you skate or ride?”
“What do you practice?”
Build connection to learning new skills.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: skate, stop, turn, can.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what the child can do.”

During Reading:

Action Tracking in Text
“What can the child do?”
Students identify actions (skate, stop, turn).
Build action vocabulary and meaning.

Meaning & Movement Connection
“Show stop/turn/smile.”
Students act out actions.
Connect words to meaning.

Reread for Fluency
Repeat sentences across pages.
Build confidence with repeated pattern “I can ___.”

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“I can ___.”
Support action sentence use.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What can you do?”
Build confidence and personal connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on skate / can.
Identify long vowel and modal word use.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::icanskate.questions_type: 1. What can the child skate? 2. What can the child do quickly? 3. What does the child practice? 4. When does the child get better? 5. Which words show movement? (skill) 6. Which words show improvement? (skill) 7. What skill do YOU practice?
1::icanskate.answers: 1. Forward. 2. Stop. 3. Riding/skating. 4. After practicing every day. 5. Skate/stop/turn. 6. Better and better. 7. I practice ___.
1::icanskate.extension: Draw movement actions; practice patterns; SEL reflection
1::icanskate.phonics_rule: long a (skate silent e), modal can

1::icecreamtime.title: Ice Cream Time
1::icecreamtime.reader_text: “We get some ice cream. We add fresh fruit. We add sweet syrup. We eat it together. Ice cream is delicious. It is a sweet treat. I like to try new flavors. Ice cream makes me happy.”
1::icecreamtime.objective: Build food-preparation vocabulary; categorize ingredients; develop describing words (sweet/delicious).
1::icecreamtime.sor:
Before Reading:

Food Experience Activation
“Do you like ice cream?”
“What do you put on ice cream?”
Build connection to real-life experience.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: fresh, sweet, delicious.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what we add to ice cream.”

During Reading:

Ingredient Identification
“What do we add?”
Students identify fruit and syrup.
Support understanding of ingredients.

Action & Meaning Connection
“We ___ the ___.”
Students read and repeat the pattern.
Connect actions (add, eat) to meaning.

Reread for Fluency
Repeat sentences across pages.
Build smooth reading and confidence.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“We ___ the ___.”
Support action + object sentence structure.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Describe sweet/delicious.”
Students use words to describe taste.
Support descriptive language.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you like on ice cream?”
Build personal connection and language use.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on ice / time / cream.
Identify long vowel and vowel team patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::icecreamtime.reading_strategy: Picture walk; echo reading; sensory prompting
1::icecreamtime.questions_type: 1. What do they add to the ice cream? 2. What do they eat together? 3. What word describes the ice cream? 4. What makes the child happy? 5. Which words describe taste? (skill) 6. What ingredients belong in the “toppings” group? (skill) 7. What flavor do YOU like?
1::icecreamtime.answers: 1. Fruit and syrup. 2. Ice cream. 3. Delicious. 4. Ice cream. 5. Sweet/delicious. 6. Fruit, syrup. 7. I like ___.
1::icecreamtime.extension: Draw an ice cream bowl; categorize toppings vs. foods; flavor chart
1::icecreamtime.phonics_rule: long i (ice, time silent e), vowel team ea (cream)
1::icecreamtime.syntax_focus: Simple time-based sentences (“It is time for ___.”)
1::icecreamtime.comprehension_tier: Expressive, joyful tone; smooth compound phrasing

1::mycolorfuleggs.title: My Colorful Eggs
1::mycolorfuleggs.reader_text: I have one white egg. I have two white eggs. I have three white eggs. I paint my eggs with bright colors. My eggs are colorful now. They are red, blue, and yellow.  My colorful eggs look beautiful.
1::mycolorfuleggs.objective: Build color vocabulary in sequence; reinforce counting; connect creation to result (cause/effect).
1::mycolorfuleggs.core_vocab_raw: white, red, blue, yellow, bright, paint, colorful
1::mycolorfuleggs.sor:
Before Reading:

Counting & Color Activation
“Let’s count to three.”
“What colors do you know?”
Build number and color awareness.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: white, red, blue, yellow.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find how the eggs change.”

During Reading:

Count & Track in Text
“How many eggs?”
Students count with each sentence.
Connect number words to quantity.

Color Change Awareness
“What color are the eggs now?”
Students notice change from white to colorful.
Support cause and effect.

Reread for Fluency
Repeat patterned sentences.
Build confidence with repetition.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“My eggs are ___.”
Support describing using color words.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show white/red/blue/yellow.”
Students point or act out colors.
Connect words to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What colors do you like?”
Build personal connection and expression.
1::mycolorfuleggs.reading_strategy: Color sort; echo reading; picture walk
1::mycolorfuleggs.reading_skill: Counting; identifying colors; sequencing a change
1::mycolorfuleggs.questions_type: 1. How many eggs do they have first? 2. What color are the eggs before painting? 3. What colors are the eggs after painting? 4. What makes the eggs colorful? 5. Which words describe the eggs? (skill) 6. What step changes the eggs? (skill) 7. What would YOU paint?
1::mycolorfuleggs.answers: 1. One, two, three. 2. White. 3. Red, blue, yellow. 4. Bright paints. 5. Colorful, bright, beautiful. 6. Painting. 7. I would paint ___.
1::mycolorfuleggs.extension: Paint sorting; sequencing “before/after”; draw egg designs

1::flyakite.title: Fly a Kite!
1::flyakite.reader_text: “I fly my kite. The kite goes up high. The kite turns in the wind. The kite falls down fast. I run with my kite. Flying a kite is fun. The bright colors shine in the sky. I want to fly it again tomorrow.”
1::flyakite.objective: Build movement/action vocabulary; reinforce direction words; connect cause/effect (wind → turning).
1::flyakite.core_vocab_raw: up, down, turn, fall, shine, sky
1::flyakite.sor:
Before Reading:

Movement & Direction Activation
“Show me up.”
“Show me down.”
Introduce direction through movement.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: up, down, turn, fall.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find how the kite moves.”

During Reading:

Direction & Action Tracking
“What is the kite doing?”
Students identify actions (goes up, turns, falls).
Build understanding of movement.

Cause & Effect Awareness
“What makes the kite turn?”
Students connect wind to movement.
Support simple cause and effect.

Reread for Fluency
Repeat patterned sentences.
Build smooth reading and confidence.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“The kite ___.”
Support action sentence use.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show up/down/turn.”
Students act out movement.
Connect words to meaning through motion.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“Do you fly a kite?”
Build personal connection and experience.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on kite / fly / sky.
Identify long i patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::flyakite.questions_type: 1. Where does the kite go? 2. What makes the kite turn? 3. What happens when the kite falls? 4. What shines in the sky? 5. Which words show movement? (skill) 6. Which words describe the kite’s colors? (skill) 7. Have YOU ever flown a kite?
1::flyakite.answers: 1. Up high. 2. Wind. 3. It goes down fast. 4. The bright colors. 5. Fly/turn/run/goes/falls. 6. Bright. 7. Yes/No.
1::flyakite.extension: Draw a kite; make paper kites; sort wind vs. no-wind pictures
1::flyakite.phonics_rule: long i (fly, sky), long i silent e (kite)
1::flyakite.comprehension_tier: Lively pacing; stress on action verbs

1::sportsweplay.title: Sports We Play
1::sportsweplay.reader_text: “A runner runs very fast. A swimmer swims in water. A player kicks the ball. A jumper jumps up high. Sports are good for you. I like to play sports. Sports make me feel strong. I play them with my friends.”
1::sportsweplay.objective: Build action vocabulary across sports; categorize by movement; support SEL (strong, healthy).
1::sportsweplay.core_vocab_raw: run, swim, kick, jump, sport, strong
1::sportsweplay.sor:
Before Reading:

Movement Activation
Teacher models actions: run, jump.
“Show me run.”
“Show me jump.”
Students repeat actions after the teacher.
Build connection through physical movement.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: run, swim, kick, jump.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what each person does.”

During Reading:

Action Categorization
“Who runs?”
“Who swims?”
Students match actions to people.
Support grouping by movement.

Meaning & Movement Connection
“Show run/swim/kick/jump.”
Students act out actions.
Connect words to meaning.

Reread for Fluency
Repeat sentences across pages.
Build confidence with repeated structure.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“A ___ ___.”
Support subject + action structure.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What sports do you play?”
Build personal connection and expression.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on run / jump / kick.
Identify short vowel sounds.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::sportsweplay.reading_skill: Matching actions to sports; describing movement
1::sportsweplay.questions_type: 1. What does a runner do? 2. Where does a swimmer swim? 3. What does a kicker kick? 4. What does a jumper do? 5. Which sports are movement sports? (skill) 6. How do sports make the child feel? (skill) 7. What sport do YOU like?
1::sportsweplay.answers: 1. Runs. 2. In water. 3. A ball. 4. Jumps high. 5. Running/swimming/kicking/jumping. 6. Strong. 7. I like ___.
1::sportsweplay.extension: Sort sports by movement; draw a sport; role-play

1::cleanordirty.title: Clean or Dirty?
1::cleanordirty.reader_text: “This shirt is clean. This shirt is dirty. I wash the dirty shirt. Now it is clean! It is good to be clean. I like clean clothes. Clean clothes feel soft and nice. Staying clean makes me happy.”
1::cleanordirty.objective: Build hygiene vocabulary; introduce opposites (clean/dirty); describe routine steps (wash → clean).
1::cleanordirty.sor:
Before Reading:

Opposites Awareness
Teacher models: clean and dirty (use gestures or examples).
“Show me clean.”
“Show me dirty.”
Build understanding through contrast.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: clean, dirty, wash.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find how something gets clean.”

During Reading:

Contrastive Thinking
“Is it clean or dirty?”
Students identify opposite states in the text.
Build comparison and meaning.

Cause & Effect Connection
“What makes it clean?”
Students connect washing to the result.
Support simple sequence and outcome.

Reread for Fluency
Repeat key sentences.
Build confidence with repeated structure.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“This is ___.”
Support describing using opposites.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show clean/dirty.”
Students act out or point.
Connect words to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“How do you get clean?”
Build real-world connection and routine awareness.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on clean / dirty.
Identify vowel patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::cleanordirty.reading_strategy: Picture walk; object sort; echo reading
1::cleanordirty.questions_type: 1. Which shirt is clean? 2. Which shirt is dirty? 3. What do we do to dirty clothes? 4. How do clean clothes feel? 5. Which words show opposites? (skill) 6. What step changes dirty to clean? (skill) 7. What do YOU wash at home?
1::cleanordirty.answers: 1. The clean shirt. 2. The dirty shirt. 3. Wash them. 4. Soft and nice. 5. Clean/dirty. 6. Washing. 7. I wash ___.
1::cleanordirty.extension: Sort pictures clean vs. dirty; washing role-play; sequence steps
1::cleanordirty.syntax_focus: Simple compare structure (“___ is clean. ___ is dirty.”)

1::ihearsounds.title: I Hear Sounds
1::ihearsounds.reader_text: “I hear a loud bell. I hear a singing bird. I hear a fast car. I hear happy music. I like to hear sounds. They are all around. Each sound is special to me. I enjoy listening every day.”
1::ihearsounds.objective: Build auditory vocabulary; identify sound sources; distinguish qualities of sound (loud/soft/fast/happy).
1::ihearsounds.core_vocab_raw: hear, bell, bird, car, music, sound, sing, enjoy
1::ihearsounds.sor:
Before Reading:

Listening Activation
Teacher models: make a loud sound, then a soft sound.
“Make loud/soft sounds.”
Students repeat and listen.
Build awareness of sound differences.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: hear, bell, bird, car.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what we can hear.”

During Reading:

Sound Source Identification
“What do you hear?”
Students identify sound sources (bell, bird, car, music).
Connect words to real-world sounds.

Sound Quality Awareness
“Is it loud or soft?”
Students describe sounds using simple adjectives.
Build descriptive listening skills.

Reread for Fluency
Repeat patterned sentences.
Build confidence with repetition.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“I hear a ___.”
Support naming sound sources.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Make loud/soft sounds.”
Students produce sounds.
Connect words to sensory experience.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you hear?”
Build personal connection and awareness.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on hear / sounds.
Identify vowel team ea and plural -s.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::ihearsounds.questions_type: 1. What sound does the bell make? 2. Who is singing? 3. What moves fast? 4. What music does the child hear? 5. Which words describe sound qualities? (skill) 6. Which sounds are special to the child? (skill) 7. What sound do YOU like?
1::ihearsounds.answers: 1. Loud bell. 2. Bird. 3. The car. 4. Happy music. 5. Loud/fast/happy. 6. All the sounds. 7. I like ___.
1::ihearsounds.extension: Draw sound sources; sound walk; match sounds to pictures

1::thingsthatmakemehappy.title: Things That Make Me Happy
1::thingsthatmakemehappy.reader_text: “My dog makes me happy. My mom makes me happy. My good friends make me happy. The warm sun makes me happy. Many things bring joy. I am a happy kid. I smile when I think about them. Happy things make my day bright.”
1::thingsthatmakemehappy.objective: Build SEL/emotion vocabulary; identify sources of joy; connect to personal experience.
1::thingsthatmakemehappy.sor:
Before Reading:

Emotion Awareness
Teacher models: smile and show a happy face.
“Show happy.”
“Show smile.”
Students repeat expressions.
Build understanding of feelings.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: family, pets, sunshine, happy.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what makes the child happy.”

During Reading:

Cause & Feeling Connection
“What makes them happy?”
Students identify people and things (dog, mom, friends, sun).
Connect causes to feelings.

Patterned Sentence Reading
“___ makes me happy.”
Students read and repeat the pattern.
Support sentence structure and meaning.

Reread for Fluency
Repeat sentences across pages.
Build confidence with repetition.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“___ makes me happy.”
Support expressing feelings in complete sentences.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show happy/smile.”
Students act out emotions.
Connect words to feelings.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What makes you happy?”
Build personal connection and expression.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on make / happy.
Identify vowel patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::thingsthatmakemehappy.reading_strategy: Picture walk; echo reading; emotion discussion
1::thingsthatmakemehappy.reading_skill: Identifying feelings; describing causes of feelings
1::thingsthatmakemehappy.questions_type: 1. Who makes the child happy? 2. What in nature makes the child happy? 3. What does the child do when they feel happy? 4. What makes their day bright? 5. Which words describe feelings? (skill) 6. Which things cause happiness? (skill) 7. What makes YOU happy?
1::thingsthatmakemehappy.answers: 1. Mom/dog/friends. 2. Warm sun. 3. Smile. 4. Happy things. 5. Happy, joy, bright. 6. Dog/mom/friends/sun. 7. ___ makes me happy.
1::thingsthatmakemehappy.extension: Draw happiness chart; feelings collage; “happy jar” activity
1::thingsthatmakemehappy.phonics_rule: long a (make silent e), short a (happy)
1::thingsthatmakemehappy.syntax_focus: Simple cause/effect structure (“___ makes me happy.”)

1::mybrotherandi.title: My Brother and I
1::mybrotherandi.reader_text: “My brother runs fast. My brother plays ball. My brother smiles big. I love him so much. We are best friends. We have fun together. We like to play outside. We laugh a lot every day.”
1::mybrotherandi.objective: Build family vocabulary; connect actions with relationships; strengthen SEL & descriptive language.
1::mybrotherandi.sor:
Before Reading:

Relationship Awareness (SEL)
“Who do you play with?”
“Do you have a brother or friend?”
Build connection to relationships.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: run, play, smile, fun.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what the brother does.”

During Reading:

Print Tracking (Concept of Print)
Students point to each word as they read.
Reinforce left-to-right reading and spacing.

Action Identification
“What does the brother do?”
Students identify actions (run, play, smile).
Connect verbs to meaning.

Meaning & Movement Connection
“Show run/play/smile.”
Students act out actions.
Support comprehension through movement.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“My brother ___.”
Support subject + action sentence building.

Relationship Connection (SEL)
“Who do you have fun with?”
Students share personal responses.
Build emotional language and connection.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Which one is playing?”
“Which one is smiling?”
Strengthen action-word understanding.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on brother.
Identify r-controlled vowel er.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::mybrotherandi.reading_strategy: Picture walk; echo reading; family connections
1::mybrotherandi.questions_type: 1. What does the brother do fast? 2. What does he play? 3. What does he do with his mouth? 4. Where do they play? 5. Which actions show they are friends? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What do YOU do with a family member?
1::mybrotherandi.answers: 1. Runs. 2. Ball. 3. Smiles. 4. Outside. 5. Run/play/laugh together. 6. The child loves spending time with his brother. 7. I ___ with my ___.
1::mybrotherandi.extension: Draw family; act out actions; family-photo talk

1::mystrongbody.title: My Strong Body
1::mystrongbody.reader_text: “My arms can move. My legs can run. My hands can clap. My body works hard. I am strong and healthy. I like my strong body. I use my body to play. My body helps me every day.”
1::mystrongbody.objective: Build body vocabulary; support health and movement concepts; promote body awareness.
1::mystrongbody.core_vocab_raw: arm, leg, hand, body, strong, healthy
1::mystrongbody.sor:
Before Reading:

Body Awareness (TPR + Labeling)
Teacher models: touch arms, legs, hands.
“Touch arms/legs/hands.”
Students repeat and identify body parts.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: arm, leg, hand, body.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what our body can do.”

During Reading:

Function Mapping (Body → Action)
“What can the arms do?”
Students connect body parts to actions (run, clap).
Build understanding of function.

Sentence Pattern Practice
“My ___ can ___.”
Students track and repeat the pattern.
Support structure and meaning.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build expression and confidence.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“My body can ___.”
Support complete sentence use.

Vocabulary Reinforcement (TPR)
“Touch arms/legs/hands.”
Students respond physically.
Connect words to body awareness.

Health Connection (Concept Building)
“How do you use your body?”
Support understanding of movement and health.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on strong / body.
Identify -ng and long vowel patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::mystrongbody.reading_skill: Identifying body parts; matching actions to parts
1::mystrongbody.questions_type: 1. What can arms do? 2. What can legs do? 3. What can hands do? 4. How does the child feel about their body? 5. Which actions match which parts? (skill) 6. What is the main idea of the story? (skill) 7. What can YOUR body do?
1::mystrongbody.answers: 1. Move. 2. Run. 3. Clap. 4. Strong/healthy. 5. Arms move/legs run/hands clap. 6. My body can do many things. 7. My body can ___.
1::mystrongbody.extension: Draw body actions; movement game; body-part sort

1::halloweennight.title: Halloween Night
1::halloweennight.reader_text: I wear a funny hat. I carry a big bag. I see bright lights. I say “Trick or treat!” I get lots of candy. Halloween is so fun! I walk with my friends. We laugh on this spooky night.
1::halloweennight.objective: Build holiday/event vocabulary; identify objects and actions; build cultural awareness.
1::halloweennight.sor:
Before Reading:

Event Activation (Cultural Awareness)
“Do you know Halloween?”
“What do people do?”
Build background knowledge of the event.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: hat, light, treat, candy.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what happens on Halloween.”

During Reading:

Sequence of Events
“What happens first?”
“What happens next?”
Students follow actions (wear, carry, say, get).
Support understanding of event sequence.

Participation & Meaning
“Why do they say ‘Trick or treat’?”
Connect actions to purpose (getting candy).

Reread for Fluency
Repeat key sentences.
Build confidence with familiar phrases.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“I ___ on Halloween.”
Support action-based sentence building.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show spooky/fun.”
Students act out or express feelings.
Connect words to experience.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you do on Halloween?”
Build personal and cultural connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on night / Halloween.
Identify long vowel patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::halloweennight.questions_type: 1. What does the child wear? 2. What does the child carry? 3. What does the child say? 4. Who walks with the child? 5. Which words describe Halloween night? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What do YOU like about Halloween?

1::safetyfirst.title: Safety First

Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

“Let’s find safe and not safe choices.”

Students identify safe choices.
Build comparison and judgment.

“What happens when we choose safe?”

Support understanding of consequences.

Reread for Fluency
Repeat key phrases.
Build confidence and understanding.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“This is ___.”
Support identifying safe choices.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show stop/think.”
Students act out steps.
Connect words to actions.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What is a safe choice?”
Build real-life application.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on safe / first.
Identify long vowel and r-controlled patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::safetyfirst.questions_type: 1. What is safe? 2. What is not safe? 3. What should we do before choosing? 4. Why is safety important? 5. Which actions show safe choices? (skill) 6. What is the author’s message? (skill) 7. What safe choice do YOU make?
1::safetyfirst.answers: 1. Safe choice. 2. Not-safe action. 3. Stop and think. 4. Keeps us from harm. 5. Stop/think/choose safe way. 6. We should choose safe actions every day. 7. I choose ___ safely.
1::safetyfirst.extension: Safe/unsafe picture sort; decision tree; role-play

1::spookyhouses.answers: 1. Bright lights. 2. Candy. 3. People/kids. 4. Fun. 5. “Not really scary,” “fun night.” 6. Spooky houses can be fun, not scary. 7. Yes/No.
1::spookyhouses.extension: Costume drawing; candy sorting; “fun vs. scary” chart

1::thanksgivingsmells.title: Thanksgiving Smells
1::thanksgivingsmells.reader_text: “I smell a pie. I smell bread. I smell soup. I smell good food. I feel happy. I like the smell.”
1::thanksgivingsmells.objective: sensory vocabulary (smell); strengthen repeated pattern text; connect smell to emotion.
1::thanksgivingsmells.core_vocab_raw: smell, pie, bread, soup, food, feel, like
1::thanksgivingsmells.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Smell + Experience)
“What foods can you smell?”
“Do you like sweet or warm smells?”
Activate prior knowledge of smell.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: smell, pie, bread, soup.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Phonological Awareness Warm-Up
Focus on sm (smell).
Blend: /s/ /m/ → smell.

During Reading:

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build confidence with the repeated pattern “I smell ___.”

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
Focus on smell / soup.
Sound → blend → confirm with meaning.

Monitor Understanding (Feeling Connection)
“How do they feel?”
Students connect smells to happiness.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“I smell ___.”
“I feel ___.”
Support sensory + emotion language.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show sweet/hot/warm.”
Students act out or react.
Connect words to sensory meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What smells do you like?”
Build personal connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on smell.
Identify blend sm- and -ell pattern.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::thanksgivingsmells.reading_strategy: Picture walk; sensory TPR (sniffing); echo reading
1::thanksgivingsmells.reading_skill: Identifying senses; labeling food; connecting senses to feelings.
1::thanksgivingsmells.questions_type: 1. What does the person smell? 2. What makes them feel happy? 3. Which word tells you the sense used? (skill) 4. Tell me something YOU like to smell.
1::thanksgivingsmells.answers: 1. Pie, bread, soup. 2. The smells. 3. Smell. 4. I like to smell ___.
1::thanksgivingsmells.extension: Draw a picture of the food smelled; sort smell vs. touch; mime different smells.

1::bakingcookies.title: Baking Cookies
1::bakingcookies.reader_text: “We bake sweet cookies together. We use butter, flour, and sugar. We share them with our friends. We eat the delicious cookies. We smile and feel very happy. Baking cookies is so fun. We do it every winter.”
1::bakingcookies.objective: Build seasonal vocabulary; describe steps (bake → share → eat); connect actions to feelings.
1::bakingcookies.core_vocab_raw: bake, share, together, delicious, butter, flour, sugar
1::bakingcookies.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Cooking Experience)
“What do you bake?”
“What do you do after you bake?”
Activate prior knowledge of baking and sharing.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: bake, share, together, delicious.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Phonics Review (Taught Patterns)
Focus on bake (silent e) and cookies (short oo).
Read and blend the words.

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Sequence)
“What do they do first?”
“What do they do next?”
Students track bake → share → eat.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build smooth phrasing and confidence.

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
Focus on bake / cookies.
Sound → blend → connect to meaning.

After Reading:

Retell the Story (Sequence)
“First… Next… Then…”
Students describe baking steps.

Sentence Production
“We ___ cookies.”
Support action-based sentences.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show bake/share/eat.”
Students act out actions.
Connect words to meaning.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on bake / cookies.
Identify silent e and short vowel patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::bakingcookies.reading_strategy: Picture walk; step cards; echo reading
1::bakingcookies.questions_type: 1. What do they bake? 2. Who do they share cookies with? 3. What do they do after baking? 4. How do they feel? 5. Which actions show the steps? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. Do YOU like cookies?
1::bakingcookies.answers: 1. Cookies. 2. Friends. 3. Eat them. 4. Happy. 5. Bake/share/eat. 6. Baking cookies together is fun. 7. Yes/No.
1::bakingcookies.extension: Draw favorite cookie; simple pretend baking; sequence chart
1::bakingcookies.phonics_rule: long a (bake silent e), short oo (cookies)

1::icanwrite.title: I Can Write!
1::icanwrite.reader_text: “I can write my own name. I can write all the letters. I can write many new words. I can write short, fun stories. Writing is a great superpower. It helps me learn a lot.”
1::icanwrite.objective: Build early literacy identity; reinforce confidence in writing; introduce concept of “superpower.”
1::icanwrite.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Writing Experience)
“What can you write?”
“Can you write your name?”
Activate prior knowledge of writing.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: name, letter, word, story.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

High-Frequency Word Preview
Focus on can.
Say → tap sounds → read in sentence:
“I can write.”

During Reading:

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build confidence with the pattern “I can write…”

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
Focus on write / name / word.
Sound → blend → confirm meaning.

Monitor Understanding (Skill Growth)
“What can the child write?”
Students identify increasing skills (name → letters → words → stories).

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“I can write ___.”
Support expressing ability and confidence.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show name/letters/words.”
Students point or act out.
Connect words to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What can you write?”
Build personal confidence and identity.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on write.
Identify long i silent e pattern.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::icanwrite.reading_strategy: Echo reading; name-writing practice; picture walk
1::icanwrite.questions_type: 1. What can the child write first? 2. What else can the child write? 3. What kind of stories can the child write? 4. What does writing help the child do? 5. Which words show confidence? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What can YOU write?
1::icanwrite.answers: 1. Their name. 2. Letters/words. 3. Short, fun stories. 4. Learn a lot. 5. Great/superpower. 6. Writing is powerful and helpful. 7. I can write ___.
1::icanwrite.extension: Write name cards; alphabet tracing; “superpower writer” badge
1::icanwrite.phonics_rule: long i (write silent e), modal can

1::manysounds.title: Many Sounds
1::manysounds.reader_text: “This sound is too loud for me. This sound is very quiet. Sounds have different volumes. This sound is really so funny. This sound is nice to hear. Sounds have different qualities. I like to hear different sounds.”
1::manysounds.objective: Build sound-describing vocabulary; distinguish qualities; enhance auditory awareness.
1::manysounds.core_vocab_raw: sound, loud, quiet, funny, nice, volume, quality
1::manysounds.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Sound Awareness)
Teacher models: make a loud sound, then a quiet sound.
“Make loud/soft sounds.”
Students listen and repeat.
Build awareness of sound differences.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: sound, loud, funny, nice.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Phonological Awareness Warm-Up
Focus on quiet (/kw/).
Say and blend the beginning sound.

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Sound Qualities)
“Is this sound loud or quiet?”
“Is it funny or nice?”
Students identify and compare sounds.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build expression with descriptive sentences.

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
Focus on sound / quiet.
Sound → blend → connect to meaning.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“This sound is ___.”
Support describing using adjectives.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Make loud/soft sounds.”
Students produce sounds.
Connect words to sensory meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What sounds do you hear?”
Build personal awareness and expression.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on quiet / sounds.
Identify qu digraph and plural -s.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::manysounds.questions_type: 1. Which sound is too loud? 2. Which sound is quiet? 3. Which sound is funny? 4. What sound is nice to hear? 5. Which words describe sound qualities? (skill) 6. How are sounds different? (skill) 7. What sound do YOU like?
1::manysounds.answers: 1. Loud sound. 2. Quiet sound. 3. Funny sound. 4. Nice sound. 5. Loud/soft/funny/nice. 6. They have different qualities. 7. I like ___.
1::manysounds.extension: Sound matching; drawing sound sources; “sound guess” game

1::eggtime.title: Egg Time
1::eggtime.reader_text: “We get fresh eggs from the farm. We cook them in a hot pan. We eat them for our breakfast. We say yum, they're so good! Eggs are very healthy food.”
1::eggtime.objective: Build food-preparation vocabulary; identify sequence (get → cook → eat); introduce “healthy food.”
1::eggtime.core_vocab_raw: farm, fresh, cook, pan, eat, egg
1::eggtime.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Food & Routine)
“Where do eggs come from?”
“What do you do before you eat?”
Activate prior knowledge of food and cooking.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: farm, cook, pan, eat.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find how we make eggs.”

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Sequence)
“What do we do first?”
“What do we do next?”
Students track get → cook → eat.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build confidence with simple sentences.

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
Focus on egg / cook.
Sound → blend → connect to meaning.

After Reading:

Retell the Story (Sequence)
“First… Next… Then…”
Students describe the steps.

Sentence Production
“We ___ eggs.”
Support action-based sentences.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show get/cook/eat.”
Students act out actions.
Connect words to meaning.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on egg / time.
Identify short e and silent e patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::eggtime.reading_strategy: Picture walk; step cards; echo reading
1::eggtime.questions_type: 1. Where do they get the eggs? 2. What do they cook the eggs in? 3. When do they eat the eggs? 4. What do they say about the eggs? 5. Which steps happen first/next/last? (skill) 6. Why are eggs healthy? (skill) 7. What breakfast do YOU like?
1::eggtime.answers: 1. The farm. 2. A hot pan. 3. Breakfast. 4. Yum/so good. 5. Get→Cook→Eat. 6. Eggs give us healthy food. 7. I like ___.
1::eggtime.extension: Sequence cooking steps; egg drawing; sort “breakfast foods”
1::eggtime.phonics_rule: short e (egg), long i (time silent e)

1::look.title: Look!
1::look.reader_text: “This elephant looks big. This mouse looks small. This snake looks long. This ball looks round. The elephant wearing a dress looks silly. Look at the mouse! The mouse wearing a hat looks funny. Look at the snake! The snake wearing glasses looks smart. How do you look?”
1::look.objective: Build comparison vocabulary; identify attributes; connect adjectives to objects.
1::look.core_vocab_raw: look, big, small, long, round, funny, silly, glasses
1::look.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Attributes)
Teacher models with gestures: big/small/long/round.
“Show big/small/long/round.”
Students copy and identify.
Build understanding of describing words.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: look, big, small, long, round.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find how things look.”

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Attributes)
“How does it look?”
Students describe objects (big, small, long, round, silly, funny, smart).
Build descriptive language.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build expression with repeated structure.

Monitor Understanding (Comparison)
“Which one is big? Which one is small?”
Students compare objects.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“It looks ___.”
Support describing using adjectives.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show big/small/long/round.”
Students act out or point.
Connect words to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“How do you look?”
Build personal connection and expression.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on look.
Identify short oo vowel pattern.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::look.reading_strategy: Picture walk; adjective matching; echo reading
1::look.questions_type: 1. What animal looks big? 2. What animal looks small? 3. What looks long? 4. What looks round? 5. What words describe how things look? (skill) 6. How are these things different? (skill) 7. What is something YOU see that looks big or small?
1::look.answers: 1. Elephant. 2. Mouse. 3. Snake. 4. Ball. 5. Big/small/long/round. 6. Sizes and shapes differ. 7. I see a ___ that looks ___.
1::look.extension: Attribute-sorting mats; draw big/small items; shape hunt

1::ourbigbuilding.title: Our Big Building
1::ourbigbuilding.reader_text:
"We live in a building.
The building is tall.
We go up.
Up. Up. Up.
We see friends.
We say hello.
"Hello friends!"
It is our home."
1::ourbigbuilding.objective: Build community/place vocabulary; identify building parts; introduce metaphor (“like a village”).
1::ourbigbuilding.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Home & Buildings)
“Where do you live?”
“Do you go up in a building?”
Activate prior knowledge of homes and buildings.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: building, friend, home, elevator.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what we do in the building.”

During Reading:

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build rhythm with “Up. Up. Up.”

Text-Based Questions (Movement & Community)
“Where do we go?”
“Who do we see?”
Students identify actions (go up, see friends, say hello).

Monitor Understanding
“Where is their home?”
Students connect building to home.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“We go up ___.”
Support simple movement-based sentences.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show up/inside/hello.”
Students act out or respond.
Connect words to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“Who do you say hello to?”
Build community connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on big / building.
Identify short i and -ld blend.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::ourbigbuilding.reading_strategy: Picture walk; echo reading; building map
1::ourbigbuilding.questions_type: 1. Where do they live? 2. How do they go up? 3. Who do they see inside? 4. What do they do to their friends? 5. What does “like a village” mean? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. Who do YOU wave hello to?
1::ourbigbuilding.answers: 1. In a big building. 2. Elevator. 3. Friends. 4. Wave hello. 5. Many people live together. 6. Their building is full of friends and feels like a community. 7. I wave to ___.
1::ourbigbuilding.extension: Draw their building; label floors; community-friends chart

1::buttonseverywhere.title: Buttons Everywhere
1::buttonseverywhere.reader_text: “I see a round, red button. I see a square, blue button. This button is huge. That button is small. I like to collect buttons. I like to count buttons. I like to stack buttons. Buttons are on my clothes.”
1::buttonseverywhere.objective: Develop shape and attribute vocabulary; compare features; identify objects in clothing.
1::buttonseverywhere.core_vocab_raw: round, square, huge, button, collect, count, stack
1::buttonseverywhere.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Objects & Features)
“Where do you see buttons?”
“What shape are buttons?”
Activate prior knowledge of everyday objects.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: round, square, huge, button.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Phonics Review (Taught Patterns)
Focus on button (short u).
Read and blend the word.

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Attributes & Comparison)
“What shape is the button?”
“Which one is big? Which one is small?”
Students identify and compare features.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build confidence with patterned sentences.

Finger Tracking (Print Concepts)
Students point to each word while reading.
Reinforce left-to-right tracking.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“This button is ___.”
Support describing objects.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show round/square/big/small.”
Students act out or point.
Connect words to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“Where do you see buttons?”
Build real-world connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on buttons / everywhere.
Identify short u and vowel team patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::buttonseverywhere.reading_strategy: Picture walk; shape sort; echo labeling
1::buttonseverywhere.reading_skill: Identifying attributes; comparing size and shape
1::buttonseverywhere.questions_type: 1. What shape is the red button? 2. What shape is the blue button? 3. Which button is big? 4. Which button is small? 5. Which words describe size and color? (skill) 6. Where can you find buttons? (skill) 7. What buttons do YOU have on your clothes?
1::buttonseverywhere.answers: 1. Round. 2. Square. 3. “This” button. 4. “That” button. 5. Big/small/red/blue. 6. On clothes. 7. I have ___ buttons.
1::buttonseverywhere.extension: Button-sorting mat; draw a button; clothing-label search

1::pumpkinsatnight.title: Pumpkins at Night
1::pumpkinsatnight.reader_text: “I see a big, orange pumpkin. I see a bright, shining light. I see a funny, carved smile. I see kids having great fun. Pumpkins at night are cool.”
1::pumpkinsatnight.objective: Build fall/Halloween vocabulary; identify descriptive words; connect visuals to mood.
1::pumpkinsatnight.core_vocab_raw: pumpkin, bright, orange, shining, carved, cool
1::pumpkinsatnight.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Fall/Night Scene)
“What do you see at night in fall?”
“What do pumpkins look like?”
Activate prior knowledge of seasonal visuals.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: pumpkin, bright, orange, shining.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what we see at night.”

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Observation)
“What do you see?”
Students identify pumpkin, light, smile, kids.
Build observation skills.

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
Focus on pumpkin / night.
Sound → blend → connect to meaning.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build expression with descriptive phrases.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“I see ___.”
Support describing what is observed.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show big/bright/cool.”
Students act out or point.
Connect words to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you see at night?”
Build personal observation skills.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on pumpkin / night.
Identify short u and long i patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::pumpkinsatnight.reading_strategy: Picture walk; echo reading; color/shape discussion
1::pumpkinsatnight.questions_type: 1. What color is the pumpkin? 2. What shines at night? 3. What does the pumpkin have on it? 4. Who is having fun? 5. Which words describe how it looks at night? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. Do YOU like pumpkins?
1::pumpkinsatnight.answers: 1. Orange. 2. A shining light. 3. A carved smile. 4. The kids. 5. Big/bright/shining/cool. 6. Pumpkins look fun and bright at night. 7. Yes/No.
1::pumpkinsatnight.extension: Pumpkin drawing; face-carving shapes; light vs. dark chart

1::letslaugh.title: Let's Laugh!
1::letslaugh.reader_text:
“Knock, knock. Who's there? Interrupting cow. Interrupt-- MOOOOOOOOO -ing cow who?
You make me laugh so hard. You tell very funny jokes. I smile from ear to ear. We laugh together all the time. Laughing is good for us.”
1::letslaugh.objective: Build SEL vocabulary; reinforce social language around humor; identify actions and effects.
1::letslaugh.sor:
Before Reading:

Build Background Knowledge (Jokes & Humor)
“Do you know a joke?”
“What makes you laugh?”
Activate prior knowledge of humor.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: knock, laugh, funny, joke.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

High-Frequency Word Preview
Focus on who’s.
Say → break apart → read in sentence.

During Reading:

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Use expression for the joke and “MOOOOO.”

Monitor Understanding (Humor & Reaction)
“Why is it funny?”
Students connect joke to laughter.

Text-Based Questions (Feelings)
“How do they feel?”
Students identify happy, laughing.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“We laugh ___.”
Support describing how we laugh.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show laugh/smile/hard.”
Students act out expressions.
Connect words to feelings.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What makes you laugh?”
Build personal connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on laugh / who’s.
Identify vowel pattern au and contraction.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::letslaugh.reading_strategy: Echo reading; mirror play; feelings chart
1::letslaugh.reading_skill: Identifying emotions; connecting actions to feelings
1::letslaugh.questions_type: 1. What makes the child laugh? 2. What does the friend tell? 3. How does the child smile? 4. What is good for us? 5. Which words describe feelings? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What makes YOU laugh?
1::letslaugh.answers: 1. A friend. 2. A funny joke. 3. From ear to ear. 4. Laughing. 5. Laugh/ smile/funny. 6. Laughing together feels good. 7. ___ makes me laugh.
1::letslaugh.extension: Funny-face game; tell simple jokes; draw “laugh moments”
1::letslaugh.phonics_rule: short a (laugh vowel team au), contraction (who's)

1::wemakeasalad.title: We Make a Salad
1::wemakeasalad.reader_text: “We cut up the green lettuce. We add some red tomato pieces. We mix them together well. We eat the fresh, tasty salad. Making salad is easy and fun. The salad is colorful and bright.”

Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Phonics Review (Taught Patterns)
Focus on salad (short a CVC pattern).
Read and blend the word.

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Sequence)
“What do we do first?”
“What do we do next?”
Students track cut → add → mix → eat.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build smooth phrasing.

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
Focus on cut / mix / salad.
Sound → blend → connect to meaning.

After Reading:

Retell the Story (Sequence)
“First… Next… Then…”
Students describe how to make the salad.

Sentence Production
“We ___ the ___.”
Support action + object sentences.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show cut/add/mix.”
Students act out actions.
Connect words to meaning.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on make / salad.
Identify silent e and short vowel patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::wemakeasalad.reading_strategy: Picture walk; step cards; echo reading
1::wemakeasalad.questions_type: 1. What do they cut first? 2. What do they add? 3. What do they mix together? 4. What do they eat at the end? 5. Which words describe the salad? (skill) 6. What steps come first and last? (skill) 7. Have YOU made a salad?
1::wemakeasalad.answers: 1. Lettuce. 2. Tomato pieces. 3. Lettuce and tomatoes. 4. Salad. 5. Fresh/tasty/colorful/bright. 6. First cut, last eat. 7. Yes/No.

1::brave.title: Brave
1::brave.reader_text: “This dark room is a little scary. This sunny park is not scary. This is fun for me to do. We smile and play together. I can be very brave today. I try new things with my friends. Being brave makes me feel strong.”
1::brave.objective: Build emotion vocabulary; contrast scary/not scary; promote SEL bravery concepts.

Teach: dark, sunny, scary, brave.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what is scary and what is not.”

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Contrast & Feelings)
“Is this scary or not scary?”
Students identify and compare situations.

Monitor Understanding (Emotion → Action)
“What does the child do?”
Students connect being brave to trying new things.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build expression with feeling words.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“This is ___.”
Support describing feelings.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show scary/not scary.”
Students act out emotions.
Connect words to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“When are you brave?”
Build personal SEL connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on brave.
Identify long a silent e pattern.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::brave.reading_strategy: Picture walk; emotion sorting; echo reading

1::littlelizard.title: Little Lizard
1::littlelizard.reader_text: “This is a small, green lizard. It is very fast and quick. It can run across the rocks. It can hide in small places. The lizard climbs up the wall. I like to follow it with my eyes.”
1::littlelizard.objective: Build animal movement vocabulary; reinforce descriptive features; introduce observation skills.
1::littlelizard.core_vocab_raw: lizard, small, fast, quick, hide, climb

“How do you show kindness?”

Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find how sharing makes people feel.”

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Cause & Effect)
“What happens when we share?”
Students connect actions (make, give) to outcomes (smiles, joy).

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build expression with feeling words.

Monitor Understanding (Feelings)
“How do they feel?”
Students identify happy and joy.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“I give ___.”
Support expressing kind actions.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show share/give/kind.”
Students act out actions.
Connect words to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What can you share?”
Build personal connection and SEL.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on sharing / hearts.
Identify -ar and r-controlled vowel patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.
1::littlelizard.questions_type: 1. What color is the lizard? 2. How does it move? 3. Where can it run? 4. Where does it hide? 5. Which words describe how fast it is? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What animal do YOU like to watch?
1::littlelizard.answers: 1. Green. 2. Fast/quick. 3. Across rocks. 4. In small places. 5. Fast/quick. 6. Lizards move quickly and hide well. 7. I like to watch ___.

1::sharinghearts.title: Sharing Hearts
1::sharinghearts.reader_text: “I make a nice, kind card. I give it to my best friend. I see happy, big smiles. Love is all around us now. Sharing makes us feel happy. We like to give kind gifts. Sharing brings joy to everyone.”
1::sharinghearts.objective: Strengthen kindness vocabulary; reinforce cause/effect (sharing → joy); SEL-based comprehension.

1::sharinghearts.extension: Make simple cards; kindness chain; draw a “sharing moment”

1::closeandfar.title: Close and Far
1::closeandfar.reader_text: “The black cat is very close. The high hill is very far. The little bird is quite near. The big moon is far away. Distance can be so different. I learn what near and far mean.”
1::closeandfar.objective: Build spatial comparison vocabulary; reinforce opposites (near/far); introduce early measurement concepts.

Build Background Knowledge (Distance Concepts)

“Show close/far.”
Students point and respond.

Teach: close, far, near, away.
Teach key words using SoR principles:
Student-friendly definitions
Picture support
Example + nonexample (if useful)


During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Comparison)
“Is it near or far?”
Students identify and compare distance.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build smooth phrasing.

Monitor Understanding

Check comprehension of spatial words.

Sentence Production
“It is ___.”

“Show near/far.”
Students act out or point.
Connect words to meaning.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on close / far.
Identify long o and r-controlled vowel patterns.
Practice blending and segmenting.

1::closeandfar.questions_type: 1. Which animal is close? 2. What is far? 3. What is near? 4. What is far away in the sky? 5. Which words show distance? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. Show something close to YOU.

1::closeandfar.extension: Near/far object sort; “walk closer/farther” game; draw near/far scenes
1::closeandfar.syntax_focus: Simple compare structure (“___ is close. ___ is far.”)

1::animalvoices.title: Animal Voices
1::animalvoices.reader_text: “The big cow says, "moo". The happy dog says, "woof". The small duck says, "quack". The soft cat says, "meow". The sheep says, "baa". The bird says, "tweet". Animals have their own voices. I like to hear animal sounds.”

Teach key words using SoR principles with visuals.

Teacher reads → students repeat.

1::animalvoices.questions_type: 1. What sound does the cow make? 2. What sound does the dog make? 3. What sound does the duck make? 4. What sound does the cat make? 5. Which words describe the sounds? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What animal sound do YOU like?
1::animalvoices.answers: 1. Moo. 2. Woof. 3. Quack. 4. Meow. 5. Loud/happy/small/soft. 6. Animals have different voices. 7. I like ___.

1::howanimalsmove.title: How Animals Move
1::howanimalsmove.reader_text: “The fish swims in the water. The bird flies in the sky. The horse runs on the land. The frog hops on a log. The cat jumps on a chair. Animals move in different ways. Each animal moves in its own way. I like to watch them move.”
1::howanimalsmove.objective: Build movement vocabulary across animals; compare actions; reinforce categorization.

“Show swim/fly/run/jump.”
Students copy and respond.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)

“The ___ ___.”
Students describe animal movement.

Categorization Task

Support comparison skills.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show swim/fly/run/jump.”
Students act out movements.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on move / how.
Identify long vowel patterns.

1::howanimalsmove.questions_type: 1. How does a fish move? 2. Where does the bird fly? 3. What does the horse do? 4. What does the frog do? 5. Which words tell how animals move? (skill) 6. How are movements different? (skill) 7. Which animal movement do YOU like?
1::howanimalsmove.answers: 1. Swims. 2. In the sky. 3. Runs. 4. Jumps. 5. Swims/flies/runs/jumps. 6. Different animals move differently. 7. I like when animals ___.
1::howanimalsmove.extension: Movement matching cards; draw each movement; TPR game

1::cleanuptime.title: Clean Up Time
1::cleanuptime.reader_text: “I pick up all my toys. I pick up all my books. I pick up my dirty clothes. I wipe my dresser. I sweep my floor. Now my room is very clean. A clean room is so nice. I can find things easily now. I feel happy in my clean room.”
1::cleanuptime.objective: Build routine vocabulary; reinforce sequencing (pick up → clean → find); support independence skills.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)

Teach key words using SoR principles with visuals.

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Sequence)

Teacher reads → students repeat.

1::timeforschool.title: Time for School
1::timeforschool.reader_text: “It is time to learn new things. It is time to read books. It is time to play outside. It is time to go home. School time is busy and fun. I like my school day. I learn something new every day.”

1::youandi.title: You and I
1::youandi.reader_text: “You and I walk to school together. You and I talk a lot every day. You and I laugh loudly at the park. You and I play fun games in your kitchen. We are the best of friends. We help each other every day. Being together feels good. We have a nice friendship.”

1::letsgo.title: Let's Go!

1::busride.title: Bus Ride
1::busride.reader_text: “We wait for the big bus. We get on the bus together. We sit down on the seats. We go to our school now. The bus ride is always fun. We talk quietly on the way. I like riding the bus.”

1::snowydayfun.title: Snowy Day Fun

“Who has a few?”

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build smooth phrasing.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“This ___ has ___ spots.”
Support describing animals.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show many/few, brown/orange/black.”
Students point or act out.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“Which animal do you like?”
Build personal connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on spot.
Identify short o and plural -s.
1::spottedanimals.reading_strategy: Picture walk; color-spot sort; echo reading
1::spottedanimals.questions_type: 1. What animal has brown spots? 2. What animal has orange spots? 3. How many spots does the ladybug have? 4. Why are spots special? 5. Which words tell how many/what color? (skill) 6. How are the animals different? (skill) 7. Which spotted animal do YOU like?
1::spottedanimals.answers: 1. Dog. 2. Fish. 3. Seven. 4. They look special. 5. Brown/orange/black/many/few/seven. 6. They have different spot colors/counts. 7. I like ___.
1::spottedanimals.extension: Spot-pattern draw; “count the spots” game; pattern-matching

1::animaltails.title: Animal Tails
1::animaltails.reader_text: “The cat has a very long tail. The bird has a very short tail. The monkey has a very strong tail. The tail helps them to move. Tails are useful for animals. Every tail has a special job. I like to watch animals use their tails.”
1::animaltails.objective: Build descriptive vocabulary focusing on length, strength, and function; compare animal features.
1::animaltails.sor:
Before Reading:

Phonological Awareness Warm-Up
Focus on tail → /t/ /ai/ /l/
Blend sounds orally.
Students repeat and segment.

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find how tails are different.”
Focus on comparing animals.

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
Focus on tail / long / short
Sound → blend → confirm meaning.

Text-Based Questions (Comparison & Function)
“Which tail is long?”
“What does the tail do?”
Students compare and connect to use.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“The ___ has a ___ tail.”
Students describe animals.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show long/short/strong.”
Students act out or point.

Identify the Central Idea
“What are tails for?”
Students explain simple function.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on tail.
Identify long ai vowel team.
1::animaltails.reading_strategy: Picture walk; compare/contrast chart; echo reading
1::animaltails.questions_type: 1. What kind of tail does the cat have? 2. What kind of tail does the bird have? 3. What kind of tail does the monkey have? 4. What does the tail help animals do? 5. Which describing words tell about tails? (skill) 6. How do tails help animals? (skill) 7. Which tail do YOU think is interesting?
1::animaltails.answers: 1. Long. 2. Short. 3. Strong. 4. Move. 5. Long/short/strong/special/useful. 6. They help animals move/do jobs. 7. I like the ___ tail.
1::animaltails.extension: Tail-matching pictures; role-play movement; “guess the animal”

1::cityrain.title: City Rain
1::cityrain.reader_text: “It rains on the tall roof. It rains on the red car. It rains on the green tree. It rains on my blue umbrella. The city is wet and quiet. I hear the raindrops fall. Rain makes the city feel calm.”
1::cityrain.objective: Build weather/place vocabulary; connect colors + locations; support visualization.
1::cityrain.core_vocab_raw: city, rain, roof, umbrella, quiet, calm
1::cityrain.sor:
Before Reading:

High-Frequency Word Preview
Focus on on.
Say → tap sounds → read in phrase:
“It rains on…”

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find where the rain falls.”

During Reading:

Finger Tracking for Print Concepts
Students point to each word while reading.
Reinforce left-to-right tracking.

Text-Based Questions (Visualization)
“Where does it rain?”
Students name roof, car, tree, umbrella.
Build mental image.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“It rains on ___.”
Students describe locations.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show roof/car/tree/umbrella.”
Students point or act out.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“Where do you see rain?”
Build personal connection.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on rain / city.
Identify ai vowel team and short i.
1::cityrain.reading_strategy: Picture walk; sound effects; echo reading
1::cityrain.questions_type: 1. What does the rain fall on? 2. What color is the car? 3. What color is the umbrella? 4. How does the city feel? 5. Which words help you picture the scene? (skill) 6. What is the setting of the story? (skill) 7. What do YOU like to do on rainy days?
1::cityrain.answers: 1. Roof/car/tree/umbrella. 2. Red. 3. Blue. 4. Calm/quiet. 5. Tall/red/green/blue/wet/quiet. 6. A rainy city. 7. I like to ___.

1::makingapplesauce.title: Making Applesauce
1::makingapplesauce.reader_text: “We wash the big, red apples. We cut the apples into pieces. We cook them in a big pot. We eat the warm, sweet applesauce. Making applesauce is easy and fun. It smells so good while it cooks. I want to make more soon.”
1::makingapplesauce.objective: Build sequencing language (wash → cut → cook → eat); develop cooking vocabulary; strengthen procedural comprehension.
1::makingapplesauce.core_vocab_raw: wash, cook, cut, eat, make, pot, applesauce
1::makingapplesauce.sor:
Before Reading:

Phonics Review (Taught Patterns)
Focus on make / sauce.
Read and blend words with silent e and au.
Students repeat and read.

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find how to make applesauce.”

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Sequence)
“What do we do first?”
“What do we do next?”
Students track wash → cut → cook → eat.

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
Focus on wash / cook / cut.
Sound → blend → connect to meaning.

After Reading:

Retell the Story (Sequence)
“First… Next… Then…”
Students describe steps.

Sentence Production
“We ___ the apples.”
Support action + object sentences.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show wash/cut/cook.”
Students act out actions.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on make / sauce.
Identify silent e and au vowel team.
1::makingapplesauce.questions_type: 1. What do they wash? 2. What do they cut the apples into? 3. Where do they cook the apples? 4. What do they eat at the end? 5. What steps come first/next/last? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What food do YOU like to help make?
1::makingapplesauce.answers: 1. Apples. 2. Pieces. 3. A pot. 4. Applesauce. 5. Wash → cut → cook → eat. 6. They make applesauce. 7. I like to make ___.
1::makingapplesauce.extension: Picture-step cards; pretend cooking; smell exploration
1::makingapplesauce.phonics_rule: long a (make silent e), vowel team au (sauce)

1::babybirdsinthenest.title: Baby Birds in the Nest
1::babybirdsinthenest.reader_text:
Three birds sleep.
Good morning birds.
Chirp. Chirp.
They want food.
Mama bird brings food.
They eat worms.
The birds are big.
They fly away.
The nest is quiet.
1::babybirdsinthenest.objective: Build life-cycle vocabulary; support sequencing; introduce early inferencing about growth.
1::babybirdsinthenest.sor:
Before Reading:

Phonological Awareness Warm-Up
Focus on chirp → /ch/ /ir/ /p/
Blend sounds orally.
Students repeat and segment.

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what happens to the birds.”

During Reading:

Monitor Understanding (Sequence)
“What happens first?”
Students track sleep → eat → grow → fly.

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
Focus on bird / nest.
Sound → blend → connect to meaning.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.

After Reading:

Retell the Story (Sequence)
“First… Next… Then…”
Students describe bird growth.

Sentence Production
“Three birds ___.”
Support simple sentence use.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show small/little/hungry/big.”
Students act out or point.

Identify the Central Idea
“What happens to the birds?”
Students explain they grow and fly.
1::babybirdsinthenest.reading_strategy: Picture walk; sequencing chart; echo read
1::babybirdsinthenest.reading_skill: Sequencing stages; identifying change over time
1::babybirdsinthenest.questions_type: 1. What are the small birds doing? 2. What do the little birds want? 3. What do the hungry birds eat? 4. What do the big birds do? 5. Which words show the birds are changing? (skill) 6. What happens last? (skill) 7. What baby animal have YOU seen?
1::babybirdsinthenest.answers: 1. Sleeping. 2. Food. 3. Worms. 4. Fly away. 5. Small/little/hungry/big/grown-up. 6. The birds fly on their own. 7. I saw a ___.
1::babybirdsinthenest.extension: Life-cycle cards; nest-building craft; sequencing retell
1::babybirdsinthenest.phonics_rule: long a (baby), r-controlled ir (birds), short e (nest)

1::bigandsmallweights.title: Big and Small Weights
1::bigandsmallweights.reader_text: “This balloon is very light. This rock is very heavy. This leaf is also light. This log is heavy. Light, light light. Heavy, heavy, heavy. Light and heavy are opposites. I can lift light things. I cannot lift heavy things. What animals are light? What animals are heavy?”
1::bigandsmallweights.objective: Build comparison vocabulary (light vs. heavy); introduce opposites; support concrete classification.
1::bigandsmallweights.core_vocab_raw: light, heavy, balloon, rock, leaf, log, lift
1::bigandsmallweights.sor:
Before Reading:

High-Frequency Word Preview
Focus on is / can.
Say → tap sounds → read: “This is… I can…”

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what is light and what is heavy.”

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Opposites)
“Is it light or heavy?”
Students identify and compare items.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build rhythm with repeated words.

Monitor Understanding
“Can you lift it?”
Students connect meaning to action.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“This ___ is ___.”
Students describe objects.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show heavy/light.”
Students act out lifting.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What is heavy for you?”
Build real-world thinking.

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on light / heavy.
Identify vowel patterns and blending.
1::bigandsmallweights.reading_strategy: Real objects; picture walk; echo reading
1::bigandsmallweights.questions_type: 1. Which object is light? 2. Which object is heavy? 3. What can you lift? 4. What can you NOT lift? 5. Which words show opposites? (skill) 6. What is the main idea? (skill) 7. What light thing can YOU lift?
1::bigandsmallweights.answers: 1. Balloon/leaf. 2. Rock/log. 3. Light things. 4. Heavy things. 5. Light/heavy. 6. Light and heavy are opposites. 7. I can lift ___.
1::bigandsmallweights.extension: Object-sorting; lift-test activity; draw light vs. heavy
1::bigandsmallweights.phonics_rule: short i (big), blend sm- (small), long a (weights vowel team ei)
1::bigandsmallweights.syntax_focus: Compare/contrast structure (“___ is big. ___ is small.”)

1::thingsthatfly.title: Things That Fly
1::thingsthatfly.reader_text: “The bird flies in the blue sky. The plane flies up very high. The kite flies on the wind. The balloon flies away too. Many things can fly around. I like to watch things in the sky.”
1::thingsthatfly.objective: Build action vocabulary (flies); categorize flying objects; support early science classification.
1::thingsthatfly.sor:
Before Reading:

Phonics Review (Taught Patterns)
Focus on fly / high.
Read and blend long i words.

High-Frequency Word Preview
Focus on the.
Read in phrase: “The bird flies…”

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what can fly.”

During Reading:

Prompt Students to Decode (NOT Guess)
Focus on fly / plane / kite.
Sound → blend → confirm meaning.

Text-Based Questions (Categorization)
“What can fly?”
Students name bird, plane, kite, balloon.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“The ___ flies ___.”
Students describe objects and movement.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show fly/high.”
Students act out movement.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you see in the sky?”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on fly / around.
Identify long i and vowel patterns.
1::thingsthatfly.reading_strategy: Picture walk; TPR (“fly” motions); echo reading
1::thingsthatfly.questions_type: 1. What flies in the sky? 2. How does the plane fly? 3. What flies on the wind? 4. What flies away? 5. What do all these things do? (skill) 6. What is the setting? (skill) 7. What flying thing do YOU like?

1::littlelights.title: Little Lights
1::littlelights.reader_text: “I see a small, bright firefly. It glows in the dark night. It flies around in the dark. It shines so very brightly. Fireflies are like little magic. I try to follow them with my eyes. I like to watch them glow.”
1::littlelights.objective: Build descriptive vocabulary around light in darkness; support visualization; develop sensory language.
1::littlelights.sor:
Before Reading:

Phonological Awareness Warm-Up
Focus on glow → /g/ /l/ /ō/
Blend sounds orally.

Vocabulary Preview (3–4 Words)
Teach: firefly, glow, shine, bright.
Use visuals and simple examples.

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what glows in the dark.”

During Reading:

Integrate Decoding + Meaning
Focus on glow / shine.
Sound → blend → connect to meaning.

Text-Based Questions (Visualization)
“What do you see in the dark?”
Students describe firefly, light, movement.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“It ___ in the dark.”
Students describe actions.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show glow/shine/dark.”
Students act out or point.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“What do you see at night?”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on light / little.
Identify long i and short i patterns.
1::littlelights.reading_strategy: Picture walk; dark/light compare; echo reading
1::littlelights.questions_type: 1. What does the firefly do? 2. When does it glow? 3. How does the firefly shine? 4. Why does the child watch them? 5. Which words help you see the picture in your mind? (skill) 6. What is the setting? (skill) 7. Have YOU seen lights at night?
1::littlelights.answers: 1. Glows/flies. 2. At night/in the dark. 3. Brightly. 4. Because they are pretty/magic. 5. Small/bright/dark/glow/shine. 6. Outside at night. 7. Yes/I saw ___.
1::littlelights.extension: Glow-stick play; night-sounds compare; draw a firefly

1::messyme.title: Messy Me
1::messyme.reader_text: “I paint with both my hands. I dig in the wet sand. I play in the warm rain. Then I clean up again. Getting messy is so fun. I smile when I make a mess. Messy days are happy days.”

1::messyme.sor:
Before Reading:

High-Frequency Word Preview
Focus on I.
Say → read in phrase: “I paint. I dig.”

Phonics Review (Taught Patterns)
Focus on messy / me.
Read and blend short e and long e.

Set a Purpose for Reading
“Let’s find what happens when we get messy.”

During Reading:

Text-Based Questions (Sequence)
“What do we do first?”
Students track paint → dig → play → clean.

Echo Reading for Fluency
Teacher reads → students repeat.
Build rhythm with action sentences.

Monitor Understanding (Contrast)
“Are we messy or clean now?”
Students identify change.

After Reading:

Sentence Production
“I ___ with my hands.”
Students describe actions.

Vocabulary Reinforcement
“Show messy/clean/wet/warm.”
Students act out meanings.

Simple Text-to-Self Connection
“Do you like messy play?”

Word Work: Phonics Transfer
Focus on messy / me.
Identify short e and long e.
1::messyme.reading_strategy: Picture walk; action-motion TPR; echo reading
1::messyme.questions_type: 1. What does the child paint with? 2. What does the child dig in? 3. Where does the child play? 4. What does the child do after getting messy? 5. Which words tell how things feel? (skill) 6. What happens first/next/last? (skill) 7. What messy thing do YOU like to do?
1::messyme.answers: 1. Both hands. 2. Wet sand. 3. Warm rain. 4. Clean up. 5. Wet/warm/sand/messy. 6. Paint → dig → play → clean. 7. I like to ___.
1::messyme.extension: Messy-art simulation; sensory table; sequencing cards

1::mytwopets.title: My Two Pets
1::mytwopets.reader_text: “I have two small, cute pets. They run around the whole house. They play with each other. They wiggle. They nibble. They cuddle. I love my pets so much. They are my best friends. I feed them every day. They make me laugh a lot.”
